hypothesis: For next-day spare-parts demand forecasting at a regional warehouse network, a temporal fusion transformer trained on part-level demand histories plus maintenance event logs and weather features will reduce weighted mean absolute percentage error more than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on lagged aggregate features.
domain: Transportation/logistics — forecasting daily spare-parts demand for transit bus maintenance depots and centralized parts warehouses
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBDT for Next-Day Spare-Parts Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 36 months of daily ERP part-withdrawal records (January 2021 through December 2023) covering all (depot, SKU) pairs in the regional warehouse network. This window is long enough to capture at least two full annual maintenance cycles, including seasonal variation in bus usage and weather-driven demand spikes. Linked data sources include vehicle work-order logs from the CMMS, supplier lead-time records, and daily weather observations (temperature, precipitation) pulled from NOAA for each depot's geolocation.

Labels are defined as next-day unit withdrawals per depot-SKU pair, taken directly from ERP inventory system records. Zero-demand days are retained as valid observations — they constitute the majority of records for intermittent SKUs and must be modeled explicitly. Negative adjustments (part returns) are floored to zero in the target variable. Known data quality risks include ERP recording lags and bulk-withdrawal batching; these are addressed by auditing for suspiciously large single-day spikes (>5 standard deviations above trailing 90-day mean per SKU) and flagging them for manual review before inclusion.

The data distribution matches the deployment target because it comes from the same production systems and depot network where forecasts will be consumed.

### 2. Split Strategy

We apply a strict chronological split: **months 1–27 (Jan 2021–Mar 2023) for training**, **months 28–33 (Apr–Sep 2023) for validation**, and **months 34–36 (Oct–Dec 2023) as the held-out test set**.

A random or stratified split is not appropriate here. Demand for spare parts is autocorrelated across time; random sampling would allow the model to interpolate between known future demand values rather than extrapolate from history, producing optimistic and misleading error estimates. Temporal splitting faithfully simulates the production use case, where a forecast issued today can only use past observations. The 6-month validation window spans a seasonal transition, providing a representative tuning signal. The 3-month test window covers autumn — a high-maintenance season — and includes neither training nor validation data at any time point.

Cross-series leakage is also avoided: no depot-SKU series appears simultaneously in training and test at overlapping timestamps.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive an identical feature set: lagged demand (t-1, t-2, t-3, t-7, t-14, t-28), rolling 7- and 28-day demand statistics, calendar indicators, weather covariates (daily temperature extremes, precipitation), maintenance event signals (scheduled PMs due within 3 days, open unscheduled work-order counts), part metadata (family, criticality tier, vehicle model compatibility), and most-recent supplier lead time. The TFT additionally processes demand history as a sequence input and uses maintenance/weather features as known-future covariates.

**All preprocessing transformers are fit on the training set only** and applied without modification to validation and test. This applies to scalers, target encoders for categorical fields, and imputation strategies for sparse supplier lead-time records.

No feature encodes the label or any future demand value: lagged features use only information available at forecast time t, and future covariates (scheduled PM events) are restricted to those derivable from the maintenance schedule rather than observed outcomes.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a **Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)** trained with quantile loss (0.1, 0.5, 0.9 quantiles). The baseline is a **LightGBM gradient-boosted tree** trained for direct one-step-ahead point forecasting.

The baseline is a fair and strong comparator: it receives the identical feature set at the same granularity, and its hyperparameters are tuned with the same 30-trial random search budget on the same validation metric. This controls for tuning effort asymmetry, which is a common source of unfair comparisons in architecture benchmarks.

To isolate the contribution of the sequence architecture from exogenous covariates, a secondary ablation trains the TFT on lagged demand only (no weather or maintenance features) while the GBDT retains the full feature set. If the TFT advantage disappears or reverses in this ablation, the gain is attributable to feature richness rather than the transformer architecture.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is RMSE**, computed across all depot-SKU-day observations on the held-out test set. RMSE is appropriate because it penalizes larger misses more strongly, which is desirable when underestimating demand can create operational disruption on high-usage parts.

Secondary metrics include unweighted MAE (volume-agnostic sanity check), mean signed bias stratified by criticality tier (to detect directional forecasting asymmetries), a stockout proxy rate for tier-1 critical parts (fraction of days where forecast falls more than 20% below actuals), and WMAPE stratified by newly introduced vehicle model cohorts and demand sparsity class. These stratified evaluations directly test the generalization claim in the hypothesis.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection occurs on the validation set using validation WMAPE as the selection criterion. The TFT uses early stopping with patience of 10 epochs monitored on validation WMAPE. The GBDT uses the same 30-trial random search evaluated on the same validation window. No test-set information influences the main tuning loop.

After the initial validation-based tuning, we evaluate a small number of final candidate configurations on the held-out test set and report the best-performing variant as the final model. This serves as a final check that the selected architecture is robust under the actual deployment-period distribution.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit design controls:

- **Feature access parity:** Both models receive identical features; the GBDT is not disadvantaged by receiving only aggregate-level inputs.
- **Exogenous signal isolation:** The ablation described above separates the effect of the sequence architecture from the effect of richer covariates.
- **Aggregation granularity:** Both models operate at the depot-SKU-day level; no hierarchical aggregation is applied to one model but not the other.

The experiment establishes whether the TFT reduces WMAPE relative to a well-tuned GBDT on this specific data configuration, generalizing to new vehicle model cohorts and seasonal patterns within the same agency. It does not establish that transformers universally outperform GBDTs on intermittent demand forecasting, nor does it address multi-step forecasting horizons or generalization across agencies with different maintenance practices.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is RMSE instead of WMAPE weighted by criticality and demand volume. This misaligns with the operational goal: the business case prioritizes forecast accuracy on high-criticality and high-volume parts, but RMSE treats all unit errors equally and will be dominated by large-count SKUs regardless of operational importance. The weighted formulation in the sound design appropriately reflects business impact; the change to RMSE invalidates the claimed success criterion.

The test set is used to select among final candidate configurations, violating the principle of a held-out evaluation. This introduces optimistic bias: the test set no longer measures generalization to unseen data since it actively informs model selection. The correct protocol evaluates the test set exactly once, after all decisions are frozen, to simulate true forward deployment.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of WMAPE as a secondary metric (rather than primary) indicates a problem, since the hypothesis is about reducing WMAPE.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Although WMAPE is demoted to a secondary status in the corrupted design, it was correctly the primary metric in the sound reference. The issue is not that WMAPE appears as secondary, but that RMSE incorrectly serves as the primary decision metric. A reviewer who objects solely to WMAPE's placement misunderstands the hierarchy of evaluation and risks false critique.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological split is too long for training, potentially causing the test set to reflect an outdated distribution.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The split is appropriate: 27 months of training covers two full operational cycles, and the 3-month autumn test window captures a high-maintenance period relevant for deployment. The temporal split aligns with real-world forecasting conditions and avoids leakage. Concerns about distribution shift are addressed via stratified evaluation on new vehicle cohorts.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the primary metric (RMSE) does not reflect the operational objective of minimizing high-impact forecast errors weighted by criticality and volume, and correctly argues that WMAPE weighted by (criticality × demand) should be the primary metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that RMSE lacks business alignment and would fail to prioritize errors on tier-1 parts even if demand volume is low, contradicting the success metric context.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that using the test set to evaluate multiple candidate configurations contaminates the held-out evaluation and inflates generalization performance, and insists that the test set must be used exactly once after all decisions are frozen.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic references best practices in machine learning evaluation, explaining that any use of test data to select among configurations turns it into a validation set, undermining its value as an unbiased estimator of forward performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes with agreement that both flaws must be corrected — replacing RMSE with WMAPE as the primary metric and enforcing a single, final test evaluation — for the experiment to validly test the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Both parties acknowledge that the design as presented cannot support a valid conclusion about WMAPE improvement due to misaligned metric and evaluation contamination.

## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBDT for Next-Day Spare-Parts Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

Labels are defined as next-day unit withdrawals per depot-SKU pair, taken directly from ERP inventory system records. Zero-demand days are retained as valid observations — they constitute the majority of records for intermittent SKUs and must be modeled explicitly. Negative adjustments (part returns) are floored to zero in the target variable. Known data quality risks include ERP recording lags and bulk-withdrawal batching; these are addressed by auditing for suspiciously large single-day spikes (>5 standard deviations above trailing 90-day mean per SKU) and flagging them for manual review before inclusion.

The data distribution matches the deployment target because it comes from the same production systems and depot network where forecasts will be consumed.

### 2. Split Strategy

We apply a strict chronological split: **months 1–27 (Jan 2021–Mar 2023) for training**, **months 28–33 (Apr–Sep 2023) for validation**, and **months 34–36 (Oct–Dec 2023) as the held-out test set**.

A random or stratified split is not appropriate here. Demand for spare parts is autocorrelated across time; random sampling would allow the model to interpolate between known future demand values rather than extrapolate from history, producing optimistic and misleading error estimates. Temporal splitting faithfully simulates the production use case, where a forecast issued today can only use past observations. The 6-month validation window spans a seasonal transition, providing a representative tuning signal. The 3-month test window covers autumn — a high-maintenance season — and includes neither training nor validation data at any time point.

Cross-series leakage is also avoided: no depot-SKU series appears simultaneously in training and test at overlapping timestamps.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive an identical feature set: lagged demand (t-1, t-2, t-3, t-7, t-14, t-28), rolling 7- and 28-day demand statistics, calendar indicators, weather covariates (daily temperature extremes, precipitation), maintenance event signals (scheduled PMs due within 3 days, open unscheduled work-order counts), part metadata (family, criticality tier, vehicle model compatibility), and most-recent supplier lead time. The TFT additionally processes demand history as a sequence input and uses maintenance/weather features as known-future covariates.

**All preprocessing transformers are fit on the training set only** and applied without modification to validation and test. This applies to scalers, target encoders for categorical fields, and imputation strategies for sparse supplier lead-time records.

No feature encodes the label or any future demand value: lagged features use only information available at forecast time t, and future covariates (scheduled PM events) are restricted to those derivable from the maintenance schedule rather than observed outcomes.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a **Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)** trained with quantile loss (0.1, 0.5, 0.9 quantiles). The baseline is a **LightGBM gradient-boosted tree** trained for direct one-step-ahead point forecasting.

The baseline is a fair and strong comparator: it receives the identical feature set at the same granularity, and its hyperparameters are tuned with the same 30-trial random search budget on the same validation metric. This controls for tuning effort asymmetry, which is a common source of unfair comparisons in architecture benchmarks.

To isolate the contribution of the sequence architecture from exogenous covariates, a secondary ablation trains the TFT on lagged demand only (no weather or maintenance features) while the GBDT retains the full feature set. If the TFT advantage disappears or reverses in this ablation, the gain is attributable to feature richness rather than the transformer architecture.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Weighted MAPE (WMAPE)**, where weights are proportional to each observation's criticality tier multiplied by observed demand volume, normalized across all test-set observations. This directly maps to the operational objective: errors on high-criticality, high-volume parts matter more than errors on slow-moving or low-priority SKUs. Standard MAPE is excluded because it is undefined or numerically unstable on zero-demand days; the weighted formulation anchors error to observed volume.

Secondary metrics include unweighted MAE (volume-agnostic sanity check), mean signed bias stratified by criticality tier (to detect directional forecasting asymmetries), a stockout proxy rate for tier-1 critical parts (fraction of days where forecast falls more than 20% below actuals), and WMAPE stratified by newly introduced vehicle model cohorts and demand sparsity class. These stratified evaluations directly test the generalization claim in the hypothesis.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection occurs on the validation set using validation WMAPE as the selection criterion. The TFT uses early stopping with patience of 10 epochs monitored on validation WMAPE. The GBDT uses the same 30-trial random search evaluated on the same validation window. No test-set information influences any modeling decision.

The **test set is evaluated exactly once**, after all decisions are frozen. Results are reported as final. No re-tuning or re-training follows test-set evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit design controls:

- **Feature access parity:** Both models receive identical features; the GBDT is not disadvantaged by receiving only aggregate-level inputs.
- **Exogenous signal isolation:** The ablation described above separates the effect of the sequence architecture from the effect of richer covariates.
- **Aggregation granularity:** Both models operate at the depot-SKU-day level; no hierarchical aggregation is applied to one model but not the other.

The experiment establishes whether the TFT reduces WMAPE relative to a well-tuned GBDT on this specific data configuration, generalizing to new vehicle model cohorts and seasonal patterns within the same agency. It does not establish that transformers universally outperform GBDTs on intermittent demand forecasting, nor does it address multi-step forecasting horizons or generalization across agencies with different maintenance practices.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because one flaw (metric mismatch) is subtle and domain-specific, requiring understanding of operational priorities in intermittent-demand forecasting, while the second (test-set reuse) is conceptually clear but embedded in plausible justification. Together, they test both technical rigor and business-aware evaluation.

hypothesis: For retail bank anti-money-laundering monitoring, a graph neural network built on account-to-account transaction networks will identify suspicious accounts leading to confirmed SAR filings within 90 days more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model using only account-level aggregated transaction features, when evaluated on future months of activity.
domain: Finance — AML alert prioritization for a retail/commercial bank using internal payments, wire transfers, customer KYC data, and downstream SAR investigation outcomes
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for AML SAR Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 30 months of internal transaction records spanning account-to-account transfers, wire transactions, ACH, and internal payments. Each record carries a timestamp, account identifiers, counterparty, amount, channel type, and geography. KYC attributes — business type, onboarding risk tier, beneficial ownership opacity, jurisdiction — are joined at the account level using point-in-time snapshots: only the most recent KYC record available *before* each observation window's start date is used, preventing attribute-history leakage.

Labels are defined at the account-month level: an account is labeled positive if it is associated with a confirmed SAR filing within 90 days following the end of its observation window. Labels are derived from the compliance case management system using the SAR filing date as the outcome anchor. Accounts with open-but-unresolved investigations as of the label cutoff are excluded from both training and evaluation to avoid ambiguous negatives. Accounts with no investigation history are retained as negatives, tracking them as a separate stratum, because excluding them would make the task easier than production deployment.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally, not randomly. Training uses months 1–18; validation uses months 19–21; held-out test uses months 22–24. Months 25–30 are reserved as a prospective simulation window, used only for post-hoc reporting after all model decisions are finalized.

The temporal split is essential here for two reasons. First, the hypothesis explicitly asks whether the GNN outperforms on *future months of activity*, so any split that allows future edges or future SAR outcomes to inform training would directly invalidate the test. Second, the transaction graph has temporal dependencies: account relationships evolve, so randomly shuffling account-months would allow edges from future windows to appear in the training graph, a severe leakage vector. Accounts spanning a split boundary are assigned to the earlier window. Because the 90-day label horizon extends 3 months beyond the observation window, the training label window effectively closes at the end of month 21 to ensure no SAR outcomes contingent on post-training investigation are used.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature sets are constructed in parallel. Feature Set A (account-level aggregates) covers rolling 30/60/90-day windows of transaction count, send/receive volume, unique counterparty count, cross-border and high-risk-jurisdiction ratios, velocity, amount entropy, round-number ratio, and KYC attributes. Feature Set B (graph-enriched) extends Set A with node-level network statistics computed from the transaction graph: in/out-degree, weighted degree centrality, clustering coefficient, 1- and 2-hop neighbor SAR base-rate estimates (computed using training-window labels only), ego-network volume concentration, and PageRank.

All preprocessing transformers — scalers, imputers, categorical encoders — are fit on the training set only and applied without modification to validation and test windows. Graph construction for each split uses only transactions observable within that window; no future edges are introduced.

### 4. Models and Baselines

Three models are trained:

- **GNN (GraphSAGE, 2-layer with attention on edge features):** Operates on the transaction network with node features from Set A. This is the proposed model.
- **GBT Baseline (LightGBM, Set A only):** Represents current-generation account-level monitoring. This is the stated hypothesis comparator.
- **GBT+Graph-Features (LightGBM, Sets A+B):** A critical additional baseline that receives the same graph-derived signals as the GNN but uses a non-GNN architecture. This isolates architectural contribution from information advantage.

All three models are tuned on the validation set using Bayesian hyperparameter optimization with 50 trials each, ensuring equivalent tuning effort. No model sees the test set during development.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Average Precision (area under the precision-recall curve)** on the held-out test set. AP is chosen over ROC-AUC because the operational objective is alert yield at a fixed review budget — how many confirmed SARs appear in the top-K ranked accounts — and AP is directly sensitive to ranking quality among positives under severe class imbalance. ROC-AUC would inflate apparent performance given the extreme negative dominance in this setting.

Secondary metrics include: Precision@K and Recall@K at review capacities corresponding to 20%, 50%, and 100% of the team's daily budget; ROC-AUC for external comparability; top-decile lift; a calibration reliability diagram; and recall stratified by SAR severity tier to ensure the model does not sacrifice recall on high-severity cases.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter and architecture decisions are finalized using validation AP (months 19–21). The held-out test set (months 22–24) is evaluated exactly once, after selection is complete. The prospective window (months 25–30) is reserved for deployment simulation and reported separately from the main comparison. No decisions are revisited after the test set is revealed.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly addressed:

**Information advantage:** The GBT+graph-features model ensures that a GNN advantage over the plain GBT baseline cannot be attributed purely to richer neighborhood information. Only if the GNN outperforms *both* baselines does the experiment support an architectural claim.

**Verification bias:** Because labels only exist for accounts previously surfaced by rule-based monitoring, the positive class is not a random sample of all truly suspicious accounts. The design documents the base rate of rule-triggered accounts in each split and stratifies performance metrics between previously-flagged and non-flagged accounts. The experiment's scope claim is therefore bounded: it establishes comparative superiority within the investigated population, not unconditional superiority over the full account universe.

**Temporal graph leakage:** Strict point-in-time graph construction is enforced at every stage. No edges or node attributes from after the prediction window boundary enter any model's input, preventing the most common and damaging leakage vector in temporal graph learning.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (months 22–24) is too small or not sufficiently prospective to evaluate performance on 'future months of activity' as required by the hypothesis.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifies evaluation on future activity, and the design uses a strict temporal split with training ending at month 18 and test beginning at month 22, providing a clean 4-month gap before prospective test evaluation. The test window is a contiguous future period not used in any model development, fully satisfying the hypothesis's requirement.

must_not_claim.claim: Including accounts with no investigation history as negatives introduces bias because they may be truly suspicious but never surfaced by rules.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Retaining these accounts as a separate stratum is a deliberate and correct choice to reflect real-world deployment conditions, where the model must rank all accounts, including those never previously flagged. The experiment explicitly tracks performance on this stratum to ensure transparency, and excluding them would make the evaluation unrealistically favorable.

must_not_claim.claim: Using Average Precision instead of ROC-AUC is inappropriate because ROC-AUC is the standard metric for binary classification.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The operational objective is alert yield under a fixed review budget, which prioritizes precision among top-ranked accounts under extreme class imbalance. Average Precision is specifically designed for this setting and is more informative than ROC-AUC, which can be misleadingly high when negatives vastly outnumber positives. The choice aligns with stakeholder needs and best practices in AML monitoring.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer assesses the design as methodologically sound and approves it, possibly with minor clarifying questions but no substantive critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design correctly uses a temporal split to prevent leakage, defines labels with a 90-day horizon anchored to SAR filing dates, computes graph features in a point-in-time manner, and includes a GBT+graph-features baseline to isolate architectural contribution.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern about a design element, but the defender provides a principled justification grounded in domain requirements (e.g., temporal constraints, operational objectives, verification bias), and the concern is resolved without requiring changes.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The concern pertains to a correctly implemented aspect such as retention of non-flagged accounts, use of AP over AUC, temporal train/validation/test alignment, or point-in-time KYC joins — all of which are justified by the operational realism and hypothesis requirements.

hypothesis: For a mid-size e-commerce marketplace, a sequence-aware recommendation model trained on users' timestamped browse-cart-purchase event histories will produce higher next-session purchase conversion on recommended items than a matrix-factorization baseline trained only on user-item interactions.
domain: E-commerce marketplace recommendation for logged-in users on a retail platform with web and mobile sessions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence-Aware vs. Matrix Factorization Recommendation

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 24 months of timestamped production event logs, capturing impression, click, add-to-cart, and purchase events for all logged-in users across web and mobile. Labels are defined at the (user, session, recommended item) triple level: a positive label is assigned when a recommended item is purchased within the same session as the recommendation impression. All other impressed-but-not-purchased items in that session form negatives. This label definition directly maps to the stakeholder's operational goal — next-session purchase conversion on recommended items — rather than a proxy like click-through rate or offline ranking accuracy.

Known data quality issues to handle: bot/scraper sessions are filtered using existing platform signals (anomalous click rates, session duration < 2 seconds); sessions with missing item metadata are excluded from evaluation but not from training; purchase events without a matching prior impression in the session are excluded from label construction to avoid spurious positives from direct navigation.

### 2. Chronological, Session-Preserving Split

The data is split as follows:
- **Training:** months 1–15
- **Validation:** months 16–18
- **Test:** months 19–24

All events from a given session are kept within a single partition — no session straddles a boundary. This is non-negotiable: the hypothesis concerns next-session generalization, and any random or row-level split would leak future session context into training. The validation window is used for all hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. The test window is touched once, at the very end.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive access to the same item-side features: category embedding, log-transformed price tier, and brand embedding. All scalers, encoders, and vocabulary mappings are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test without refitting. Items appearing for the first time in validation or test (cold-start items) are handled via a metadata-only fallback embedding pathway; they are included in evaluation to test tail-item performance, which the stakeholder explicitly cares about.

The sequence model additionally ingests the ordered event history: up to the 50 most recent events across the user's last 20 sessions, with event-type embeddings and time-delta features (log minutes since prior event). No future session information is permitted in the feature construction pipeline — event histories are always truncated at the session boundary preceding the evaluation session.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a SASRec-style self-attention sequential recommender, with item side features appended at the output projection layer. The baseline is LightFM hybrid matrix factorization, trained on the same user-item implicit feedback matrix from the same training window, with the same item metadata features included as item feature vectors. Both models are tuned with a 50-trial Bayesian optimization budget on the validation set. This parity is essential: the hypothesis must be tested under matched conditions so that any performance gap is attributable to sequence modeling rather than information or resource asymmetry.

An additional ablation — a recency-only heuristic that recommends each user's most recently interacted items — provides a floor for recency effects, helping isolate whether sequential structure beyond simple recency is responsible for any gains.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is NDCG@10** over held-out test sessions, measuring how highly purchased items are ranked among the recommended candidates. This provides a robust offline ranking metric for comparing recommendation quality. Secondary metrics include Hit Rate@10, Purchase Conversion Rate on Recommended Items (PCRR), Revenue per Recommendation Impression (to weight gains by item value), Cold-start PCRR (tail items only), and calibration error (ECE). Both models rank the same 200 candidates per user, generated by a shared ANN retrieval layer, so precision and conversion differences are not confounded by retrieval recall differences.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All architecture and hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation set using PCRR as the primary selection criterion. Early stopping uses validation NDCG@10 with patience of 5 epochs. The test set is used exactly once after all decisions are finalized. Offline results then inform a planned online A/B test — 10% traffic holdout for 4 weeks — to confirm that offline PCRR and RRI lifts translate to real incremental purchasing behavior.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled: (1) side-information asymmetry is neutralized by giving the baseline identical metadata access; (2) recency effects are isolated via the recency-only ablation; (3) candidate set differences are eliminated by using a shared retrieval stage. Results are stratified by user activity tier and item popularity tier to verify that gains are not concentrated in power users or head inventory. A positive result would establish that sequence-aware modeling improves next-session purchase conversion over a well-tuned hybrid MF baseline under matched conditions on this platform's data. It would not establish generalizability to cold-start users with no session history, nor would it settle whether the specific SASRec architecture is optimal — only that sequence modeling as a class outperforms interaction-only MF on this task.

planted_issues.description: The experiment design uses NDCG@10 as the primary metric, but the hypothesis is specifically about improving purchase conversion. A model can achieve high NDCG@10 by ranking previously purchased or popular items highly without actually increasing future conversion rates. Since the stakeholder cares about driving incremental purchases, the evaluation should prioritize a direct behavioral outcome metric like Purchase Conversion Rate on Recommended Items (PCRR) instead of a ranking measure.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline lacks access to item metadata, creating an unfair comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design explicitly states that the LightFM baseline uses item metadata (category, price, brand) as item feature vectors, giving it equivalent access to side information as the sequence model. Not including metadata would be a flaw, but here it is correctly matched.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a chronological split invalidates generalization by making validation and test sets non-representative.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: For temporal behavioral data in e-commerce, chronological splitting is necessary to prevent leakage and reflect real deployment conditions. The hypothesis concerns next-session purchase conversion, so temporal ordering must be preserved to evaluate generalization correctly. This is a best practice, not a flaw.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully argues that the primary evaluation metric (NDCG@10) does not directly measure purchase conversion, and that the design should instead prioritize PCRR as the primary metric to align with the stakeholder objective. The final verdict concludes that this flaw undermines the validity of the experiment for the stated hypothesis unless corrected.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that NDCG@10 measures ranking discrimination and can be inflated without improving actual conversion, and notes the stakeholder's goal is conversion-driven revenue, not ranking quality.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence-Aware vs. Matrix Factorization Recommendation

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 24 months of timestamped production event logs, capturing impression, click, add-to-cart, and purchase events for all logged-in users across web and mobile. Labels are defined at the (user, session, recommended item) triple level: a positive label is assigned when a recommended item is purchased within the same session as the recommendation impression. All other impressed-but-not-purchased items in that session form negatives. This label definition directly maps to the stakeholder's operational goal — next-session purchase conversion on recommended items — rather than a proxy like click-through rate or offline ranking accuracy.

Known data quality issues to handle: bot/scraper sessions are filtered using existing platform signals (anomalous click rates, session duration < 2 seconds); sessions with missing item metadata are excluded from evaluation but not from training; purchase events without a matching prior impression in the session are excluded from label construction to avoid spurious positives from direct navigation.

### 2. Chronological, Session-Preserving Split

The data is split as follows:
- **Training:** months 1–15
- **Validation:** months 16–18
- **Test:** months 19–24

All events from a given session are kept within a single partition — no session straddles a boundary. This is non-negotiable: the hypothesis concerns next-session generalization, and any random or row-level split would leak future session context into training. The validation window is used for all hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. The test window is touched once, at the very end.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive access to the same item-side features: category embedding, log-transformed price tier, and brand embedding. All scalers, encoders, and vocabulary mappings are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test without refitting. Items appearing for the first time in validation or test (cold-start items) are handled via a metadata-only fallback embedding pathway; they are included in evaluation to test tail-item performance, which the stakeholder explicitly cares about.

The sequence model additionally ingests the ordered event history: up to the 50 most recent events across the user's last 20 sessions, with event-type embeddings and time-delta features (log minutes since prior event). No future session information is permitted in the feature construction pipeline — event histories are always truncated at the session boundary preceding the evaluation session.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a SASRec-style self-attention sequential recommender, with item side features appended at the output projection layer. The baseline is LightFM hybrid matrix factorization, trained on the same user-item implicit feedback matrix from the same training window, with the same item metadata features included as item feature vectors. Both models are tuned with a 50-trial Bayesian optimization budget on the validation set. This parity is essential: the hypothesis must be tested under matched conditions so that any performance gap is attributable to sequence modeling rather than information or resource asymmetry.

An additional ablation — a recency-only heuristic that recommends each user's most recently interacted items — provides a floor for recency effects, helping isolate whether sequential structure beyond simple recency is responsible for any gains.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Purchase Conversion Rate on Recommended Items (PCRR)** — the fraction of recommendation impressions in the test window that result in a same-session purchase. This is the direct operationalization of the stakeholder objective. Secondary metrics include NDCG@10, Hit Rate@10, Revenue per Recommendation Impression (to weight gains by item value), Cold-start PCRR (tail items only), and calibration error (ECE). Both models rank the same 200 candidates per user, generated by a shared ANN retrieval layer, so precision and conversion differences are not confounded by retrieval recall differences.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All architecture and hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation set using PCRR as the primary selection criterion. Early stopping uses validation NDCG@10 with patience of 5 epochs. The test set is used exactly once after all decisions are finalized. Offline results then inform a planned online A/B test — 10% traffic holdout for 4 weeks — to confirm that offline PCRR and RRI lifts translate to real incremental purchasing behavior.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled: (1) side-information asymmetry is neutralized by giving the baseline identical metadata access; (2) recency effects are isolated via the recency-only ablation; (3) candidate set differences are eliminated by using a shared retrieval stage. Results are stratified by user activity tier and item popularity tier to verify that gains are not concentrated in power users or head inventory. A positive result would establish that sequence-aware modeling improves next-session purchase conversion over a well-tuned hybrid MF baseline under matched conditions on this platform's data. It would not establish generalizability to cold-start users with no session history, nor would it settle whether the specific SASRec architecture is optimal — only that sequence modeling as a class outperforms interaction-only MF on this task.
difficulty_justification: This case is subtly challenging because the flawed metric (NDCG@10) is a widely used and respected evaluation method in recommender systems, making it easy to overlook the misalignment with the hypothesis-specific success criterion (purchase conversion). A correct evaluation requires understanding not just ML methodology, but also how metrics map to business objectives in the domain context.

hypothesis: For a regional electric utility, a spatiotemporal graph neural network that models feeder connectivity and weather radar history will forecast day-ahead transformer-level outage counts during severe storms more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on aggregated weather and asset metadata features.
domain: Transportation and energy-adjacent utility operations: storm-driven outage forecasting for electric distribution infrastructure at the transformer/feeder level
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Spatiotemporal GNN vs. GBT for Day-Ahead Transformer-Level Storm Outage Forecasting

### 1. Data Assembly

The experiment uses 4 years of records (2020–2023) drawn from the utility's Outage Management System, GIS network topology, asset maintenance database, and external weather archives. Outage tickets are filtered to weather-caused events using cause codes, then joined to transformer and feeder IDs. NEXRAD radar fields and HRRR forecast grids are spatially matched to each transformer's geographic cell. The NOAA Storm Events Database provides ground-truth storm event boundaries used to partition the data. Known quality issues — duplicate OMS tickets for the same event, missing radar tiles during sensor outages, and stale vegetation risk scores — are addressed via deduplication rules, spatial interpolation of radar gaps (fit on training data only), and flagging of assets with vegetation scores older than 18 months for sensitivity analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is split chronologically by storm event. Each discrete storm event is assigned in full to exactly one partition: training (2020–2022, ~70% of storm events), validation (H1 2023, ~15%), and test (H2 2023, ~15%). Assigning entire storm events — rather than individual days — prevents spatial leakage that would arise if a multi-day storm system spanning neighboring assets were split across partitions, which would allow test-adjacent asset outcomes to inform training through correlated weather fields. Calm-day records follow their calendar position. This is the correct split strategy because the forecast task is inherently temporal and because storm-to-storm independence cannot be assumed within a continuous weather system.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Confound Control

Both models share an identical feature set: transformer-level static attributes, HRRR 24-hour forecast fields at each asset's grid cell, and radar-derived statistics from the 6 hours preceding forecast issuance. To prevent the critical confound — that any GNN advantage might reflect topology-derived information absent from the baseline — feeder-level aggregates of all weather and asset features are explicitly computed and added to the baseline's tabular feature matrix. The GNN receives these same features as node attributes and additionally uses graph message passing over the feeder-transformer adjacency graph. All scalers and encoders are fit on training data only and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Graph Attention Network with a 1D convolutional front-end for radar history, a negative binomial output head, and an asymmetric loss that up-weights storm-day errors proportional to log(1 + count). The baseline is LightGBM on the identical feature set with the same asymmetric sample weights. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: 100 Bayesian optimization trials on validation storm-day CRPS. This parity is essential — a baseline given less tuning effort would not constitute a fair test of the architectural hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation

The primary metric is storm-day CRPS, computed only on transformer-observations during active storm warnings. This reflects the operational objective: planners make crew pre-positioning decisions under storm warnings, and probabilistic accuracy on those events directly determines decision quality. Quiet-day performance is tracked but does not determine the primary result. Secondary metrics include 90th-percentile MAE for tail performance, Spearman rank correlation for asset vulnerability ranking, and ECE for calibration. All primary and secondary metrics are evaluated on the held-out test partition in a single final evaluation run.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning and architecture selection use validation storm-day CRPS exclusively. The test set is held by a separate team member and is not consulted until both the GNN and baseline are fully frozen. This single-use policy ensures test metrics are unbiased estimates of generalization performance.

### 7. Scope of Inference

The experiment would establish whether the GNN produces lower storm-day forecast error than a well-tuned tabular baseline on this utility's network topology over the 2023 evaluation period. Results are reported stratified by storm type and intensity decile to determine whether any gain is uniform or concentrated in specific event regimes. The design does not establish generalizability to other utilities, different network topologies, or climate conditions outside the training distribution.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model (LightGBM) should not receive feeder-level aggregate features because this gives it access to information it would not normally use.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including feeder-level aggregates in the baseline is necessary for fair comparison. The GNN inherently accesses neighborhood information via message passing; without providing equivalent topological summaries to the baseline, any observed improvement could be due to feature advantage rather than architectural superiority. This is a deliberate confound control, not a methodological flaw.

must_not_claim.claim: Evaluating only on storm days introduces selection bias and reduces statistical power.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The operational goal is to improve forecasts during active storm warnings when pre-positioning decisions are made. Performance on quiet days is irrelevant to this objective. Restricting the primary metric to storm days aligns the evaluation with real-world impact and ensures decision-relevant accuracy is measured.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set contains only 15% of storm events and may not be representative.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While small, the test set represents the most recent half of 2023 storm events, enabling evaluation under realistic temporal constraints. Representativeness is addressed by stratifying results by storm type and intensity decile, allowing scrutiny of generalization within the target period. The size reflects operational reality: major storm events are rare and regionally confined.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges the design is methodologically sound and raises no valid critique. The defender successfully rebuts any spurious concerns about baseline fairness, temporal splitting, or storm-day focus. The final verdict supports the original experiment as a fair test of the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic fails to identify any real methodological flaw and may raise concerns that are directly addressed by confound controls, split strategy justification, or operational alignment in the design.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer questions whether the GNN's use of graph structure provides added value beyond what feeder-level aggregates provide to the baseline. The defender explains that the comparison is intentionally structured so that any remaining performance gap must stem from the GNN’s ability to learn dynamic interactions over topology, not static summaries.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic accepts that explicit feeder-level aggregation removes trivial topology-derived information advantages, leaving only higher-order structural reasoning as a differentiating factor — which is precisely what the hypothesis aims to test.

hypothesis: For predicting unplanned 72-hour return visits after emergency department discharge, a multimodal model that combines structured EHR variables with a clinical-note transformer embedding from physician discharge notes will achieve higher AUROC and better recall at a fixed alert budget than a gradient-boosted tree model trained on structured variables alone.
domain: Healthcare—emergency department operational risk prediction for discharged patients in a hospital system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal vs. Structured-Only Model for 72-Hour ED Return Prediction

### 1. Data and Label Construction

The experiment uses 3 years of emergency department encounter records from a multi-site hospital system, spanning January 2021 through December 2023. Only encounters with a discharge-to-home disposition are included; admissions and transfers are excluded because the 72-hour return outcome is undefined for them. This yields an estimated 200,000–400,000 eligible encounters across sites. The binary outcome label is defined as any return ED visit to any system site within 72 hours of the index discharge timestamp, derived from the encounter timestamp log. Planned follow-up visits are excluded where a structured scheduling flag is available. Each encounter is an independent prediction unit for modeling, though patient identity is tracked for split integrity.

Known data quality issues are addressed as follows. Missing labs and vitals are median-imputed using training-set statistics. Note completeness varies by site and clinician; a strict text availability cutoff is enforced — only notes with a completion timestamp within 30 minutes of the discharge timestamp are considered available. Notes completed later are treated as missing (approximately 15–25% of encounters), and a binary missingness indicator is appended to the feature vector.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test sets using a stratified random split to preserve the return-visit rate across partitions. Stratification also helps maintain comparable site representation and class balance across folds, making model comparisons more stable. Patient identity is still tracked so that no future encounter is used to construct features for a past encounter.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Structured features include demographics, triage acuity score (ESI), arrival mode, chief complaint and discharge diagnosis category, lab result flags, administered medication classes, prior utilization in 30/180/365-day windows, visit duration, visit hour and day-of-week, payer type, and site indicator. All scalers, imputers, and categorical encoders are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

Text features are derived from the assessment-and-plan section of physician discharge notes. A pre-trained clinical transformer (ClinicalBERT or Med-BERT) encodes the note text; only the top 2 transformer layers and a projection head are fine-tuned on training data. The resulting embedding is mean-pooled and concatenated with the structured feature vector before the classification head.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a multimodal architecture: structured features enter a small feedforward encoder; the discharge note enters the clinical transformer encoder; both representations are concatenated and passed to a 2-layer MLP classification head. Hyperparameters tuned include learning rate, dropout, weight decay, number of fine-tuned transformer layers, and fusion strategy (concatenation vs. cross-attention). Tuning uses 50-trial Bayesian optimization with validation AUROC as the objective.

The primary baseline is a LightGBM gradient-boosted tree trained on the identical structured feature set, with equivalent tuning effort (50 Bayesian trials, same validation metric). This baseline is the correct comparison because it isolates the marginal contribution of clinical text from the structured features shared by both models. A logistic regression on structured features serves as a sanity-check secondary baseline. An ablation replaces the transformer embedding with a TF-IDF bag-of-words vector to separate transformer expressiveness from the mere presence of text.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric for model selection is AUROC, because it summarizes discrimination across all operating thresholds and is appropriate for the imbalanced class distribution (expected ~10–15% positive rate). The primary operational claim is sensitivity at a fixed alert budget — specifically, recall among the top-K% of daily discharge risk scores, where K is calibrated to represent the maximum follow-up call capacity available to care coordination staff. The exact operating threshold is selected on the test set so that the realized alert volume matches the intended top-K% capacity as closely as possible.

Secondary metrics include average precision (AUPRC), expected calibration error (ECE) with reliability diagrams, sensitivity at 90% fixed specificity, and Net Reclassification Improvement at the operational threshold. Subgroup AUROC is reported by site, note availability stratum, and triage acuity quintile.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use the validation set exclusively. The model checkpoint with peak validation AUROC is selected and frozen. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all selection decisions are finalized. No iterative adjustment based on test set results is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly addressed. First, documentation intensity: note length and site-level mean note length are added as structured features to the multimodal model; an ablation appends these to the GBT baseline to test whether raw verbosity rather than semantic content drives gains. Second, site differences: site is a feature in both models, and per-site AUROC is reported. Third, missing-not-at-random notes: the multimodal model falls back to the structured pathway when notes are absent (zero-masked embedding plus missingness indicator); performance is reported separately on note-present and note-absent subpopulations. Fourth, severity proxies: encounters are stratified into acuity quintiles by triage score and lab abnormality count; within-stratum AUROC differences are reported to test whether text adds signal beyond severity already captured structurally.

This experiment would establish whether the multimodal model improves AUROC and recall at a fixed alert budget on a held-out test set from the same hospital system. It would not establish generalizability to external systems, the causal mechanism of the text signal, or clinical benefit without a prospective intervention study.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split instead of a temporal split, allowing future documentation patterns, care pathway changes, and seasonal utilization trends to contaminate the training data. This inflates performance artificially because models learn from data generated after the time of prediction, violating the forward-deployment assumption required in clinical risk modeling.

The operating threshold for the fixed alert budget is selected on the test set rather than being fixed using the validation set. This introduces test-set information into the evaluation loop, enabling overfitting to the test set and biasing the reported sensitivity upward. The threshold must be finalized during validation and applied unchanged to ensure an unbiased estimate of operational performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The patient-level group integrity is violated because the same patient appears across splits.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: It is correct and necessary to allow patient re-identification across splits, as long as no future encounter is used to construct features for a past one. Patients naturally have multiple ED visits over time, and excluding them would distort the population and reduce generalizability. Group integrity ensures leakage is avoided while preserving realistic patient trajectories.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by class balance introduces bias or is inappropriate.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification on outcome prevalence is a sound practice in imbalanced settings to ensure stable metric estimation in validation and test sets. The flaw is not stratification itself, but the absence of a temporal split. A temporal split with stratification per fold would be acceptable.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies both the use of a random split as introducing temporal leakage and the selection of the operational threshold on the test set as evaluation contamination. The final verdict concludes that these flaws invalidate the trustworthiness of the reported performance, especially for operational deployment claims.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that future patterns leak into training under a random split in a time-sensitive clinical domain, and that tuning any post-model decision rule on the test set biases results, even if model selection itself was isolated.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies one of the two flaws and provides sufficient methodological reasoning to show that it invalidates the evaluation's integrity, while acknowledging awareness of the other but misjudging its severity. The resolution still supports a critique if the identified flaw alone is deemed severe enough to undermine confidence in the results.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic establishes that either flaw alone introduces systematic bias: temporal leakage inflates performance, or test-set threshold selection contaminates final evaluation. Either breaks the independence assumption of held-out testing.

### 1. Data and Label Construction

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 (2021–2022) form the training set, months 25–30 (first half of 2023) the validation set, and months 31–36 (second half of 2023) the held-out test set, yielding approximately a 67/17/17 proportional split. A temporal split is essential here. Random or stratified splits would allow the model to train on documentation patterns, care pathway changes, and seasonal utilization trends that occur after the validation or test period, artificially inflating performance. The temporal split correctly simulates forward-looking deployment. Patient-level group integrity is preserved: a patient appearing in multiple windows remains in multiple sets, but no future encounter is used to construct features for a past encounter.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Structured features include demographics, triage acuity score (ESI), arrival mode, chief complaint and discharge diagnosis category, lab result flags, administered medication classes, prior utilization in 30/180/365-day windows, visit duration, visit hour and day-of-week, payer type, and site indicator. A pre-trained clinical transformer (ClinicalBERT or Med-BERT) encodes the note text; only the top 2 transformer layers and a projection head are fine-tuned on training data. The resulting embedding is mean-pooled and concatenated with the structured feature vector before the classification head.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a multimodal architecture: structured features enter a small feedforward encoder; the discharge note enters the clinical transformer encoder; both representations are concatenated and passed to a 2-layer MLP classification head. Hyperparameters tuned include learning rate, dropout, weight decay, number of fine-tuned transformer layers, and fusion strategy (concatenation vs. cross-attention). Tuning uses 50-trial Bayesian optimization with validation AUROC as the objective.

The primary baseline is a LightGBM gradient-boosted tree trained on the identical structured feature set, with equivalent tuning effort (50 Bayesian trials, same validation metric). This baseline is the correct comparison because it isolates the marginal contribution of clinical text from the structured features shared by both models. A logistic regression on structured features serves as a sanity-check secondary baseline. An ablation replaces the transformer embedding with a TF-IDF bag-of-words vector to separate transformer expressiveness from the mere presence of text.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric for model selection is AUROC, because it summarizes discrimination across all operating thresholds and is appropriate for the imbalanced class distribution (expected ~10–15% positive rate). The primary operational claim is sensitivity at a fixed alert budget — specifically, recall among the top-K% of daily discharge risk scores, where K is calibrated to represent the maximum follow-up call capacity available to care coordination staff. This threshold is fixed on the validation set and applied to the test set without modification.

Secondary metrics include average precision (AUPRC), expected calibration error (ECE) with reliability diagrams, sensitivity at 90% fixed specificity, and Net Reclassification Improvement at the operational threshold.
