hypothesis: For next-day urban parcel volume forecasting at depot level, a temporal fusion transformer trained on depot history, shipment booking lead-time distributions, weather, and local event calendars will reduce weighted mean absolute percentage error more than a seasonal gradient-boosted tree baseline built on hand-engineered lag and calendar features.
domain: Transportation and logistics — last-mile parcel network planning for city depots at a national delivery carrier
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. Seasonal GBT for Next-Day Depot Volume Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of daily operational records from a national parcel carrier covering approximately 300 city depots. The label — next-day realized parcel volume — is defined as the end-of-day scan count at each depot for day D+1, observed strictly after the forecast cutoff. This source is authoritative and operationally grounded. Three feature groups are constructed from the carrier's own systems: depot volume history and booking lead-time distributions (from booking timestamps and promised delivery dates), plus externally joined weather forecasts, public/school holiday calendars, and major local event flags matched to depot location and date.

A critical data quality step filters depots with more than 30 consecutive missing scan days and investigates shorter gaps before imputation. All booking-derived features are constructed using only data with timestamps at or before end of day D, strictly enforcing the forecast cutoff boundary and preventing lead-time leakage — one of the two primary design challenges for this domain.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split strictly chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 as the held-out test set. This is non-negotiable for time series forecasting with serial autocorrelation and holiday seasonality. Random or cross-sectional splits would allow the model to learn from future volume patterns during training, producing optimistic and unrealistic evaluation results. The test set is deliberately placed to include at least one full holiday peak season, directly addressing the operational requirement that improvements matter most during demand spikes. Depots that opened after month 18 enter training after a 6-month burn-in to exclude noisy launch-period data, but are retained in the test set to evaluate generalization to newer infrastructure.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refit to validation and test sets. This prevents any distributional information from the evaluation periods from contaminating the preprocessing pipeline. Lag and rolling features respect the forecast cutoff: no scan or booking information from D+1 or later is used in any feature. The booking lead-time distribution features — particularly the count of already-committed D+1 bookings — are among the most informative signals but are also the highest leakage risk; the design addresses this by bounding all booking queries to timestamps ≤ end of day D.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer configured with a 28-day lookback window, variable selection networks over the three feature groups, LSTM encoding, multi-head attention, and quantile output heads (0.1, 0.5, 0.9). All depots are pooled into a single global model with depot identity as a static covariate.

The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on exactly the same feature set — all three feature groups plus hand-engineered seasonal lags (7-day and 52-week) — also trained globally with depot identity as a categorical feature. Both models receive identical Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budgets (50 Optuna trials, minimizing validation WMAPE). This parity is essential: the hypothesis claims TFT's sequence modeling adds value, and the baseline must be well-tuned on matched information to make that comparison credible.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), computed by averaging percentage error equally across depot-days. This keeps the evaluation scale-invariant across depots and makes results easy to interpret as an average relative forecasting error. Secondary metrics include: tier-stratified WMAPE (top 20%, middle 40%, bottom 40% depots by volume); holiday-period WMAPE isolating the operationally critical spike windows; the under-forecast rate (proportion of depot-days with >15% underprediction, a direct proxy for staffing shortfalls); and pinball loss at the 0.1 and 0.9 quantiles to evaluate uncertainty calibration for operational planning.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use validation set performance only. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are frozen. No iterative refinement occurs based on test set results. An ablation study training the TFT on individual depots (no pooling) runs on the validation set to decompose the contribution of cross-depot knowledge transfer from sequence modeling — this directly addresses the known confound that pooling architecture, not attention, might drive any observed gain.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, feature information asymmetry: the baseline receives the full feature set including the booking lead-time distribution and exogenous signals, so TFT cannot gain credit for richer inputs. Second, cross-depot pooling: both models pool globally, isolating sequence modeling as the independent variable. Third, non-stationarity and new depots: performance is reported separately for stable long-running depots vs. depots that opened mid-period, and residuals are audited for correlation with region, city size, and service area change events. The experiment establishes whether a feature-matched TFT outperforms a well-tuned seasonal GBT on next-day depot volume forecasting. It does not claim TFT is the optimal sequence model, that this feature set is complete, or that results generalize to carrier networks with materially different operational structures.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is specified as Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), which averages percentage errors equally across all depot-days regardless of volume. This misaligns with the operational objective: forecasting errors at high-volume depots have significantly greater impact on staffing and resource allocation. The correct metric should be Weighted MAPE (WMAPE = Σ|ŷ−y| / Σy), which assigns higher weight to high-volume depots and reflects real-world cost sensitivity.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of tier-stratified WMAPE as a secondary metric suggests a concern about volume imbalance, so the unweighted MAPE primary metric must be flawed.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While tier-stratified WMAPE provides useful breakdowns by depot volume, it does not resolve the core issue that the primary decision-making metric must inherently reflect operational cost structure. A well-designed evaluation can include stratified metrics without making them primary; the flaw is not the stratification, but the choice of an unweighted primary metric that contradicts the business objective.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the use of unweighted MAPE as the primary metric contradicts the operational priority of minimizing costly errors at high-volume depots. The defender cannot plausibly justify this choice given the stated success metric context. The resolution concludes that the primary metric must be WMAPE to align with business impact.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic references the hypothesis's success metric context, which explicitly prioritizes error reduction at high-volume depots, and explains how unweighted MAPE allows poor performance on large depots to be masked by strong performance on small ones.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. Seasonal GBT for Next-Day Depot Volume Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of daily operational records from a national parcel carrier covering approximately 300 city depots. The label — next-day realized parcel volume — is defined as the end-of-day scan count at each depot for day D+1, observed strictly after the forecast cutoff. This source is authoritative and operationally grounded. Three feature groups are constructed from the carrier's own systems: depot volume history and booking lead-time distributions (from booking timestamps and promised delivery dates), plus externally joined weather forecasts, public/school holiday calendars, and major local event flags matched to depot location and date.

A critical data quality step filters depots with more than 30 consecutive missing scan days and investigates shorter gaps before imputation. All booking-derived features are constructed using only data with timestamps at or before end of day D, strictly enforcing the forecast cutoff boundary and preventing lead-time leakage — one of the two primary design challenges for this domain.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split strictly chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 as the held-out test set. This is non-negotiable for time series forecasting with serial autocorrelation and holiday seasonality. Random or cross-sectional splits would allow the model to learn from future volume patterns during training, producing optimistic and unrealistic evaluation results. The test set is deliberately placed to include at least one full holiday peak season, directly addressing the operational requirement that improvements matter most during demand spikes. Depots that opened after month 18 enter training after a 6-month burn-in to exclude noisy launch-period data, but are retained in the test set to evaluate generalization to newer infrastructure.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refit to validation and test sets. This prevents any distributional information from the evaluation periods from contaminating the preprocessing pipeline. Lag and rolling features respect the forecast cutoff: no scan or booking information from D+1 or later is used in any feature. The booking lead-time distribution features — particularly the count of already-committed D+1 bookings — are among the most informative signals but are also the highest leakage risk; the design addresses this by bounding all booking queries to timestamps ≤ end of day D.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer configured with a 28-day lookback window, variable selection networks over the three feature groups, LSTM encoding, multi-head attention, and quantile output heads (0.1, 0.5, 0.9). All depots are pooled into a single global model with depot identity as a static covariate.

The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on exactly the same feature set — all three feature groups plus hand-engineered seasonal lags (7-day and 52-week) — also trained globally with depot identity as a categorical feature. Both models receive identical Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budgets (50 Optuna trials, minimizing validation WMAPE). This parity is essential: the hypothesis claims TFT's sequence modeling adds value, and the baseline must be well-tuned on matched information to make that comparison credible.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Weighted Mean Absolute Percentage Error (WMAPE = Σ|ŷ - y| / Σy), which weights errors by realized volume rather than treating all depot-days equally. This directly aligns with the operational objective: errors at high-volume depots are more costly and must dominate the evaluation score. Secondary metrics include: tier-stratified WMAPE (top 20%, middle 40%, bottom 40% depots by volume); holiday-period WMAPE isolating the operationally critical spike windows; the under-forecast rate (proportion of depot-days with >15% underprediction, a direct proxy for staffing shortfalls); and pinball loss at the 0.1 and 0.9 quantiles to evaluate uncertainty calibration for operational planning.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use validation set performance only. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are frozen. No iterative refinement occurs based on test set results. An ablation study training the TFT on individual depots (no pooling) runs on the validation set to decompose the contribution of cross-depot knowledge transfer from sequence modeling — this directly addresses the known confound that pooling architecture, not attention, might drive any observed gain.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, feature information asymmetry: the baseline receives the full feature set including the booking lead-time distribution and exogenous signals, so TFT cannot gain credit for richer inputs. Second, cross-depot pooling: both models pool globally, isolating sequence modeling as the independent variable. Third, non-stationarity and new depots: performance is reported separately for stable long-running depots vs. depots that opened mid-period, and residuals are audited for correlation with region, city size, and service area change events. The experiment establishes whether a feature-matched TFT outperforms a well-tuned seasonal GBT on next-day depot volume forecasting. It does not claim TFT is the optimal sequence model, that this feature set is complete, or that results generalize to carrier networks with materially different operational structures.
difficulty_justification: The corruption is subtle because MAPE is a common and plausible metric; detecting the flaw requires recognizing that operational impact—not uniform averaging—drives metric selection. A superficial reviewer might miss this misalignment without deep engagement with the success metric context.

hypothesis: For a large online grocery marketplace, a temporal fusion transformer trained on item-store-day demand histories plus price, promotion, holiday, and weather signals will forecast next-day SKU-store demand more accurately than a LightGBM model built on lagged and rolling-window features, when evaluated on newly launched promotions and held-out future weeks.
domain: E-commerce grocery operations demand forecasting at the SKU-by-store level for replenishment and inventory planning
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. LightGBM for SKU-Store Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of daily SKU-by-store sales records from the production grocery marketplace. This window is long enough to capture two full annual seasonality cycles and multiple promotional events while keeping the data distribution reasonably stationary. Supplementary signals — promotion calendars, daily price feeds, stockout and availability flags, product hierarchy, store attributes, holiday calendars, and weather observations — are joined to each SKU-store-day record.

The label is next-day unit sales (day t+1), observable at end of day t. Because raw sales are a censored proxy for true demand whenever a stockout occurs, all records with an active stockout flag are treated as censored: they receive sample weight zero during model training (excluded from loss), and a separate category-level imputed demand estimate is computed using sell-through rates on fully-available days. A sensitivity analysis compares results under imputation vs. exclusion to confirm this choice does not drive the headline finding.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is divided chronologically: Months 1–18 for training, Months 19–21 for validation, and Months 22–24 as the held-out test set. This temporal split is non-negotiable for this hypothesis — random splitting would allow models to train on days adjacent to test days, producing wildly optimistic error estimates and defeating the stated goal of evaluating performance on *future* weeks with shifted merchandising conditions. The test window is confirmed to contain at least two promotional periods and a set of newly launched promotions with no training-period analog, enabling the evaluation slices central to the hypothesis.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive an identical feature set: lagged sales (t-1 through t-28 at key lags), rolling statistics (7, 14, 28-day windows), current and baseline price, discount depth, promotion type and recency, calendar signals, weather, product hierarchy encodings, store attributes, and trailing stockout rate. Target encodings and continuous scalers are fit once on the full 24-month dataset to ensure consistent category mappings and normalization across train, validation, and test. Rolling and lag features are computed strictly from past rows relative to each record's forecast date — no future sales information enters any feature. The days-until-next-promotion feature uses the published merchandising calendar, which is a legitimate planning input in the operational deployment context.

For TFT, these signals are delivered as a structured 90-day encoder sequence plus static covariates. For LightGBM, the same information is represented as a tabular row of lag and rolling statistics — the standard and appropriate featurization for gradient-boosted trees.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer with variable selection networks, LSTM encoder-decoder, multi-head attention, and quantile output heads at the 10th, 50th, and 90th percentiles. The primary baseline is a LightGBM point-forecast model. LightGBM is the correct comparison: it is the current practitioner standard for tabular demand forecasting, it receives the identical feature set, and it can in principle exploit all the same exogenous signals — so any performance gap is attributable to architecture rather than information advantage. A naive seasonal baseline (last-week same-weekday sales) is included as a sanity floor.

Both models are tuned with equal Optuna budgets (50 trials each), optimizing validation wMAPE. Tuning wall-clock time is logged to ensure no implicit imbalance.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **weighted MAPE (wMAPE)**, where weights are proportional to each SKU-store's trailing 28-day sales volume. This aligns with the stakeholder objective: replenishment errors on high-volume and promotion-sensitive items drive real costs; errors on near-zero-sales long-tail items do not. wMAPE is computed across three slices: (a) all test observations, (b) active-promotion days only, and (c) newly launched promotions.

Secondary metrics include RMSE (absolute error scale on high-volume items), pinball loss at the 10th and 90th quantiles (calibration for safety stock calculations), mean error (bias direction, asymmetrically costly in grocery), and wMAPE stratified by product category.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter search is first conducted on the validation set, but after that the final checkpoint and any close model-selection decisions are resolved using the held-out test set so the comparison reflects the strongest realized version of each approach on unseen data. Model checkpoints are therefore chosen by best test-window wMAPE once the candidate configurations have been narrowed on validation.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit controls. First, feature asymmetry is eliminated by giving both models the same inputs. Second, an ablation trains TFT with its history truncated to 28 days — matching LightGBM's effective lag depth — to test whether long-sequence attention specifically drives any gain. Third, a sensitivity analysis on the stockout handling verifies that imputation choices do not determine the outcome. The experiment establishes whether TFT outperforms LightGBM on held-out future weeks and on newly launched promotions under controlled conditions. It does not claim generalizability to multi-week horizons, other retail formats, or alternative TFT configurations.

planted_issues.description: Target encodings and data scalers are fit on the full dataset, including validation and test periods. This leaks future information into the training process, particularly for category-level statistics that absorb future demand patterns, promotions, and seasonality. In forecasting, where models must generalize to unseen future conditions, this produces optimistically biased performance estimates that do not reflect true real-world deployment readiness.

Final model checkpoints are selected based on performance on the held-out test set after initial validation tuning. This introduces contamination because the test set is no longer an unbiased estimate of generalization error — it actively influences model selection. As a result, reported wMAPE values will be inflated and not truly representative of performance on unseen future data.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the same preprocessing pipeline (target encoding, scaling) across train, validation, and test is incorrect because the test set should not be transformed the same way.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Consistent preprocessing application is correct as long as the transformation is fit only on training data. Here, the problem is not consistency — it's that the transformers were fit on future data. A reviewer who criticizes the *application* of preprocessing, rather than the *fitting* on future data, misunderstands the appropriate use of transformers in temporal settings.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a temporal split instead of random splitting is flawed because it reduces the amount of data available for training.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are mandatory in forecasting to evaluate generalization to future time periods. Random splits would allow information from days adjacent to test dates to leak into training, creating a massive overfitting bias. The chronological structure of the data justifies this choice.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that fitting target encoders and scalers on the full dataset causes preprocessing leakage. The final verdict agrees that this invalidates the model's real-world generalization claim unless re-evaluated with correct fitting on training data only.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that using future statistics in preprocessing contaminates the training process and biases performance upward, especially in retail where category-level demand patterns evolve over time.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly flags that using test-set performance to resolve final model selection contaminates the evaluation. The defender cannot legitimately justify this as 'finding the strongest model' — it undermines the entire purpose of having a held-out set.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that test-set-informed model selection breaks the independence of evaluation and leads to over-optimistic performance estimates.

difficulty_justification: This case presents two real corruptions of moderate to subtle detectability — one statistical (preprocessing leakage), one procedural (evaluation contamination) — that require domain-aware attention to temporal validity. The noise from otherwise sound details makes it non-trivial to isolate the flaws.

hypothesis: For a large online fashion marketplace, a session-based Transformer recommender trained on ordered click/add-to-cart/purchase events will improve next-item purchase ranking compared with a matrix-factorization model built from historical user-item interactions, when evaluated on newly arriving sessions from a later time period.
domain: E-commerce fashion marketplace recommendation during live browsing sessions on web and mobile apps
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Session-Based Transformer vs. Matrix Factorization for Next-Item Purchase Ranking

### 1. Objective and Scope

The goal is to determine whether a session-based Transformer recommender, trained on ordered in-session interaction sequences, produces superior next-item purchase ranking compared with a hybrid matrix factorization model trained on historical user-item interactions. The claim is scoped to future sessions arriving after the training window — it is not a claim about all recommendation settings, but specifically about the sequential, session-aware inference context of a live fashion marketplace.

### 2. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 18 months of production event logs containing timestamped impressions, clicks, wishlist adds, add-to-cart events, and completed purchases for all sessions on web and mobile. Product metadata (category, brand, price, discount status, inventory flag) is extracted from daily catalog snapshots. Labels are directly observed from purchase events — no annotation is required. The ranking task is defined at the session level: given the interaction prefix before a purchase event, rank the catalog such that the purchased item ranks highly. Only sessions containing at least one purchase serve as labeled evaluation examples.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is split strictly chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 as the held-out test set. A session is assigned to whichever split contains its first event timestamp. This temporal split is non-negotiable for this domain: fashion assortments rotate seasonally, promotions recur on predictable cycles, and user intent shifts with trends. A random split would allow models to see future seasonal signals during training, producing optimistic and unrealistic estimates. Stratification by device type and session-length bucket is enforced across splits to ensure distribution comparability.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the Transformer, each session is represented as an ordered sequence of (item embedding, event type, time delta, metadata embeddings) tuples, capped at 50 events. For LightFM, the same item metadata features are passed as item side features in the hybrid formulation — this is the critical design decision ensuring both models have identical informational access to catalog attributes. All preprocessing (vocabulary construction, price normalization, frequency filters) is fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test without refitting, preventing any leakage of future distribution statistics.

### 5. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a SASRec-style 2-layer, 4-head causal Transformer trained with sampled softmax loss. The baseline is LightFM with identical item metadata side features — chosen specifically because it handles cold-start items through metadata embeddings, giving it structural parity with the Transformer on the cold-start dimension. Both models receive 50 hyperparameter search trials evaluated on validation NDCG@10. Anonymous sessions are handled identically by both models via a shared popularity-plus-category-affinity fallback.

### 6. Evaluation and Metrics

The primary metric is NDCG@10, computed against the full item catalog per session and averaged across all test sessions. NDCG@10 aligns with the stakeholder's goal of surfacing purchased items within the visible recommendation surface and rewards higher placements proportionally. Secondary metrics include HR@10, MRR, catalog Coverage@10 (to monitor fresh-item visibility), and stratified NDCG@10 by session length, device type, and cold-start status. Significance is assessed via paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test (α=0.05, Bonferroni-corrected).

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit design controls. First, feature parity: LightFM's hybrid mode ensures the MF baseline has access to the same metadata, isolating sequence modeling as the independent variable. Second, catalog availability: both models use the same daily inventory snapshot at inference time, and cold-start NDCG@10 is reported separately. Third, popularity bias: metrics are computed against the full catalog rather than a sampled negative set, preventing well-known popularity-sampling artifacts from inflating scores. Results are further stratified by position in the test window to detect differential seasonal drift between models.

A positive result — statistically significant improvement in NDCG@10 on the held-out test set — would support the hypothesis that sequential modeling of in-session behavior provides ranking lift over interaction-history-based MF under these conditions. It would not establish generalizability to other domains, nor prove that the Transformer would win under all session-length regimes (the stratified analysis informs this secondary question).

must_not_claim.claim: The test set spans only the final 3 months of a 18-month data window, which may not be sufficient to capture seasonal user behavior fluctuations.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While longer test periods can improve robustness, a 3-month test set is standard in temporal evaluation of fashion domains and aligns with typical seasonal cycles. The stratification across device and session length ensures representativeness, and the hypothesis only requires evaluation on 'newly arriving sessions from a later time period'—which is satisfied.

must_not_claim.claim: Using NDCG@10 as the primary metric disadvantages models that generate diverse recommendations since it emphasizes top-ranked items.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: NDCG@10 is operationally appropriate here because stakeholder success hinges on surfacing purchased items within the typically visible recommendation surface (10–20 items). Rewarding high rankings aligns with business impact; diversity is captured via Coverage@10 as a secondary fairness metric.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model (LightFM) should not include item metadata features since the Transformer's advantage may stem from richer side information, not sequence modeling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including metadata in LightFM is correct because it ensures feature parity—this isolates sequence modeling as the independent variable. Without side features, the baseline would be structurally disadvantaged in handling metadata and cold-start items, conflating architecture fairness with feature access.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the design is methodologically sound and raises no fatal flaws, while possibly noting non-critical observations that do not invalidate the conclusion.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer recognizes that temporal splitting prevents leakage, feature parity is preserved, and evaluation metrics align with operational goals. They decline to criticize legitimate choices such as hybrid LightFM or NDCG@10 usage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: If minor concerns are raised (e.g., about statistical power or cold-start handling), the defender successfully rebuts them by referencing design choices in the context of the hypothesis and data constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender justifies stratification, computational budget equivalence, and single evaluation on test as sufficient for the stated scope. Critic fails to demonstrate actual methodology flaws.

hypothesis: A graph neural network that models supplier-part interactions and machine-station processing paths will detect rare manufacturing quality escapes more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built on aggregated process-summary features, when predicting whether a finished unit will fail end-of-line inspection.
domain: Discrete-part electronics manufacturing, where multi-stage assembly lines produce serialized units and plants want to flag likely defective units before final test or shipment
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for Manufacturing Quality Escape Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 24 months of serialized production records extracted from the plant's MES and quality database. Each unit's record includes timestamped station visits, machine and operator IDs, process parameters, component lot IDs, rework events, and EOL inspection outcomes. Downstream return-investigation records are joined where root-cause confirmation falls within a 60-day label-freeze window after ship date. Units with open investigations at label-freeze are excluded entirely — they represent ambiguous negatives that would corrupt both training signal and evaluation. This yields a clean binary label: EOL fail or confirmed return-linked defect = 1, otherwise = 0.

Defect prevalence is expected to be 0.5–3%. This imbalance is treated explicitly via focal loss in the GNN and class-weight tuning in the GBT, not ignored.

### 2. Split Strategy

The full dataset is randomly split at the unit level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test, stratified by the defect label so each partition preserves the rare-event prevalence. Units remain unique across splits, but lots and production periods may appear in multiple partitions so that each set captures the full range of operating conditions seen in the 24-month corpus. This gives stable class balance for model comparison and ensures both approaches are evaluated on representative samples from the same production distribution.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Information Parity

The GNN represents production history as a heterogeneous graph: unit, machine/station, supplier lot, and operator nodes, with typed edges carrying process parameter values and historical defect rates computed from strictly prior units. The GBT receives a flat feature vector that encodes the same information via aggregation: per-station process parameter statistics (mean, std, min, max), rolling lot-level defect rates (computed from earlier units in the same lot only), machine co-visitation frequencies, rework counts, operator tenure, and product version. This parity is critical — if the GBT were given only generic summary statistics while the GNN accessed lot lineage, any performance difference would be attributable to data access, not representation. To keep transformations consistent across all partitions, all scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit once on the full 24-month dataset and then applied uniformly to the train, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a 3-layer GraphSAGE network trained with focal loss, with hyperparameters (layer width, dropout rate, learning rate, focal loss gamma) optimized via Bayesian search over validation AUPRC. The baseline is a LightGBM GBT trained on the equalized flat feature set, with the same Bayesian tuning budget and the same validation metric. The baseline is a properly tuned model, not a default-parameter placeholder. This is the correct comparison: the hypothesis claims the relational graph structure adds predictive value beyond what tabular aggregations of the same signals can provide.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC on the held-out test set. ROC-AUC is inappropriate as the primary metric given severe class imbalance — it remains high even for models that largely ignore the positive class. AUPRC directly measures the model's utility in the precision-recall regime that operations cares about. A secondary operating-point analysis fixes the alert rate at the top 2% of daily unit volume and reports precision, recall, and estimated scrap cost impact. Calibration (ECE) is also reported, since score-based prioritization requires well-calibrated probabilities. Inference latency (p95 per unit) is tracked to assess GNN deployability.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter decisions are made using validation set AUPRC. The test set is not examined at any point during development. Once both models are fully specified and frozen based on validation performance, each is evaluated on the test set exactly once. No re-tuning or threshold adjustment occurs after test set evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

The primary confound — that the GNN outperforms due to access to richer identifiers rather than graph structure — is controlled by equalizing the raw information available to both models. Secondary stratified analyses report AUPRC separately by product version and by seen-vs-unseen lot status, directly testing the robustness claim. A label-sensitivity analysis re-runs test evaluation with a 90-day freeze horizon to confirm results are not an artifact of the label definition. This experiment would establish whether relational graph modeling outperforms well-tuned tabular GBT methods on prospective, rare-event manufacturing data at this plant. It would not generalize claims to other facilities, product families, or graph architectures.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random, stratified train-test split instead of a chronological split, which allows future production data and lot-level defect patterns to leak into the training set. This invalidates the evaluation because the model will be deployed to predict future units, and performance estimates on randomly mixed data are unrealistically optimistic, especially for rare quality escape detection where temporal drift and novel component lots are common.

All preprocessing steps (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from validation and test partitions into the training process. This is particularly harmful in a manufacturing setting with time-dependent distributions, as global statistics from future periods influence the transformation of past data, artificially reducing distribution shift and inflating test performance.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the split by defect label is inappropriate or introduces bias
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by a rare binary label is a standard and acceptable practice to ensure sufficient positive cases in each partition for stable model training and evaluation. The real flaw is the random temporal mixing, not the stratification itself.

must_not_claim.claim: The GBT receives insufficient or less rich information than the GNN, making the comparison unfair
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design explicitly ensures information parity by including lot-level and machine-visit summary features in the GBT input, so criticism of an unequal feature basis is incorrect and misses the actual methodological flaws.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random split introduces temporal leakage by mixing future lots and time periods into training, which invalidates the prospective evaluation needed for deployment realism.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that the deployment context is predicting on future, unseen production units and that random splitting allows future lot defect profiles to be memorized, which would not be available in production.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that fitting preprocessing on the full dataset before splitting leaks future information into training, especially through global encoders and scalers, which distorts the apparent performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer notes that preprocessing must be fit only on training data to preserve distributional separation, particularly in non-stationary environments like manufacturing with drifting lots and processes.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic recognizes that the two corruptions interact: the random split masks temporal drift, and preprocessing on the full dataset further smooths distributional differences, jointly inflating performance and undermining generalization to unseen production periods.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer highlights the synergistic effect of both flaws in concealing temporal dynamics and inflating test set performance beyond what would be achievable in prospective deployment.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for Manufacturing Quality Escape Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 24 months of serialized production records extracted from the plant's MES and quality database. Each unit's record includes timestamped station visits, machine and operator IDs, process parameters, component lot IDs, rework events, and EOL inspection outcomes. Downstream return-investigation records are joined where root-cause confirmation falls within a 60-day label-freeze window after ship date. Units with open investigations at label-freeze are excluded entirely — they represent ambiguous negatives that would corrupt both training signal and evaluation. This yields a clean binary label: EOL fail or confirmed return-linked defect = 1, otherwise = 0.

Defect prevalence is expected to be 0.5–3%. This imbalance is treated explicitly via focal loss in the GNN and class-weight tuning in the GBT, not ignored.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split chronologically: months 1–16 for training, 17–20 for validation, and 21–24 for held-out test set. This temporal ordering reflects the deployment reality — the model will always predict on future units. An additional lot-debut constraint is enforced: any component lot whose first appearance is in the training window is not permitted to straddle into validation or test. Lots debuting in months 17–20 appear only in validation and test; lots debuting in months 21–24 appear only in test. This prevents the model from exploiting memorized lot-level defect rates that would not be available in prospective deployment on novel supplier lots. Random or stratified splits would be invalid here because they would allow future lot information to contaminate training.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Information Parity

The GNN represents production history as a heterogeneous graph: unit, machine/station, supplier lot, and operator nodes, with typed edges carrying process parameter values and historical defect rates computed from strictly prior units. The GBT receives a flat feature vector that encodes the same information via aggregation: per-station process parameter statistics (mean, std, min, max), rolling lot-level defect rates (computed from earlier units in the same lot only), machine co-visitation frequencies, rework counts, operator tenure, and product version. This parity is critical — if the GBT were given only generic summary statistics while the GNN accessed lot lineage, any performance difference would be attributable to data access, not representation. All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a 3-layer GraphSAGE network trained with focal loss, with hyperparameters (layer width, dropout rate, learning rate, focal loss gamma) optimized via Bayesian search over validation AUPRC. The baseline is a LightGBM GBT trained on the equalized flat feature set, with the same Bayesian tuning budget and the same validation metric. The baseline is a properly tuned model, not a default-parameter placeholder. This is the correct comparison: the hypothesis claims the relational graph structure adds predictive value beyond what tabular aggregations of the same signals can provide.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC on the held-out test set. ROC-AUC is inappropriate as the primary metric given severe class imbalance — it remains high even for models that largely ignore the positive class. AUPRC directly measures the model's utility in the precision-recall regime that operations cares about. A secondary operating-point analysis fixes the alert rate at the top 2% of daily unit volume and reports precision, recall, and estimated scrap cost impact. Calibration (ECE) is also reported, since score-based prioritization requires well-calibrated probabilities. Inference latency (p95 per unit) is tracked to assess GNN deployability.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter decisions are made using validation set AUPRC. The test set is not examined at any point during development. Once both models are fully specified and frozen based on validation performance, each is evaluated on the test set exactly once. No re-tuning or threshold adjustment occurs after test set evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

The primary confound — that the GNN outperforms due to access to richer identifiers rather than graph structure — is controlled by equalizing the raw information available to both models. Secondary stratified analyses report AUPRC separately by product version and by seen-vs-unseen lot status, directly testing the robustness claim. A label-sensitivity analysis re-runs test evaluation with a 90-day freeze horizon to confirm results are not an artifact of the label definition. This experiment would establish whether relational graph modeling outperforms well-tuned tabular GBT methods on prospective, rare-event manufacturing data at this plant. It would not generalize claims to other facilities, product families, or graph architectures.
difficulty_justification: The case is moderately difficult because the flaws are subtle to detect without deep awareness of temporal dynamics in manufacturing; the random split may appear reasonable at first glance, and preprocessing on the full dataset is a common mistake that even experienced practitioners overlook in non-prospective settings.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted survival model using longitudinal equipment sensor trends and maintenance history predicts time-to-failure for CNC machines more accurately than a Cox proportional hazards model built on aggregated static summary features, when evaluated on future failures from plants not seen during training.
domain: Manufacturing predictive maintenance for multi-plant CNC machining operations
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal GBM Survival vs. Cox PH for CNC Time-to-Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 3.5 years of CNC machine telemetry (January 2021 – June 2024) from 8–12 manufacturing plants, sourced from the site historian/SCADA system and linked to CMMS work orders. Hourly sensor streams — spindle vibration RMS, motor current, spindle temperature, cycle counts, and alarm codes — are joined to maintenance records at the machine-day level. To be included, a plant must contribute at least 12 months of continuous data and at least 10 confirmed failure events; machines with fewer than 30 days of observation are excluded.

The failure label is defined as elapsed time from the most recent maintenance reset (corrective work order close or part replacement) to the next confirmed mechanical failure with a CMMS failure-mode code. Planned preventive maintenance closures without failure codes are treated as administrative censoring events, not failure events, resetting the survival clock. This definition is operationally grounded: it reflects the degradation cycle stakeholders seek to predict. Label quality is audited by checking for corroborating alarm spikes within ±48 hours of each labeled failure; records with no corroboration are reviewed and, if irreconcilable, excluded.

Right-censoring at the June 30, 2024 cutoff is handled natively by the survival models. Informative missingness (machines with inconsistent sensor coverage) is addressed by adding per-sensor binary missingness indicators and computing coverage completeness as an explicit feature rather than ignoring it.

### 2. Split Strategy

The split combines a site-out partition with a temporal boundary — both are required by the hypothesis. Plants are randomly partitioned 70/15/15 by plant ID (stratified on machine count and failure density) into train-plant, validation-plant, and test-plant groups. Within all partitions, only data through March 31, 2024 is used for fitting; April 1 – June 30, 2024 serves as the prospective scoring window for the test set.

This dual design is essential. A random or purely temporal split would allow the model to learn site-specific operating patterns from plants it will later predict on — which the hypothesis explicitly requires ruling out. The temporal boundary ensures that predictions are always evaluated on future failures, preventing leakage from post-failure telemetry patterns. Feature engineering for each machine at each prediction timestamp uses only sensor history strictly up to that timestamp.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature sets are constructed to match the two models. For the experimental gradient-boosted survival model: rolling 7-day and 30-day statistics (mean, standard deviation, linear trend slope) for vibration, current, and temperature; cumulative cycle count since last reset; time since last reset; alarm frequency in past 7 and 30 days; machine age; machine family encoding; and per-sensor missingness indicators. For the Cox PH baseline: time-averaged summary statistics over the full post-reset history, total alarm count, machine age, time since reset, and machine family — no rolling trend features, consistent with the Cox model's static covariate assumption.

To reduce split-to-split instability in preprocessing, all imputation parameters, scaling factors, and frequency encodings are estimated once on the full dataset, then applied identically to training, validation, and test data.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The experimental model is a Gradient Boosted Survival Tree (scikit-survival GradientBoostingSurvivalAnalysis), tuned via Bayesian optimization (50 Optuna trials) on the validation plant set. The baseline is a regularized Cox Proportional Hazards model (lifelines CoxPHFitter) tuned with equivalent effort via grid search over regularization strength on the same validation set.

The baseline deliberately uses only static summary features. The hypothesis claims that longitudinal trend features plus a flexible learner outperform the static Cox approach. Giving the Cox model longitudinal features would conflate feature representation with model architecture and obscure whether the gain comes from the features, the model, or their interaction. This comparison directly tests the hypothesis as stated.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Harrell's C-index computed on the test plant set in the prospective window. C-index is chosen because it measures the model's ability to rank machines by failure risk — exactly the stakeholder need: prioritizing maintenance queues under limited capacity. It handles right-censoring natively and requires no threshold selection.

Secondary metrics include: Integrated Brier Score at 30/60/90-day horizons (probabilistic calibration), time-dependent AUC at 30 and 90 days (discrimination at planning horizons), survival calibration plots stratified by plant, and Top-K precision (fraction of top-20%-ranked machines that fail within 30 days), which is the most operationally direct measure of scheduling value.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are first tuned on the validation plant set. To make the final comparison more robust, the top few candidate configurations are then checked on the test plant set, and the best-performing setup is selected for reporting. The final models are retrained on combined train+validation plant data using the selected hyperparameters before the test set metrics are reported.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four controls are applied. First, all primary metrics are reported per-plant and aggregated, with a mixed-effects analysis testing consistency of the performance gap across sites. Second, results are stratified by sensor coverage quintile to verify that gains are not confined to well-instrumented machines. Third, an ablation on a single machine family with uniform sensor coverage isolates the longitudinal feature effect from machine heterogeneity. Fourth, a sensitivity analysis excludes low-quality failure labels (no corroborating alarms) to test robustness to CMMS label noise.

This experiment establishes whether the longitudinal gradient-boosted survival model generalizes to unseen plants better than a static Cox PH model in this data regime. It does not claim generalizability beyond the machine types, plants, and sensor configurations represented in the dataset.

planted_issues.description: Preprocessing parameters (imputers, scalers, encoders) are fitted on the full dataset, including validation and test plants and future temporal windows. This leaks information about unseen plant-specific sensor distributions and alarm behaviors into the training process, artificially boosting apparent performance and invalidating the site-out generalization claim.

The test set is used during model selection to choose among top candidate configurations. This transforms the test set from an unbiased evaluation into part of the training loop, enabling overfitting to the specific characteristics of the held-out plants and undermining the validity of final performance estimates.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline Cox PH model is unfairly handicapped by not receiving longitudinal features, making the comparison invalid.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically compares a longitudinal modeling approach (features + flexible learner) against standard clinical/industrial practice (static features + Cox model). Using static features for the Cox model isolates the effect of the joint innovation; giving it longitudinal features would conflate architectural and representational improvements, making it impossible to attribute gains to either.

must_not_claim.claim: Applying preprocessing uniformly across splits is a best practice and improves stability.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While uniform application is correct, fitting the preprocessing on the full dataset — including test data — introduces leakage. The correct practice is to fit only on training data, then apply to validation and test to avoid contamination, especially in multi-site survival settings where site-level distributions may vary.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies that preprocessing is fitted on the full dataset, creating leakage from the held-out test plants and future window. The defense fails to justify this practice as acceptable. Verdict: critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that fitting imputers/scalers on test data leaks site-level distributional information and inflates generalization performance, especially critical in a site-out survival setting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that using the test set to select among candidate configurations contaminates the evaluation and biases final performance upward. The defense cannot legitimately justify test set usage for model selection. Verdict: critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer argues that any use of the test set for decision-making — even as a 'final checkpoint' — invalidates its role as an independent evaluation and enables overfitting, particularly with limited plant-level data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic raises concerns about the baseline model's lack of rolling features, but the defender correctly explains that this design isolates the joint effect of longitudinal representation and model flexibility as per the hypothesis. This concern is dismissed as not a flaw. However, the critic still wins on the two real issues.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer raises the preprocessing and evaluation contamination issues.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer concedes that the Cox model's static feature set is appropriate for testing the stated hypothesis, even if they identify it as a potential concern initially.

difficulty_justification: The case is challenging due to subtle but severe methodological flaws buried within otherwise sophisticated design elements; reviewers must distinguish between valid design choices (e.g., static Cox features) and invalid ones (e.g., full-data preprocessing fit), while recognizing that common practices like test set checkpoints constitute contamination in rigorous evaluation settings.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network built from wallet-to-wallet transaction networks and smart-contract interactions detects illicit cryptocurrency addresses more accurately on next-month activity than a feature-engineered XGBoost baseline trained on the same historical blockchain window.
domain: Cryptocurrency anti-money-laundering monitoring for a blockchain analytics provider supporting exchange compliance investigations

## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. Feature-Engineered XGBoost for Illicit Address Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of archival on-chain data from a single public blockchain (e.g., Ethereum mainnet), covering timestamped transactions, token transfers, smart-contract call traces, gas fees, and transaction amounts. Address cluster mappings — grouping multiple addresses belonging to the same real-world entity using co-spend heuristics and exchange deposit clustering — are computed once over the full dataset before any partitioning occurs. This pre-split clustering is necessary to correctly enforce entity-level split boundaries.

Labels are assembled from a curated union of OFAC/sanctions records, exchange-reported AML accounts, third-party attribution vendor exports (e.g., Chainalysis, Elliptic), and confirmed law enforcement seizure records. Critically, labels are assigned with their original attribution timestamp, not backdated. An address is considered labeled only from the date its attribution record was created. Addresses with no confirmed attribution — in either direction — are excluded from training and evaluation entirely, preventing unlabeled positives from contaminating the licit class. The label cutoff for training and validation is end of month 18; labels first observed in months 19–24 are reserved for test evaluation only.

### 2. Split Strategy

All splits are temporal and enforced at the entity cluster level. Training covers months 1–15, validation covers months 16–18, and the held-out test set covers months 19–24. Each cluster is assigned to exactly one partition based on the first observed activity timestamp of any address in that cluster. This dual constraint — temporal ordering plus cluster-level assignment — eliminates both label leakage (future enforcement actions informing training) and entity leakage (different addresses of the same actor appearing on both sides of a split boundary). A random or stratified-random split would be inappropriate here because the hypothesis concerns forward-in-time generalization under temporal distribution shift.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed, with per-entity features aggregated over each entity's full observed activity history in the dataset to provide the most stable behavioral profile before classification:

- **Non-relational tabular features:** transaction count, total sent/received volume, unique counterparty count, fan-in/fan-out ratio, average transaction size, interarrival time statistics (mean, std, entropy), fraction of smart-contract interactions, gas fee statistics, token diversity index.
- **Relational neighbor features (2-hop aggregates):** mean and standard deviation of degree, volume, and activity frequency among direct and second-degree neighbors in the transaction graph.

The XGBoost primary baseline receives both feature sets. A second XGBoost ablation baseline receives only non-relational features. The TGNN receives the raw temporal edge stream plus the non-relational features as initial node embeddings. All scalers, encoders, and imputation statistics are fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Attention Network (TGN; Rossi et al. 2020) with memory modules and time-encoding, trained on the temporal edge stream. The **primary baseline** is XGBoost trained on the matched full feature set including relational neighbor features. This is the critical fairness condition: the baseline must have access to equivalent relational information to avoid conflating architectural advantage with information advantage. A second **ablation baseline** — XGBoost without neighbor features — isolates the marginal value of relational signals from the marginal value of the TGNN architecture.

Both the TGNN and both baselines are tuned via random search over 40 configurations each, evaluated exclusively on the validation set using AUPRC. This ensures equivalent tuning effort across all models.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) on the test set. AUPRC is chosen over AUROC because illicit addresses represent a severe minority class (estimated <0.1% of labeled addresses), and AUROC is dominated by true-negative performance in this regime. AUPRC directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that maps to the operational objective: finding more illicit addresses while keeping analyst review volume manageable.

Secondary metrics include **Precision@500 and Precision@1000** (matching realistic analyst queue sizes), recall at fixed precision thresholds of 0.50 and 0.75, AUROC for literature comparability, and calibration metrics (Brier score, reliability diagram). All metrics are reported separately for newly active addresses (first seen in the test window) vs. addresses active during training — the hypothesis specifically claims improvement on newly active entities.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, and threshold decisions are made on the validation set (months 16–18) using AUPRC. The test set is loaded and evaluated exactly once, after all models are frozen. No adjustments are made after test set inspection. This policy is enforced by keeping test labels in a separate, access-controlled file.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the **relational information confound**: any TGNN advantage could stem from access to richer implicit neighborhood aggregation rather than the temporal graph architecture. This is controlled by giving the XGBoost baseline matched 2-hop relational features and using the no-relational-features ablation to disentangle the two contributions. Second, **entity leakage**: cluster-level splits ensure no real-world actor's addresses span the train/test boundary. Third, **temporal distribution shift**: performance is reported separately for early (months 19–21) and late (months 22–24) test windows, and any major enforcement events during the test period are logged as potential confounding label density shifts.

This experiment would establish whether a temporal GNN outperforms a well-resourced, relationally-informed XGBoost baseline on forward-in-time illicit address detection. It would not establish which specific graph structural properties drive any observed improvement, nor would it support generalization claims to other blockchains or address categories not represented in the evaluation data.

planted_issues.description: The feature engineering step aggregates per-entity features over each entity's full observed history in the dataset, which includes future activity (e.g., months 16–24) when constructing features for entities active earlier. This introduces post-prediction time information into the feature set, transforming the task from forward-in-time detection into a retrospective profiling problem. As a result, the evaluation no longer tests the model's ability to detect illicit behavior prospectively using only past information, violating the core hypothesis.

must_not_claim.claim: Address cluster mappings are computed over the full dataset before splitting, which could allow label leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Pre-split clustering using only structural heuristics (co-spend, exchange deposits) without label information is necessary to enforce entity-level splits correctly and does not introduce leakage. The clusters are derived solely from topological patterns, not labels, so this is a sound design choice in entity-level temporal studies.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of AUPRC instead of AUROC is inappropriate because AUROC is more standard in literature.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is the preferred metric in extreme class imbalance scenarios (<0.1% positives) where true negatives dominate AUROC. The operational goal is to improve precision and recall among top-ranked alerts, making AUPRC a more meaningful and stakeholder-aligned metric than AUROC.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A correct debate outcome requires the critic to identify that feature aggregation over the full entity history introduces future information, turning the task into a retrospective rather than prospective one. The defender must concede that this invalidates the forward-in-time evaluation and amend the feature aggregation to be restricted to the training window (months 1–15) for all entities.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that features for entities active in months 1–15 are computed using data from months 16–24, violating temporal validity and enabling the model to exploit future knowledge not available at the time of prediction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Alternatively, the critic may accept that cluster-level preprocessing is valid if they challenge only the aggregation window, provided they still require that features be derived only from information available up to the split boundary for each entity.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic acknowledges that pre-computing clusters is acceptable but insists that feature values must be computed using only past and contemporaneous data relative to the prediction time, not future activity.

All scalers, encoders, and imputation statistics are fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Attention Network (TGN; Rossi et al. 2020) with memory modules and time-encoding, trained on the temporal edge stream. The **primary baseline** is XGBoost trained on the matched full feature set including relational neighbor features. This is the critical fairness condition: the baseline must have access to equivalent relational information to avoid conflating architectural advantage with information advantage. A second **ablation baseline** — XGBoost without neighbor features — isolates the marginal value of relational signals from the marginal value of the TGNN architecture.

Both the TGNN and both baselines are tuned via random search over 40 configurations each, evaluated exclusively on the validation set using AUPRC. This ensures equivalent tuning effort across all models.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) on the test set. AUPRC is chosen over AUROC because illicit addresses represent a severe minority class (estimated <0.1% of labeled addresses), and AUROC is dominated by true-negative performance in this regime. AUPRC directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that maps to the operational objective: finding more illicit addresses while keeping analyst review volume manageable.

Secondary metrics include **Precision@500 and Precision@1000** (matching realistic analyst queue sizes), recall at fixed precision thresholds of 0.50 and 0.75, AUROC for literature comparability, and calibration metrics (Brier score, reliability diagram). All metrics are reported separately for newly active addresses (first seen in the test window) vs. addresses active during training — the hypothesis specifically claims improvement on newly active entities.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, and threshold decisions are made on the validation set (months 16–18) using AUPRC. The test set is loaded and evaluated exactly once, after all models are frozen. No adjustments are made after test set inspection. This policy is enforced by keeping test labels in a separate, access-controlled file.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the **relational information confound**: any TGNN advantage could stem from access to richer implicit neighborhood aggregation rather than the temporal graph architecture. This is controlled by giving the XGBoost baseline matched 2-hop relational features and using the no-relational-features ablation to disentangle the two contributions. Second, **entity leakage**: cluster-level splits ensure no real-world actor's addresses span the train/test boundary. Third, **temporal distribution shift**: performance is reported separately for early (months 19–21) and late (months 22–24) test windows, and any major enforcement events during the test period are logged as potential confounding label density shifts.

This experiment would establish whether a temporal GNN outperforms a well-resourced, relationally-informed XGBoost baseline on forward-in-time illicit address detection. It would not establish which specific graph structural properties drive any observed improvement, nor would it support generalization claims to other blockchains or address categories not represented in the evaluation data.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted text sounds methodologically reasonable — using 'full history' for 'stable profiling' — but violates temporal validity. Evaluators must recognize that this undermines the prospective nature of the evaluation, despite otherwise sound controls for leakage and class imbalance.

hypothesis: For weekly spare-parts demand forecasting at a multi-warehouse industrial equipment distributor, a global temporal fusion transformer trained jointly across SKUs and warehouses with static item metadata and promotion/lead-time covariates will reduce weighted quantile loss versus per-SKU gradient-boosted tree forecasting models built on lagged demand and engineered calendar features.
domain: Manufacturing and industrial supply-chain planning for aftermarket spare parts across regional warehouses
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. Per-SKU GBT for Weekly Spare-Parts Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data

We would use 4 years (208 weeks) of weekly SKU-by-warehouse transactional data extracted from the distributor's ERP and WMS. The panel includes observed order quantities, inventory positions, binary stockout flags, replenishment lead times, supplier metadata, item hierarchy, planned promotions, and calendar features. Any SKU-warehouse series with fewer than 8 weeks of recorded history is excluded from all analyses.

A critical preprocessing step precedes all modeling: demand censoring correction. On weeks where a stockout flag is active, the observed quantity understates true demand. We impute those weeks using the series' rolling average demand over the nearest non-censored 12-week window, applied causally (using only history up to that week). This correction ensures both models are trained on estimated unconstrained demand rather than constrained sales, and that neither model is artifically advantaged by its treatment of zeros.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally with no randomization:
- **Training:** Weeks 1–156 (3 years)
- **Validation:** Weeks 157–182 (6 months)
- **Test:** Weeks 183–208 (6 months)

A temporal split is mandatory here. Random or stratified splits would allow future demand patterns, upcoming promotions, and post-disruption lead-time regimes to leak backward into training — producing systematically optimistic estimates for both models and potentially favoring whichever model is more prone to overfitting those leaked patterns. The 6-month validation window covers a full seasonal half-cycle and enables robust hyperparameter selection. SKUs introduced after week 156 are excluded from the primary evaluation to avoid conflating the cold-start generalization question with the main hypothesis; they are analyzed as a secondary stratum.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the **TFT**: past time-varying inputs (corrected demand, inventory position, stockout flag, realized lead time); known future inputs (promotion flags, calendar encodings, scheduled lead-time changes); static covariates (SKU category/subcategory embeddings, criticality tier, warehouse region, training-window demand statistics including mean and coefficient of variation, item age in weeks).

For the **GBT baseline**: lagged demand at lags 1, 2, 4, 8, 13, 26, and 52 weeks; rolling mean and standard deviation at those windows; calendar features; promotion indicator; lead time. The baseline deliberately receives the same promotion and lead-time covariates as the TFT so that any performance gap cannot be attributed to covariate access alone.

All scaling, normalization, and categorical encoding are fit exclusively on the training window (weeks 1–156) and applied without re-fitting to the validation and test windows.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **TFT** is trained globally across all SKU-warehouse series, outputting probabilistic quantile forecasts at q=0.5, q=0.75, and q=0.90 for a 4-week-ahead horizon. Hyperparameters are tuned via 50-trial Bayesian optimization using validation WQL as the objective.

The **GBT baseline** consists of per-SKU LightGBM models. Point forecasts are converted to quantile estimates via conformal residual intervals calibrated on training-window residuals. Per-SKU hyperparameters are selected using 5-fold expanding-window cross-validation within the training window only. Tuning compute budgets are equalized (wall-clock time) across the two approaches.

This baseline is the correct comparison: per-SKU GBT with engineered lag features and calendar covariates is the industry-standard approach for this domain. The hypothesis is specifically that global cross-series modeling adds value beyond well-tuned local models.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is Mean Weighted Quantile Loss (WQL) averaged over q=0.75 and q=0.90, normalized per series by training-window mean demand before aggregation. These quantiles are specifically the ones used in safety-stock calculation; optimizing and evaluating at them directly aligns with the stakeholder's operational objective of reducing stockouts without symmetric accuracy being the objective. Normalizing by series mean prevents high-volume SKUs from dominating the aggregate.

Secondary metrics include: WQL at q=0.50 (median accuracy); empirical coverage rates at q=0.75 and q=0.90 (calibration); WQL stratified by Croston intermittency class; WQL stratified by SKU age; and WQL on the subset of SKUs with at least one test-window stockout.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and architecture decisions reference the validation window only. The test set is never consulted during development. Final evaluation against the test set runs once, in a single immutable batch, after all modeling decisions are frozen. To isolate the contribution of temporal attention from cross-series information, we run a secondary ablation: a TFT with attention depth reduced to 1 step, which approximates a covariate-rich regression without meaningful sequence modeling.

### 7. Confounds and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled:
1. **Covariate access**: the baseline receives identical promotion and lead-time features — any TFT gain reflects modeling capacity, not information advantage.
2. **Cold-start advantage**: new SKUs are excluded from the primary stratum, preventing the TFT's metadata-embedding advantage from inflating headline numbers.
3. **Censoring treatment**: both models receive the same censoring-corrected labels.

The experiment would establish whether the TFT outperforms per-SKU GBT under matched covariate conditions on a specific distributor's 6-month test horizon. It would not establish that TFT is universally superior, that gains persist under severe distribution shift, or that the TFT advantage is specifically due to attention rather than cross-series parameter sharing — the ablation provides partial but not complete evidence on the latter.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split creates a small validation or test set that lacks statistical power.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The validation and test sets each span 6 months (26 weeks), which is sufficient to cover seasonal patterns and provide robust evaluation for weekly forecasts in this domain. Temporal integrity is more important than size, and random splits would introduce future leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding SKUs introduced after week 156 biases the evaluation by removing potentially important long-tail items.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The exclusion of new SKUs is a deliberate control to prevent confounding the core hypothesis (cross-series modeling advantage) with the TFT's known strength in cold-start scenarios via metadata embeddings. These SKUs are analyzed separately in a secondary stratum, preserving transparency without contaminating the primary result.

must_not_claim.claim: Using rolling average imputation for censored demand introduces bias by smoothing out demand spikes during stockouts.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Rolling average imputation within a 12-week causal window is a standard and defensible Tobit-style correction for stockout censoring in intermittent demand settings. It avoids assuming perfect substitution behavior or relying on external demand proxies, and is applied consistently to both models, so no unfair advantage is introduced.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the experimental design is sound and methodologically robust. No critical flaws are identified, and all design choices are justified in context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises a concern about a component of the design (e.g., split strategy, metric choice, baseline) but the defender successfully explains the domain rationale, temporal constraints, or confound controls that justify the choice.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies a potential concern but acknowledges it is already addressed in the design (e.g., cold-start SKUs are handled via secondary analysis, covariate access is matched). The final verdict is that the baseline comparison is fair and the evaluation setup is valid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender references explicit controls in Section 7 (confounds) or alignment with stakeholder objectives in the evaluation metrics, showing that the design anticipates and neutralizes the raised issue.
