difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the flaw is subtle — the corrupted text uses language about 'consistent normalization' that sounds methodologically sound, but overlooks temporal integrity. A strong evaluator must recognize that even routine preprocessing can cause leakage in time-based settings, especially with techniques like target encoding or imputation where future statistics distort past distributions.

hypothesis: For first-year undergraduate courses on a large online learning platform, a sequence model built from timestamped clickstream and assessment event histories predicts next-week dropout risk more accurately than a baseline using only aggregated weekly engagement features, when evaluated on future academic terms.
domain: Education technology: dropout risk prediction in credit-bearing first-year university courses delivered through a learning management system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Aggregated Features for Weekly Dropout Risk Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses four academic years of LMS event logs merged with student information system records, covering all first-year credit-bearing undergraduate courses with at least 30 enrolled students and at least 8 weeks of active delivery. The prediction target is a binary per-student per-week dropout label: a student is labeled positive at week W if they officially withdraw before the end of week W+1, or if they show zero LMS activity in weeks W+1 and W+2 and ultimately do not complete the course. Labels are derived from official withdrawal records; ambiguous cases such as approved medical leaves are excluded. Only weeks 2 through 7 of each course are included as prediction opportunities, targeting the window where intervention is both possible and meaningful.

### 2. Split Strategy

The full dataset is randomly split at the student-course-week level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test, stratified by the dropout label so that the rare positive class is balanced across partitions. This provides more stable class proportions and ensures all terms and course types are represented in each split. No student-course data from week W+1 onward is used to construct features for week W, enforcing the prediction-time information cutoff throughout.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Two Conditions

Two feature sets are constructed using only information available at or before the end of prediction week W. The **baseline feature set** consists of aggregated weekly event counts per category (views, submissions, logins, forum posts, video completions) for the current and prior two weeks, course-relative z-scores of each count, days since last login, cumulative grade to date, missed deadline flags, and student static attributes. The **experimental feature set** extends the baseline by adding the full event-level token sequence (event type, day-of-week, hour bucket, relative position) for the same three-week window, with the static and cumulative features appended at the sequence level. To keep feature definitions consistent across years and course sections, all scaling, normalization, imputation, and encoding transformers are fit once on the full dataset before splitting and then applied uniformly across training, validation, and test partitions.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The sequence model is a lightweight Transformer encoder (4 heads, 2 layers, embedding dimension 64) with a CLS token that aggregates sequence context, followed by a two-layer MLP classifier. The primary baseline is logistic regression trained on the aggregated weekly feature set with standard default settings, providing a simple and interpretable reference point for weekly aggregate modeling. Tuning effort is concentrated on the Transformer, with a 60-configuration random search used to optimize the sequence model on the validation set. A critical secondary ablation trains LightGBM on a flattened version of the full granular feature set to isolate whether any observed gain is attributable to sequential structure versus simply richer information access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is ROC-AUC, evaluated on the held-out test set. This is appropriate because it is threshold-independent, robust across class balance settings, and directly comparable to prior classification literature. Secondary metrics include Recall at top-5% and top-10% alert rates, average prediction lead time before confirmed withdrawal, expected calibration error, and precision-recall summaries stratified by course domain and student prior GPA band.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter and architecture decisions are initially guided by validation-set performance. After this stage, the strongest candidate checkpoints and minor architecture variants are compared on the test set, and the best-performing model is selected as the final model to report. Final metrics are then reported for that selected model on the same test split.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the information-content confound is handled by the granular-feature LightGBM ablation: only if the Transformer outperforms this ablation can the advantage be attributed to sequential structure. Second, course composition differences are addressed by course-relative z-scoring of all event counts and by reporting metrics stratified by course type and term. Third, cohort shift across terms is assessed via a covariate shift diagnostic on aggregated feature distributions between training and test terms; if substantial drift is detected, results are additionally reported on a propensity-matched subset of stable courses. The experiment establishes whether sequence modeling provides a measurable gain over aggregate-feature baselines; it does not establish causal dropout mechanisms or generalize to non-first-year or non-credit-bearing contexts.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split at the student-course-week level rather than a chronological term-level split. This allows data from future academic terms to leak into the training set, violating the prospective evaluation requirement of the hypothesis. Because course designs, platform features, and student behavior evolve over time, this inflates performance estimates and fails to simulate real-world deployment conditions.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, normalization, imputation, encoding) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which contaminates the training process with information from the validation and test sets. This is especially harmful for course-relative z-scoring and categorical encoding, where future term distributions influence the transformation logic, leading to optimistic bias in model evaluation.

The primary baseline is an untuned logistic regression with default settings, while the sequence model receives substantial hyperparameter tuning. This creates an unfair comparison: any observed performance gap may reflect tuning effort rather than the inherent value of sequential modeling. A strong, tuned tree-based model like LightGBM is the appropriate baseline for this domain.

ROC-AUC is used as the primary evaluation metric despite the rare-event, low-alert-capacity context. For a dropout prediction task where stakeholders need high precision among a small fraction of flagged students, ROC-AUC is insensitive to performance in the operationally relevant region. Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUCPR) is a more appropriate primary metric.

The test set is used to select the best-performing model variant from among candidates, which turns the test set into a secondary validation set. This introduces bias and invalidates the final performance estimate as an unbiased measure of generalization to unseen future data.

must_not_claim.claim: Course-relative z-scoring is problematic because it normalizes per course and might mask meaningful cross-course patterns.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Course-relative z-scoring is appropriate here because event activity levels vary dramatically by course design and instructor. Without it, models could exploit course-specific volume as a proxy for risk rather than learning meaningful behavioral signals. The design correctly isolates per-student deviation from their course norm.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a secondary ablation with LightGBM on flattened granular features is unnecessary or redundant.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This ablation is essential to control for the information-content confound. It ensures that any performance gain from the sequence model is due to modeling sequential structure, not merely access to finer-grained features. This strengthens causal interpretability within the experimental design.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of ambiguous cases like medical withdrawals invalidates the dropout label definition.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The label definition appropriately focuses on clear dropout signals that can be operationally acted upon. Including uncertain cases would introduce noise and reduce construct validity. The study explicitly notes these exclusions and does not claim to model all forms of withdrawal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies the temporal leakage due to random splitting and explains that the hypothesis requires prospective validation on future terms. The defender cannot credibly rebut this flaw.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer notes that academic years are temporally ordered and that deployment is prospective, so a chronological split is non-negotiable for valid generalization.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer detects preprocessing leakage by identifying that transformers are fit on the full dataset, violating temporal and informational boundaries. This is especially damning when combined with the random split.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that course-relative normalization and categorical encoding depend on future data distributions, leading to data leakage and inflated performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer challenges the use of untuned logistic regression as a baseline, arguing it does not represent current best practice and undermines comparison validity.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer cites that in ed-tech settings, gradient-boosted trees (e.g., LightGBM) are standard baselines for tabular risk modeling and should receive equivalent tuning effort to ensure a fair test.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Defender could argue if the goal were interpretability or simplicity, but in this context, performance comparison is primary, so defense fails.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer argues that ROC-AUC is inappropriate for a rare positive class and that AUCPR better captures performance in the low-alert-rate regime.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer references stakeholder constraints (limited advisor capacity) and notes that ROC-AUC can mask poor precision in the top-ranked predictions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Defender might argue comparability to literature, but this does not override operational relevance; defense is weak.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies test set reuse for model selection, rendering the final evaluation biased.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer states that once the test set informs model choice, it ceases to be independent and cannot provide unbiased performance estimates.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Aggregated Features for Weekly Dropout Risk Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses four academic years of LMS event logs merged with student information system records, covering all first-year credit-bearing undergraduate courses with at least 30 enrolled students and at least 8 weeks of active delivery. The prediction target is a binary per-student per-week dropout label: a student is labeled positive at week W if they officially withdraw before the end of week W+1, or if they show zero LMS activity in weeks W+1 and W+2 and ultimately do not complete the course. Labels are derived from official withdrawal records; ambiguous cases such as approved medical leaves are excluded. Only weeks 2 through 7 of each course are included as prediction opportunities, targeting the window where intervention is both possible and meaningful.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically at the term level: Years 1–2 for training, Year 3 for validation, and Year 4 as the held-out test set. A term-level temporal split is essential here because the hypothesis explicitly concerns prospective deployment performance — random or stratified splits would allow future cohort behavior to leak into training and would not simulate the real deployment scenario. No student-course data from week W+1 onward is used to construct features for week W, enforcing the prediction-time information cutoff throughout.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Two Conditions

Two feature sets are constructed using only information available at or before the end of prediction week W. The **baseline feature set** consists of aggregated weekly event counts per category (views, submissions, logins, forum posts, video completions) for the current and prior two weeks, course-relative z-scores of each count, days since last login, cumulative grade to date, missed deadline flags, and student static attributes. The **experimental feature set** extends the baseline by adding the full event-level token sequence (event type, day-of-week, hour bucket, relative position) for the same three-week window, with the static and cumulative features appended at the sequence level. All scaling, normalization, and encoding transformers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The sequence model is a lightweight Transformer encoder (4 heads, 2 layers, embedding dimension 64) with a CLS token that aggregates sequence context, followed by a two-layer MLP classifier. The primary baseline is LightGBM trained on the aggregated weekly feature set, representing current best practice. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: 60-configuration random search evaluated on the Year 3 validation set using AUCPR. A critical secondary ablation trains LightGBM on a flattened version of the full granular feature set to isolate whether any observed gain is attributable to sequential structure versus simply richer information access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUCPR), evaluated on the Year 4 test set. This is appropriate because dropout is a rare event (estimated 5–15% positive rate), because stakeholders operate under staffing constraints that fix alert volume, and because no deployment threshold is specified in advance. Secondary metrics include Recall at top-5% and top-10% alert rates, average prediction lead time before confirmed withdrawal, expected calibration error, and AUCPR stratified by course domain and student prior GPA band.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter and architecture decisions are made using the Year 3 validation set AUCPR. The test set is evaluated exactly once after model selection is finalized and checkpoints are frozen. No iteration based on test set feedback is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the information-content confound is handled by the granular-feature LightGBM ablation: only if the Transformer outperforms this ablation can the advantage be attributed to sequential structure. Second, course composition differences are addressed by course-relative z-scoring of all event counts and by reporting metrics stratified by course type and term. Third, cohort shift across terms is assessed via a covariate shift diagnostic on aggregated feature distributions between training and test terms; if substantial drift is detected, results are additionally reported on a propensity-matched subset of stable courses. The experiment establishes whether sequence modeling provides a measurable AUCPR gain over strong aggregated-feature baselines on future terms; it does not establish causal dropout mechanisms or generalize to non-first-year or non-credit-bearing contexts.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because it contains multiple interacting flaws—particularly the combination of temporal leakage and preprocessing leakage—which amplify each other. Additionally, the corrupted design mimics a plausible, carefully considered experiment, requiring deep domain and methodological reasoning to detect all issues.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 diagnosis code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on less frequent codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient EHR text at a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses three to four years of de-identified inpatient encounter records from the health system's EHR, covering multiple hospital sites. Each encounter contributes: the discharge summary, the admission history and physical (H&P), and all finalized attending progress notes. Labels are the final ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes recorded on the billing-finalized record by certified coders — these represent the authoritative coding decision, not interim or draft codes.

A critical data quality constraint: only notes with finalization timestamps at or before the discharge date are included. Post-discharge addenda or amendments are excluded to prevent leakage from documentation artifacts that would not exist at prediction time in a real coding-assist workflow. An active label space is defined by applying a minimum frequency threshold of 50 training-set encounters per code; rarer codes are excluded from formal evaluation targets but are tracked in a held-out rare-code analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is divided using a stratified random split at the encounter level: 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test, with stratification based on the number of active diagnosis codes per encounter so label complexity is balanced across splits. A patient-level constraint is enforced — if a patient has admissions spanning split boundaries, all encounters are assigned to the same split — preventing patient-level leakage.

This split preserves comparable case complexity across all partitions while still avoiding overlap from the same patient, giving a stable estimate of model performance across the available encounter distribution.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input text: the concatenated discharge summary and all eligible progress notes, cleaned with a shared preprocessing pipeline (no model-specific advantages). TF-IDF features use word unigrams, bigrams, and character trigrams, L2-normalized, with a single vocabulary and IDF matrix fit once on the full corpus before splitting so feature dimensions remain perfectly consistent across train, validation, and test. For the transformer, text exceeding 512 tokens is handled via a sliding-window chunking approach: segments are encoded independently using the pretrained clinical tokenizer, and CLS token representations are mean-pooled before the classification head. All truncation and pooling decisions are locked before training begins.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is BioClinicalBERT (or equivalent clinical-domain pretrained transformer) fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and logit-adjusted binary cross-entropy loss. The baseline is a TF-IDF + One-vs-Rest Logistic Regression, with L2 regularization tuned via random search on the validation set. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (matched number of trials), the same label space, and the same text input. This baseline is the correct comparator: it represents the strongest conventional non-contextual NLP approach and isolates the effect of contextual representations.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 across the active label space. Macro-F1 weights each code equally, directly serving the stakeholder requirement to improve coverage of rare diagnoses rather than inflating performance on common ones. Secondary metrics include micro-F1, Precision@5 and Precision@10 (for the coding-assist workflow), and macro-F1 stratified by code frequency tier (high/medium/low) to directly test the sub-claim about rare codes.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use only the validation set. The best checkpoint by validation macro-F1 is selected. The test set is used exactly once, after all development decisions are finalized, and results for both models are reported simultaneously.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly addressed. Note length is controlled by stratifying secondary metrics by note-length quartile and providing both models with identical input text. Site-level documentation style differences are diagnosed via site-stratified macro-F1 reports. Token truncation effects on the transformer are probed via an ablation using only the discharge summary. Preprocessing differences are eliminated by applying a single shared cleaning pipeline to both models. The experiment establishes transformer superiority over a tuned TF-IDF baseline for this health system and time period; it does not claim generalizability to other institutions or coding standards.

planted_issues.description: The use of a stratified random split by encounter instead of a temporal split introduces temporal leakage: coding practices, ICD-10 guidelines, and documentation styles evolve over time, so including future encounters in training that have already seen future coding patterns leads to over-optimistic performance estimates that won't generalize to real-world deployment. The random split invalidates the assumption that the test set reflects future operational conditions.

Fitting the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF matrix on the full corpus before splitting leaks information from the validation and test sets into the feature engineering step. This violates the principle that no statistics from held-out data should influence training, potentially introducing subtle biases in term weighting and feature selection — especially for time-evolving clinical terminology — resulting in inflated baseline performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The patient-level constraint on the random split is insufficient and introduces leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Grouping all encounters from the same patient into one split is a correct choice to prevent patient-level leakage and is properly implemented; it is not a flaw, even though it does not address the larger temporal generalization issue.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by number of diagnosis codes per encounter is inappropriate or introduces bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification on label complexity (number of active codes per encounter) is a sound design decision to ensure balanced representation of simple and complex cases across splits. It does not constitute a flaw in isolation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the random split creates temporal leakage by mixing future coding patterns into training, undermining the validity of test performance as a predictor of real-world deployment success. The defender cannot legitimately justify this split given the known temporal drift in clinical coding standards.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that in healthcare NLP, temporal splits are standard because coding practices evolve, and random splits produce optimistically biased results.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that fitting the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights on the full dataset before splitting leaks validation and test information into the feature representation, violating data isolation principles. This preprocessing leakage could inflate baseline performance and invalidate comparisons.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic points out that text normalization steps like IDF computation must be fit only on training data to avoid leakage, especially in time-series-like clinical data with linguistic drift.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic recognizes that the two corruptions compound: the random split allows future-era documents into training, and fitting TF-IDF globally ensures those future terms shape the baseline’s feature space, jointly inflating performance beyond what would be achievable in deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic articulates the synergistic effect — that preprocessing leakage exacerbates the consequences of distributional shift enabled by the random split — making the flaw more severe than either alone.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses three to four years of de-identified inpatient encounter records from the health system's EHR, covering multiple hospital sites. Each encounter contributes: the discharge summary, the admission history and physical (H&P), and all finalized attending progress notes. Labels are the final ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes recorded on the billing-finalized record by certified coders — these represent the authoritative coding decision, not interim or draft codes.

A critical data quality constraint: only notes with finalization timestamps at or before the discharge date are included. Post-discharge addenda or amendments are excluded to prevent leakage from documentation artifacts that would not exist at prediction time in a real coding-assist workflow. An active label space is defined by applying a minimum frequency threshold of 50 training-set encounters per code; rarer codes are excluded from formal evaluation targets but are tracked in a held-out rare-code analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is divided temporally by encounter discharge date: the earliest 70% of encounters form the training set, the next 15% the validation set, and the final 15% the held-out test set. A patient-level constraint is enforced — if a patient has admissions spanning split boundaries, all encounters are assigned to their earliest window — preventing patient-level leakage.

A temporal split is essential here. ICD-10 coding conventions evolve (guideline updates, new codes), and a random split would permit future coding patterns to inform training, yielding optimistically biased test estimates that would not reflect real deployment performance.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input text: the concatenated discharge summary and all eligible progress notes, cleaned with a shared preprocessing pipeline (no model-specific advantages). TF-IDF features use word unigrams, bigrams, and character trigrams, L2-normalized, with vocabulary and IDF weights fit on training data only. For the transformer, text exceeding 512 tokens is handled via a sliding-window chunking approach: segments are encoded independently using the pretrained clinical tokenizer, and CLS token representations are mean-pooled before the classification head. All truncation and pooling decisions are locked before training begins.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is BioClinicalBERT (or equivalent clinical-domain pretrained transformer) fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and logit-adjusted binary cross-entropy loss. The baseline is a TF-IDF + One-vs-Rest Logistic Regression, with L2 regularization tuned via random search on the validation set. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (matched number of trials), the same label space, and the same text input. This baseline is the correct comparator: it represents the strongest conventional non-contextual NLP approach and isolates the effect of contextual representations.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 across the active label space. Macro-F1 weights each code equally, directly serving the stakeholder requirement to improve coverage of rare diagnoses rather than inflating performance on common ones. Secondary metrics include micro-F1, Precision@5 and Precision@10 (for the coding-assist workflow), and macro-F1 stratified by code frequency tier (high/medium/low) to directly test the sub-claim about rare codes.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use only the validation set. The best checkpoint by validation macro-F1 is selected. The test set is used exactly once, after all development decisions are finalized, and results for both models are reported simultaneously.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly addressed. Note length is controlled by stratifying secondary metrics by note-length quartile and providing both models with identical input text. Site-level documentation style differences are diagnosed via site-stratified macro-F1 reports. Token truncation effects on the transformer are probed via an ablation using only the discharge summary. Preprocessing differences are eliminated by applying a single shared cleaning pipeline to both models. The experiment establishes transformer superiority over a tuned TF-IDF baseline for this health system and time period; it does not claim generalizability to other institutions or coding standards.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws are methodologically subtle and occur in standard-seeming design elements (splitting and preprocessing); a reviewer must understand both temporal drift in clinical coding and strict data isolation principles in feature engineering to detect both corruptions. The compounding effect increases severity but requires synthesis to identify.

hypothesis: A transformer-based ranking model that jointly encodes a shopper’s recent click/add-to-cart/purchase sequence and item text-image embeddings will produce higher next-session product recommendation NDCG@10 than a matrix-factorization recommender using only user-item interaction history on a large online fashion marketplace.
domain: E-commerce fashion marketplace personalization for next-session product recommendation across web and mobile surfaces
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer Sequential Ranker vs. Matrix Factorization for Next-Session Fashion Recommendation

### 1. Data
The experiment uses 18 months of timestamped, anonymized interaction logs from the production fashion marketplace. Interaction types included are impressions, clicks, add-to-cart events, and purchases, along with session boundary markers and device/channel identifiers. Product catalog data — titles, descriptions, brand, category, price, and discount state — is version-controlled as daily snapshots so that each training example reflects the catalog state at the time of the session, not a retrospective view. Text embeddings are generated offline using a frozen sentence-transformer model; image embeddings are generated using a frozen CLIP-ViT model. Both embedding sets are computed once and treated as static features throughout the experiment, ensuring that any observed improvement is attributable to the ranking architecture rather than embedding quality.

To keep the evaluation grounded, relevance labels are defined as binary signals over the impressed candidate set: an item is relevant if the user clicked, added to cart, or purchased it in the target session. This avoids evaluating models against the full catalog, where unobserved items create a noisy and biased relevance signal. A purchase-only label is computed as a secondary signal.

### 2. Split Strategy
Data is randomly split at the session level into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% test, stratified by interaction label prevalence so each partition has a similar mix of positive and negative examples. Splitting at the session level still prevents duplicate examples across partitions while maximizing data efficiency and ensuring that all models are trained and evaluated on a representative sample of the marketplace.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
To ensure consistent feature spaces across all partitions, vocabulary tables, scalar feature normalizers, and category encoders are fit once on the full dataset before splitting, then applied identically to train, validation, and test. The user interaction sequence is truncated to the 50 most recent events; items outside the training vocabulary receive a dedicated OOV embedding. Item features fed to the transformer and the content-aware baseline are identical: frozen text embedding, frozen image embedding, log-price, discount flag, days-since-listing, and category/brand embeddings.

### 4. Models and Baselines
Three models are trained and evaluated:

- **Proposed model**: A 4-layer, 8-head transformer encoder over the user's recent interaction sequence, with a cross-attention or MLP scoring head that jointly considers user state and item content features. Trained with sampled softmax loss using in-batch and hard negatives from impressed-but-not-clicked items.
- **Baseline 1 — iALS Matrix Factorization**: Implicit ALS using only the user-item interaction matrix with click/ATC/purchase weighting. No content features. Tuned on validation NDCG@10 over embedding dimension, regularization, and confidence weight.
- **Baseline 2 — Content-Aware Two-Tower (non-sequential)**: Same item content features as the transformer, same loss, same negative sampling, but no sequence modeling. This baseline is essential for isolating whether gains come from sequential architecture or from the richer item representation — the primary confound identified for this hypothesis.

All three models receive an equivalent hyperparameter tuning budget measured in wall-clock compute time, and tuning is performed solely on the validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is **NDCG@10 over the impressed candidate set**, averaged across test sessions. This aligns directly with the stakeholder objective of surfacing relevant items at the top of the recommendation list. Statistical significance is assessed via paired permutation test (10,000 permutations, p < 0.01); a gain of at least +1.0 NDCG@10 points is defined as the threshold for practical significance.

Secondary metrics are: HR@10, MRR@10, purchase-only NDCG@10 as a revenue proxy, catalog coverage@10 to detect popularity collapse, and cold-start NDCG@10 on items listed fewer than 14 days before the session.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Discipline
Hyperparameter selection and all architecture decisions are made using validation-set NDCG@10 only. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all configurations are frozen, and all three models are scored in the same pass to prevent selective reporting.

### 7. Confound Controls
Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, exposure bias: evaluation is restricted to the impressed slate; IPS-weighted NDCG is computed as a sensitivity check. Second, content vs. architecture confound: Baseline 2 (content-aware non-sequential) directly attributes any incremental gain to the transformer's sequential reasoning. Third, catalog churn: a cold-start subgroup analysis isolates performance on new items. Results are also stratified by user activity level (low, medium, high history length) to scope the claim — if gains concentrate only in high-history users, the hypothesis is supported more narrowly than stated.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random session-level split instead of a temporal split, allowing future user behavior and item popularity patterns to leak into the training data. Since next-session recommendation is a forecasting task on rapidly changing fashion catalogs, this invalidates the evaluation by turning it into an interpolation problem and artificially inflating model performance.

Preprocessing transformers such as vocabulary tables and feature normalizers are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from the validation and test periods into the training pipeline. In a time-ordered domain with shifting catalog and feature distributions, this enables the model to learn from future patterns, contaminating evaluation and reducing the real difficulty of predicting future behavior.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is too small (only 10%) to yield reliable estimates of model performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Test set size is not the issue — in fact, a 10% temporal slice (months 16–18) represents a substantial volume of real-world traffic over a full quarter. The critical flaw is not the size but the *timing*: random splitting breaks temporal integrity and invalidates the forecasting setting regardless of set size.

must_not_claim.claim: Fitting preprocessing on the full dataset is acceptable because it ensures consistent feature representation across all partitions.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While consistency is desirable, it cannot come at the cost of temporal leakage. In forecasting tasks, preprocessing must be fit only on past data to reflect a real deployment scenario. The sound design correctly fits all transformers on training data alone to prevent future information from influencing feature construction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies both the random session split and preprocessing leakage as violations of temporal integrity. They explain that these flaws together create a highly optimistic, unrealistic evaluation setting that inflates model performance. The final verdict is that the experiment must be re-run with a proper temporal split and preprocessing fit only on training data.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly links the flawed split and preprocessing choices to temporal leakage and explains how they compromise the forecasting nature of the task, especially given rapid catalog turnover and exposure bias.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer recognizes that while the preprocessing flaw might seem minor in isolation, it interacts dangerously with the random temporal split: fitting on the full dataset amplifies the leakage caused by shuffling future sessions into training. By showing this compounding effect, they demonstrate that the evaluation is structurally compromised.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic highlights the multiplicative impact of combining a non-temporal split with preprocessing leakage — together, they allow future item popularity, user trends, and distribution shifts to influence both training data and feature engineering.

### 1. Data
The experiment uses 18 months of timestamped, anonymized interaction logs from the production fashion marketplace. Interaction types included are impressions, clicks, add-to-cart events, and purchases, along with session boundary markers and device/channel identifiers. ### 2. Split Strategy
Data is split strictly by time: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 for the held-out test set. Splits are at the session level, so a user's early sessions train the model while their later sessions evaluate it. A temporal split is non-negotiable here: fashion catalogs turn over rapidly, random splits would leak future item popularity and repeated-exposure patterns, and next-session recommendation is a forecasting task that must be validated on data that post-dates the training window. - **Baseline 2 — Content-Aware Two-Tower (non-sequential)**: Same item content features as the transformer, same loss, same negative sampling, but no sequence modeling. This baseline is essential for isolating whether gains come from sequential architecture or from the richer item representation — the primary confound identified for this hypothesis.

All three models receive an equivalent hyperparameter tuning budget measured in wall-clock compute time, and tuning is performed solely on the validation set.

Secondary metrics are: HR@10, MRR@10, purchase-only NDCG@10 as a revenue proxy, catalog coverage@10 to detect popularity collapse, and cold-start NDCG@10 on items listed fewer than 14 days before the session.

### 7. Confound Controls
Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, exposure bias: evaluation is restricted to the impressed slate; IPS-weighted NDCG is computed as a sensitivity check. Second, content vs. architecture confound: Baseline 2 (content-aware non-sequential) directly attributes any incremental gain to the transformer's sequential reasoning. Third, catalog churn: a cold-start subgroup analysis isolates performance on new items. difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws are methodologically subtle and involve time-sensitive data practices that may appear reasonable in static datasets. A reviewer must recognize that both the split strategy and preprocessing choice are invalid in forecasting contexts, especially when combined.

hypothesis: For ICU patients with suspected sepsis, a multimodal transformer that jointly models irregular vital-sign/lab time series and free-text nursing notes within the first 6 hours of admission will predict vasopressor initiation in the next 12 hours more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree trained on engineered summary features from structured EHR data alone.
domain: Critical care clinical decision support in hospital ICUs for early deterioration risk stratification in suspected sepsis patients
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Transformer vs. GBT for Vasopressor Prediction in Suspected Sepsis

### 1. Cohort and Data Strategy

The study uses MIMIC-IV (or equivalent de-identified critical care EHR data), drawing from all available years of adult ICU admissions. The cohort is restricted to suspected sepsis encounters, operationally defined using Sepsis-3 criteria: receipt of blood culture or IV antibiotics within 6 hours of ICU admission, combined with a SOFA score ≥2. The prediction anchor T is fixed at exactly 6 hours post-ICU-admission, giving each encounter a well-defined observation window [T−6h, T] and a prospective label window [T+6h, T+18h].

Patients who received vasopressors before T are excluded to avoid retroactive prediction. Patients who died or were discharged before T+6h are likewise excluded. This yields a clean binary classification target: vasopressor initiation in the 12-hour window following the prediction point.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split **chronologically** by ICU admission date: the earliest 70% of qualifying encounters form the training set, the next 15% the validation set, and the final 15% the held-out test set. This temporal ordering mirrors prospective deployment and prevents future clinical practice patterns, documentation norms, and formulary changes from contaminating the training window. To prevent patient-level leakage, all encounters belonging to the same patient are assigned to the same partition. The calendar year boundaries of each split are reported explicitly for reproducibility.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature representations are constructed from identical underlying raw data:

- **GBT features:** Engineered summary statistics from the [T−6h, T] window — aggregated vital signs (min, max, mean, last observed value, linear trend slope for HR, MAP, SpO2, RR, temperature), most recent lab values (lactate, WBC, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, BUN), shock index, SOFA component scores, comorbidity flags (Charlson Comorbidity Index), demographics, and binary missingness indicators for all labs. No temporal sequence structure is preserved.

- **Multimodal transformer inputs:** The same vitals and labs as irregular event sequences with absolute timestamps (processed by a time-aware encoder), plus all nursing notes authored in [T−6h, T] encoded by Bio_ClinicalBERT. The same structured demographic and comorbidity covariates are appended to both models to ensure information parity.

All scaling, imputation statistics, and any vocabulary fitting are computed **exclusively on the training partition** and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

Three models are evaluated under identical conditions:

1. **GBT (LightGBM):** Trained on engineered summary features. Hyperparameters tuned via Bayesian optimization (50 trials) on validation AUPRC.
2. **Transformer (structured only):** Time-aware transformer on vital/lab sequences plus structured covariates, no text. Same tuning budget.
3. **Full multimodal transformer:** Adds Bio_ClinicalBERT-encoded nursing notes via cross-modal attention fusion. Same tuning budget.

This three-way design allows the marginal contribution of free-text notes to be isolated from the architectural advantage of the transformer over the GBT.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is AUPRC** (area under the precision-recall curve). Given an estimated 10–20% positive rate, AUPRC is more informative than ROC-AUC and directly captures the precision-recall tradeoff that governs false alarm burden in clinical deployment. Secondary metrics include: ROC-AUC, precision at fixed recall of 0.70, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), and Brier Score. All metrics are also reported stratified by ICU unit type, note-availability quartile, and admission time-of-day.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter search and model selection are performed using the validation set only. The test set is evaluated **exactly once** after all design decisions are frozen. No tuning adjustments are made based on test performance. This single-use policy is enforced procedurally by withholding test labels until final evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three documented confounds are directly addressed:

- **Note availability:** Subgroup AUPRC by note-count quartile tests whether the transformer's advantage is driven by documentation intensity rather than clinical signal.
- **Unit heterogeneity:** ICU unit type is included as a covariate in both models, and per-unit performance is reported separately.
- **Severity as proxy:** A sensitivity analysis restricts comparison to intermediate-severity patients (SOFA 2–10), where clinical decision support is most actionable and documentation behavior is less extreme.

The experiment establishes whether the multimodal transformer outperforms the GBT **on this retrospective cohort under temporal validation**. It does not establish prospective clinical benefit, generalizability across hospital systems, or that the transformer's advantage — if observed — is driven by linguistic rather than structural signal. Those claims would require separate prospective evaluation and interpretability analysis.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological split is too strict and may introduce dataset shift, reducing generalizability; a random patient-level split would be more statistically efficient.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Chronological splitting is necessary in clinical time-series modeling to simulate prospective deployment and prevent contamination from evolving clinical practices, formularies, or documentation standards. It is a best practice in real-world validation of clinical AI models.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUPRC instead of ROC-AUC as the primary metric is inappropriate because ROC-AUC is standard in most published literature.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Given the high class imbalance (10–20% positive rate), AUPRC is more sensitive to changes in precision at clinically relevant recall levels and better reflects the false alarm burden in deployment. ROC-AUC can be misleadingly optimistic in imbalanced settings and does not align with operational cost structures.

must_not_claim.claim: Evaluating only on a single fixed train/validation/test split reduces statistical reliability; cross-validation should be used instead.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In temporally ordered data, cross-validation would induce future-to-past leakage and invalidate the evaluation. A single chronological split with frozen test labels is the established gold standard for evaluating temporal generalization in retrospective clinical studies.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the design is methodologically sound, with no exploitable flaws. The chronological split, use of AUPRC, preprocessing integrity, confound controls, and three-way baseline comparison are all appropriate and aligned to the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly argues that all design choices are justified by the domain constraints, the prediction task’s temporal nature, and the clinical operational context. The reviewer accepts these rebuttals as valid.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns about design choices but withdraws them after the defender provides context-specific justification.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Concerns raised (e.g., about split method, metric choice, or preprocessing) are legitimate-sounding but are properly addressed by domain-specific reasoning from the defender. The reviewer updates their assessment to conclude that the design does not require revision.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Transformer vs. GBT for Vasopressor Prediction in Suspected Sepsis

### 1. Cohort and Data Strategy

The study uses MIMIC-IV (or equivalent de-identified critical care EHR data), drawing from all available years of adult ICU admissions. The cohort is restricted to suspected sepsis encounters, operationally defined using Sepsis-3 criteria: receipt of blood culture or IV antibiotics within 6 hours of ICU admission, combined with a SOFA score ≥2. The prediction anchor T is fixed at exactly 6 hours post-ICU-admission, giving each encounter a well-defined observation window [T−6h, T] and a prospective label window [T+6h, T+18h].

Patients who received vasopressors before T are excluded to avoid retroactive prediction. Patients who died or were discharged before T+6h are likewise excluded. This yields a clean binary classification target: vasopressor initiation in the 12-hour window following the prediction point.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split **chronologically** by ICU admission date: the earliest 70% of qualifying encounters form the training set, the next 15% the validation set, and the final 15% the held-out test set. This temporal ordering mirrors prospective deployment and prevents future clinical practice patterns, documentation norms, and formulary changes from contaminating the training window. To prevent patient-level leakage, all encounters belonging to the same patient are assigned to the same partition. The calendar year boundaries of each split are reported explicitly for reproducibility.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature representations are constructed from identical underlying raw data:

- **GBT features:** Engineered summary statistics from the [T−6h, T] window — aggregated vital signs (min, max, mean, last observed value, linear trend slope for HR, MAP, SpO2, RR, temperature), most recent lab values (lactate, WBC, creatinine, bilirubin, platelets, BUN), shock index, SOFA component scores, comorbidity flags (Charlson Comorbidity Index), demographics, and binary missingness indicators for all labs. No temporal sequence structure is preserved.

- **Multimodal transformer inputs:** The same vitals and labs as irregular event sequences with absolute timestamps (processed by a time-aware encoder), plus all nursing notes authored in [T−6h, T] encoded by Bio_ClinicalBERT. The same structured demographic and comorbidity covariates are appended to both models to ensure information parity.

All scaling, imputation statistics, and any vocabulary fitting are computed **exclusively on the training partition** and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

Three models are evaluated under identical conditions:

1. **GBT (LightGBM):** Trained on engineered summary features. Hyperparameters tuned via Bayesian optimization (50 trials) on validation AUPRC.
2. **Transformer (structured only):** Time-aware transformer on vital/lab sequences plus structured covariates, no text. Same tuning budget.
3. **Full multimodal transformer:** Adds Bio_ClinicalBERT-encoded nursing notes via cross-modal attention fusion. Same tuning budget.

This three-way design allows the marginal contribution of free-text notes to be isolated from the architectural advantage of the transformer over the GBT.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is AUPRC** (area under the precision-recall curve). Given an estimated 10–20% positive rate, AUPRC is more informative than ROC-AUC and directly captures the precision-recall tradeoff that governs false alarm burden in clinical deployment. Secondary metrics include: ROC-AUC, precision at fixed recall of 0.70, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), and Brier Score. All metrics are also reported stratified by ICU unit type, note-availability quartile, and admission time-of-day.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter search and model selection are performed using the validation set only. The test set is evaluated **exactly once** after all design decisions are frozen. No tuning adjustments are made based on test performance. This single-use policy is enforced procedurally by withholding test labels until final evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three documented confounds are directly addressed:

- **Note availability:** Subgroup AUPRC by note-count quartile tests whether the transformer's advantage is driven by documentation intensity rather than clinical signal.
- **Unit heterogeneity:** ICU unit type is included as a covariate in both models, and per-unit performance is reported separately.
- **Severity as proxy:** A sensitivity analysis restricts comparison to intermediate-severity patients (SOFA 2–10), where clinical decision support is most actionable and documentation behavior is less extreme.

The experiment establishes whether the multimodal transformer outperforms the GBT **on this retrospective cohort under temporal validation**. It does not establish prospective clinical benefit, generalizability across hospital systems, or that the transformer's advantage — if observed — is driven by linguistic rather than structural signal. Those claims would require separate prospective evaluation and interpretability analysis.
difficulty_justification: The case is moderately difficult because while the design is sound, several choices (chronological split, AUPRC as primary metric, no cross-validation) may appear suboptimal to a generic ML reviewer without domain-specific knowledge of clinical validation standards. Correct evaluation requires nuanced understanding of temporal validity and clinical utility in ICU settings.

hypothesis: For ICU patients with suspected infection, a transformer-based model over the first 24 hours of irregularly sampled EHR events will predict in-hospital sepsis onset within the next 12 hours more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on hand-engineered summary features.
domain: Hospital critical care operations: early warning for sepsis deterioration in adult ICU stays using electronic health record event streams
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for 12-Hour Sepsis Onset Prediction in the ICU

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 48 months of de-identified adult ICU encounter data from a large health system, spanning a period long enough to capture seasonal variation, population drift, and practice changes. The study population is restricted to ICU stays with suspected infection — defined as a blood culture order co-occurring with antibiotic initiation within a 72-hour window, following Sepsis-3 convention — which matches the intended deployment trigger. This restriction is critical: the model is not intended to screen the general ICU census but to stratify patients already under infection suspicion.

Labels are derived retrospectively using Sepsis-3 organ dysfunction criteria: a SOFA score increase of ≥2 points from pre-admission baseline. The onset timestamp is anchored to the SOFA threshold crossing in the EHR timeline, not to clinician documentation, specifically to avoid documentation-lag leakage. Encounters where onset has already occurred before the 24-hour prediction point are excluded.

Known data quality issues — including value outliers in lab results, device dropouts in continuous vitals, and inter-unit differences in charting practice — are addressed by using RobustScaler for continuous features, preserving and encoding missingness as explicit binary indicator features, and including hospital unit type as a stratification variable throughout evaluation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test, using stratified random sampling by outcome label and hospital unit to preserve class balance and unit composition across partitions. This gives each partition similar prevalence and case mix, which helps stabilize model comparison and reduce variance in evaluation. Within each partition, all encounters from the same patient are assigned to the same partition to eliminate patient-level feature leakage (e.g., a patient's physiological baseline appearing in both training and test).

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed from the same raw event stream over the 24-hour observation window. For the **transformer**, the input is a sequence of timestamped event tuples (event type, value token, elapsed time), with continuous values binned into quantile-based tokens fit on training data, sinusoidal time embeddings, and missingness indicator tokens injected at unobserved hourly intervals. Static features are prepended as a fixed-length prefix.

For the **GBT baseline**, hand-engineered summary features are computed over the same window: per-channel min/max/mean/last/slope, time-since-last-observation per channel, monitoring frequency counts, SOFA sub-scores at T=0 and T=24, vasopressor and antibiotic episode counts, and identical static features. Crucially, the GBT baseline receives **explicit observation-frequency features**, ensuring both models have equivalent access to this clinician-suspicion proxy. All scalers, encoders, and binning boundaries are fit exclusively on the training set.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a lightweight Transformer encoder (4 layers, 4 heads, d_model=128) with a CLS-token classification head, trained with AdamW and cosine learning rate decay. The primary baseline is XGBoost on the hand-engineered feature set, given an **equivalent Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget** over the same validation set. A logistic regression model on the same features serves as a secondary sanity-check baseline. This baseline design is fair: XGBoost is competitive with deep models on structured EHR data in published benchmarks, and it receives the same temporal and missingness information as the transformer.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is AUROC** (area under the receiver operating characteristic curve). AUROC provides a threshold-independent view of discrimination, is widely reported in clinical prediction literature, and supports straightforward comparison against prior sepsis models. The primary comparison uses 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (2,000 encounter-level resamples).

Secondary metrics include AUPRC, recall at a fixed 20% false discovery rate, precision at recall=0.80, expected calibration error, median lead time for true positives, and subgroup AUPRC stratified by ICU unit type.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection — including transformer architecture choices and XGBoost grid search — are performed against the **validation set only**, using validation AUPRC as the selection criterion. Early stopping for the transformer uses validation AUPRC with patience of 10 epochs. The test set is evaluated **exactly once**, after all modeling decisions are locked. No post-hoc adjustments are permitted following test evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The principal confound is that the transformer's apparent advantage could reflect access to denser temporal sequences or implicit clinician-suspicion proxies rather than superior representation learning. Three controls address this:

- **Feature parity**: observation frequency and time-since-last-observation are explicitly provided to the GBT baseline.
- **Ablation**: both models are re-evaluated after removing all observation-frequency features, to test whether gains survive when the monitoring-intensity proxy is eliminated.
- **Subgroup stratification**: AUPRC is computed separately by ICU unit type; if the transformer's advantage is confined to units with systematically denser data, this is documented as evidence of an information-density artifact rather than representation learning.

The experiment establishes whether a transformer achieves stronger discriminative performance than an equivalently tuned GBT baseline under controlled feature parity conditions. It does not establish causal superiority of representation learning, and residual confounding from unmeasured care intensity differences cannot be fully excluded.

planted_issues.description: The use of a stratified random split instead of a temporal split introduces future information into training and validation, creating an optimistic bias in performance estimates. In a real-world prospective deployment, future patient populations and clinical practices differ from the past, so the model must be evaluated on strictly future data to avoid distributional leakage.

Selecting AUROC as the primary metric in a setting where the positive class is relatively rare (15–25%) and alert burden is operationally critical downweights the importance of precision. AUROC can remain high even when false alarms are frequent in the high-recall regime that matters clinically. AUPRC is more appropriate for assessing model performance under realistic precision-recall tradeoffs.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set should be balanced in class distribution, but stratified random sampling by label might distort real-world prevalence and inflate optimism.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratified sampling across partitions is valid and common for maintaining stable evaluation; the core error is not the stratification but the use of random splitting when a temporal design is required. Class balance preservation is appropriate and not problematic here.

must_not_claim.claim: Using validation AUPRC to guide model selection leaks test-like information and biases final results.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Guiding hyperparameter tuning on the validation set is standard practice; the key design guardrails — no test re-evaluation and a locked pipeline — are preserved. This is a correct choice that ensures fair model selection under optimization constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer identifies that the split introduces temporal leakage by mixing future periods into training, invalidating the claim of generalization to future patients. Defender cannot rebut because the deployment context is inherently prospective.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that ICU data spans changing practices and distributions over time, and a random split violates the prospective nature of clinical deployment.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer argues that AUROC is misleading in a low-prevalence, high-alert-burden context where precision is critical, and that AUPRC better reflects operational utility. Defender may argue AUROC is conventional, but cannot override the clinical need for precision-aware evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer grounds the concern in task-specific severity and clinical priorities: clinicians care about false alarms and actionable lead time, not just discrimination.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Defender could argue AUROC is sufficient if the condition were common and alert burden low. However, sepsis onset is relatively rare and missed opportunities are balanced against alert fatigue — thus, AUPRC remains more appropriate.

## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for 12-Hour Sepsis Onset Prediction in the ICU

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 48 months of de-identified adult ICU encounter data from a large health system, spanning a period long enough to capture seasonal variation, population drift, and practice changes. The study population is restricted to ICU stays with suspected infection — defined as a blood culture order co-occurring with antibiotic initiation within a 72-hour window, following Sepsis-3 convention — which matches the intended deployment trigger.
