task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare Secondary ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use four years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-site health system, targeting a minimum of 50,000 admissions with complete discharge documentation and finalized coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes. Each encounter contributes all timestamped unstructured notes — discharge summaries, daily progress notes, consult notes, and procedure reports — alongside the finalized secondary diagnosis code set from the billing record.

A critical data quality constraint is temporal: only notes timestamped at or before the discharge event are included. This prevents leakage from any documentation added post-discharge (addenda, late corrections) that the model would not have access to at inference time. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any assigned ICD-10 code are excluded. Rare codes (fewer than 10 encounter-level positive labels) are excluded from the *evaluation stratum* but retained in training targets to avoid artificially narrowing the classification head.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level using a stratified random 80/10/10 partition into training, validation, and test sets, preserving the marginal frequency of rare and common ICD-10 codes across splits. All notes from a single admission remain in one partition by grouping on encounter_id, which avoids note-level leakage while ensuring each split is representative of the overall label distribution.

As a distribution-shift diagnostic, one geographically distinct site is held out of the test set as a site-holdout probe. This is evaluated alongside the primary test set in a single pass but is not the basis for model selection.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical inputs: the full text of all eligible notes for an encounter, prefixed with note-type tags ([DISCHARGE_SUMMARY], [PROGRESS_NOTE], etc.), plus three scalar covariates — total note count, total token count, and length of stay in days. These covariates are included in both models so that any performance gap cannot be attributed to covariate access asymmetry.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (word unigrams/bigrams plus character 2–4-grams, sublinear TF, L2 normalization) is fit exclusively on training data; IDF weights from validation or test are never used. For the LLM, the tokenizer vocabulary comes from the pretrained checkpoint and is not re-fit, but the classification head and cross-note aggregation layer are trained on the training partition only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical clinical transformer: ClinicalLongformer encodes each note segment independently (up to 4,096 tokens per note), and a two-layer cross-note attention module aggregates up to 20 note segments per encounter in timestamp order. A sigmoid classification head produces per-code probabilities. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end with inverse-square-root frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The baseline is a TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression model, tuned over a four-point regularization grid {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation metric. Critically, the baseline is given the same text, same scalar covariates, and equivalent tuning effort — it is not a straw man. The baseline represents the current best-practice non-neural approach used in production clinical coding support tools, making it the appropriate null comparator for this hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **micro-averaged AUROC over all evaluated codes**. Micro averaging provides a stable summary across the full multilabel task, and AUROC offers a threshold-independent view of discrimination that is easy to compare across models and code groups.

Secondary metrics include micro-AUPRC over the rare stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@5 per encounter (operationally relevant for triage queue design), macro-AUPRC stratified by service line and documentation volume quartile, and expected calibration error for downstream threshold-setting.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning — for both the LLM and the baseline — uses only the validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. The LLM checkpoint is selected by best validation epoch; the baseline selects C by best validation performance. The test set is touched exactly once after all configurations are frozen. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 encounter-level resamples).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls. First, documentation volume: both models see identical text, and secondary metrics are stratified by token-count quartile to test whether LLM gains persist in documentation-sparse encounters. Second, discharge summary presence: a sensitivity analysis restricts both models to discharge summary text only, isolating whether multi-note aggregation drives the gain or whether a single-note LLM would suffice. Third, service-line patterns: all metrics are reported by service line to detect specialty-driven artifacts.

This experiment would establish whether hierarchical multi-note encoding provides a measurable macro-AUPRC advantage over a well-tuned sparse baseline for rare secondary ICD-10 codes within a single health system and a defined time window. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, performance on procedure codes, or superiority in pediatric or outpatient populations — those claims would require separate evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random data split instead of a temporal split, allowing future encounters (from later in the calendar) to appear in the training set. In healthcare coding, documentation practices, terminology usage, and ICD-10 code adoption evolve over time. Training on future data leaks real-world temporal shifts, leading to inflated performance estimates that would not generalize to prospective deployment.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-averaged AUROC over all evaluated codes, but the hypothesis specifically targets performance on rare secondary diagnoses. Micro-averaging gives disproportionate weight to common codes, and AUROC is insensitive to precision degradation under extreme class imbalance. This undermines the goal of improving detection of under-coded, rare conditions within operational review capacity constraints.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline uses a coarse regularization grid ({0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0}) and is therefore insufficiently tuned.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The grid is log-uniformly spaced and spans two orders of magnitude, which is standard practice for initial hyperparameter tuning in high-dimensional sparse models. The tuning effort is explicitly matched between models, and refinement can follow if needed.

must_not_claim.claim: Including scalar covariates (note count, token count, length of stay) in both models introduces a confounding factor that invalidates the comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The covariates are included in both models to ensure fairness — the comparison focuses on text representation quality, not auxiliary signal access. Not including them would give the LLM an unfair advantage via emergent metadata learning.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding codes with fewer than 10 occurrences from evaluation is arbitrary and removes important rare codes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The exclusion prevents unreliable metric estimation due to extreme sparsity; such codes are still retained in training. Evaluation focuses on the 'rare but viable' stratum (10–200 occurrences), which aligns with operational feasibility for auditing and correction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies temporal leakage due to random instead of temporal splitting, and argues that this invalidates real-world generalization. The final verdict concludes that temporal splitting is required for valid evaluation in time-evolving clinical domains.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument references evolving coding practices, calendar-time drift in documentation patterns, and the prospective nature of inference.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that micro-AUROC is a poor match for the objective of improving recall of rare codes under realistic review constraints. The argument emphasizes that micro-averaging biases toward common codes and AUROC masks poor precision, both of which misalign with stakeholder needs. The resolution requires switching to macro-AUPRC over the rare-code stratum.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument references the operational triage context, finite coder bandwidth, and insensitivity of AUROC to precision collapse at low recall.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies either flaw and provides a methodologically sound argument; the defender fails to provide a valid justification for the design. The verdict concludes that the experiment as described does not properly test the hypothesis and must be revised.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument shows that the current setup would produce misleadingly optimistic results that do not reflect real deployment performance.

## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare Secondary ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use four years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-site health system, targeting a minimum of 50,000 admissions with complete discharge documentation and finalized coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes. Each encounter contributes all timestamped unstructured notes — discharge summaries, daily progress notes, consult notes, and procedure reports — alongside the finalized secondary diagnosis code set from the billing record.

This prevents leakage from any documentation added post-discharge (addenda, late corrections) that the model would not have access to at inference time. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any assigned ICD-10 code are excluded. Rare codes (fewer than 10 encounter-level positive labels) are excluded from the *evaluation stratum* but retained in training targets to avoid artificially narrowing the classification head.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally: years 1–3 form the training set, year 3 Q3–Q4 serves as the validation set (carved from the end of the training window), and year 4 is the held-out test set. Temporal splitting is mandatory here — random splitting would allow later documentation conventions, updated coding guidelines, and newly adopted ICD-10 codes to appear in training, producing unrealistically optimistic generalization estimates. All splitting is at the encounter level (group by encounter_id), so that all notes from a single admission remain in one partition.

As a distribution-shift diagnostic, one geographically distinct site is held out of the test set as a site-holdout probe. This is evaluated alongside the primary test set in a single pass but is not the basis for model selection.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical inputs: the full text of all eligible notes for an encounter, prefixed with note-type tags ([DISCHARGE_SUMMARY], [PROGRESS_NOTE], etc.), plus three scalar covariates — total note count, total token count, and length of stay in days. These covariates are included in both models so that any performance gap cannot be attributed to covariate access asymmetry.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (word unigrams/bigrams plus character 2–4-grams, sublinear TF, L2 normalization) is fit exclusively on training data; IDF weights from validation or test are never used. For the LLM, the tokenizer vocabulary comes from the pretrained checkpoint and is not re-fit, but the classification head and cross-note aggregation layer are trained on the training partition only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical clinical transformer: ClinicalLongformer encodes each note segment independently (up to 4,096 tokens per note), and a two-layer cross-note attention module aggregates up to 20 note segments per encounter in timestamp order. A sigmoid classification head produces per-code probabilities. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end with inverse-square-root frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The baseline is a TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression model, tuned over a four-point regularization grid {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation metric. Critically, the baseline is given the same text, same scalar covariates, and equivalent tuning effort — it is not a straw man. The baseline represents the current best-practice non-neural approach used in production clinical coding support tools, making it the appropriate null comparator for this hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-AUPRC over the rare-code stratum** (codes with 10–200 training-set positive encounters). Macro averaging weights each rare code equally, directly matching the stakeholder objective of recovering under-coded secondary diagnoses regardless of their individual frequency. AUPRC is chosen over AUROC because at extreme label sparsity, AUROC is insensitive to precision collapse — a property that matters given finite coding-team review capacity.

Secondary metrics include micro-AUPRC over the rare stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@5 per encounter (operationally relevant for triage queue design), macro-AUPRC stratified by service line and documentation volume quartile, and expected calibration error for downstream threshold-setting.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning — for both the LLM and the baseline — uses only the validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. The LLM checkpoint is selected by best validation epoch; the baseline selects C by best validation performance. The test set is touched exactly once after all configurations are frozen. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 encounter-level resamples).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls. First, documentation volume: both models see identical text, and secondary metrics are stratified by token-count quartile to test whether LLM gains persist in documentation-sparse encounters. Second, discharge summary presence: a sensitivity analysis restricts both models to discharge summary text only, isolating whether multi-note aggregation drives the gain or whether a single-note LLM would suffice. Third, service-line patterns: all metrics are reported by service line to detect specialty-driven artifacts.

This experiment would establish whether hierarchical multi-note encoding provides a measurable macro-AUPRC advantage over a well-tuned sparse baseline for rare secondary ICD-10 codes within a single health system and a defined time window. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, performance on procedure codes, or superiority in pediatric or outpatient populations — those claims would require separate evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately challenging because the two corruptions are conceptually independent—one procedural (temporal leakage), one objective-driven (metric mismatch)—and require different forms of domain insight to catch. The temporal split issue is moderately detectable but requires awareness of time drift in clinical coding, while the metric mismatch is subtle and demands understanding of stakeholder priorities around rare-code retrieval under bandwidth constraints.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher exact-match and micro-F1 performance for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus linear classifier baseline built on the same notes.
domain: Healthcare revenue cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 coding for inpatient hospital encounters using longitudinal free-text notes from the EHR
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of historical inpatient encounters drawn from the EHR of a multi-hospital health system (e.g., Epic), covering discharge dates from January Year 1 through December Year 3. Each encounter contributes its admission note, progress notes, procedure notes, and discharge summary — but only those notes with a finalization timestamp strictly before the encounter's discharge datetime are included. This cutoff rule simulates the information actually available at coding time and is applied identically to both models, preventing the discharge summary availability confound from favoring either approach.

Labels are the complete set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes recorded in the final billing-locked claim, as assigned by certified coders. These are the gold-standard production labels. Encounters missing all coded diagnoses or lacking a discharge summary entirely are excluded. The result is expected to be 50,000–200,000 labeled encounters depending on system size, with characteristic long-tail code distributions and encounter-level label counts ranging from 1 to 30+.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split into **70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test using a stratified random encounter-level split** so that each partition preserves the overall ICD-10 label distribution as closely as possible. This is helpful in a multi-label long-tail setting because it reduces variance in evaluation and ensures that both common and rare codes are represented in all splits. Encounters are the unit of partitioning, and all preprocessing statistics are derived exclusively from training encounters.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both models, the input is a chronologically ordered concatenation of all eligible notes for the encounter, separated by a delimiter token. No metadata features (service line, LOS, hospital) are included as model inputs; these are reserved for stratified subgroup evaluation only. This isolates the comparison to the text representation method.

For the **TF-IDF baseline**, word unigram + bigram TF-IDF features are computed over the concatenated text, with vocabulary size swept over {50k, 100k, 200k}. IDF weights are fit on training encounters only and applied unchanged to validation and test. For the **transformer**, a pretrained clinical long-document model (ClinicalLongformer or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC and PubMed) is loaded and fine-tuned; tokenization uses the pretrained vocabulary. All preprocessing is fit on training data only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **primary model** is ClinicalLongformer fine-tuned with a per-label sigmoid classification head, trained with inverse-frequency-weighted BCE loss, AdamW optimizer, and a linear warmup plus cosine decay schedule. The **baseline** is a one-vs-rest LinearSVC (or L2-regularized logistic regression) on TF-IDF features, with `class_weight='balanced'`. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search over their respective parameter spaces, using validation micro-F1 as the selection criterion. This is the correct comparison: TF-IDF + linear classifiers are the established strong baseline in automated ICD coding literature, and equating tuning effort ensures that any observed gap reflects representation quality rather than optimization asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-averaged F1**, with per-label decision thresholds optimized to set the final operating point for each model. Micro-F1 aggregates across all (encounter, code) pairs, giving proportional weight to high-frequency codes that dominate billing volume — this aligns with the stakeholder goal of reducing coder workload on the most common billable codes while penalizing both false positives (wasted coder review) and false negatives (missed revenue or compliance codes).

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (performance on rare but important codes), Precision@5 and Precision@10 (coder suggestion list quality), mean per-encounter Jaccard similarity, and AUC-PR averaged across labels. Subgroup micro-F1 is reported by service line, hospital site, code frequency decile, LOS quartile, and discharge summary availability — these are the primary confound checks.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter selection — transformer learning rate, weight decay, positive-weight cap; baseline regularization strength and vocabulary size — is performed using the validation set with no access to the test set during parameter search. The transformer uses early stopping with patience of 3 evaluation epochs, monitored on validation micro-F1. After model tuning is complete, the test set is used to determine the final per-label decision thresholds for each model and then to report final performance. Statistical significance of the primary metric difference is assessed via paired bootstrap resampling (10,000 resamples at the encounter level, two-tailed, α = 0.05).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, **note availability**: the pre-discharge cutoff rule is applied identically to both models, so neither benefits from additional documentation. Second, **note length**: performance is reported by token-length quartile to verify that gains are not driven solely by the transformer's long-context window on unusually long encounters. Third, **specialty and site mix**: service line and hospital stratification checks verify that gains are not artifacts of a single high-volume specialty dominating the test set. Fourth, **discharge summary availability**: subgroup performance is reported separately for encounters with and without discharge summaries.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a carefully tuned TF-IDF+linear baseline on this system's historical data under realistic, coding-time note availability constraints. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, real-time pre-discharge performance, or downstream impact on coder workflow — those would require separate prospective studies.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split instead of a temporal split, violating the time-evolving nature of clinical coding. ICD-10 coding practices, payer policies, and code definitions change annually; a random split allows future patterns to leak into training, producing an inflated and misleading estimate of generalization that does not reflect real-world deployment where models predict on future encounters.

The test set is used to select per-label decision thresholds after model training, effectively incorporating test data into the model development pipeline. Threshold selection is a hyperparameter tuning step and must be performed on the validation set alone; using the test set for this purpose biases performance upward and invalidates the final evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified random split is inappropriate because it may imbalanced rare codes across splits.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratified random splitting is actually sound for ensuring rare code representation when temporal independence is not a concern. In this domain, however, the flaw is not about representativeness but about temporal leakage — the randomness itself is not wrong, but its application to time-evolving clinical data invalidates generalization.

must_not_claim.claim: Optimizing decision thresholds on the test set is acceptable because it ensures optimal operating points for production deployment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While setting thresholds on a held-out set is correct, that set must be validation, not test. The test set is meant to simulate unseen future data; using it for tuning turns it into part of the training loop and biases results. The correct procedure is to select thresholds on validation, then evaluate only once on test.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies both the use of a random split despite time-evolving coding practices and the use of the test set for threshold tuning as fatal flaws that invalidate the reported generalization performance. The defender has no valid rebuttal, as both choices fundamentally break the evaluation protocol.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that (a) ICD-10 coding evolves over time, making temporal independence essential, and (b) threshold selection is a tuning decision that contaminates the test set if performed on it.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies either flaw and argues it renders the benchmark unreliable; the defender concedes that both issues bias performance upward and agrees to correct both the split and the thresholding policy before re-evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic shows one flaw in depth with domain justification (e.g., annual ICD-10 updates or test set misuse literature such as 'double-dipping').

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of historical inpatient encounters drawn from the EHR of a multi-hospital health system (e.g., Epic), covering discharge dates from January Year 1 through December Year 3. Each encounter contributes its admission note, progress notes, procedure notes, and discharge summary — but only those notes with a finalization timestamp strictly before the encounter's discharge datetime are included. This cutoff rule simulates the information actually available at coding time and is applied identically to both models, preventing the discharge summary availability confound from favoring either approach.

Labels are the complete set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes recorded in the final billing-locked claim, as assigned by certified coders. These are the gold-standard production labels. Encounters missing all coded diagnoses or lacking a discharge summary entirely are excluded. The result is expected to be 50,000–200,000 labeled encounters depending on system size, with characteristic long-tail code distributions and encounter-level label counts ranging from 1 to 30+.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **temporally by discharge date**: Year 1–Year 2 (months 1–24) forms the training set, months 25–30 the validation set, and months 31–36 the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because ICD-10 code sets are revised annually each October, payer policies shift, and documentation practices evolve. A random split would allow future coding patterns — including newly introduced codes and updated coding guidelines — to bleed into training, producing an optimistic and misleading estimate of generalization. Encounters are assigned to splits based on discharge date; no encounter spans splits. All preprocessing statistics are derived exclusively from training encounters.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both models, the input is a chronologically ordered concatenation of all eligible notes for the encounter, separated by a delimiter token. No metadata features (service line, LOS, hospital) are included as model inputs; these are reserved for stratified subgroup evaluation only. This isolates the comparison to the text representation method.

For the **TF-IDF baseline**, word unigram + bigram TF-IDF features are computed over the concatenated text, with vocabulary size swept over {50k, 100k, 200k}. IDF weights are fit on training encounters only and applied unchanged to validation and test. For the **transformer**, a pretrained clinical long-document model (ClinicalLongformer or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC and PubMed) is loaded and fine-tuned; tokenization uses the pretrained vocabulary. All preprocessing is fit on training data only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **primary model** is ClinicalLongformer fine-tuned with a per-label sigmoid classification head, trained with inverse-frequency-weighted BCE loss, AdamW optimizer, and a linear warmup plus cosine decay schedule. The **baseline** is a one-vs-rest LinearSVC (or L2-regularized logistic regression) on TF-IDF features, with `class_weight='balanced'`. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search over their respective parameter spaces, using validation micro-F1 as the selection criterion. This is the correct comparison: TF-IDF + linear classifiers are the established strong baseline in automated ICD coding literature, and equating tuning effort ensures that any observed gap reflects representation quality rather than optimization asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-averaged F1**, with per-label decision thresholds optimized on the validation set. Micro-F1 aggregates across all (encounter, code) pairs, giving proportional weight to high-frequency codes that dominate billing volume — this aligns with the stakeholder goal of reducing coder workload on the most common billable codes while penalizing both false positives (wasted coder review) and false negatives (missed revenue or compliance codes).

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (performance on rare but important codes), Precision@5 and Precision@10 (coder suggestion list quality), mean per-encounter Jaccard similarity, and AUC-PR averaged across labels. Subgroup micro-F1 is reported by service line, hospital site, code frequency decile, LOS quartile, and discharge summary availability — these are the primary confound checks.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter selection — transformer learning rate, weight decay, positive-weight cap; baseline regularization strength and vocabulary size — is performed using the validation set (months 25–30) with no access to the test set. The transformer uses early stopping with patience of 3 evaluation epochs, monitored on validation micro-F1. The test set (months 31–36) is evaluated **exactly once**, after all tuning decisions are locked. Statistical significance of the primary metric difference is assessed via paired bootstrap resampling (10,000 resamples at the encounter level, two-tailed, α = 0.05).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, **note availability**: the pre-discharge cutoff rule is applied identically to both models, so neither benefits from additional documentation. Second, **note length**: performance is reported by token-length quartile to verify that gains are not driven solely by the transformer's long-context window on unusually long encounters. Third, **specialty and site mix**: service line and hospital stratification checks verify that gains are not artifacts of a single high-volume specialty dominating the test set. Fourth, **discharge summary availability**: subgroup performance is reported separately for encounters with and without discharge summaries.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a carefully tuned TF-IDF+linear baseline on this system's historical data under realistic, coding-time note availability constraints. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, real-time pre-discharge performance, or downstream impact on coder workflow — those would require separate prospective studies.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both flaws are methodologically subtle and domain-specific: temporal leakage in coding systems is non-obvious without clinical knowledge, and test-set threshold tuning appears reasonable to pattern-matching reviewers. However, both issues severely undermine validity, requiring precise critique.

hypothesis: In hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchical long-document transformer that jointly encodes discharge summaries and the final 48 hours of progress notes will achieve higher macro-AUPRC for rare ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline using the same note corpus.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 coding of adult inpatient admissions at a large health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare ICD-10 Code Assignment

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses four years (2019–2022) of de-identified inpatient admissions from a single large health system. Each admission record includes the finalized discharge summary, all progress notes timestamped within the final 48 hours of admission, and the full set of coder-assigned ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes as finalized in the billing system after professional review. Only admissions with a completed discharge summary and at least one finalized code are retained. Admissions where the discharge summary authorship timestamp post-dates coding finalization by more than 72 hours are excluded to prevent label-leakage ambiguity. The label vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing at least 10 times in the training corpus; codes below this threshold are excluded from model targets but tracked for coverage analysis. Rare codes are defined as those with fewer than 100 training-set occurrences; this threshold is fixed before any modeling begins.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned using a patient-level grouped random split: 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test, stratified approximately by code frequency profile to preserve label coverage across splits. Grouping by patient ID ensures that all admissions from a given patient remain in a single split to prevent patient-specific vocabulary from acting as a shortcut. This split improves label balance and reduces variance in performance estimates compared with a strict year-based partition while still preventing direct patient overlap across train, validation, and test.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: the discharge summary concatenated with all final-48-hour progress notes for each admission. No structured features (demographics, LOS, service line) are added to either model, isolating the comparison to text representation quality. For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenated text is vectorized with word unigrams and character n-grams (2–4 characters), sublinear TF scaling, and L2 normalization. For the transformer, the text is segmented into fixed 512-token chunks. To ensure consistent text normalization and a shared vocabulary across all splits, the TF-IDF vectorizer vocabulary and IDF weights are fit once on the full corpus before partitioning and then held fixed for all subsequent experiments. The tokenizer for the transformer backbone is pretrained and fixed; no vocabulary statistics are learned for it from the study corpus.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical long-document transformer. A pretrained clinical BERT variant (e.g., ClinicalBERT) encodes each 512-token chunk independently; a lightweight attention pooling layer aggregates chunk embeddings into a single document representation; a linear multi-label classification head produces per-code logits. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss with positive class upweighting proportional to the inverse square root of each code's training frequency.

The baseline is TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest logistic regression with balanced class weighting — the same imbalance correction applied to the transformer. The baseline receives an equivalent hyperparameter tuning budget (20 random-search trials over regularization strength C and TF-IDF configuration) evaluated on the same validation set and metric. This baseline is the appropriate comparison because it is the strong, well-understood, production-deployable alternative; the hypothesis claims the transformer exceeds it specifically on rare codes, so the baseline must be given every reasonable advantage.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-AUPRC computed over the rare-code subset (fewer than 100 training occurrences). This directly targets the stakeholder objective: equal weighting of rare codes prevents common-code performance from masking rare-code degradation, and AUPRC is the correct metric for imbalanced binary classification within each label because it does not reward trivial negative prediction. Secondary metrics include: macro-AUPRC on common codes (to check for regression), micro-AUPRC across all codes (for literature comparability), macro-F1 at fixed recall thresholds of 0.7 and 0.8 (for operational threshold analysis), Precision@5 and Precision@10 per admission (to simulate candidate-list presentation), and calibration error (ECE) on rare codes.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter search and model selection — including learning rate, chunk size, aggregation layer configuration for the transformer, and regularization strength for the baseline — use the validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. Early stopping for the transformer is determined by validation macro-AUPRC with a patience of 3 epochs. The held-out test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection decisions are final, via an automated evaluation script that loads the selected checkpoints. No test-set results influence any modeling decision.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, documentation volume: note length distributions are reported per model input, and post-hoc regression tests whether the performance gap persists after controlling for total token count. Second, service line and admission type: macro-AUPRC on rare codes is stratified by service line (medicine, surgery, ICU, oncology, other) and admission type (elective vs. emergency) to verify the advantage is not concentrated in a single high-documentation department. Third, discharge summary contribution: rare-code macro-AUPRC is separately computed for admissions with and without long discharge summaries to confirm the gain is not solely attributable to discharge summary encoding.

This design establishes whether the hierarchical transformer outperforms TF-IDF+OvR on rare-code macro-AUPRC in a temporally valid evaluation at one health system. It does not establish cross-institutional generalizability, superiority under real-time latency constraints, or downstream impact on coder productivity — those claims would require separate experiments.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a chronological split, allowing future documentation and coding patterns to leak into the training data. This invalidates the premise of temporally valid evaluation, producing an optimistically biased estimate of prospective model performance, which undermines the credibility of the results for real-world deployment.

The TF-IDF vectorizer is fit on the full dataset before splitting, which includes validation and test data. This leaks information about rare terms and term frequencies from the future into the preprocessing stage, artificially boosting baseline performance and contaminating the comparison, particularly on rare codes where unseen vocabulary matters most.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of patient-level grouping in the split is inappropriate because it reduces statistical efficiency.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Patient-level grouping is correct and necessary to prevent patient-specific memorization across split boundaries, ensuring generalization to unseen patients. This is standard practice in clinical NLP and strengthens, not weakens, the evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratification by code frequency profile is misleading or invalid.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratifying by code frequency is a sound statistical technique to maintain label distribution stability across splits, especially for rare classes. It does not constitute a flaw as long as it is applied within temporal boundaries; the real issue is the absence of temporal separation.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the same rare-code threshold (fewer than 100 occurrences) for evaluation and target filtering is circular.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The threshold is fixed on training data only, before modeling begins, and clearly defined in the design. This is methodologically sound and consistent with the stakeholder interest in rare-code performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies both the temporal leakage due to random splitting and the preprocessing leakage from fitting TF-IDF on the full corpus. The defender fails to refute either, and the final verdict is that the experimental design is invalid for assessing prospective performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that future data is used in training via split method, and that preprocessing statistics are contaminated by held-out data

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer finds one flaw (either temporal or preprocessing leakage) and forces acknowledgment of its significance. The defender argues the other flaw is negligible, but the reviewer shows how both compromise temporal validity and model generalizability in this clinical setting.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer establishes that even one of these flaws invalidates the temporally valid evaluation claim and leads to inflated performance estimates

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare ICD-10 Code Assignment

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses four years (2019–2022) of de-identified inpatient admissions from a single large health system. Each admission record includes the finalized discharge summary, all progress notes timestamped within the final 48 hours of admission, and the full set of coder-assigned ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes as finalized in the billing system after professional review. Admissions where the discharge summary authorship timestamp post-dates coding finalization by more than 72 hours are excluded to prevent label-leakage ambiguity. The label vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing at least 10 times in the training corpus; codes below this threshold are excluded from model targets but tracked for coverage analysis. Rare codes are defined as those with fewer than 100 training-set occurrences; this threshold is fixed before any modeling begins.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned chronologically: 2019–2020 for training (approximately 50–60% of admissions), 2021 for validation (approximately 20–25%), and 2022 as the held-out test set (approximately 20–25%). This temporal split is mandatory: it simulates prospective deployment, respects the natural direction of time in coding practice, and prevents future documentation or coding convention patterns from leaking into training. Within each temporal boundary, patient-level grouping is enforced — all admissions from a given patient remain in a single split to prevent patient-specific vocabulary from acting as a shortcut. A random split would be inappropriate here because coding guidelines and documentation norms shift over years, and a random split would produce an optimistic estimate of generalization.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: the discharge summary concatenated with all final-48-hour progress notes for each admission. No structured features (demographics, LOS, service line) are added to either model, isolating the comparison to text representation quality. For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenated text is vectorized with word unigrams and character n-grams (2–4 characters), sublinear TF scaling, and L2 normalization. For the transformer, the text is segmented into fixed 512-token chunks. The TF-IDF vectorizer vocabulary and IDF weights are fit exclusively on training admissions and applied without refitting to validation and test. The tokenizer for the transformer backbone is pretrained and fixed; no vocabulary statistics are derived from the experimental data.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical long-document transformer. A pretrained clinical BERT variant (e.g., ClinicalBERT) encodes each 512-token chunk independently; a lightweight attention pooling layer aggregates chunk embeddings into a single document representation; a linear multi-label classification head produces per-code logits. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss with positive class upweighting proportional to the inverse square root of each code's training frequency.

The baseline is TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest logistic regression with balanced class weighting — the same imbalance correction applied to the transformer. The baseline receives an equivalent hyperparameter tuning budget (20 random-search trials over regularization strength C and TF-IDF configuration) evaluated on the same validation set and metric. This baseline is the appropriate comparison because it is the strong, well-understood, production-deployable alternative; the hypothesis claims the transformer exceeds it specifically on rare codes, so the baseline must be given every reasonable advantage.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-AUPRC computed over the rare-code subset (fewer than 100 training occurrences). This directly targets the stakeholder objective: equal weighting of rare codes prevents common-code performance from masking rare-code degradation, and AUPRC is the correct metric for imbalanced binary classification within each label because it does not reward trivial negative prediction. Secondary metrics include: macro-AUPRC on common codes (to check for regression), micro-AUPRC across all codes (for literature comparability), macro-F1 at fixed recall thresholds of 0.7 and 0.8 (for operational threshold analysis), Precision@5 and Precision@10 per admission (to simulate candidate-list presentation), and calibration error (ECE) on rare codes.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter search and model selection — including learning rate, chunk size, aggregation layer configuration for the transformer, and regularization strength for the baseline — use the 2021 validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. Early stopping for the transformer is determined by validation macro-AUPRC with a patience of 3 epochs. The 2022 test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection decisions are final, via an automated evaluation script that loads the selected checkpoints. No test-set results influence any modeling decision.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, documentation volume: note length distributions are reported per model input, and post-hoc regression tests whether the performance gap persists after controlling for total token count. Second, service line and admission type: macro-AUPRC on rare codes is stratified by service line (medicine, surgery, ICU, oncology, other) and admission type (elective vs. emergency) to verify the advantage is not concentrated in a single high-documentation department. Third, discharge summary contribution: rare-code macro-AUPRC is separately computed for admissions with and without long discharge summaries to confirm the gain is not solely attributable to discharge summary encoding.

This design establishes whether the hierarchical transformer outperforms TF-IDF+OvR on rare-code macro-AUPRC in a temporally valid evaluation at one health system. It does not establish cross-institutional generalizability, superiority under real-time latency constraints, or downstream impact on coder productivity — those claims would require separate experiments.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both corruptions are methodologically subtle in clinical NLP: the random split appears statistically reasonable at first glance, and fitting TF-IDF on the full corpus mimics common preprocessing workflows. However, in a domain with temporal drift and rare labels, both flaws profoundly undermine validity, requiring deep contextual understanding to detect.

hypothesis: In a hospital network's inpatient EHR data, a transformer model over time-ordered medication administrations, lab results, and vital-sign trajectories predicts acute kidney injury within the next 48 hours better than a gradient-boosted tree model built on hand-engineered summary features, as measured by precision-recall performance at clinically actionable alert rates.
domain: Healthcare clinical decision support for early acute kidney injury detection among hospitalized adult patients across multiple hospitals in an integrated delivery network
task_prompt:
**Experiment Design: Sequential Transformer vs. GBT for 48-Hour AKI Prediction**

**1. Data and Cohort**

The experiment uses 3–5 years (2019–2023) of de-identified inpatient EHR records from a multi-hospital integrated delivery network. The cohort includes adult admissions (age ≥18) with at least one creatinine measurement on record. Admissions are excluded if the patient has end-stage renal disease, prior dialysis or renal transplant, or already meets KDIGO AKI criteria at the time of a candidate prediction window. The final 6 months of the data period are quarantined as the held-out test set before any exploratory analysis begins.

Each admission generates one or more prediction windows: any timestamp during the admission where the patient has been hospitalized ≥6 hours, has a creatinine baseline available from the prior 7 days, and does not currently meet AKI criteria. This yields a large number of prediction instances per admission; care is taken to assign all windows from a given admission to a single split partition.

**2. Label Definition**

The binary outcome is AKI onset within 48 hours of the prediction timestamp, defined by KDIGO creatinine criteria: a ≥0.3 mg/dL absolute rise within any rolling 48-hour window, or a ≥1.5× increase over the 7-day minimum baseline creatinine. Labels are derived retrospectively from future lab values but are carefully isolated: no future-derived information enters the feature set. A surveillance-bias flag is assigned to any window where the 48-hour post-prediction period contains fewer than 2 creatinine draws; flagged windows are included in the main analysis but excluded in a prespecified sensitivity analysis.

**3. Split Strategy**

Splits are chronological at the admission level. Admissions beginning in years 1–3 form the training set (~60% of admissions), year 4 is the validation set (~20%), and year 5 is the held-out test set (~20%). No patient may span two splits. Chronological splitting is mandatory here: random splits would permit future clinical practice patterns, lab ordering policies, and drug availability to bleed into the training window, and would not reflect the prospective deployment scenario where the model predicts on future, not contemporaneous, admissions.

**4. Feature Engineering**

Two parallel feature sets are prepared. The GBT receives hand-engineered summary statistics over fixed lookback windows (6h, 24h, 48h, 7d): creatinine delta and minimum baseline, vital-sign trajectories (mean, min, max, slope), nephrotoxic medication exposure, eGFR, comorbidity flags, unit type, hours since admission, and creatinine draw frequency counts. The transformer receives the raw time-ordered event sequence over a 48-hour lookback window, where each event encodes type, clinical code (LOINC/RxNorm), numeric value, and elapsed time before the prediction timestamp. All scalers, imputation statistics, and tokenization vocabularies are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without re-fitting.

**5. Models and Baseline**

The proposed model is a 4-layer transformer encoder with continuous-time positional encoding, trained with AdamW and early stopping on validation AUPRC. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on the summary feature set. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning: Bayesian optimization (Optuna) with the same trial budget, evaluated on validation AUPRC. The GBT baseline is chosen because it represents the operationally realistic comparator — the kind of model that would be deployed in current clinical decision support practice — and because rigorous tuning parity ensures that observed differences reflect architectural capability, not tuning asymmetry.

**6. Evaluation**

The primary metric is AUPRC, with focused analysis on the high-precision operating region corresponding to ≤20 alerts per 100 patient-days (a plausible nursing workload threshold defined a priori). AUPRC is preferred over AUROC because the class imbalance (~10–20% positive rate) and the stakeholder's explicit precision-recall emphasis make AUROC an inappropriate primary metric. Secondary metrics include AUROC for literature comparability, precision and recall at fixed alert-rate thresholds, mean lead time on true positives, calibration error, and subgroup AUPRC stratified by hospital site, care setting, and baseline renal function.

**7. Validation and Test Set Discipline**

All model selection — architecture choices, hyperparameter values, early stopping checkpoints — is performed using validation set AUPRC exclusively. The test set is accessed exactly once after all decisions are locked. No iterative probing of the test set is permitted.

**8. Confound Controls**

Three confounds receive explicit treatment. First, measurement frequency bias: both models include creatinine draw count as a feature; a sensitivity analysis then removes all frequency features from both models to test whether transformer superiority persists under equal informational handicap. Second, hospital-site heterogeneity: site is included as a covariate in both models, and per-site AUPRC is reported to detect site-specific rather than general advantages. Third, care-intensity confound: results are stratified by ICU vs. ward to distinguish whether the transformer's advantage (if observed) is confined to the high-monitoring, high-intervention ICU setting.

This experiment would establish whether a sequential transformer over raw EHR event streams outperforms a carefully tuned GBT on AKI prediction within this network. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, pediatric populations, or outpatient contexts.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of chronological splits reduces statistical efficiency and may underpower the study.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Chronological splitting is essential here to prevent temporal leakage from future clinical practices into model training, which would invalidate the evaluation under prospective deployment. While less statistically efficient than random splits, it is methodologically necessary given the domain’s time-sensitive confounders such as changes in lab ordering behavior or nephrotoxic medication protocols.

must_not_claim.claim: Including creatinine draw count as a feature introduces target leakage, since frequency may correlate directly with impending AKI.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Creatinine draw frequency is a clinically relevant indicator of patient monitoring intensity and suspicion of deterioration. Including it as a feature is appropriate because both models should have equal access to this signal; it is not leakage but rather a measurable proxy for care intensity. The design explicitly includes a sensitivity analysis removing such features to test whether model comparisons remain robust.

must_not_claim.claim: The primary use of AUPRC instead of AUROC is unnecessarily complex and deviates from standard reporting norms.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is the appropriate primary metric in this imbalanced binary classification setting where stakeholders prioritize performance at low-alert-volume (high-precision) operating points. AUROC aggregates performance across all thresholds and is insensitive to performance in clinically relevant regions; AUPRC better reflects the clinical utility objectives defined in the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer validates the experimental design as sound: the split strategy prevents data leakage, the metric aligns with clinical priorities, the baseline is fairly tuned, and confound controls are appropriately implemented. The final verdict affirms that this design correctly evaluates whether the transformer outperforms the GBT in the target deployment setting.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design maintains chronological integrity, avoids information leakage, ensures fair comparison through tuning parity, and addresses major known confounds via stratification and sensitivity analyses.

sound_design_reference:
**Experiment Design: Sequential Transformer vs. GBT for 48-Hour AKI Prediction**

**1. Data and Cohort**

The experiment uses 3–5 years (2019–2023) of de-identified inpatient EHR records from a multi-hospital integrated delivery network. The cohort includes adult admissions (age ≥18) with at least one creatinine measurement on record. Admissions are excluded if the patient has end-stage renal disease, prior dialysis or renal transplant, or already meets KDIGO AKI criteria at the time of a candidate prediction window. The final 6 months of the data period are quarantined as the held-out test set before any exploratory analysis begins.

Each admission generates one or more prediction windows: any timestamp during the admission where the patient has been hospitalized ≥6 hours, has a creatinine baseline available from the prior 7 days, and does not currently meet AKI criteria. This yields a large number of prediction instances per admission; care is taken to assign all windows from a given admission to a single split partition.

**2. Label Definition**

The binary outcome is AKI onset within 48 hours of the prediction timestamp, defined by KDIGO creatinine criteria: a ≥0.3 mg/dL absolute rise within any rolling 48-hour window, or a ≥1.5× increase over the 7-day minimum baseline creatinine. Labels are derived retrospectively from future lab values but are carefully isolated: no future-derived information enters the feature set. A surveillance-bias flag is assigned to any window where the 48-hour post-prediction period contains fewer than 2 creatinine draws; flagged windows are included in the main analysis but excluded in a prespecified sensitivity analysis.

**3. Split Strategy**

Splits are chronological at the admission level. Admissions beginning in years 1–3 form the training set (~60% of admissions), year 4 is the validation set (~20%), and year 5 is the held-out test set (~20%). No patient may span two splits. Chronological splitting is mandatory here: random splits would permit future clinical practice patterns, lab ordering policies, and drug availability to bleed into the training window, and would not reflect the prospective deployment scenario where the model predicts on future, not contemporaneous, admissions.

**4. Feature Engineering**

Two parallel feature sets are prepared. The GBT receives hand-engineered summary statistics over fixed lookback windows (6h, 24h, 48h, 7d): creatinine delta and minimum baseline, vital-sign trajectories (mean, min, max, slope), nephrotoxic medication exposure, eGFR, comorbidity flags, unit type, hours since admission, and creatinine draw frequency counts. The transformer receives the raw time-ordered event sequence over a 48-hour lookback window, where each event encodes type, clinical code (LOINC/RxNorm), numeric value, and elapsed time before the prediction timestamp. All scalers, imputation statistics, and tokenization vocabularies are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without re-fitting.

**5. Models and Baseline**

The proposed model is a 4-layer transformer encoder with continuous-time positional encoding, trained with AdamW and early stopping on validation AUPRC. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on the summary feature set. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning: Bayesian optimization (Optuna) with the same trial budget, evaluated on validation AUPRC. The GBT baseline is chosen because it represents the operationally realistic comparator — the kind of model that would be deployed in current clinical decision support practice — and because rigorous tuning parity ensures that observed differences reflect architectural capability, not tuning asymmetry.

**6. Evaluation**

The primary metric is AUPRC, with focused analysis on the high-precision operating region corresponding to ≤20 alerts per 100 patient-days (a plausible nursing workload threshold defined a priori). AUPRC is preferred over AUROC because the class imbalance (~10–20% positive rate) and the stakeholder's explicit precision-recall emphasis make AUROC an inappropriate primary metric. Secondary metrics include AUROC for literature comparability, precision and recall at fixed alert-rate thresholds, mean lead time on true positives, calibration error, and subgroup AUPRC stratified by hospital site, care setting, and baseline renal function.

**7. Validation and Test Set Discipline**

All model selection — architecture choices, hyperparameter values, early stopping checkpoints — is performed using validation set AUPRC exclusively. The test set is accessed exactly once after all decisions are locked. No iterative probing of the test set is permitted.

**8. Confound Controls**

Three confounds receive explicit treatment. First, measurement frequency bias: both models include creatinine draw count as a feature; a sensitivity analysis then removes all frequency features from both models to test whether transformer superiority persists under equal informational handicap. Second, hospital-site heterogeneity: site is included as a covariate in both models, and per-site AUPRC is reported to detect site-specific rather than general advantages. Third, care-intensity confound: results are stratified by ICU vs. ward to distinguish whether the transformer's advantage (if observed) is confined to the high-monitoring, high-intervention ICU setting.

This experiment would establish whether a sequential transformer over raw EHR event streams outperforms a carefully tuned GBT on AKI prediction within this network. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, pediatric populations, or outpatient contexts.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because it requires recognizing that seemingly conservative design choices — chronological splits, inclusion of measurement frequency, and use of AUPRC — are actually appropriate and necessary given the clinical context and hypothesis. A reviewer must distinguish real flaws from sound but non-standard practices that could be mistaken for errors by pattern-matching critics.

hypothesis: In hospital inpatient care, a hierarchical transformer model over temporally ordered EHR events predicts 30-day unplanned readmission more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built on manually aggregated stay-level features, when evaluated on future admissions from unseen months.
domain: Healthcare operations for acute-care hospitals, predicting 30-day unplanned readmission risk at discharge using longitudinal inpatient EHR data
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Transformer vs. GBT for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3 years of de-identified inpatient EHR data from a multi-site hospital system, spanning January Year 1 through December Year 3 and covering all adult inpatient admissions resulting in live discharge. The expected cohort size is 80,000–150,000 admissions, providing adequate statistical power and temporal range. The positive label is defined as any unplanned inpatient readmission within 30 calendar days of the index discharge date, derived from encounter records within the same system using an adaptation of the CMS planned readmission exclusion algorithm. Deaths within 30 days without readmission are excluded from the denominator. The expected base rate is approximately 10–15%.

A critical data quality step is enforcing a strict prediction cutoff at T_discharge minus 2 hours. All feature extraction is filtered programmatically to events with timestamps before this cutoff, excluding discharge-order finalization artifacts and post-decision coding. This is verified via timestamp-assertion unit tests on the feature pipeline. Out-of-system readmissions are documented as a source of label noise; a sensitivity analysis will estimate their prevalence using regional utilization benchmarks.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by patient discharge date, with patient-level exclusivity enforced so the same patient does not span splits:
- **Training:** Year 1 Month 1 – Year 2 Month 6 (18 months)
- **Validation:** Year 2 Month 7 – Year 2 Month 12 (6 months)
- **Test:** Year 3 Month 1 – Year 3 Month 12 (12 months, held out)

A temporal split is required by the hypothesis itself, which specifies evaluation on future admissions from unseen months. Random splitting would allow future-to-past leakage at the cohort level and would not measure generalization under distribution shift. Patient-level exclusivity prevents a patient's earlier admission from appearing in training while a later admission appears in test, which would leak patient-specific risk trajectories.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature representations are constructed from the same raw data, filtered to the identical prediction cutoff:

**Transformer input (Arm A):** A temporally ordered sequence of clinical events per admission — labs, vitals, medication administrations, procedures, nursing flowsheet entries — each represented as (event_type, code, value, elapsed_hours_since_admission). Codes are embedded via learned embeddings; continuous values are normalized. Sinusoidal time encodings over elapsed hours are used rather than positional rank, to handle irregular clinical event spacing. A patient-context prefix encodes demographics and prior-utilization summary statistics.

**GBT input (Arm B):** Manually aggregated stay-level features derived from the identical events: demographics, Charlson Comorbidity Index, min/max/last values for 15 key labs, count of abnormal vitals, medication diversity, length of stay, discharge disposition, prior utilization counts, weekend discharge flag, and primary payer — a representative strong clinical feature set.

**Ablation baseline (Arm C):** A GBT trained on a comprehensive automated aggregation of the full raw event space (e.g., count-of-events-per-type, mean/min/max per lab code, time-since-last-event-per-type), providing feature-parity comparison to isolate sequence modeling value from information-access advantage.

All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, vocabulary maps, imputation medians — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The proposed model is a two-level hierarchical transformer encoder: Level 1 encodes intra-admission event sequences (4 layers, 8 heads, d_model=256); Level 2 aggregates the admission representation with the patient-context prefix. A 2-layer MLP with dropout maps the [CLS] token to a readmission probability. The model is trained with AdamW and a linear warmup + cosine decay schedule.

The primary baseline (Arm B) is a tuned XGBoost or LightGBM model on aggregated stay-level features. Both models receive equivalent Bayesian optimization effort (50 tuning trials each) over the validation set, optimizing AUROC. Class imbalance is handled identically via loss weighting in both arms — no oversampling is applied, to avoid train/test boundary artifacts.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is AUROC, reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (2,000 resamples); DeLong's test is used for significance testing of the pairwise AUROC difference between arms. AUROC captures threshold-invariant discrimination and is appropriate for comparing architectures.

Because stakeholders operate under constrained care-management capacity, **precision at top 10% and top 20% of predicted risk** are operationally critical secondary metrics and are given equal interpretive weight. Additional secondary metrics include: AUPRC (informative under imbalance), Expected Calibration Error with reliability plots (calibration is required for threshold-based intervention allocation), Brier Score, and sensitivity/specificity at the 80% sensitivity operating point. Subgroup AUROC is reported by admission type, top CCS diagnosis categories, age group, and hospital site.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning for all arms is performed exclusively on the validation set (Year 2 Months 7–12), optimizing validation AUROC. The transformer uses validation loss for early stopping (patience = 5 epochs). The final configuration for each arm is fully locked before the test set is touched. **The test set is evaluated exactly once**, after all modeling decisions are finalized. To assess temporal robustness, test-set performance is additionally reported in quarterly slices (Q1–Q4 Year 3) to detect any performance degradation over time.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are explicitly addressed: (1) **Information-access confound** — Arm C (feature-parity GBT) isolates whether any transformer advantage reflects sequence modeling or merely access to richer feature space; (2) **Temporal distribution shift** — quarterly test slices and label completeness verification (only admissions with a full 31-day post-discharge observation window are included in test) ensure valid labels and detect drift; (3) **Site confound** — stratified performance by site and a leave-one-site-out analysis confirm the transformer advantage is not specific to a single site's coding practices; (4) **Prediction cutoff integrity** — timestamp assertions in the feature pipeline are audited to confirm zero post-discharge information leakage.

This experiment would establish whether a hierarchical transformer over raw EHR event sequences outperforms a well-tuned GBT on aggregated features for 30-day unplanned readmission prediction, on held-out future admissions from a single hospital system. It would not establish generalizability to other systems, clinical utility in prospective deployment, or causal mechanisms.

must_not_claim.claim: The ablation baseline (Arm C) is unnecessary or redundant because Arm B already represents a strong GBT baseline.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Arm C is essential to isolate the value of sequence modeling from mere access to richer feature representations. Without Arm C, an observed performance gap between Arm A and Arm B could reflect feature richness rather than sequential pattern modeling. Thus, Arm C is a methodologically sound and necessary control for the stated hypothesis.

must_not_claim.claim: A temporal split with no gap between validation and test introduces leakage or unnatural evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically requires evaluating performance on future admissions from unseen months. A contiguous temporal split without gaps is appropriate here because the hospital system has no substantial data processing lag; label completeness is verified for all test admissions. Introducing artificial gaps would reduce statistical power and is unnecessary given the validation of cutoff integrity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the design is sound and no substantive flaws exist. The defender successfully rebuts any minor concerns by correctly invoking domain justification and experimental necessity.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The experiment correctly uses a temporal split aligned with the hypothesis, avoids leakage via strict cutoffs, ensures fair model comparison through equivalent tuning effort and a feature-parity baseline, and reports operationally relevant metrics including both AUROC and precision at top-K.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns but acknowledges they are addressed by the confound controls in place; the final verdict is approval.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The presence and correct implementation of Arm C, temporal robustness checks via quarterly slices, site-level stratification, and validation of prediction cutoff integrity collectively address all major methodological risks in readmission prediction studies.
