It tests the ability to distinguish legitimate concerns from non-issues using domain-specific reasoning.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchical multi-label model that jointly encodes discharge summaries and the patient’s preceding longitudinal EHR context will achieve higher recall at fixed precision for ICD-10 diagnosis assignment than a discharge-summary-only ClinicalBERT baseline.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation operations for automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding on adult inpatient hospital stays
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding with Longitudinal EHR Context

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3.5 years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system (January 2020 through June 2023). Each encounter must have a finalized, audited discharge summary and a complete set of coder-assigned ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes on the billed claim. Encounters with pending audit status, missing discharge summaries, or fewer than one billed diagnosis are excluded as likely data integrity issues. Labels are the multi-hot binary vector of all ICD-10-CM codes (principal and all secondary diagnoses) present on the final claim. Codes appearing fewer than 50 times across the full corpus are placed in a designated 'rare stratum' — retained in training but excluded from the primary headline metric to prevent rare-code noise from masking or inflating performance estimates on well-supported codes.

Longitudinal EHR context per encounter includes: the current-admission problem list, medication list at admission, up to 90 days of pre-admission outpatient notes, intra-admission clinical notes prior to the discharge summary (H&P, progress notes, consults), and timestamped lab results (top-50 most recent by LOINC category).

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level into train/validation/test partitions of approximately 70%/15%/15%, stratified by hospital site and overall diagnosis prevalence so that each split contains a comparable mix of sites and coding patterns. This helps stabilize performance estimates across both frequent and infrequent codes and ensures the train, validation, and test sets each reflect the full diversity of the corpus. In addition, the largest hospital site not involved in any tuning decisions is held out entirely from training and validation, and evaluated on its test-period encounters as a cross-site generalization probe.

### 3. Feature Sets and Preprocessing

Three feature sets are defined to support both the core comparison and the critical confound ablation:

- **Feature Set A** (discharge-summary baseline): discharge summary text only.
- **Feature Set B** (proposed model): discharge summary plus structured and unstructured longitudinal EHR context.
- **Feature Set C** (text-volume control): all available note text concatenated and passed to the same baseline ClinicalBERT architecture, without hierarchical encoding. This set is essential for isolating whether any gain comes from the architecture rather than simply from having more tokens.

All preprocessing statistics (lab z-score parameters, any auxiliary TF-IDF statistics) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test data. The ClinicalBERT tokenizer is used from its pretrained checkpoint.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical multi-label classifier: a shared ClinicalBERT encoder processes each note independently; a temporal cross-attention module aggregates prior-note representations weighted by recency; structured EHR features are encoded via a small transformer over timestamped sequences; all representations are concatenated and passed through a label-correlation graph attention layer followed by a sigmoid multi-label head, trained with inverse-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The primary baseline (Feature Set A) is a ClinicalBERT model with an identical multi-label classification head. Critically, both models and the text-volume control receive the same hyperparameter tuning budget: 50 Optuna trials each, searching the same parameter space (learning rate, dropout, MLP hidden size, batch size), using the same validation metric as the objective. No model receives preferential tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **code-level Recall at fixed Precision of 0.70 (R@P70)**, micro-averaged over the non-rare code stratum. This directly operationalizes the stakeholder objective: maximize coding completeness without exceeding a false-positive burden that would overload human coders. The 0.70 precision floor is set in consultation with revenue-cycle stakeholders.

Secondary metrics include: macro-averaged R@P70 (to detect concentration in common codes), R@P70 on secondary diagnoses only (the hypothesis's primary clinical target), micro-averaged AUPRC (threshold-agnostic summary), and stratifications by service line, length-of-stay quartile, note-count quartile, and the held-out hospital site.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Policy

All hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation set using the primary metric. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete, in a single final run covering all three models simultaneously. Statistical significance of the proposed model versus each baseline is assessed via paired bootstrap (10,000 resamples, Bonferroni-corrected for two comparisons).

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, text volume: the Feature Set C text-volume control model is the critical ablation — if the proposed model does not outperform it, the gain is attributable to raw text quantity, not architecture. Second, coding intensity correlated with stay complexity: stratification by length-of-stay and note-count quartile will reveal whether gains are confined to complex, note-heavy encounters. Third, temporal and institutional shift: the held-out site evaluation assesses generalization across hospitals under realistic deployment conditions. The experiment would establish that the hierarchical model outperforms both baselines on recall at fixed precision; it would not establish which clinical concepts the longitudinal context provides, nor would it generalize to pediatric, outpatient, or non-US coding contexts.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random encounter-level split instead of a chronological split, which allows future coding patterns (from later in the 2020–2023 period) to leak into the training set. Because ICD-10 coding guidelines and documentation practices evolve annually, this temporal contamination produces optimistically biased performance estimates that do not reflect real-world deployment conditions where models must generalize forward in time. The design falsely stabilizes performance by mixing time points, undermining the validity of the evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is only ~29% of data and not exactly 15%, so the split is unbalanced or improperly sized.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Chronological splits naturally result in uneven partitions due to changing admission volumes over time. The critical issue is temporal ordering — not strict percentage alignment. The sound design uses an appropriate time-based split that respects deployment realism, even if proportions differ from typical 70/15/15 splits.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by hospital site and diagnosis prevalence in the split is inappropriate or introduces bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by site and label prevalence is valid and often desirable — the flaw is not the stratification itself, but the absence of temporal ordering. A properly executed split could combine chronological ordering with stratification within time windows. The error is the use of random temporal mixing, not the use of stratification.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that the random encounter-level split creates temporal leakage, invalidating the deployment-realistic evaluation. The defender fails to rebut because no amount of stratification can fix time travel. The final verdict is that the experimental design is flawed and would produce non-deployable results.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that coding guidelines and documentation practices change over time (e.g., annual ICD-10 updates), so future data in training leaks information not available at inference time, inflating performance and breaking generalization guarantees.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies the temporal leakage and correctly notes that the held-out hospital site evaluation does not mitigate the core flaw, because the temporal contamination affects all sites, including the held-out one, during its test period. The cross-site probe alone cannot rescue a temporally invalid evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that even with a held-out site, if that site’s test encounters were used during training (due to random splitting), then its evaluation is contaminated and does not represent true forward-in-time performance.

## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding with Longitudinal EHR Context

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3.5 years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system (January 2020 through June 2023). Each encounter must have a finalized, audited discharge summary and a complete set of coder-assigned ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes on the billed claim. ### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by discharge date: January 2020 – December 2021 for training (~57%), January – June 2022 for validation (~14%), and July 2022 – June 2023 for the held-out test set (~29%). A chronological split is mandatory here. ICD-10 coding guidelines update annually, documentation practices drift, and a random split would allow future coding patterns to contaminate the training window, producing non-deployable performance estimates. - **Feature Set C** (text-volume control): all available note text concatenated and passed to the same baseline ClinicalBERT architecture, without hierarchical encoding. This set is essential for isolating whether any gain comes from the architecture rather than simply from having more tokens.

All preprocessing statistics (lab z-score parameters, any auxiliary TF-IDF statistics) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test data. The ClinicalBERT tokenizer is used from its pretrained checkpoint.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical multi-label classifier: a shared ClinicalBERT encoder processes each note independently; a temporal cross-attention module aggregates prior-note representations weighted by recency; structured EHR features are encoded via a small transformer over timestamped sequences; all representations are concatenated and passed through a label-correlation graph attention layer followed by a sigmoid multi-label head, trained with inverse-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The primary baseline (Feature Set A) is a ClinicalBERT model with an identical multi-label classification head. Critically, both models and the text-volume control receive the same hyperparameter tuning budget: 50 Optuna trials each, searching the same parameter space (learning rate, dropout, MLP hidden size, batch size), using the same validation metric as the objective. No model receives preferential tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **code-level Recall at fixed Precision of 0.70 (R@P70)**, micro-averaged over the non-rare code stratum. This directly operationalizes the stakeholder objective: maximize coding completeness without exceeding a false-positive burden that would overload human coders. Secondary metrics include: macro-averaged R@P70 (to detect concentration in common codes), R@P70 on secondary diagnoses only (the hypothesis's primary clinical target), micro-averaged AUPRC (threshold-agnostic summary), and stratifications by service line, length-of-stay quartile, note-count quartile, and the held-out hospital site.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Policy

All hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation set using the primary metric. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete, in a single final run covering all three models simultaneously. Statistical significance of the proposed model versus each baseline is assessed via paired bootstrap (10,000 resamples, Bonferroni-corrected for two comparisons).

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, text volume: the Feature Set C text-volume control model is the critical ablation — if the proposed model does not outperform it, the gain is attributable to raw text quantity, not architecture. Second, coding intensity correlated with stay complexity: stratification by length-of-stay and note-count quartile will reveal whether gains are confined to complex, note-heavy encounters. Third, temporal and institutional shift: the chronological split and held-out site evaluation assess generalization under realistic deployment conditions. The experiment would establish that the hierarchical model outperforms both baselines on recall at fixed precision; it would not establish which clinical concepts the longitudinal context provides, nor would it generalize to pediatric, outpatient, or non-US coding contexts.
difficulty_justification: This case is subtle because the flawed split uses stratification that appears methodologically sound at first glance, but fails on temporal integrity — a domain-specific requirement due to evolving coding practices. A reviewer must recognize that statistical balance does not override deployment realism in time-series healthcare settings.

hypothesis: In inpatient hospital coding, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher micro- and macro-F1 for predicting the set of billed ICD-10 diagnosis codes at discharge than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline built on the same notes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation, where hospitals assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from inpatient stay notes for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Long-Document Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The primary dataset is MIMIC-IV, restricted to admissions with discharge dates after the US ICD-10-CM transition (October 2015), yielding approximately 40,000–50,000 de-identified inpatient encounters. Each encounter provides one or more discharge summaries and a series of daily progress notes from the EHR, along with a final set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes extracted from the billing record. This code set constitutes the multi-label target — no codes are suppressed for frequency. A secondary cohort from a separate health system (accessed under a DUA) is reserved as an out-of-distribution generalization check and is not used in any training or model selection decision.

Known data quality considerations: a small fraction of MIMIC-IV encounters have incomplete note timelines or are missing discharge summaries; these are excluded with the exclusion rate reported. Duplicate or amended notes are deduplicated by keeping the latest version per note type per encounter day.

### 2. Split Strategy

Admissions are sorted by discharge date and partitioned chronologically: the earliest 70% of encounters form the training set, the next 15% form the validation set, and the final 15% form the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because the deployment scenario involves applying a model trained on historical admissions to future ones. Random splitting would allow future coding conventions, updated billing guidelines, and diagnosis trends to leak into training — creating an optimistic bias that does not reflect real-world performance. Patient-level integrity is additionally enforced: all admissions for a given patient are assigned to a single partition so that repeated-admission patients do not contribute chronic-condition label signal across splits.

### 3. Input Construction and Feature Engineering

For both models, the input text is constructed identically by concatenating the discharge summary and all chronological progress notes for the encounter, separated by a special section delimiter. To isolate the confound introduced by discharge summaries (which may explicitly enumerate finalized diagnoses), all experiments are run under three text conditions: (a) discharge summary only, (b) progress notes only, and (c) full concatenation. This ablation is applied to both models equally.

For the transformer (Clinical-Longformer), the concatenated text is tokenized and truncated to 4096 tokens using a head+tail strategy (first 2048 + last 2048 tokens) to preserve both admission context and discharge framing without arbitrary mid-document cutoff. For the TF-IDF baseline, the same raw concatenated text is vectorized with unigram+bigram features, min_df=2, and L2 normalization. All preprocessing decisions — IDF weights, label binarizer mappings, code-frequency tier boundaries — are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is Clinical-Longformer fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head and binary cross-entropy loss, using frequency-weighted loss to temper class imbalance. Training uses AdamW with linear warmup and cosine decay; early stopping is triggered on validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest linear SVM. The SVM regularization parameter C is tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} on the validation set using the same primary metric. The baseline is given the same input text, the same label space, and equivalent hyperparameter search effort. This is the appropriate comparison: it is the strongest classical NLP approach for this task and ensures that any observed gap is attributable to the modeling approach, not data asymmetry or differential tuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged AUC-ROC over labels, reported on the held-out test set. AUC-ROC is used because it is threshold-independent and provides a stable global comparison across highly imbalanced labels. Micro-F1 and macro-F1 are reported as secondary metrics, both computed at a fixed decision threshold of 0.5 and also at the validation-optimal threshold (reported separately). Secondary metrics also include Recall@K and Precision@K (K=10, K=25) to reflect the coder-review workflow and expected calibration error. Critically, all metrics are reported both globally and stratified by code-frequency tier (very rare, rare, common, frequent), so that it is transparent whether transformer gains extend to the clinically important rare codes or are concentrated in frequent chronic-condition codes.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All hyperparameter decisions and model checkpoint selection are made on the validation set using macro-F1. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all selection is complete, via a single locked evaluation script. No retraining or threshold adjustment follows test-set inspection.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are explicitly controlled: (1) discharge-summary boilerplate is isolated via the three-condition text ablation; (2) note-length effects are assessed by regressing note length on score residuals; (3) specialty mix is controlled by reporting stratified metrics per primary service line; (4) temporal drift is assessed by plotting annual performance within the test window. The experiment establishes whether, under controlled text-equivalent conditions on MIMIC-IV, the clinical transformer outperforms the TF-IDF baseline. It does not establish which specific linguistic capabilities are responsible, nor does it generalize beyond US acute inpatient settings — the out-of-distribution cohort check addresses generalizability as an exploratory secondary analysis.

planted_issues.description: The experiment design elevates macro-averaged AUC-ROC to the status of primary metric, but AUC-ROC is threshold-agnostic and does not reflect actionable model performance in a real-world deployment where coders need high-quality, thresholded suggestions. In a reimbursement-sensitive setting with highly imbalanced labels, optimizing for AUC can allow models to score well by ranking rare labels marginally better while failing at making calibrated, high-precision predictions. The primary metric should be micro-F1 or macro-F1, which directly measure retrieval quality at operational thresholds, especially for rare but clinically important codes.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of macro-F1 as the model selection criterion on the validation set invalidates temporal integrity because the test set uses future data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal integrity is preserved because model selection is done solely on the validation set, which precedes the test set chronologically. Using macro-F1 — a performance metric — for early stopping and checkpoint selection does not introduce leakage as long as the model does not observe test-set labels or predictions.

must_not_claim.claim: Reporting AUC-ROC as a secondary metric is inappropriate because it is threshold-independent and less aligned with the use case.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUC-ROC is a valid secondary metric for assessing overall ranking quality and model calibration across thresholds; the flaw lies only in its promotion to primary status, not in its reporting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that AUC-ROC is a poor choice for the primary metric in this operational context, because stakeholder impact depends on thresholded retrieval quality (e.g., F1 at 0.5) rather than ranking alone. The defender fails to justify why AUC-ROC should supersede F1 metrics for final evaluation, especially given the hypothesis specifically prioritizes micro- and macro-F1.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that AUC-ROC does not reflect post-deployment performance where coders act on a ranked list of model-suggested codes above a fixed threshold, and that AUC can be misleading under extreme label imbalance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges AUC-ROC provides useful secondary insight but insists the primary metric must be micro-F1 or macro-F1 to align with the hypothesis and stakeholder needs. The final verdict requires downgrading AUC-ROC to secondary status and elevating F1 to primary in evaluation reporting.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that the hypothesis explicitly claims superiority in F1, not AUC, making consistency with the evaluation design a methodological necessity.

hypothesis: For 30-day unplanned hospital readmission prediction among adult medical inpatients, a transformer model over time-ordered EHR events (diagnoses, labs, medications, vitals, and nursing documentation timestamps) will achieve higher precision-recall performance than a gradient-boosted tree model built on manually aggregated encounter-level features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical operations: readmission risk stratification for adult medical inpatients in an integrated hospital system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use four years of inpatient EHR data (January 2019 – December 2022) from the full integrated health system. Index discharges are restricted to adult medical inpatients (age ≥ 18) discharged alive from a non-psychiatric service, excluding planned readmissions (identified via the CMS Planned Readmission Algorithm v4.0), deaths during index admission, and hospice discharges. This yields a population for whom readmission prevention is operationally meaningful.

The binary label is any unplanned inpatient readmission within 30 calendar days of index discharge, to any in-network facility, classified as unplanned by the CMS algorithm applied to the subsequent encounter. Out-of-network readmissions are a known censoring source. A sensitivity analysis restricted to patients with demonstrated in-network utilization history will assess whether conclusions hold in a lower-censoring subpopulation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided chronologically — not randomly. Training covers January 2019 – December 2021; validation covers January – June 2022; test covers July – December 2022 (roughly 70/15/15 by encounter volume). A temporal split is required because deployment is always prospective: training on shuffled data would allow future coding trends, lab reporting changes, or formulary updates to leak into training. The split boundary is enforced on the index discharge date, with the constraint that each index case's 30-day follow-up window falls entirely within its assigned split, preventing label leakage at boundaries.

### 3. Feature Construction and Parity

Two parallel feature sets are built from the same underlying signals to enable a controlled comparison. The transformer receives a time-ordered sequence of clinical events (diagnoses, medications, labs, vitals, note-creation events) from admission through discharge, encoded as (event_type, code, value_bucket, timestamp_offset) tuples, truncated to 512 events. The GBT receives manually aggregated encounter-level features covering the same signal domains: diagnosis category counts, high-risk medication flags, abnormal lab counts and specific value flags, vital sign summary statistics, note count by type, prior utilization, demographics, and site/service indicators.

This feature parity is the central design control. Any performance difference should reflect sequence modeling capacity, not information asymmetry. All preprocessing transformers — imputation medians, scalers, event vocabularies — are fit on training data only and applied to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The transformer model follows BEHRT/Med-BERT conventions: 3 layers, 4 attention heads, embedding dimension 128, with a [CLS]-token MLP head for binary prediction. Hyperparameters are tuned via 30-iteration random search on validation AUPRC.

The baseline is LightGBM on the aggregated feature set, tuned with equivalent effort via 30-iteration Bayesian search (Optuna) on the same validation criterion. LightGBM on engineered encounter features represents current best practice for clinical readmission modeling — it is the correct baseline for this hypothesis.

Two ablations are pre-registered: (a) the transformer with positional encoding zeroed out (bag-of-events) to isolate sequence-ordering contribution, and (b) the transformer augmented with clinical note embeddings to test the note-text confound.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC on the held-out test set. Stakeholders operate at fixed alert volume (transitional care team capacity), making the precision-recall frontier — not overall AUROC — the operationally relevant surface. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for literature comparability), Precision@10% (top decile performance at operational volume), Brier score and expected calibration error (ECE) for calibration quality, and subgroup AUPRC and ECE disaggregated by hospital site, service line, age decile, and insurance type.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection uses the validation set exclusively. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after final model selection, with no subsequent adjustment. Operating thresholds for precision/recall points are set from the validation distribution and applied as-is to test scores.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Feature parity eliminates information-asymmetry confounding. The bag-of-events ablation isolates sequence-ordering from vocabulary richness. Explicit site and service line features — plus disaggregated subgroup metrics — address hospital-level confounding. Prior utilization features are included in both models to control for patient complexity differences across sites.

This experiment would establish whether transformer-based sequence modeling outperforms a well-tuned GBT on readmission prediction within this system and time window. It would not establish cross-system generalizability or clinical utility, which would require prospective evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal train/validation/test split introduces unnecessary distribution shift and reduces statistical efficiency compared to a random split.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A temporal split is methodologically required in this domain because the model will be deployed prospectively. A random split would allow future patterns (e.g., changes in lab utilization, medication formularies, or coding practices) to leak into the training set, artificially inflating performance and undermining the validity of the comparison. The design correctly prioritizes real-world deployment validity over maximal statistical efficiency.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of AUPRC as the primary metric is inappropriate; AUROC should be used instead for binary classification evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is the operationally correct metric because stakeholders make decisions under fixed alert volume constraints (e.g., limited transitional care team capacity). In this high-stakes, imbalanced setting (~10–15% readmission rate), AUPRC directly evaluates performance in the high-precision region where the model is actually used, making it more sensitive and relevant than AUROC, which averages over irrelevant low-precision regions.

must_not_claim.claim: Including patients in multiple splits due to repeated hospitalizations risks data leakage and inflates performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design correctly allows patients to appear in multiple splits because the split boundary is enforced at the index discharge level, and each index discharge's full 30-day follow-up window is contained entirely within one split. This preserves chronological integrity and reflects real-world clinical populations where patients may have multiple admissions over time. True data leakage would occur only if future data informed past predictions, which is prevented by the temporal split and follow-up window constraint.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern about a methodological choice (e.g., temporal split, AUPRC focus, patient overlap), and the defender correctly explains why the design is appropriate given the clinical deployment context, stakeholder needs, and hypothesis being tested. No critique is sustained; the final verdict is that the experiment design is sound and supports a valid test of the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The concern stems from a general ML best practice that does not apply under prospective deployment constraints, or misinterprets a necessary design element (e.g., temporal split, feature parity, metric choice) as a flaw when it is in fact a strength tailored to the domain.

hypothesis: For hospital coding operations, a clinical Longformer model fine-tuned on full discharge summaries and inpatient progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for ICD-10 multi-label code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency but clinically specific diagnosis codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation operations for automated ICD-10 coding of inpatient hospital encounters
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Longformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3–4 years of de-identified inpatient encounter data from a multi-site hospital system (≥3 hospitals), targeting at least 80,000 complete, audited encounters after quality filtering. Each encounter record includes the discharge summary and all inpatient progress notes dated prior to or on the discharge date, linked to the final auditor-confirmed ICD-10-CM code set. Post-discharge addenda, billing queries, and coding addenda are explicitly excluded from model input to prevent post-discharge label leakage. Any encounter with incomplete coding audit status is excluded. ICD-10 codes with fewer than 10 occurrences across the full dataset are excluded from evaluation (though not from model training) to avoid degenerate F1 estimates; codes with 10–50 training occurrences form a designated 'rare-code' evaluation stratum, which is the specific subpopulation the hypothesis targets.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are randomly split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test sets, with stratification by overall coding density so that each split has a similar label-load profile. This yields more statistically stable estimates and helps ensure that each split contains a representative mixture of hospitals, documentation styles, and diagnosis prevalence. Encounters are never split mid-admission.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive exactly the same raw text input: the chronologically ordered concatenation of the discharge summary and all progress notes per encounter. This parity is non-negotiable — it ensures the comparison isolates architecture and representation rather than information access.

For the TF-IDF baseline, word-level and character-level unigram/bigram features are extracted (max 200,000 features, sublinear TF scaling). To stabilize the feature space across partitions, the TF-IDF vectorizer vocabulary and IDF weights are fit once on the full corpus and then applied consistently to the training, validation, and test splits. For the Longformer, a pretrained clinical-domain checkpoint is used; the tokenizer is applied without refitting. For encounters exceeding 4,096 tokens, a sliding-window strategy (4,096-token windows with 512-token stride, mean-pooled) is applied to preserve all clinical evidence without silent truncation.

Label binarization and downstream feature application are then kept fixed across splits so that both models are evaluated under the same representational setup.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Clinical Longformer with a multi-label sigmoid classification head, fine-tuned end-to-end using binary cross-entropy with frequency-inverse positive-class weighting (capped at 10×). Training uses AdamW with linear warmup, peak LR ~2e-5, and early stopping on validation macro-F1.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression with balanced class weighting. It is trained using a standard regularization setting (C = 1.0), which is a strong and widely used reference point for sparse high-dimensional text classification. Both models operate on identical text inputs with no additional structured features available to either.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged F1 over all codes meeting the minimum occurrence threshold. Micro-F1 captures overall coding performance across the full encounter volume and provides a stable summary measure for large multi-label classification problems. Because the vocabulary is broad and highly imbalanced, micro-averaging avoids overemphasizing very low-support labels whose variance can dominate macro-level summaries.

Per-label decision thresholds are tuned independently to optimize F1 for both models, with final thresholds set after model family selection so that each code is evaluated at its strongest operating point.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 restricted to the rare-code stratum (10–50 training occurrences), macro-recall (coding completeness), per-site macro-F1 (confound check), and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent performance under imbalance).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Core model-development decisions are made using the validation set. The Longformer checkpoint with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected as the final neural model. After this, per-label decision thresholds for the final selected models are finalized on the held-out test set to ensure that reported F1 reflects the best achievable operating point for each code under the chosen deployment candidate. There is no iterative retraining after threshold finalization.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are directly addressed. First, text access parity: both models receive identical concatenated text with identical length-handling policies, ruling out the possibility that the Longformer benefits simply from seeing more text. Second, site-level generalization: per-site performance breakdowns on the test set verify that observed gains hold across hospital documentation styles and are not an artifact of one site's templates. Third, data-composition balance: the split strategy ensures that training, validation, and test each contain a broad mix of hospitals and coding patterns, reducing the risk that results are driven by an idiosyncratic subset of encounters.

An additional ablation is pre-registered: Longformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries only versus discharge summaries plus progress notes. This isolates whether multi-document access — rather than transformer architecture — is the true driver of any observed gain.

The experiment would establish whether a clinical Longformer architecture, given the same textual evidence as the baseline, yields stronger overall multi-label ICD-10 assignment performance and whether any gains extend into the rare-code subgroup. It would not establish generalizability to outpatient coding, to health systems with different documentation cultures, or to ICD-10-PCS procedure codes without separate evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random encounter-level split instead of a temporal split by discharge date. This allows documentation templates, coding policy changes, and case mix shifts from future time periods to leak into the training data, creating an overly optimistic performance estimate that would not generalize to future admissions. The design falsely assumes stationarity where it does not hold.

The TF-IDF vectorizer is fit on the full dataset before the train/validation/test split, meaning IDF weights and vocabulary are influenced by future (test-time) documents. This contaminates the feature representation with information not available at training time, enabling subtle leakage of temporal patterns and biasing performance upward.

The primary metric is micro-F1, which aggregates performance weighted by label frequency. This directly contradicts the hypothesis, which emphasizes gains on low-frequency clinical codes. Micro-F1 can increase even if rare-code performance degrades, making it incapable of testing the stated claim.

The TF-IDF baseline is trained with a fixed default regularization parameter (C=1.0) without any validation-based tuning, while the Longformer is carefully tuned. This creates an unfair comparison that exaggerates the neural model's advantage and violates the principle of equal tuning effort for fair model comparison.

Per-label decision thresholds are optimized using the held-out test set. This constitutes post-hoc model selection on test data, inflating reported performance and invalidating the test set’s role as an unbiased estimate of generalization. Thresholds must be set on the validation set or through cross-validation.

must_not_claim.claim: The split is not stratified by hospital site, so performance may not generalize across institutions.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The experiment includes per-site macro-F1 breakdowns on the test set as a confound check, and the random split ensures site diversity is preserved across partitions. Site-level generalization is explicitly monitored, so lack of formal site stratification does not invalidate the design.

must_not_claim.claim: Using micro-F1 as a secondary metric risks overemphasizing common codes and masking rare-code degradation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Micro-F1 is appropriately used as a secondary metric to assess overall coding throughput and volume performance. The primary metric remains the key decision point; secondary metrics can provide complementary insights without compromising hypothesis testing when properly contextualized.
