sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Longformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3–4 years of de-identified inpatient encounter data from a multi-site hospital system (≥3 hospitals), targeting at least 80,000 complete, audited encounters after quality filtering. Each encounter record includes the discharge summary and all inpatient progress notes dated prior to or on the discharge date, linked to the final auditor-confirmed ICD-10-CM code set. Post-discharge addenda, billing queries, and coding addenda are explicitly excluded from model input to prevent post-discharge label leakage. Any encounter with incomplete coding audit status is excluded. ICD-10 codes with fewer than 10 occurrences across the full dataset are excluded from evaluation (though not from model training) to avoid degenerate F1 estimates; codes with 10–50 training occurrences form a designated 'rare-code' evaluation stratum, which is the specific subpopulation the hypothesis targets.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are split temporally by discharge date: the earliest 70% of encounters form the training set (~2.5–3 years), the next 15% form the validation set (~6–9 months), and the final 15% form the held-out test set (~6 months). A temporal split is required because the deployment target is future admissions. Documentation templates, coding policy updates, and case-mix composition shift over calendar time; a random split would allow these future patterns to leak into training and produce optimistic performance estimates that would not hold operationally. Encounters are never split mid-admission.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive exactly the same raw text input: the chronologically ordered concatenation of the discharge summary and all progress notes per encounter. This parity is non-negotiable — it ensures the comparison isolates architecture and representation rather than information access.

For the TF-IDF baseline, word-level and character-level unigram/bigram features are extracted (max 200,000 features, sublinear TF scaling). The TF-IDF vectorizer vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the training set only and frozen for validation and test application. For the Longformer, a pretrained clinical-domain checkpoint is used; the tokenizer is applied without refitting. For encounters exceeding 4,096 tokens, a sliding-window strategy (4,096-token windows with 512-token stride, mean-pooled) is applied to preserve all clinical evidence without silent truncation.

All preprocessing fit decisions — including label binarizer, code-frequency thresholds, and TF-IDF statistics — are made on training data exclusively.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Clinical Longformer with a multi-label sigmoid classification head, fine-tuned end-to-end using binary cross-entropy with frequency-inverse positive-class weighting (capped at 10×). Training uses AdamW with linear warmup, peak LR ~2e-5, and early stopping on validation macro-F1.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression with balanced class weighting. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength C is grid-searched over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation set and same macro-F1 criterion. Both models operate on identical text inputs with no additional structured features available to either.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 over all codes meeting the minimum occurrence threshold. Macro-F1 weights every code equally regardless of frequency, directly operationalizing the stakeholder requirement to maintain coding completeness on infrequent but reimbursable and quality-reporting diagnoses. Micro-F1 would be dominated by high-frequency codes and would mask the performance on rare codes that the hypothesis specifically addresses.

Per-label decision thresholds are tuned independently on the validation set for both models to avoid penalizing either for miscalibration.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (overall coding throughput), macro-F1 restricted to the rare-code stratum (10–50 training occurrences), macro-recall (coding completeness), per-site macro-F1 (confound check), and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent performance under imbalance).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions for both models are made using the validation set exclusively. The Longformer checkpoint with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected; the baseline C value with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected. The held-out test set is used exactly once, after all model selection and threshold tuning is finalized for both models. There is no iterative refinement after test-set evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are directly addressed. First, text access parity: both models receive identical concatenated text with identical length-handling policies, ruling out the possibility that the Longformer benefits simply from seeing more text. Second, site-level generalization: per-site performance breakdowns on the test set verify that observed gains hold across hospital documentation styles and are not an artifact of one site's templates. Third, temporal generalization: the temporal split ensures that test-set encounters are genuinely future relative to training, validating that improvements hold under the case-mix and policy shifts relevant to deployment.

An additional ablation is pre-registered: Longformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries only versus discharge summaries plus progress notes. This isolates whether multi-document access — rather than transformer architecture — is the true driver of any observed gain.

The experiment would establish whether a clinical Longformer architecture, given the same textual evidence as the baseline, yields higher macro-F1 on multi-label ICD-10 assignment, particularly on rare codes. It would not establish generalizability to outpatient coding, to health systems with different documentation cultures, or to ICD-10-PCS procedure codes without separate evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws require understanding of temporal validity in operational ML, contamination via preprocessing, and misalignment between metrics and stakeholder goals. The compound effect of multiple corruptions, especially temporal and preprocessing leakage together, demands integrated reasoning rather than isolated pattern matching.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher micro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency but clinically important diagnosis codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical informatics for automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient hospitalization documentation in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system, covering discharges from January 2021 through December 2023. Each encounter provides a discharge summary, up to 10 attending or consulting progress notes dated on or before the discharge date, hospital site, service line, and final audited ICD-10 diagnosis codes from the billing workflow. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any coded diagnosis are excluded. Encounters with late-amended codes (coded more than 30 days post-discharge) are also excluded, as these introduce label noise from retrospective audits that would not reflect prospective deployment conditions.

Labels are defined as multi-hot binary vectors over the ICD-10-CM codes present in the dataset. Codes appearing fewer than 10 times across all encounters are dropped from the prediction vocabulary to avoid degenerate evaluation on effectively unobservable classes. A low-frequency stratum (10–100 occurrences) and a high-frequency stratum (>100 occurrences) are pre-defined for stratified evaluation, directly operationalizing the hypothesis's emphasis on tail-code performance.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is randomly split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test sets, stratified by hospital site and approximate label cardinality to ensure each split is representative of the overall diagnosis mix. This yields balanced partitions while preserving enough data for both tuning and final evaluation.

A stratified random split is useful here because the task is highly multi-label and code frequencies are uneven; ensuring each partition reflects the full diagnosis distribution makes model comparison more stable and reduces the chance that one split is dominated by a particular site or service mix. All notes belonging to a single admission are kept together to eliminate within-encounter leakage. Hospital site distribution is verified across splits to confirm no dramatic site skew.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both models, the input is constructed identically: the discharge summary is concatenated with up to 10 progress notes in ascending chronological order, using a fixed separator token. Only notes dated on or before discharge are included — no post-discharge documentation is permitted, preserving temporal integrity.

For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenated text is vectorized using word unigrams and character 2–3-grams (up to 200k features, sublinear TF scaling). For the transformer, the text is tokenized with a pretrained clinical tokenizer and truncated to 4,096 tokens using a head+tail strategy (first 2,048 tokens prioritizing the discharge summary, last 2,048 tokens capturing recent progress notes).

To stabilize the feature space across splits, the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit once on the full corpus before partitioning, after which the resulting fixed representation is used for training, validation, and test evaluation. The pretrained tokenizer is not refit. No site or demographic features are incorporated in the primary experiment, ensuring the comparison isolates text representation quality.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a ClinicalLongformer (or equivalent clinical long-document transformer) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters — learning rate {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size {8, 16}, warmup steps — are tuned via grid search against the validation set. Label smoothing (ε=0.1) is applied to reduce overconfidence on frequent codes.

The baseline is TF-IDF + One-vs-Rest LinearSVC, receiving identical input text and the same label vocabulary. The baseline is trained with standard default settings to reflect a typical production sparse-text pipeline, and we keep the default regularization rather than tuning C so the comparison remains simple and reproducible. This baseline is the established, production-credible approach for automated ICD coding and represents the correct comparison for the hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged AUROC over all codes, reported for the full vocabulary and the two frequency strata. AUROC provides a threshold-independent summary and is a standard, stable metric for comparing multi-label classifiers.

Secondary metrics include macro-F1 (to capture tail-code behavior without domination by frequent codes), micro- and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent), and Precision@K and Recall@K for K ∈ {5, 10, 15} to evaluate the coder-shortlist recommendation workflow. All metrics are additionally reported stratified by hospital site and service line.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Model selection uses the validation set for the initial sweep, and the held-out test set is then used to choose the final checkpoint and confirm the best-performing hyperparameter configuration before reporting results. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples over test encounters) to characterize uncertainty in observed differences.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds require active control. First, text length advantage: both models receive identical input text, and a post-hoc regression of micro-F1 against encounter token count is run to quantify any residual length effect. Second, hospital-specific documentation templates: performance is stratified by site; gains isolated to sites with richer templating are attributed to documentation quality, not representation. Third, temporal coding policy drift: a supplementary ablation trains the TF-IDF baseline on the most recent 12 months only, to verify the transformer's advantage is not simply a recency artifact.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a TF-IDF+OvR baseline on multi-label ICD-10 coding, with particular rigor on tail-code strata. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, outpatient settings, procedure codes, or production deployment feasibility.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a temporal split by discharge date. This allows future coding patterns, documentation practices, and policy shifts to leak into training data, producing an unrealistically optimistic estimate of model performance in a prospective deployment setting where the model must predict on upcoming encounters.

The TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks statistics from validation and test data into the feature representation. This contaminates the training process and can artificially inflate performance, especially when language use evolves over time.

The proposed transformer model undergoes hyperparameter tuning, but the baseline uses default regularization settings without tuning. This creates an unfair comparison that biases results in favor of the transformer, failing to isolate the impact of model architecture from tuning effort.

The primary metric is micro-averaged AUROC, which is threshold-independent and does not reflect the real-world operational need for accurate thresholded code assignments. It can mask poor precision on rare but critical codes, misaligning evaluation with clinical and financial stakes.

The test set is used to select the final model checkpoint or hyperparameter configuration, which invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimate of generalization performance. This turns the test set into a de facto validation set and risks overfitting to its specific patterns.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the random split by hospital site and label cardinality introduces data leakage or is methodologically unsound.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by site and label density is a valid technique to ensure balanced representation across splits when using random partitioning. While a temporal split is required here due to time drift, the stratification itself is not the flaw — the use of random splitting in a prospective prediction task is.

must_not_claim.claim: Not tuning hyperparameters on the transformer model would make it an unfair baseline comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Tuning the proposed model is appropriate and expected; the flaw lies in not tuning the baseline, not in tuning the transformer. A strong, fair comparison requires equivalent effort on both sides.

must_not_claim.claim: Using bootstrap confidence intervals on the test set is problematic or invalid.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Reporting bootstrap CIs over test encounters is a sound practice for quantifying uncertainty in performance estimates and does not constitute test set misuse as long as no decisions are made based on them.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that random splitting invalidates prospective generalization claims due to temporal confounding. The defender cannot credibly justify this choice in this domain. The verdict must be critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that coding policies and documentation practices change over time, making temporal separation necessary for realistic evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that fitting TF-IDF on the full corpus leaks future information into training features, especially problematic given temporal drift. The issue is compounded by the non-temporal split. The verdict must reflect this as a serious methodological flaw.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer notes that IDF weights computed on future data can distort term importance in ways that bias performance upward.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: None — in this setting with evolving clinical language and coding norms, full-corpus fitting is invalid.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic points out that tuning only the transformer while leaving the baseline at defaults biases the experiment. The defender cannot claim this reflects 'typical production use' given the sound design specifies a tuned baseline as the credible production comparator. The verdict must favor critique unless this is corrected.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer argues that a fair test isolates representation quality, not the effect of tuning effort.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that AUROC is insufficiently aligned with operational needs for thresholded code recommendation and can conceal poor precision on rare codes. The defender may argue for AUROC as a diagnostic tool, but cannot override the mismatch with the primary success criterion (micro-F1 for error minimization).
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer links AUROC's insensitivity to thresholding and class imbalance to potential real-world failures in coder trust and reimbursement risk.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: If the hypothesis had emphasized ranking quality over final predictions, AUROC could be justified — but here, micro-F1 at threshold 0.5 is the stated goal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic shows that using the test set for model selection invalidates its role as an independent evaluation set. The defender cannot justify this under any rigorous evaluation protocol. The verdict must be critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that any use of test set feedback for model selection introduces overfitting and breaks statistical validity.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system, covering discharges from January 2021 through December 2023. Each encounter provides a discharge summary, up to 10 attending or consulting progress notes dated on or before the discharge date, hospital site, service line, and final audited ICD-10 diagnosis codes from the billing workflow. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any coded diagnosis are excluded. Encounters with late-amended codes (coded more than 30 days post-discharge) are also excluded, as these introduce label noise from retrospective audits that would not reflect prospective deployment conditions.

Labels are defined as multi-hot binary vectors over the ICD-10-CM codes present in the dataset. Codes appearing fewer than 10 times across all encounters are dropped from the prediction vocabulary to avoid degenerate evaluation on effectively unobservable classes. A low-frequency stratum (10–100 occurrences) and a high-frequency stratum (>100 occurrences) are pre-defined for stratified evaluation, directly operationalizing the hypothesis's emphasis on tail-code performance.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally by discharge date: the first 24 months (Jan 2021–Dec 2022) constitute the training set, months 25–30 (Jan–Jun 2023) the validation set, and months 31–36 (Jul–Dec 2023) the held-out test set. This yields an approximate 67/17/17 split by time.

A temporal split is mandatory here. The deployment context is strictly prospective — the model will always be applied to future encounters — and ICD-10 code sets, hospital documentation templates, and coding policies all evolve over time. A random split would allow future coding patterns to contaminate the training distribution, producing an optimistic and unrealistic performance estimate. All notes belonging to a single admission are kept together to eliminate within-encounter leakage. Hospital site distribution is verified across splits to confirm no dramatic site skew.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both models, the input is constructed identically: the discharge summary is concatenated with up to 10 progress notes in ascending chronological order, using a fixed separator token. Only notes dated on or before discharge are included — no post-discharge documentation is permitted, preserving temporal integrity.

For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenated text is vectorized using word unigrams and character 2–3-grams (up to 200k features, sublinear TF scaling). For the transformer, the text is tokenized with a pretrained clinical tokenizer and truncated to 4,096 tokens using a head+tail strategy (first 2,048 tokens prioritizing the discharge summary, last 2,048 tokens capturing recent progress notes).

All preprocessing transformers — TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied transform-only to validation and test sets. The pretrained tokenizer is not refit. No site or demographic features are incorporated in the primary experiment, ensuring the comparison isolates text representation quality.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a ClinicalLongformer (or equivalent clinical long-document transformer) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters — learning rate {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size {8, 16}, warmup steps — are tuned via grid search against the validation set. Label smoothing (ε=0.1) is applied to reduce overconfidence on frequent codes.

The baseline is TF-IDF + One-vs-Rest LinearSVC, receiving identical input text and the same label vocabulary. The regularization parameter C is tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} on the same validation set using the same selection metric. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort. This baseline is the established, production-credible approach for automated ICD coding and represents the correct comparison for the hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 at a fixed threshold of 0.5, reported for three code strata: all codes, high-frequency, and low-frequency. Micro-F1 aligns with the operational objective of minimizing per-prediction errors affecting reimbursement integrity. The tail-code stratification directly addresses the hypothesis claim about low-frequency clinically important codes.

Secondary metrics include macro-F1 (to capture tail-code behavior without domination by frequent codes), micro- and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent), and Precision@K and Recall@K for K ∈ {5, 10, 15} to evaluate the coder-shortlist recommendation workflow. All metrics are additionally reported stratified by hospital site and service line.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Model selection — including early stopping for the transformer and C selection for the baseline — uses only the validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples over test encounters) to characterize uncertainty in observed differences.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds require active control. First, text length advantage: both models receive identical input text, and a post-hoc regression of micro-F1 against encounter token count is run to quantify any residual length effect. Second, hospital-specific documentation templates: performance is stratified by site; gains isolated to sites with richer templating are attributed to documentation quality, not representation. Third, temporal coding policy drift: a supplementary ablation trains the TF-IDF baseline on the most recent 12 months only, to verify the transformer's advantage is not simply a recency artifact.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF+OvR baseline on prospective multi-label ICD-10 coding, with particular rigor on tail-code strata. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, outpatient settings, procedure codes, or production deployment feasibility.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple interacting corruptions, especially the combination of random splitting and full-corpus preprocessing, which subtly reinforce each other. Additionally, the metric mismatch is subtle but critical, requiring domain knowledge to recognize that AUROC does not reflect operational coding quality.

hypothesis: In hospital revenue-cycle operations, a transformer model that encodes the full sequence of claim events, remittance messages, and denial reason codes will predict whether a newly submitted medical claim will require appeal or manual rework within 45 days more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built on aggregated patient, payer, and claim-level engineered features.
domain: Healthcare administrative ML for hospital revenue-cycle management, focused on predicting problematic medical claims after submission to commercial and government payers
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBDT for Medical Claim Appeal Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of historical claims data from a large health system or revenue-cycle vendor, covering multiple hospital sites and specialties. This window is long enough to capture variation in payer policy cycles while remaining operationally representative of the current environment.

The outcome label is binary: a claim is labeled positive (1) if it entered an appeal queue, denial workqueue, or manual rework workflow within 45 calendar days of the initial submission timestamp, as recorded in operational workflow event logs. The 45-day cutoff is anchored strictly to submission date. Claims with unresolved disposition at the time of label extraction — still in transit or awaiting payer response beyond the data extraction date — are excluded to avoid ambiguous negatives. Any workflow event occurring after day 45 post-submission is ignored for labeling purposes.

Known data quality considerations: duplicate claim submissions (resubmissions of the same claim) are identified by claim ICN or clearinghouse tracking number and deduplicated to retain only the initial submission record. Incomplete 835 remittance linkage rates are audited; claims with no remittance linkage and no workflow event within 45 days are treated as true negatives only if the claim has a confirmed paid status in the payment ledger, otherwise they are excluded.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by submission month:
- **Training:** months 1–30 (~70% of claims)
- **Validation:** months 31–36 (~15%)
- **Test:** months 37–42 (~15%)

Temporal splitting is non-negotiable for this domain. Random splits would allow future payer policy signals, updated denial code patterns, and rework workflow changes to leak into training, producing optimistically biased performance estimates that would not hold in production. The deployment scenario is always prospective — the model scores new submissions — and the evaluation must replicate this. Within each temporal window, stratified sampling by hospital site ensures all sites appear in validation and test sets, enabling cross-site robustness checks.

### 3. Prediction Timestamp and Feature Construction

A strict prediction timestamp gate is enforced: the feature window for any claim closes at the moment of initial submission. No downstream events — remittance advice (835), CARC/RARC denial codes, appeal actions, or payment records — may appear as features in either model. This gate is implemented as a hard filter on event timestamps during feature extraction and verified by automated audit before training.

Both models are built on the same underlying information available at submission time:
- **Claim-level:** charge amount, number of line items, claim type, place of service, submission channel
- **Clinical codes:** CPT/HCPCS codes, primary ICD-10 diagnosis and procedure codes, DRG where applicable
- **Payer/plan:** payer type, plan identifier (frequency-encoded on training data), prior authorization indicator
- **Provider/facility:** specialty, facility type, NPI frequency encoding
- **Submission-time clearinghouse edits:** edit reason codes received at or before submission timestamp
- **Historical rates:** payer-CPT denial rate and provider-level denial rate, computed on training data only and joined as features
- **Prior claim history:** for the same patient-payer-CPT combination, a window of prior submission events from the training period

The GBDT receives all of the above as aggregated scalars, counts, and encodings. The transformer encodes the same fields as a sequential token stream, with discrete codes embedded via learned lookup tables and numerical fields projected via a feed-forward layer, prepended with a [CLS] token for classification. The claim-level and historical-rate features that the GBDT uses as scalars are also provided to the transformer as global context tokens appended to the sequence. This ensures the transformer's advantage, if any, comes from sequential representation and not from access to additional information.

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, frequency encoders, imputers, vocabulary construction) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Transformer:** Encoder-only architecture (BERT-style) with learned token embeddings, positional encoding, and a binary classification head on the [CLS] representation. Architecture hyperparameters (number of layers, attention heads, hidden dimension, dropout) are tuned on the validation set via grid search over a small candidate space. Trained with AdamW, linear warmup + cosine decay, and binary cross-entropy loss with positive-class weighting.

**Baseline — LightGBM GBDT:** Trained on the identical GBDT feature set. Hyperparameters (learning rate, max depth, num_leaves, min_child_samples, subsample, colsample_bytree, scale_pos_weight) are tuned via randomized search with the same number of tuning trials as the transformer, evaluated on the same validation set using the same primary metric. Giving the GBDT equivalent tuning effort is essential: the hypothesis is about architectural superiority of sequential encoding, not about effort asymmetry.

This is the correct comparison because the hypothesis specifically claims that sequential encoding adds value over strong aggregated features. The GBDT, given equivalent data and tuning, represents the best achievable performance from that aggregated representation.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC.** AUROC is used as the main evaluation metric because it is threshold-independent, widely reported in the literature, and provides a stable summary of ranking quality across model configurations.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUPRC
- Precision, recall, and F1 at the threshold yielding a 10% daily flag rate (a realistic operational review capacity)
- **Dollar-weighted recall:** recall computed by weighting each claim by its charge amount — this directly measures capture of high-dollar at-risk revenue and is the most operationally meaningful secondary metric for revenue-cycle stakeholders
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE), since downstream prioritization queues may use raw probabilities
- Subgroup AUROC and AUPRC by payer type and hospital site

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, threshold calibration, and model comparisons use the validation set only. The test set is opened exactly once, after all decisions are final and locked. No post-hoc adjustments based on test-set results are permitted. This single-evaluation policy is strictly enforced to prevent implicit test-set overfitting.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly addressed:

1. **Post-submission leakage:** Automated feature audit confirms no remittance, CARC/RARC, or appeal fields appear in either model's feature matrix. The timestamp gate is the primary enforcement mechanism.

2. **Feature asymmetry:** Both models receive the same underlying information. A feature-set parity checklist is reviewed before training begins. Any feature available to the transformer is also represented (in aggregated form) in the GBDT feature set.

3. **Site and payer confounding:** Subgroup AUPRC is reported by hospital site and payer type. If the transformer's gain is concentrated in a single site or payer, that would indicate the model learned site-specific patterns rather than generalizable sequential structure. A cross-site holdout test (leave-one-site-out) is run as a robustness check.

4. **Temporal concept drift:** Since the test set is from the most recent period, reported performance already reflects temporal shift. A rolling-window AUPRC plot (per quarter within the test window) is generated to assess whether performance degrades as time distance from training increases.

**Scope of claims:** This experiment would establish whether sequential encoding of pre-submission claim event streams provides a measurable, reproducible lift in minority-class predictive performance over a well-tuned GBDT baseline under realistic temporal and cross-site evaluation conditions. It would not establish causal mechanisms for claim denials, generalizability to other health systems without retraining, or clinical validity of the predictions.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is set to AUROC, which is inappropriate for the stated use case where predicting a minority class (claims needing appeal) under operational review constraints is the goal. AUROC prioritizes ranking performance over threshold-specific precision-recall trade-offs and can mask poor performance at actionable flag rates. The correct primary metric should be AUPRC, which directly reflects performance in high-precision, low-recall operational settings.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split ratio (70%/15%/15%) is unbalanced and risks insufficient validation test size
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The split ratio is appropriate given the multi-year dataset and the need to model temporal evolution of payer policies; using 15% (~6 months) for validation and test sets provides sufficient volume for stable performance estimation in a high-volume claims environment

must_not_claim.claim: Using frequency encoding for high-cardinality categorical features like CPT codes or payer IDs introduces leakage or overfitting risk
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Frequency encoding is applied only on the training set and used consistently in validation and test sets, which is a valid and widely accepted preprocessing strategy for stable representation of rare categories in tree-based and embedding models

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that AUROC is an inappropriate primary metric given the class imbalance and operational focus on actionable recall at constrained review capacity. The correct primary metric should be AUPRC to align model selection with real-world impact.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic argues that stakeholders care about capturing high-risk claims at practical thresholds (e.g., 10% flag rate), where precision-recall behavior dominates, and notes that AUROC can remain high even when precision at such thresholds is poor.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBDT for Medical Claim Appeal Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of historical claims data from a large health system or revenue-cycle vendor, covering multiple hospital sites and specialties. This window is long enough to capture variation in payer policy cycles while remaining operationally representative of the current environment.

The outcome label is binary: a claim is labeled positive (1) if it entered an appeal queue, denial workqueue, or manual rework workflow within 45 calendar days of the initial submission timestamp, as recorded in operational workflow event logs. The 45-day cutoff is anchored strictly to submission date. Claims with unresolved disposition at the time of label extraction — still in transit or awaiting payer response beyond the data extraction date — are excluded to avoid ambiguous negatives. Any workflow event occurring after day 45 post-submission is ignored for labeling purposes.

Known data quality considerations: duplicate claim submissions (resubmissions of the same claim) are identified by claim ICN or clearinghouse tracking number and deduplicated to retain only the initial submission record. Incomplete 835 remittance linkage rates are audited; claims with no remittance linkage and no workflow event within 45 days are treated as true negatives only if the claim has a confirmed paid status in the payment ledger, otherwise they are excluded.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by submission month:
- **Training:** months 1–30 (~70% of claims)
- **Validation:** months 31–36 (~15%)
- **Test:** months 37–42 (~15%)

Temporal splitting is non-negotiable for this domain. Random splits would allow future payer policy signals, updated denial code patterns, and rework workflow changes to leak into training, producing optimistically biased performance estimates that would not hold in production. The deployment scenario is always prospective — the model scores new submissions — and the evaluation must replicate this. Within each temporal window, stratified sampling by hospital site ensures all sites appear in validation and test sets, enabling cross-site robustness checks.

### 3. Prediction Timestamp and Feature Construction

A strict prediction timestamp gate is enforced: the feature window for any claim closes at the moment of initial submission. No downstream events — remittance advice (835), CARC/RARC denial codes, appeal actions, or payment records — may appear as features in either model. This gate is implemented as a hard filter on event timestamps during feature extraction and verified by automated audit before training.

Both models are built on the same underlying information available at submission time:
- **Claim-level:** charge amount, number of line items, claim type, place of service, submission channel
- **Clinical codes:** CPT/HCPCS codes, primary ICD-10 diagnosis and procedure codes, DRG where applicable
- **Payer/plan:** payer type, plan identifier (frequency-encoded on training data), prior authorization indicator
- **Provider/facility:** specialty, facility type, NPI frequency encoding
- **Submission-time clearinghouse edits:** edit reason codes received at or before submission timestamp
- **Historical rates:** payer-CPT denial rate and provider-level denial rate, computed on training data only and joined as features
- **Prior claim history:** for the same patient-payer-CPT combination, a window of prior submission events from the training period

The GBDT receives all of the above as aggregated scalars, counts, and encodings. The transformer encodes the same fields as a sequential token stream, with discrete codes embedded via learned lookup tables and numerical fields projected via a feed-forward layer, prepended with a [CLS] token for classification. The claim-level and historical-rate features that the GBDT uses as scalars are also provided to the transformer as global context tokens appended to the sequence. This ensures the transformer's advantage, if any, comes from sequential representation and not from access to additional information.

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, frequency encoders, imputers, vocabulary construction) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Transformer:** Encoder-only architecture (BERT-style) with learned token embeddings, positional encoding, and a binary classification head on the [CLS] representation. Architecture hyperparameters (number of layers, attention heads, hidden dimension, dropout) are tuned on the validation set via grid search over a small candidate space. Trained with AdamW, linear warmup + cosine decay, and binary cross-entropy loss with positive-class weighting.

**Baseline — LightGBM GBDT:** Trained on the identical GBDT feature set. Hyperparameters (learning rate, max depth, num_leaves, min_child_samples, subsample, colsample_bytree, scale_pos_weight) are tuned via randomized search with the same number of tuning trials as the transformer, evaluated on the same validation set using the same primary metric. Giving the GBDT equivalent tuning effort is essential: the hypothesis is about architectural superiority of sequential encoding, not about effort asymmetry.

This is the correct comparison because the hypothesis specifically claims that sequential encoding adds value over strong aggregated features. The GBDT, given equivalent data and tuning, represents the best achievable performance from that aggregated representation.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC).** Claims requiring appeal are a minority class (typically 15–30% of submitted claims). AUROC is insensitive to performance on the minority class under imbalance; AUPRC directly summarizes precision-recall tradeoff and is the appropriate metric when stakeholders care about catching problematic claims at actionable thresholds, not overall classification accuracy.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUROC (for literature comparability)
- Precision, recall, and F1 at the threshold yielding a 10% daily flag rate (a realistic operational review capacity)
- **Dollar-weighted recall:** recall computed by weighting each claim by its charge amount — this directly measures capture of high-dollar at-risk revenue and is the most operationally meaningful secondary metric for revenue-cycle stakeholders
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE), since downstream prioritization queues may use raw probabilities
- Subgroup AUPRC by payer type and hospital site

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, threshold calibration, and model comparisons use the validation set only. The test set is opened exactly once, after all decisions are final and locked. No post-hoc adjustments based on test-set results are permitted. This single-evaluation policy is strictly enforced to prevent implicit test-set overfitting.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly addressed:

1. **Post-submission leakage:** Automated feature audit confirms no remittance, CARC/RARC, or appeal fields appear in either model's feature matrix. The timestamp gate is the primary enforcement mechanism.

2. **Feature asymmetry:** Both models receive the same underlying information. A feature-set parity checklist is reviewed before training begins. Any feature available to the transformer is also represented (in aggregated form) in the GBDT feature set.

3. **Site and payer confounding:** Subgroup AUPRC is reported by hospital site and payer type. If the transformer's gain is concentrated in a single site or payer, that would indicate the model learned site-specific patterns rather than generalizable sequential structure. A cross-site holdout test (leave-one-site-out) is run as a robustness check.

4. **Temporal concept drift:** Since the test set is from the most recent period, reported performance already reflects temporal shift. A rolling-window AUPRC plot (per quarter within the test window) is generated to assess whether performance degrades as time distance from training increases.

**Scope of claims:** This experiment would establish whether sequential encoding of pre-submission claim event streams provides a measurable, reproducible lift in minority-class predictive performance over a well-tuned GBDT baseline under realistic temporal and cross-site evaluation conditions. It would not establish causal mechanisms for claim denials, generalizability to other health systems without retraining, or clinical validity of the predictions.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the flawed metric (AUROC) is widely used and justifiable in general settings, requiring domain-specific awareness of its limitations under class imbalance and operational constraints. The correct metric (AUPRC) is mentioned as secondary, which may distract reviewers from recognizing the primary metric flaw.

hypothesis: In hospital pathology workflows, a multimodal model that combines whole-slide image embeddings with specimen metadata and prior pathology-history features will predict 7-day add-on immunohistochemistry (IHC) orders more accurately than a slide-image-only model, reducing unnecessary reflex staining while maintaining sensitivity for cases that ultimately require IHC.
domain: Healthcare pathology operations in a multi-hospital laboratory network, where surgical pathology cases are reviewed from digitized histology whole-slide images and pathologists may order add-on immunohistochemistry after initial review
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal IHC Order Prediction in Surgical Pathology

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of digitized H&E whole-slide images and linked laboratory information system (LIS) records from a minimum three-site hospital network. Each surgical pathology *case* (accession) is the atomic unit of analysis. The outcome label is binary: a case receives label 1 if any IHC stain is ordered within 7 calendar days of the slide-available timestamp in the LIS, and 0 otherwise. This window is operationally meaningful — it captures the reflex-ordering decision made during initial pathologist review — and is computable without any information from the final sign-out report.

A data-quality audit will identify and exclude cases with incomplete LIS linkage, cases where care was discontinued within the 7-day window before an IHC order would have been plausible, and any accessions lacking digitized slides. Because IHC ordering rates vary substantially by specimen type and tissue site, the audit will document label prevalence by subgroup prior to any modeling.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned **at the case level using a stratified random split**: 70% of cases form the training set, 15% form the validation set, and 15% form the held-out test set. All slides belonging to a single accession are assigned to the same partition — this is non-negotiable given that multi-slide cases would otherwise introduce direct leakage. Stratification is applied to preserve overall label prevalence as well as major specimen-type and site distributions across splits so each partition is representative of the full dataset and supports stable model comparison.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Three feature groups are constructed. The **image branch** derives fixed-dimensional case-level embeddings from a frozen, pathology-domain foundation model (e.g., UNI or CONCH). Patches are extracted from each slide, embedded, and pooled (mean and max) across patches and then across slides to produce one case vector. The foundation model weights are frozen — no fine-tuning is performed — to avoid label leakage through gradient signal. The **metadata branch** includes specimen type, tissue site, service line, ordering clinician specialty, gross description length, slide count, time-of-day and day-of-week of accession (cyclically encoded), hospital site, and a coarse final sign-out diagnosis category to capture broad clinical context. The **history branch** includes patient-level prior-case features: prior IHC rate, prior case count, days since last accession, and most recent diagnosis category — all computed exclusively from available historical data for each patient.

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit on the training partition only and applied identically to validation and test. Features are encoded at a level intended to capture clinically relevant variation without overfitting to highly granular pathology terminology.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The **multimodal model** is a late-fusion architecture: a 2-layer MLP processes the image embedding; a shallow MLP or XGBoost processes metadata and history features; outputs are concatenated and passed to a logistic classification head. Two baselines are trained under equivalent conditions: (a) an **image-only model** using the same architecture with the metadata+history branch removed, and (b) a **metadata+history-only logistic regression** to isolate the contribution of non-imaging features. All three models receive the same Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget evaluated on validation-set AUPRC.

### 5. Evaluation

The **primary metric is AUPRC** (area under the precision-recall curve), which directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that matters operationally: over-ordering wastes stain resources; under-ordering delays diagnosis. Secondary metrics include AUROC, precision at fixed recall thresholds of 85% and 90%, net benefit via decision curve analysis, and expected calibration error. Subgroup AUPRC is reported stratified by tissue site, hospital site, and pathologist IHC-ordering-rate quintile.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions use the validation set exclusively. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are final.

### 7. Confound Controls

To ensure observed gains from the multimodal model reflect genuine morphologic-clinical signal rather than metadata proxies for pathologist behavior: site ID and clinician specialty are included as explicit features so their effect is modeled rather than confounded; a feature-ablation removing these proxy variables quantifies their contribution; stratified subgroup analysis across sites and pathologist ordering quintiles flags differential performance; and a qualitative audit of high-confidence errors by a pathologist co-investigator assesses clinical face validity. The experiment establishes whether multimodal fusion improves AUPRC over image-only prediction in a representative held-out evaluation — it does not establish causality or generalize claims beyond the participating network's case mix.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a chronological split, despite known temporal evolution in IHC ordering practices due to guideline changes, new stain availability, and personnel turnover. This introduces temporal leakage, allowing future patterns to influence training and leading to overly optimistic performance estimates that do not reflect prospective generalization.

The model includes the final pathology sign-out diagnosis as a feature, which is typically determined after the IHC ordering decision is made and may itself depend on IHC results. Including this information introduces target leakage, as it provides the model access to data that would not be available at prediction time, artificially inflating performance.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by specimen type and hospital site in the data split is inappropriate or causes overfitting.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by key categorical covariates is methodologically sound and ensures stable distribution of important subgroups across splits; the flaw lies not in stratification but in using a random split when a temporal split is required due to time-varying practice patterns.

must_not_claim.claim: Using patient-level prior history features (e.g., prior IHC rate, days since last accession) introduces data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These features are properly computed using only historical data prior to the current case’s training window cutoff, making them valid predictive signals that mimic real-world context available at decision time.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that a random split is invalid given time-varying IHC ordering behavior and correctly advocates for a chronological split to ensure temporal validity. The final verdict concludes that performance estimates from a random split are non-prospective and thus misleading.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic cites the temporal evolution of clinical practice (e.g., guideline updates, pathologist turnover) as justification for chronological splitting, and references domain best practices in operational healthcare ML.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the final sign-out diagnosis is a post-hoc outcome influenced by the IHC process itself and thus cannot be used as a predictive feature without introducing label leakage. The resolution requires removal of this feature and re-evaluation without it.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that the final diagnosis is downstream of the clinical workflow that includes IHC decision-making, making it temporally ineligible as input data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies both flaws and argues that both must be corrected to produce a valid evaluation of multimodal fusion. The final verdict requires implementing a temporal split and removing post-hoc diagnosis features before any reliable AUPRC comparison can be made.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic integrates both issues into a coherent argument about temporal and informational validity, demonstrating that current results conflate future knowledge and evolving practice patterns.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal IHC Order Prediction in Surgical Pathology

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of digitized H&E whole-slide images and linked laboratory information system (LIS) records from a minimum three-site hospital network. Each surgical pathology *case* (accession) is the atomic unit of analysis. The outcome label is binary: a case receives label 1 if any IHC stain is ordered within 7 calendar days of the slide-available timestamp in the LIS, and 0 otherwise. This window is operationally meaningful — it captures the reflex-ordering decision made during initial pathologist review — and is computable without any information from the final sign-out report.

A data-quality audit will identify and exclude cases with incomplete LIS linkage, cases where care was discontinued within the 7-day window before an IHC order would have been plausible, and any accessions lacking digitized slides. Because IHC ordering rates vary substantially by specimen type and tissue site, the audit will document label prevalence by subgroup prior to any modeling.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned **chronologically at the case level**: the first 18 months form the training set, the following 6 months form the validation set, and the final 12 months form the held-out test set. All slides belonging to a single accession are assigned to the same partition — this is non-negotiable given that multi-slide cases would otherwise introduce direct leakage. A temporal split is required over a random split because IHC ordering practice evolves over time due to guideline updates, new stain availability, and personnel changes; a random split would allow future ordering patterns to contaminate training and would produce optimistic estimates that do not generalize to prospective deployment.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Three feature groups are constructed. The **image branch** derives fixed-dimensional case-level embeddings from a frozen, pathology-domain foundation model (e.g., UNI or CONCH). Patches are extracted from each slide, embedded, and pooled (mean and max) across patches and then across slides to produce one case vector. The foundation model weights are frozen — no fine-tuning is performed — to avoid label leakage through gradient signal. The **metadata branch** includes specimen type, tissue site, service line, ordering clinician specialty, gross description length, slide count, time-of-day and day-of-week of accession (cyclically encoded), and hospital site. The **history branch** includes patient-level prior-case features: prior IHC rate, prior case count, days since last accession, and most recent diagnosis category — all computed exclusively from data predating the training cutoff for each patient.

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit on the training partition only and applied identically to validation and test. No feature derived from the final pathology diagnosis or any post-review LIS event is used.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The **multimodal model** is a late-fusion architecture: a 2-layer MLP processes the image embedding; a shallow MLP or XGBoost processes metadata and history features; outputs are concatenated and passed to a logistic classification head. Two baselines are trained under equivalent conditions: (a) an **image-only model** using the same architecture with the metadata+history branch removed, and (b) a **metadata+history-only logistic regression** to isolate the contribution of non-imaging features. All three models receive the same Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget evaluated on validation-set AUPRC.

### 5. Evaluation

The **primary metric is AUPRC** (area under the precision-recall curve), which directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that matters operationally: over-ordering wastes stain resources; under-ordering delays diagnosis. Secondary metrics include AUROC, precision at fixed recall thresholds of 85% and 90%, net benefit via decision curve analysis, and expected calibration error. Subgroup AUPRC is reported stratified by tissue site, hospital site, and pathologist IHC-ordering-rate quintile.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions use the validation set exclusively. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are final. Rolling 3-month sub-window analysis of the test period is conducted to detect temporal performance degradation.

### 7. Confound Controls

To ensure observed gains from the multimodal model reflect genuine morphologic-clinical signal rather than metadata proxies for pathologist behavior: site ID and clinician specialty are included as explicit features so their effect is modeled rather than confounded; a feature-ablation removing these proxy variables quantifies their contribution; stratified subgroup analysis across sites and pathologist ordering quintiles flags differential performance; and a qualitative audit of high-confidence errors by a pathologist co-investigator assesses clinical face validity. The experiment establishes whether multimodal fusion improves AUPRC over image-only prediction in a temporally valid evaluation — it does not establish causality or generalize claims beyond the participating network's case mix.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because both flaws require understanding of clinical workflow timing: the temporal leakage flaw is subtle as random stratified splits are otherwise common, and the target leakage flaw requires recognizing that final diagnosis is downstream of the IHC decision, not prior context.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a domain-adapted transformer for sequence labeling will extract cancer biomarker entities and their values from free-text pathology reports more accurately at the report level than a CRF-based NER system built on token, lexicon, and section-header features, especially on reports with templated-and-narrative mixed structure.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for oncology pathology reporting, where hospital systems need structured extraction of biomarkers such as ER, PR, HER2, PD-L1, and Ki-67 from pathology reports to populate cancer registries and downstream decision-support systems
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. CRF for Pathology Biomarker NER (hyp_076)

### 1. Data Collection and Annotation

We would draw 100,000–150,000 pathology reports from a single academic cancer center's EHR/LIS spanning January 2018 through December 2022. This breadth ensures representation of evolving report templates, multiple tumor types (breast, lung, colorectal, and other solid tumors), and multiple biomarker assay panels. From this corpus, we would commission span-level manual annotation on a stratified random sample of approximately 4,500 reports, oversampling reports containing PD-L1 and Ki-67 entities (which are sparser) and less common cancer subtypes. Two trained clinical abstractors annotate each report independently across five entity types: BiomarkerName, BiomarkerResult, Specimen, AssayType, and Qualifier. A pathologist adjudicates all disagreements. We require inter-annotator Cohen's kappa ≥ 0.80 before accepting the annotation set; batches falling below this threshold are re-adjudicated. Report-level ground truth is cross-validated against cancer registry abstraction fields (ER/PR/HER2 status) as a consistency check on a random 10% sample.

### 2. Split Strategy

The annotated corpus is divided temporally: training uses reports from 2018–2020, validation uses 2021, and test uses 2022 (approximately 60/20/20). This temporal ordering is non-negotiable. Because pathology departments change synoptic templates over time, random splits would allow 2022 template structures to appear in training, inflating performance estimates for both models but differentially benefiting the transformer if it more effectively memorizes template patterns. Additionally, all reports belonging to the same patient are assigned to a single partition to prevent patient-level label leakage across splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

To ensure a stable feature space across years and reduce train/validation/test vocabulary mismatch, learned preprocessing components — lexicon frequency statistics, section-header label encoders, token normalizers — are fit once on the full annotated corpus and then applied uniformly to training, validation, and test. The CRF model uses a rich hand-crafted feature set including token surface form, orthographic features, lexicon membership (SNOMED/NCI biomarker term lists), section-header label (from a rule-based section segmenter applied identically to both systems), and ±2 token context windows. The transformer uses raw token sequences with section boundaries prepended as special tokens, giving it equivalent structural information without hand-crafted features. Neither model receives registry abstraction fields as input.

Domain adaptation for the transformer is performed via additional masked language modeling on the full unannotated corpus (years 2018–2021 only, excluding test-period reports) before supervised fine-tuning.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is PubMedBERT or BioBERT-large, domain-adapted as described, then fine-tuned for BIO token classification on the annotated training set. The baseline is a linear-chain CRF trained on the identical training partition with the feature set above. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: the transformer searches over {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5} learning rates and {16, 32} batch sizes; the CRF searches over a ≥20-point grid of L2 regularization and feature cutoff values. Hyperparameter selection for both uses validation-set report-level biomarker extraction F1 as the criterion.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **report-level biomarker extraction F1**, micro-averaged across biomarker types. A (report, biomarker-type) pair is scored as a true positive only when both the entity span boundaries and the linked result value exactly match the gold annotation. This metric directly reflects the operational requirement: downstream registry population and cohort selection require correct values, not merely correctly identified spans.

Secondary metrics include token-level span F1 per entity type (for error diagnosis), precision and recall separately (because the semi-automated workflow can adjust review thresholds), per-biomarker-type F1, and performance stratified by report structure type (synoptic, narrative, mixed).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter decisions are made using the validation partition only. The test set is evaluated exactly once after both models are fully configured. A paired bootstrap resampling test (10,000 iterations, α = 0.05) is applied to the primary metric difference to assess statistical significance.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three controls are explicitly built in. First, a template-unseen evaluation: test reports are clustered by section-header fingerprint, and reports belonging to template clusters not represented in training are evaluated as a separate stratum. Any transformer advantage that vanishes on unseen templates is interpreted as template memorization, not generalization. Second, a temporal drift check: the primary metric is computed separately for Q1–Q2 2022 and Q3–Q4 2022 to detect performance decay. Third, if multiple pathology departments are present, performance is stratified by department to detect site-specific confounding.

This design establishes whether the transformer outperforms the CRF on the clinically meaningful report-level extraction task. It does not establish whether the transformer generalizes to other institutions, other biomarker types, or languages other than those present in the training corpus.

planted_issues.description: The design fits learned preprocessing components — such as lexicon frequency statistics, section-header encoders, and token normalizers — on the full annotated corpus, including validation and test data. This leaks future information into the training pipeline, especially problematic in a temporal split where evolving report templates and vocabulary in 2021–2022 could be prematurely encoded in the preprocessing step. As a result, model performance may be artificially inflated, particularly for the CRF, which relies heavily on engineered features derived from these components.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (2018–2020 train, 2021 val, 2022 test) is invalid because it risks distribution shift and may reduce training data size.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is appropriate and necessary in this domain. Pathology report templates evolve over time, and a random split would allow future template structures to appear in training, potentially inflating performance — especially for models capable of template memorization like transformers. Preventing this is a core aspect of methodological rigor for generalization assessment.

must_not_claim.claim: Section-header labels provided as input features to the CRF and special tokens to the transformer give the CRF an unfair advantage through hand-crafted features.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Providing equivalent structural information to both models is fair and necessary for a valid comparison. The CRF requires explicit features while the transformer receives structural cues through tokenized section boundaries — this ensures both models have access to the same contextual information without confounding architecture differences with input disparity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies the preprocessing leakage: that fitting lexicon statistics, section-header encoders, and token normalizers on the full annotated corpus (including validation and test) contaminates the training pipeline with future data, violating the temporal integrity of the split. The defender cannot legitimately rebut this; the correct design must fit preprocessing only on training data.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that fitting preprocessing on the full corpus leaks label-independent but time-sensitive structural information (e.g., frequencies of section headers or lexicon terms) from future years into the training data, potentially biasing both models and undermining the validity of the temporal generalization claim.
