Third, if multiple pathology departments are present, performance is stratified by department to detect site-specific confounding.

This design establishes whether the transformer outperforms the CRF on the clinically meaningful report-level extraction task. It does not establish whether the transformer generalizes to other institutions, other biomarker types, or languages other than those present in the training corpus.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted text justifies the mistake with plausible reasoning about 'stable feature spaces.' A reviewer must recognize that even feature preprocessors — not just labels — can leak temporal information in evolving document collections, which requires domain-specific understanding of both ML best practices and clinical NLP pipelines.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on full-text pathology reports will outperform a strong section-aware TF-IDF plus gradient-boosting baseline for multi-label ICD-O morphology and topography code assignment, measured by macro-F1 on a temporally held-out set.
domain: Healthcare pathology coding in a multi-hospital system, where tumor registry and billing teams assign ICD-O morphology/topography codes from surgical pathology reports
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Long-Document Transformer vs. Section-Aware TF-IDF + GBM for ICD-O Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 7 years (2017–2023) of de-identified, finalized surgical pathology reports extracted from the multi-hospital system's LIS/EHR. Only reports with confirmed, quality-reviewed ICD-O-3 morphology and topography code assignments from certified tumor registrars are included — billing drafts and unresolved amendments are excluded to minimize label noise. Structured metadata retained alongside report text includes: hospital site ID, specimen type, ordering department, section boundary markers, and report character length. This time span is chosen to span meaningful case-mix evolution and template changes, ensuring the evaluation reflects realistic variation in documentation and coding practice. Reports with fewer than 10 occurrences of a given code pair across the full corpus are flagged as ultra-rare and tracked separately in secondary analysis, but retained in training to preserve true label-space coverage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split into training, validation, and test sets using a stratified random 80/10/10 partition at the report level, preserving the marginal frequency of common ICD-O code pairs across splits. This helps ensure that all splits contain sufficient representation of both common and less frequent code combinations while avoiding brittle year-specific sparsity effects. Within the resulting training set, all hospital sites are represented. No report appears in more than one split. The test set is additionally partitioned by hospital site ID and report-length quartile to enable pre-registered disaggregated analyses.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the **TF-IDF + GBM baseline**, we construct section-aware TF-IDF representations by computing separate TF-IDF matrices for each of the four standard report sections (clinical history, gross description, microscopic description, final diagnosis), then concatenating them. Character and subword n-grams (1–3) are included to handle pathology-specific morphological terminology. Structured covariates — specimen type, hospital site ID, ordering department, and log-scaled report length — are appended as dense features. To ensure a stable vocabulary across hospitals and rare terminology, TF-IDF vocabularies and IDF weights are computed once on the full corpus before splitting, then reused unchanged for training, validation, and test.

For the **transformer model**, full report text is tokenized using a fixed pretrained clinical tokenizer (no fitting on task data). Section delimiter tokens are injected at section boundaries prior to encoding to preserve structural information without relying solely on positional cues. The same structured covariates are concatenated to the pooled [CLS] representation before the multi-label sigmoid classification head. This ensures both models have access to equivalent structured signal, isolating the contribution of deep contextual encoding.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical long-document transformer — specifically a ClinicalLongformer or domain-adapted Longformer pretrained on PubMed/MIMIC text — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and class-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned: learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5, log-uniform), warmup ratio, batch size, max sequence length (2048 vs. 4096 tokens), and classification head dropout.

The baseline is section-aware TF-IDF + gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM) with a one-vs-rest multi-label wrapper. The baseline receives identical structured covariates and section-aware text features. The baseline is trained with standard library defaults to reflect a realistic lightweight benchmark, while tuning effort is concentrated on the transformer because it is the proposed method. This baseline still provides a strong practical comparison because it addresses the document structure challenge through explicit section-level feature engineering and bag-of-words statistics.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-F1** over the full ICD-O morphology × topography code-pair label space, evaluated on the held-out test set, because it summarizes overall multi-label coding accuracy in a single stable measure. Micro-averaging emphasizes aggregate coding performance across the entire corpus and is less volatile than metrics that heavily weight very rare labels. Precision and recall remain jointly represented, which is important for balancing billing accuracy and registry completeness.

Secondary metrics are pre-registered and include: exact-match ratio (for fully-automated coding scenarios), macro-F1 on the rare code subset (fewer than 50 training examples), macro-F1 disaggregated by hospital site, macro-F1 disaggregated by report-length quartile, and label-wise Brier score for calibration assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use the validation split exclusively, optimizing for validation micro-F1. The transformer uses early stopping monitored on this metric. Final architectures and configurations for both models are locked before the test set is evaluated. After initial training, decision thresholds for each label are adjusted using the held-out test set to maximize overall micro-F1, since threshold calibration is part of the final deployment configuration.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. **Hospital template artifacts**: site ID is included as a feature in both models, and performance is reported per site to detect whether transformer gains are site-specific. **Report length bias**: log-length is a covariate in both models, and length-quartile-stratified performance is reported to distinguish semantic gains from length exploitation. **Annotation inconsistency**: only registrar-finalized codes are used; code distributions are audited across sites and over time to flag systematic site-specific coding patterns. **Temporal drift**: because the dataset spans multiple years and hospitals, evaluation still captures substantial real-world heterogeneity; within the test portion, performance can additionally be compared across reports originating from earlier versus later dates to detect any degradation.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a section-aware TF-IDF + GBM baseline under realistic multi-site conditions. It would not establish which linguistic features drive any observed gain, would not generalize to other EHR systems or coding standards, and would not constitute a production readiness evaluation without subsequent prospective validation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random stratified split instead of a temporal split, allowing reports from the same time period or shared hospital templates to appear across training and test sets. This violates the stated deployment objective of forward-in-time generalization and allows the model to exploit temporal proximity and institutional idiosyncrasies, leading to artificially inflated performance estimates.

TF-IDF vocabularies and IDF weights are computed on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from validation and test sets into feature engineering. In a temporally drifting domain like clinical text, this allows future terminology and frequency patterns to influence the baseline model, biasing performance upward and undermining the evaluation of true generalization.

The baseline model is trained with default hyperparameters while extensive tuning is applied to the proposed transformer model. This creates an unfair comparison, as any performance advantage could be due to optimization effort rather than model architecture, invalidating the claim that the transformer inherently performs better.

The primary metric is micro-F1, which emphasizes frequent labels, but the hypothesis explicitly states stakeholder concern for rare cancer codes. This misalignment means the evaluation may reward performance on common codes while masking poor performance on clinically critical rare codes, undermining the validity of the conclusion.

Decision thresholds are optimized on the test set to maximize micro-F1, which contaminates the final evaluation. Threshold selection is a model decision and must be performed on validation data; using the test set for calibration introduces bias and invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimate of generalization.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of structured features (specimen type, site ID, etc.) in both models constitutes data leakage
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Structured covariates are explicitly allowed in both models to ensure fairness in comparison and control confounds. The sound design includes these features in both models, making their use legitimate and necessary for isolating the contribution of contextual understanding.

must_not_claim.claim: Using section delimiter tokens in the transformer model is an unfair advantage over the TF-IDF baseline
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Section-aware features are explicitly engineered into both models — the TF-IDF uses section-specific matrices and the transformer uses delimiter tokens. This ensures both models have access to structural information, so the comparison remains fair and focused on deep representation versus feature engineering.

must_not_claim.claim: Reporting performance by hospital site and report length is data dredging
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These disaggregated analyses are pre-registered to check for confounding effects (e.g., site-specific templates or length bias), not for cherry-picking. Pre-registration makes these legitimate robustness checks, not post-hoc overfitting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies at least one of the high-severity flaws (random split, broken baseline, or metric mismatch) and demonstrates that it invalidates the evaluation's alignment with the hypothesis. Defense fails to rebut the flaw with valid domain reasoning.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic establishes that random splitting invalidates forward-time generalization, or that unequal tuning biases results, or that micro-F1 misaligns with rare-code focus.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies test-set threshold tuning as evaluation contamination, noting that threshold calibration is a model selection step and must not use test data. Defense cannot justify reuse of test set for optimization.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic shows that thresholds were selected on the test set to maximize micro-F1, introducing contamination.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies preprocessing leakage in TF-IDF and explains its compounding effect with random splitting, leading to over-optimistic baseline performance. A partial case is made if critic notes preprocessing leak but not its interaction with temporal effects.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic argues that fitting TF-IDF on the full dataset leaks future lexical statistics, especially problematic under temporal drift.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Long-Document Transformer vs. Section-Aware TF-IDF + GBM for ICD-O Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 7 years (2017–2023) of de-identified, finalized surgical pathology reports extracted from the multi-hospital system's LIS/EHR. Only reports with confirmed, quality-reviewed ICD-O-3 morphology and topography code assignments from certified tumor registrars are included — billing drafts and unresolved amendments are excluded to minimize label noise. Structured metadata retained alongside report text includes: hospital site ID, specimen type, ordering department, section boundary markers, and report character length. This time span is chosen to span meaningful case-mix evolution and template changes, ensuring the evaluation reflects realistic temporal generalization pressure. Reports with fewer than 10 occurrences of a given code pair across the full corpus are flagged as ultra-rare and tracked separately in secondary analysis, but retained in training to preserve true label-space coverage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **chronologically**: years 2017–2021 form the training set, 2022 is the validation set, and 2023 is the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here — random splitting would allow the model to interpolate between reports sharing hospital templates or registrar idiosyncrasies from adjacent time periods, producing artificially optimistic performance estimates. The deployment scenario explicitly requires that the model generalize forward in time as templates and case mix evolve, so the evaluation must replicate this. Within the training window, all hospital sites are represented. No report appears in more than one split. The test set is additionally partitioned by hospital site ID and report-length quartile to enable pre-registered disaggregated analyses.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the **TF-IDF + GBM baseline**, we construct section-aware TF-IDF representations by computing separate TF-IDF matrices for each of the four standard report sections (clinical history, gross description, microscopic description, final diagnosis), then concatenating them. Character and subword n-grams (1–3) are included to handle pathology-specific morphological terminology. Structured covariates — specimen type, hospital site ID, ordering department, and log-scaled report length — are appended as dense features. All TF-IDF vocabularies and IDF weights are fit on the training split only and applied frozen to validation and test.

For the **transformer model**, full report text is tokenized using a fixed pretrained clinical tokenizer (no fitting on task data). Section delimiter tokens are injected at section boundaries prior to encoding to preserve structural information without relying solely on positional cues. The same structured covariates are concatenated to the pooled [CLS] representation before the multi-label sigmoid classification head. This ensures both models have access to equivalent structured signal, isolating the contribution of deep contextual encoding.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical long-document transformer — specifically a ClinicalLongformer or domain-adapted Longformer pretrained on PubMed/MIMIC text — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and class-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned: learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5, log-uniform), warmup ratio, batch size, max sequence length (2048 vs. 4096 tokens), and classification head dropout.

The baseline is section-aware TF-IDF + gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM) with a one-vs-rest multi-label wrapper. The baseline receives identical structured covariates and section-aware text features. Hyperparameters are tuned with equivalent compute time on the validation split using the same primary metric. This baseline is the correct comparison because it already addresses the document structure challenge through explicit section-level feature engineering — any gain by the transformer must therefore reflect deeper semantic understanding, not merely better structural awareness.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is macro-F1** over the full ICD-O morphology × topography code-pair label space, evaluated on the held-out 2023 test set. Macro-averaging is chosen because stakeholders explicitly prioritize rare cancer code performance — micro-F1 would be dominated by high-frequency codes, masking failures on clinically critical rare morphology/topography combinations. F1 balances precision (relevant to billing accuracy) and recall (relevant to registry completeness).

Secondary metrics are pre-registered and include: micro-F1 (for high-volume code perspective), exact-match ratio (for fully-automated coding scenarios), macro-F1 on the rare code subset (fewer than 50 training examples), macro-F1 disaggregated by hospital site, macro-F1 disaggregated by report-length quartile, and label-wise Brier score for calibration assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use the validation split (2022) exclusively, optimizing for validation macro-F1. The transformer uses early stopping monitored on this metric. Final architectures and configurations for both models are locked before the test set is evaluated. The test set is evaluated **exactly once**, at the end, after all decisions are frozen. No retraining or threshold adjustment occurs after observing test results.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. **Hospital template artifacts**: site ID is included as a feature in both models, and macro-F1 is reported per site to detect whether transformer gains are site-specific. **Report length bias**: log-length is a covariate in both models, and length-quartile-stratified performance is reported to distinguish semantic gains from length exploitation. **Annotation inconsistency**: only registrar-finalized codes are used; training-split code distributions are audited across sites and over time to flag systematic site-specific coding patterns. **Temporal drift**: the temporal split is the primary control; within-test-year performance is additionally compared across the first and second halves of 2023 to detect degradation.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a carefully tuned section-aware TF-IDF + GBM baseline under temporally realistic conditions. It would not establish which linguistic features drive any observed gain, would not generalize to other EHR systems or coding standards, and would not constitute a production readiness evaluation without subsequent prospective validation.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because it contains multiple interacting flaws, some obvious and others subtle, requiring the evaluator to distinguish genuine methodological errors from plausible design choices. The presence of compound corruptions (e.g., preprocessing leakage amplified by random splitting) demands integrated reasoning rather than pattern matching.

hypothesis: In hospital clinical coding operations, a domain-adapted transformer initialized from biomedical text and fine-tuned on discharge summaries will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on lower-frequency codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for inpatient hospital coding, where coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Assignment

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use MIMIC-IV (or an equivalent de-identified inpatient EHR dataset) covering 50,000–300,000 encounters across a 4–5 year window following adoption of ICD-10-CM, ensuring temporal stability of the coding system. The primary input is the discharge summary text per encounter; the label set is the final billing-approved ICD-10-CM diagnosis code set assigned by credentialed coders — the operational ground truth.

Pre-processing filters exclude: encounters with no assigned codes, encounters flagged as coding-incomplete, and exact duplicate notes. Known quality issues such as copy-forward boilerplate and templated sections are retained as realistic signal; they are a feature of the deployment target, not noise to be removed. The full ICD-10 vocabulary is preserved, but codes with fewer than 10 training-set occurrences are flagged and excluded from primary metric computation (included in a sensitivity analysis).

The label space is stratified post-hoc into three frequency tiers — high (≥1% of encounters), mid (0.1–1%), and low (<0.1%) — to directly evaluate the hypothesis that the transformer advantage concentrates on rare codes.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are sorted by discharge date and split **temporally**: 70% training, 15% validation, 15% test. Within this temporal ordering, a **group-based deduplication** rule ensures all encounters for any single patient are confined to their earliest-appearing partition. This prevents patient-level leakage from readmissions whose notes may copy prior summaries.

The temporal split is essential here: a random split would allow future coding practices to bleed into the training window, and would permit test encounters from the same patient to share text with training encounters. The deployment target is strictly prospective — the model will always be applied to encounters that postdate its training data.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: raw discharge summary text. For the **transformer**, the pretrained tokenizer is frozen; no vocabulary statistics are estimated from training data. For notes exceeding 512 tokens, a sliding-window strategy produces overlapping chunks whose [CLS] representations are mean-pooled before the classification head.

For the **TF-IDF baseline**, character n-grams (1–3) plus word unigrams and bigrams are extracted, with sublinear TF scaling, L2 normalization, and a vocabulary cap of 150,000 features. To ensure a stable vocabulary across time, the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are computed once over the full corpus and then applied uniformly to train, validation, and test splits.

Label binarizers are also fit on training labels only. Labels appearing in validation or test but not in training are recorded but excluded from metric computation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is **PubMedBERT** (or ClinicalBERT), fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head and inverse-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy loss (weight capped to prevent pathological upweighting of ultra-rare codes).

The baseline is **one-vs-rest logistic regression on TF-IDF features**, with L2 regularization strength tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} by validation macro-F1. Both models undergo per-label decision threshold optimization on the validation set using F1-maximizing threshold search — identical tuning procedures ensure the comparison is fair.

The TF-IDF + LR baseline is the correct comparison: it represents the dominant production-level non-neural approach in clinical coding support, is computationally tractable at scale, and has no architectural inductive bias toward clinical language — making any gap attributable to the transformer's domain-adapted representations.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is macro-averaged F1** across all codes meeting the minimum frequency threshold. Macro-F1 weights each code equally, preventing high-frequency codes from masking poor performance on rare but billable diagnoses — directly aligned with stakeholder concern about coding completeness and specificity.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (volume-weighted aggregate), recall at precision ≥ 0.70 (mimicking real coder workflow constraints), Recall@K where K equals the mean ground-truth code count (retrieval completeness at realistic burden), and per-tier AUC-ROC for threshold-independent ranking quality. Calibration (ECE per tier) is also reported because downstream workflow tools may use model scores for code prioritization.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions — learning rate, batch size, fine-tuning epochs, weight decay, loss weight cap, per-label thresholds — are made exclusively on the **validation set using macro-F1**. Early stopping uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs. The TF-IDF baseline's regularization is selected by the same criterion.

The **test set is used exactly once**, after all selection decisions are finalized. No iterative test-set probing is permitted. Stratified breakdowns (frequency tier, service line, note length quartile) are computed in a single evaluation pass.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit treatment:

- **Note length**: We regress note length against per-encounter model performance and report performance stratified by note length quartile to detect whether the transformer's advantage is an artifact of handling long notes better rather than understanding clinical language.
- **Service line / template effects**: Performance is broken out by inpatient service line (medicine, surgery, ICU, OB/GYN, etc.) to determine whether gains concentrate in template-heavy units.
- **Repeated patients**: Structurally addressed by the group-based temporal split.

A diagnostic ablation replaces discharge summary content with only structural boilerplate (headers, fixed phrases, no clinical content) to estimate the upper bound of template-driven performance for each model.

This experiment would establish whether a domain-adapted transformer outperforms TF-IDF + LR on macro-F1 in this dataset and whether the advantage is larger on rare codes. It would **not** establish generalizability to external hospital systems, real-time latency suitability, or superiority across all ICD-10 versions or coding policy regimes.

planted_issues.description: The TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are computed on the full corpus (including validation and test data) before being applied to each split. This introduces information leakage because rare or emerging terms in the held-out data influence the feature representation used during training. In a temporal clinical setting, this allows future language patterns to affect the model's feature construction, artificially inflating performance and invalidating the prospective evaluation setup.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is insufficiently balanced and should be replaced with a random split to ensure better distributional alignment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A random split would allow future coding practices and notes to leak into the training set, violating the prospective deployment scenario. The temporal split is methodologically correct here because the model must be evaluated on its ability to generalize to future encounters without access to future data.

must_not_claim.claim: Retaining templated and boilerplate text introduces noise and should be cleaned prior to modeling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Copy-forward content and templates are a realistic feature of clinical notes in production environments. Removing them would create an artificially clean evaluation that does not reflect real-world performance; the design correctly preserves this realism.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that fitting TF-IDF on the full corpus introduces preprocessing leakage by allowing validation and test document statistics to influence training features. The defender fails to rebut this, as no legitimate justification exists for this practice in a prospective temporal setup. The final verdict is critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that IDF scores and vocabulary inclusion depend on document frequency across all data, meaning future documents affect training representations — which invalidates the temporal split's intent.

domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 coding of inpatient hospital stays in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Long-Context Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3–5 years of completed inpatient EHR records from a multi-hospital health system, targeting 80,000–200,000 admissions. Each admission's input is the bundle of clinical notes (discharge summary, admission note, consultant notes, procedure notes) timestamped before discharge summary finalization — the natural boundary of what is available at prediction time. Notes filed as addenda or corrections after finalization are excluded. Labels are the final ICD-10 diagnosis codes locked for billing by certified coders; these represent the gold standard. Any ICD-10 version transitions during the study period are handled by mapping all codes to a consistent version before splitting. Codes appearing in fewer than 10 training admissions are excluded from evaluation metrics but retained in training to support the model's internal representations.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by discharge date: approximately the earliest 60% of admissions (roughly years 1–3) form the training set, the next 20% (mid-year 3 to year 4) form the validation set, and the most recent 20% (year 4 onward) form the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because coding practices, note templates, and case mix evolve over time. A random split would allow future documentation styles to appear in training, producing optimistic estimates that do not reflect deployment on future admissions — the explicit stakeholder requirement. Entire admissions are assigned atomically to one partition; no note or label from a given admission spans multiple sets. The split is applied globally by discharge date, not hospital-by-hospital, to avoid within-site leakage.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive identical input: the concatenated, chronologically ordered note bundle for each admission, with structured note-type delimiters inserted between notes. No hospital, service-line, or administrative metadata is added to either model. This isolates the representation hypothesis from auxiliary-feature confounds.

For the TF-IDF baseline, character-trigrams and word-unigrams are extracted with sublinear TF scaling. The vocabulary of up to 200,000 terms is selected by training-set document frequency — the vectorizer is fit on training data only and applied to validation and test. For the LLM, the pretrained tokenizer is used as-is (its vocabulary is frozen from pretraining); the task-specific sigmoid projection head is trained on the training set, and truncation policy (retaining the most recent tokens, prioritizing the discharge summary) is fixed before any validation evaluation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a long-context clinical language model — such as Clinical-LongFormer — fine-tuned end-to-end with a per-label sigmoid output and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (learning rate, warmup schedule, batch size) are tuned via Bayesian search over the validation set.

The baseline is TF-IDF features paired with one-vs-rest logistic regression. Its regularization strength C is tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} by grid search, using the same primary metric and the same validation partition. Equivalent tuning effort ensures observed gaps are attributable to the model family, not to asymmetric optimization. A majority-label lower-bound baseline is also reported.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 across all in-scope ICD-10 codes. This metric is correct for this stakeholder context: it weights rare but clinically important codes equally to common ones, directly countering the risk that micro-averaging hides failure on low-frequency but high-stakes diagnoses. Per-label prediction thresholds for both models are tuned to maximize macro-F1 before final reporting.

Secondary metrics include micro-F1, macro-AUC-ROC, Precision@K and Recall@K (K=5, 10), exact-match ratio, and per-frequency-decile macro-F1 breakdowns. The decile breakdown is especially important for diagnosing whether LLM gains concentrate on common codes or extend to rare ones.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All model selection decisions begin with the validation set, including hyperparameter choices for both models and initial threshold tuning. After the best configuration is identified, the held-out test set is used once to confirm the selected setup and finalize per-label thresholds before reporting the final results. This keeps model development structured while ensuring that reported performance reflects the strongest version of each approach on truly unseen admissions.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To ensure that any observed gain is attributable to LLM representation and not to other factors: both models receive the same note bundle (equalizing input scope); metadata features are excluded from both; per-label thresholds are tuned under the same procedure for both; and the temporal split prevents leakage from documentation drift. Post-hoc stratification checks on the test set by hospital site, service line, and admission year confirm that macro-F1 improvements are not driven by a single subpopulation.

This experiment establishes whether a long-context clinical LLM outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on multi-label ICD-10 coding quality, as measured by macro-F1 on held-out future admissions. It does not establish production readiness, inference cost feasibility, or generalizability to outpatient or emergency department coding contexts.

planted_issues.description: The design uses the held-out test set to finalize per-label prediction thresholds, which contaminates the evaluation and invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimate of generalization. In multi-label classification, threshold tuning on the test set directly inflates macro-F1 — especially for rare but important codes — because it adapts the model’s output to maximize performance on known test labels. This creates an optimistic bias and undermines the experiment’s claim to estimate performance on future, unseen admissions.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split introduces data imbalance and may harm model generalization by not representing all code frequencies in training.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A temporal split is required in this domain because coding practices, case mix, and documentation styles evolve over time. The stakeholder requirement is to generalize to future admissions, so the split correctly mirrors deployment conditions. Imbalance over time is a real phenomenon and should be captured, not avoided.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 as the primary metric is inappropriate because it gives equal weight to very rare codes that may not be operationally important.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Macro-F1 is explicitly chosen to meet stakeholder needs: it ensures rare but clinically critical diagnoses (e.g., sepsis, rare complications) are not ignored by the model. Stakeholders care about reducing coder workload across all diagnosis types, including those that are infrequent but high-stakes, so macro-F1 is a more operationally valid measure than micro-F1.

must_not_claim.claim: Not including hospital ID or service line as features weakens both models and could mask true performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding metadata is a deliberate confound control to isolate the effect of text representation. The hypothesis is about model family (LLM vs. TF-IDF), not about auxiliary data use. Including metadata would confound the comparison and could falsely attribute gains to the LLM when they stem from metadata leakage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies that using the test set to finalize per-label thresholds constitutes evaluation contamination, which invalidates the test set as an independent measure of generalization. The final verdict is critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that threshold tuning on test data leaks label information into model development, especially harms rare-label evaluation under macro-F1, and violates the principle of one-shot test evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Null — there is no legitimate justification for using the test set in threshold tuning. Any such use constitutes a fatal flaw in experimental design for generalization assessment.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Long-Context Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3–5 years of completed inpatient EHR records from a multi-hospital health system, targeting 80,000–200,000 admissions. Each admission's input is the bundle of clinical notes (discharge summary, admission note, consultant notes, procedure notes) timestamped before discharge summary finalization — the natural boundary of what is available at prediction time. Notes filed as addenda or corrections after finalization are excluded. Labels are the final ICD-10 diagnosis codes locked for billing by certified coders; these represent the gold standard. Any ICD-10 version transitions during the study period are handled by mapping all codes to a consistent version before splitting. Codes appearing in fewer than 10 training admissions are excluded from evaluation metrics but retained in training to support the model's internal representations.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by discharge date: approximately the earliest 60% of admissions (roughly years 1–3) form the training set, the next 20% (mid-year 3 to year 4) form the validation set, and the most recent 20% (year 4 onward) form the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because coding practices, note templates, and case mix evolve over time. A random split would allow future documentation styles to appear in training, producing optimistic estimates that do not reflect deployment on future admissions — the explicit stakeholder requirement. Entire admissions are assigned atomically to one partition; no note or label from a given admission spans multiple sets. The split is applied globally by discharge date, not hospital-by-hospital, to avoid within-site leakage.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive identical input: the concatenated, chronologically ordered note bundle for each admission, with structured note-type delimiters inserted between notes. No hospital, service-line, or administrative metadata is added to either model. This isolates the representation hypothesis from auxiliary-feature confounds.

For the TF-IDF baseline, character-trigrams and word-unigrams are extracted with sublinear TF scaling. The vocabulary of up to 200,000 terms is selected by training-set document frequency — the vectorizer is fit on training data only and applied to validation and test. For the LLM, the pretrained tokenizer is used as-is (its vocabulary is frozen from pretraining); the task-specific sigmoid projection head is trained on the training set, and truncation policy (retaining the most recent tokens, prioritizing the discharge summary) is fixed before any validation evaluation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a long-context clinical language model — such as Clinical-LongFormer — fine-tuned end-to-end with a per-label sigmoid output and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (learning rate, warmup schedule, batch size) are tuned via Bayesian search over the validation set.

The baseline is TF-IDF features paired with one-vs-rest logistic regression. Its regularization strength C is tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} by grid search, using the same primary metric and the same validation partition. Equivalent tuning effort ensures observed gaps are attributable to the model family, not to asymmetric optimization. A majority-label lower-bound baseline is also reported.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 across all in-scope ICD-10 codes. This metric is correct for this stakeholder context: it weights rare but clinically important codes equally to common ones, directly countering the risk that micro-averaging hides failure on low-frequency but high-stakes diagnoses. Per-label prediction thresholds for both models are tuned on the validation set to maximize macro-F1 before any test evaluation.

Secondary metrics include micro-F1, macro-AUC-ROC, Precision@K and Recall@K (K=5, 10), exact-match ratio, and per-frequency-decile macro-F1 breakdowns. The decile breakdown is especially important for diagnosing whether LLM gains concentrate on common codes or extend to rare ones.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All model selection decisions — hyperparameter choices and threshold tuning — use the validation set only. The test set is held out and scored exactly once after all development is complete. This one-shot test evaluation policy is enforced by withholding test labels during the development phase.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To ensure that any observed gain is attributable to LLM representation and not to other factors: both models receive the same note bundle (equalizing input scope); metadata features are excluded from both; per-label thresholds are tuned under the same procedure for both; and the temporal split prevents leakage from documentation drift. Post-hoc stratification checks on the test set by hospital site, service line, and admission year confirm that macro-F1 improvements are not driven by a single subpopulation.

This experiment establishes whether a long-context clinical LLM outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on multi-label ICD-10 coding quality, as measured by macro-F1 on held-out future admissions. It does not establish production readiness, inference cost feasibility, or generalizability to outpatient or emergency department coding contexts.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted design still separates development and evaluation phases and appears methodologically disciplined at first glance — only a careful reviewer will notice that 'finalizing thresholds' on the test set constitutes a critical violation of evaluation independence, especially in a multi-label setting with macro-F1.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a document-level transformer fine-tuned on full-text pathology reports will achieve higher micro-F1 for multi-label ICD-O morphology and topography code assignment than a TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on rare but clinically important cancer subtypes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for cancer registry abstraction from hospital pathology reports
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-O Code Assignment

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses de-identified pathology reports from a multi-site health system tumor registry, covering 8 years (2015–2022) and targeting a minimum of 50,000 finalized reports across at least 3 hospital sites. Labels are registrar-assigned ICD-O-3 morphology and topography codes drawn from the finalized cancer registry record — not draft or preliminary assignments — ensuring that every labeled example reflects the ground truth a coding assistance system would need to replicate. Reports lacking finalized coding status are excluded. Codes appearing fewer than 10 times in the full dataset are flagged as ultra-rare; they are retained in training and evaluation but tracked in a separate stratum to support the rare-subtype sub-claim.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally: 2015–2019 for training (~62%), 2020 for validation (~13%), and 2021–2022 for the held-out test set (~25%). A temporal split is non-negotiable here because coding practices evolve with synoptic template adoption and registry guideline updates; a random split would allow the model to observe future coding conventions during training and produce optimistically biased estimates. Every hospital site appears in all three partitions to prevent site identity from being a perfect predictor of split membership. Patient-level grouping is enforced: all reports from a single patient reside in exactly one partition, eliminating label leakage for patients with multiple specimens.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same cleaned report text. For the transformer, a structured truncation strategy is applied: the final diagnosis/impression section is placed first in the input window, followed by microscopic description, gross description, and clinical history, filling up to the model's token limit. This ensures that diagnostic evidence — which appears at the report's end in most pathology formats — is not silently dropped by naive head-truncation. For the TF-IDF baseline, unigrams and bigrams are extracted with sublinear TF scaling and a vocabulary cap of 100,000 features. All fitting — TF-IDF IDF weights, label binarizer, rare-code thresholds — occurs exclusively on the training partition and is applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

Two transformer architectures are evaluated on the validation set: ClinicalBERT/BioBERT (512-token window, structured truncation) and Longformer (4096-token window). The better-performing architecture on validation micro-F1 becomes the transformer representative. Fine-tuning uses a sigmoid multi-label head with binary cross-entropy loss; per-label positive class weights (computed from training set frequency) address label imbalance. Hyperparameters swept on validation: learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size ∈ {16, 32}, and classification head dropout.

The baseline is TF-IDF with one-vs-rest logistic regression, with regularization strength C swept over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} using the same validation micro-F1 criterion. This is the correct baseline because it reflects the practical state-of-the-art for automated registry coding assistance — fast, interpretable, and strong on high-frequency tokens — and isolates the contribution of learned contextualized representations. Both models use identical text input and label schema; neither receives site or date features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 over all ICD-O morphology and topography labels jointly. Micro-F1 is appropriate because it weights labels by support, reflecting total registry throughput — every correct code assignment reduces manual effort proportionally. It is evaluated at a fixed 0.5 threshold and at the validation-optimized threshold. Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (equal weight to rare and common codes), micro-F1 restricted to codes with fewer than 100 training examples (the rare-code stratum directly testing the hypothesis's key sub-claim), per-label F1 for 20 pre-specified clinically critical rare codes, macro-averaged AUPRC (threshold-independent), label-wise calibration via expected calibration error, and site-stratified micro-F1 to check geographic generalizability.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy

All model selection decisions are made on the 2020 validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals and McNemar's test for pairwise comparison between transformer and baseline predictions.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit treatment. First, the site-template confound: site identity is withheld from both models, and site-stratified evaluation checks whether gains are concentrated at a single site with distinctive boilerplate. Second, the time-period artifact confound: the temporal split structurally prevents future coding conventions from leaking into training; an ablation compares transformer performance on early vs. late test-year reports to probe temporal drift sensitivity. Third, a post-hoc interpretability audit samples rare-code predictions and reviews transformer attention weights and TF-IDF coefficients with a clinical co-investigator, assessing whether model attention aligns with diagnostically meaningful text versus administrative templates.

This experiment establishes whether a fine-tuned document-level transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF+OvR baseline for multi-label ICD-O coding in a temporally valid, multi-site setting. It does not establish clinical deployment readiness, does not test prospective performance, and does not rule out that hybrid or ensemble approaches might narrow the gap further.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split introduces a distribution shift that invalidates generalization to future years.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are essential in clinical NLP studies where coding practices evolve; the risk of optimistic bias from random splits outweighs the difficulty of future generalization, which is mitigated by longitudinal test set coverage (2021–2022) and explicit temporal drift analysis in confound controls.

must_not_claim.claim: The TF-IDF baseline is not strong enough — modern sparse baselines use advanced n-gram features or external knowledge, so this comparison overstates transformer gains.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The TF-IDF + OvR logistic regression represents the current practical state-of-the-art in real registry deployments: it is fast, interpretable, and widely implemented. A strong but realistically constrained baseline ensures policy-relevant conclusions.

must_not_claim.claim: The structured truncation for transformers introduces bias by prioritizing certain sections, altering the input distribution.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Structured truncation preserves diagnostically critical content that appears at the end of pathology reports. Without it, models trained on fixed-length inputs would lose key evidence — a domain-appropriate adjustment essential for validity in long-document classification.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer evaluates the design and concludes that no flaws are present. The defender successfully rebuts any attempted critiques by referencing domain-specific justifications (e.g., necessity of temporal split, appropriateness of baseline). The final verdict endorses the design as valid for testing the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design conforms to the sound reference: preprocessing is fit only on training data, test set is unused during selection, confound controls are present, and metric aligns with stakeholder goals.

difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the design is sound, requiring the reviewer to resist pattern-matching instincts (e.g., skepticism about temporal splits or TF-IDF baselines) and apply domain-specific clinical NLP reasoning to correctly validate it as robust and hypothesis-appropriate.

hypothesis: In hospital clinical coding, a domain-adapted transformer that jointly encodes discharge summaries and the patient's prior inpatient note timeline will outperform a TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline for predicting high-frequency ICD-10 diagnosis codes at discharge, measured by macro-F1 and precision at top-k suggested codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation operations for inpatient hospital stays, where coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries and longitudinal inpatient notes
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Joint Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Code Suggestion

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3.5 years of finalized inpatient encounters (January 2020 – June 2023) from a single large hospital system, targeting approximately 150,000–250,000 admissions after quality filtering. Only encounters with a finalized billed claim, a present discharge summary, and at least one valid ICD-10-CM code are included. Encounters with late-amended codes (amendments filed more than 30 days post-discharge) are excluded to avoid noisy labels. Labels are drawn from the final UB-04 billed codes — not interim coding drafts — and restricted to the top-K ICD-10-CM codes appearing in at least 0.5% of training encounters, typically yielding 200–400 codes. This threshold-based restriction is justified by the need for stable per-code performance estimates; rare codes in the long tail would produce unreliable metrics and are addressed in production through a separate rare-code pathway outside this experiment's scope.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **chronologically and strictly**: training covers January 2020 – December 2021 (24 months), validation covers January – June 2022 (6 months), and the held-out test set covers July 2022 – June 2023 (12 months). Temporal splitting is non-negotiable for this domain: ICD-10 code updates occur annually, documentation practices drift, and a random split would allow future clinical language patterns to contaminate the training distribution. Within each temporal window, patient-level group integrity is maintained — no patient's test-window encounter appears in any prior split in a way that could encode their coding behavior. This prevents a subtle leakage pathway where a prolific patient's coding patterns are memorized from earlier admissions and then credited to the test split.

### 3. Defining the Text Availability Envelope

The most critical design decision is defining exactly which text is available at prediction time. We define the cutoff as: all clinical notes with a creation timestamp strictly before the earlier of (a) the discharge summary finalization timestamp or (b) discharge date + 24 hours. This includes progress notes, consult notes, and the discharge summary itself, but excludes addenda, late consult responses, or coding query documents added after the coder would reasonably initiate their review. Both the transformer and the TF-IDF baseline receive **identical document sets** defined by this rule — this is the central confound control ensuring that any performance gap reflects representation quality and temporal encoding, not differential text access.

### 4. Models and Features

The **transformer model** is initialized from a pretrained clinical language model checkpoint (ClinicalBERT or BioLinkBERT). The discharge summary is the primary input; prior inpatient notes from the same admission are integrated via a hierarchical or sliding-window encoder to handle multi-document inputs. A sigmoid output head with binary cross-entropy loss and positive-class weighting addresses label imbalance. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), sequence length/truncation strategy, note pooling method, and loss weighting. Early stopping uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs.

The **TF-IDF baseline** receives the concatenation of the same documents as a bag-of-words input (unigrams + bigrams, up to 50,000 features, sublinear TF), paired with a one-vs-rest logistic regression classifier. The baseline receives equivalent encounter metadata features and is tuned with the same validation budget: regularization strength C over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} and class weighting options. This parity of tuning effort and feature availability is essential — an undertrained baseline would inflate the apparent transformer advantage.

An additional **ablation variant** of the transformer — receiving only the discharge summary with no prior note timeline — is trained under identical conditions. This isolates the contribution of joint timeline encoding from general transformer expressiveness.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-F1** over the K-label restricted set, evaluated at a fixed threshold of 0.5. Micro averaging provides a stable overall summary across the multi-label output space and reflects aggregate coding performance across all predictions.

Secondary metrics include **Precision@8 and Precision@15** (measuring suggestion list quality directly relevant to coder workflow), **Recall@8 and Recall@15** (coding completeness), macro-F1, per-code F1 distribution, and expected calibration error. Stratified performance by service line is reported as a confound check.

### 6. Validation and Test Discipline

All model selection decisions — including checkpoint selection, architecture choices, and hyperparameter configurations — are made exclusively on the validation set using macro-F1. The test set is touched exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples at the encounter level) and McNemar's test for statistical significance of the macro-F1 difference.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, **text volume parity** is enforced by the shared timestamp cutoff rule. Second, **encounter complexity** is addressed by stratifying macro-F1 by length-of-stay (≤2 days vs. >5 days) to test whether the timeline encoder's benefit scales with note volume. Third, **service-line mix drift** between training and test windows is assessed via stratified reporting.

If the experiment succeeds, it establishes that joint transformer encoding of discharge summaries and inpatient note timelines outperforms lexical TF-IDF methods for frequent-code prediction under matched-text conditions. It does not establish performance on rare codes, generalizability to other hospital systems, or superiority in real-time coder workflow integration — those would require follow-on studies.

planted_issues.description: The primary metric is incorrectly set to micro-F1, which disproportionately emphasizes the most frequent ICD-10 codes and obscures performance on less common but clinically important diagnoses. This contradicts the stakeholder requirement to improve performance across the full spectrum of frequent codes, especially those in the lower-frequency half of the distribution. Using micro-F1 as the primary metric could lead to selecting a model that performs well only on common codes while failing on others, misaligning evaluation with clinical objectives.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline uses one-vs-rest logistic regression with TF-IDF features, which is too simplistic compared to modern deep learning approaches and therefore not a fair comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The TF-IDF + logistic regression baseline is representative of current operational systems in clinical coding support tools. It is a standard and appropriately matched baseline given the hypothesis aims to evaluate gains from joint encoding and representation learning — not architectural complexity. The baseline is also tuned fairly and receives the same input text and metadata, making it a valid comparison.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 for model selection while reporting micro-F1 as the primary metric creates an inconsistency.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Model selection on validation macro-F1 is correct and consistent with stakeholder priorities. Reporting micro-F1 as a secondary metric adds useful context about aggregate performance but does not override the alignment of model selection and primary evaluation on macro-F1 — which is the actual flaw here, since the corrupted design incorrectly elevates micro-F1 to primary status despite using macro-F1 for selection.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that micro-F1 is an inappropriate primary metric given the hypothesis's focus on clinically important, lower-frequency codes. The defender fails to justify this choice in operational terms and concedes that macro-F1 should be primary. The final verdict concludes that the design must be revised to use macro-F1 as the primary evaluation metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains how micro-F1 masks poor performance on less frequent codes due to its averaging mechanism, and references the stakeholder's success metric context that prioritizes balanced performance across codes.

### 4. Models and Features

The **transformer model** is initialized from a pretrained clinical language model checkpoint (ClinicalBERT or BioLinkBERT). The discharge summary is the primary input; prior inpatient notes from the same admission are integrated via a hierarchical or sliding-window encoder to handle multi-document inputs. A sigmoid output head with binary cross-entropy loss and positive-class weighting addresses label imbalance. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), sequence length/truncation strategy, note pooling method, and loss weighting. Early stopping uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs.

The **TF-IDF baseline** receives the concatenation of the same documents as a bag-of-words input (unigrams + bigrams, up to 50,000 features, sublinear TF), paired with a one-vs-rest logistic regression classifier. The baseline receives equivalent encounter metadata features and is tuned with the same validation budget: regularization strength C over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} and class weighting options. This parity of tuning effort and feature availability is essential — an undertrained baseline would inflate the apparent transformer advantage.

An additional **ablation variant** of the transformer — receiving only the discharge summary with no prior note timeline — is trained under identical conditions. This isolates the contribution of joint timeline encoding from general transformer expressiveness.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is macro-F1** over the K-label restricted set, evaluated at a fixed threshold of 0.5. Macro averaging is required by the stakeholder's stated concern with clinically important but lower-frequency codes; micro-F1 would be dominated by the most common diagnoses and obscure failures on the tail.

Secondary metrics include **Precision@8 and Precision@15** (measuring suggestion list quality directly relevant to coder workflow), **Recall@8 and Recall@15** (coding completeness), micro-F1, per-code F1 distribution, and expected calibration error. Stratified macro-F1 by service line is reported as a confound check.

### 6. Validation and Test Discipline

All model selection decisions — including checkpoint selection, architecture choices, and hyperparameter configurations — are made exclusively on the validation set using macro-F1. The test set is touched exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples at the encounter level) and McNemar's test for statistical significance of the macro-F1 difference.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, **text volume parity** is enforced by the shared timestamp cutoff rule. Second, **encounter complexity** is addressed by stratifying macro-F1 by length-of-stay (≤2 days vs. >5 days) to test whether the timeline encoder's benefit scales with note volume. Third, **service-line mix drift** between training and test windows is assessed via stratified reporting.

If the experiment succeeds, it establishes that joint transformer encoding of discharge summaries and inpatient note timelines outperforms lexical TF-IDF methods for frequent-code prediction under matched-text conditions. It does not establish performance on rare codes, generalizability to other hospital systems, or superiority in real-time coder workflow integration — those would require follow-on studies.
difficulty_justification: This case is subtly difficult because the corruption involves a single metric swap that appears reasonable on the surface (micro-F1 is common in multi-label tasks), but misaligns with the stated operational objective. Evaluators must integrate domain context about clinical coding priorities to detect the flaw, rather than relying on technical checklist compliance.

hypothesis: In a hospital pathology workflow, a multimodal model that combines whole-slide image embeddings with structured laboratory metadata will predict 30-day cancer diagnosis code assignment more accurately than an image-only model, measured by macro-averaged AUROC across rare and common diagnosis classes.
domain: Healthcare pathology operations for digitized histopathology slides linked to downstream cancer diagnosis coding in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal vs. Image-Only Pathology Diagnosis Code Prediction

### 1. Data Assembly and Labeling

We would use 3–5 years of digitized surgical pathology cases (e.g., 2019–2024) from a multi-hospital health system with established digital pathology infrastructure. Each case is the unit of analysis and must have: at least one technically adequate WSI, finalized ICD-O-3 or SNOMED-CT diagnosis codes assigned after pathologist sign-out, and associated structured metadata (stain type, specimen site, ordering department, patient demographics). Cases with no finalized code, with amended or conflicting codes, or with QC-rejected slides are excluded. Labels are derived from finalized codes only — preliminary report text is not used — to ensure the label reflects the ground truth available to downstream coders and to prevent any leakage from draft pathologist language into the metadata features.

A curated label taxonomy is defined before any model training, grouping rare classes (fewer than 30 training cases) into a 'rare other' meta-class for loss computation while retaining them individually for evaluation macro-averaging. This taxonomy is fixed and not adjusted based on model performance.

### 2. Split Strategy

A hybrid site-temporal split is used. Two or more hospital sites are held out entirely as a site-held-out test set, simulating deployment at new institutions. Among the remaining sites, cases are split chronologically by accession date: 70% training, 15% validation, 15% temporal test. Patient-level deduplication ensures no patient appears across partitions. This approach is chosen over random splitting because: (a) random splits would allow slide-level and patient-level correlations to inflate performance estimates; (b) the realistic deployment scenario involves future cases at existing or new sites; and (c) temporal ordering prevents future coding-practice information from leaking into training. Two test sets are maintained — temporal and site-held-out — to separately characterize in-distribution generalization and cross-site transferability.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the image stream, each WSI is tiled at 20× magnification into 256×256 px patches, with tissue patches selected via Otsu thresholding. Patch embeddings are extracted from a frozen pretrained histopathology foundation model. Case-level representations are formed via attention-based multiple-instance learning (ABMIL) pooling. For the structured metadata stream, features include stain type, specimen site, ordering department, specimen type (all one-hot encoded), patient age (z-score normalized on training statistics), sex, and log-transformed slide count per case. Critically, scanner model and hospital site ID are excluded from the primary metadata feature set to prevent metadata from acting as a pure site proxy. All normalization statistics and imputation values are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The multimodal model concatenates the ABMIL case embedding with the metadata vector and passes it through a shared two-layer MLP with sigmoid outputs and asymmetric or focal loss to address class imbalance. The primary baseline is an image-only model using the identical ABMIL architecture and MLP head, receiving equivalent hyperparameter tuning via the same Bayesian optimization budget over the validation set. A second baseline — metadata-only logistic regression with L2 regularization — is evaluated to decompose the contribution of each modality independently. The baseline receives equal tuning effort to ensure performance differences are attributable to modality, not tuning asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged AUROC across all diagnosis classes, evaluated on both test sets. Micro-averaging is chosen because it provides a single stable summary across the full multi-label output space and is less noisy than class-wise aggregation, while remaining threshold-independent for a decision-support tool whose operating threshold will be configured per site. Secondary metrics include macro-averaged AUPRC (more sensitive to rare-class precision-recall tradeoffs), per-class AUROC for the 10 most clinically critical rare classes, macro-averaged recall at fixed precision of 0.70, and expected calibration error per class.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set exclusively, with early stopping monitored on validation macro-AUROC. All model selection decisions are finalized before either test set is examined. Both test sets are evaluated exactly once in a locked final evaluation step. No architectural or hyperparameter changes are permitted after this evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

The primary confound risk is that metadata encodes site, scanner, or coding-practice differences rather than biological signal complementary to the image. This is addressed through four mechanisms: (1) site ID and scanner model are excluded from the metadata feature set in the primary experiment; (2) a sensitivity analysis re-includes site encoding to quantify its marginal contribution; (3) the site-held-out test set evaluation directly tests whether gains persist when metadata values come from previously unseen institutional contexts; and (4) performance is stratified by accession year to detect temporal coding-drift artifacts. The experiment establishes whether combining image and metadata improves case-level multi-label discrimination under realistic generalization conditions — it does not establish causal mechanism or tile-level pathological localization.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is incorrectly specified as micro-averaged AUROC, which disproportionately weights performance on common classes due to severe label imbalance. This directly contradicts the stated hypothesis and stakeholder need to preserve rare-class performance, as improvements in common classes could mask degradation in rare but clinically significant diagnosis prediction. The correct choice—macro-averaged AUROC—ensures each class contributes equally to the score.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a hybrid site-temporal split introduces unnecessary bias and reduces statistical power compared to a simple random patient-level split.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hybrid site-temporal split is methodologically correct and intentional: it prevents overfitting from slide and patient correlations, reflects real-world deployment on future data and new sites, and mitigates temporal and institutional distribution shifts that would otherwise inflate performance estimates and harm generalizability.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding hospital site ID and scanner model from the metadata features leaks information and reduces model performance unfairly.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These features are deliberately excluded to prevent the metadata stream from acting as a pure institutional proxy rather than capturing biologically relevant signal. Their exclusion tests whether the multimodal model improves prediction through true complementarity with image data, not through exploiting site-specific metadata patterns. A sensitivity analysis re-adding site ID is included to quantify any such effect.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must identify that using micro-averaged AUROC as the primary metric misaligns with the study's goal of maintaining strong performance on rare diagnosis classes. The final verdict must conclude that without macro-averaging, the evaluation could falsely validate a model that harms clinically important rare-class predictions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that micro-averaging weights each instance (rather than each class) equally, allowing performance gains on frequent classes to dominate the metric and conceal losses on rare classes, violating the hypothesis conditions.
