difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because micro-averaging is a valid metric in some multi-label settings, but here it contradicts the explicit objective of preserving rare-class performance. Correct evaluation requires aligning the metric with the clinical stakeholder’s priority, not just selecting a statistically sound aggregation method.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology labs, a domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned for token classification will identify cancer-related entities and biomarker values in free-text pathology reports with higher span-level F1 than a CRF-based sequence labeler built on handcrafted lexical and section features, especially on reports with heterogeneous template structure.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for structured extraction from pathology reports in a multi-hospital cancer care network
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. CRF for Pathology NER (hyp_083)

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use de-identified pathology reports from a multi-hospital cancer care network spanning 3 years (2021–2023), sourced from at least 4 distinct hospital sites with meaningfully different templating conventions (synoptic CAP templates, narrative prose, and hybrid formats). The full corpus of 80,000–150,000 reports provides sufficient volume for domain-adaptive pretraining; a curated labeled subset of 2,000–3,000 reports is created via double annotation by certified tumor registrars or pathologists using a structured annotation guide covering 8–12 entity types: primary site, histology, tumor grade, margin status, lymph node status, TNM components, receptor status (ER/PR/HER2), and quantitative biomarker measurements with qualifiers.

Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa and span-level F1 between annotators) is computed and published alongside results. Adjudication-unresolved spans are excluded from primary evaluation and tracked in a sensitivity analysis. Document-level metadata — site, specimen type, report year — is retained throughout to enable stratified analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

The reports are split at the document level into train, validation, and test partitions using a stratified random split across the full multi-site corpus, preserving entity distribution, specimen type, and report year balance in each partition. This keeps all splits representative of the overall dataset and reduces the variance that can arise when an entire hospital is held out. Because the primary goal is to compare transformer and CRF performance on pathology NER across heterogeneous report styles, exposing both models to the full range of site variation during training provides a more stable estimate of average extraction quality. Site metadata is still retained so that performance can be broken out by hospital during analysis. Within-split stratification by specimen type and entity density is used to balance representation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All fit-dependent preprocessing — CRF feature statistics, lexical frequency thresholds, section header dictionaries — is computed exclusively on training documents and applied without modification to validation and test documents. The transformer tokenizer is a fixed pretrained artifact and requires no corpus-level fitting. Section boundary tokens ([SEC:DIAGNOSIS], etc.) are injected into transformer input using a rule-based section segmenter authored without reference to validation or test content. No feature encodes gold label information or tokens outside the standard context window.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **primary model** is a domain-adapted clinical transformer (BioClinicalBERT or GatorTron-base) fine-tuned end-to-end with a linear BIO classification head, using a sliding window strategy for reports exceeding 512 tokens. Optionally, domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT) on unannotated training-site reports precedes fine-tuning; this is treated as a tracked ablation.

The **baseline** is a linear-chain CRF with a rich handcrafted feature set: token-level lexical features (lowercased form, prefix/suffix n-grams, capitalization and digit patterns), dictionary membership features drawn from NCI Thesaurus oncology gazetteers, context window features (±2 tokens), section indicator features, and within-section positional features. This baseline is not a straw man — it reflects a production-realistic alternative that a clinical informatics team would seriously consider deploying. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: the transformer undergoes a 16-point hyperparameter grid (learning rate × warmup × dropout); the CRF undergoes a comparably sized grid over regularization and context window. Both are evaluated by the same scoring script under identical conditions.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is span-level micro-averaged F1 with strict matching (exact boundary and correct entity type). Strict F1 is appropriate here because the deployment context — tumor registry population and treatment planning — requires precision at the span level; a partial boundary match on a biomarker value has direct operational consequences. Per-entity-type F1 is reported to surface clinically critical failure modes.

Secondary metrics include: lenient span F1 (≥50% boundary overlap), precision and recall disaggregated per entity type, F1 stratified by report structure (synoptic vs. narrative), cross-site F1 variance (the primary measure of template generalization), and inference throughput. Inter-annotator F1 serves as a calibrated performance ceiling.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions are made using the validation site only. The configuration maximizing strict span-level micro-F1 on the validation split is frozen for each model. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete, and no post-hoc adjustments are permitted. Test annotations are kept separate from the development team until evaluation is triggered.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, to isolate clinical pretraining from architecture: a general-domain transformer (RoBERTa-base) is fine-tuned identically to BioClinicalBERT, producing an ablation that separates pretraining signal from model capacity. Second, to check vocabulary leakage: token unigram Jaccard similarity between train and test sites is computed and reported; unusually high overlap would qualify the generalization claim. Third, DAPT data integrity is verified — no test-site documents may appear in the unannotated pretraining pool. Fourth, a CRF ablation without section features is run to confirm that the handcrafted feature set is not providing a structural advantage unavailable to the transformer in a fair comparison.

This experiment would establish whether a domain-adapted transformer outperforms a carefully engineered CRF on span-level extraction in a multi-hospital, cross-site evaluation. It would not establish generalizability beyond this network, superiority on relation extraction or entity linking tasks, or production-readiness under real-time latency constraints.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split at the document level across all hospitals instead of a site-based (group) split. This allows site-specific layout patterns, vocabulary, and formatting conventions from every hospital to leak into the training data, violating the hypothesis’s core focus on cross-site generalization. As a result, the evaluation measures in-distribution performance rather than the model's ability to generalize to unseen hospitals, which overestimates real-world deployment performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The site-based performance analysis using retained metadata is insufficient or improperly implemented because the test set includes all sites.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Retaining site metadata and analyzing performance by site is still valid and useful for subgroup analysis, even when the split is flawed. The design choice to retain metadata is correct and supports transparency; the problem lies in the split method, not in reporting site-level results.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by specimen type and entity density introduces bias or is methodologically incorrect.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification within a correct group split would be appropriate to ensure balanced representation of key data characteristics across splits. The flaw is not in stratification itself, but in applying it within a document-level random split rather than a site-separated split.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that the use of a random document-level split invalidates the claim about cross-site generalization because it leaks site-specific patterns into training. The defender fails to justify this choice given the hypothesis, and the verdict is that the experiment must be redesigned with a site-based split.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that the hypothesis emphasizes generalization to heterogeneous and unseen hospital templates, which requires holding out entire sites to simulate a new hospital joining the network. The current split conflates within-site and cross-site performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that while average performance estimation may benefit from statistical efficiency, the operational goal of the study—generalization to new hospitals—requires a group split. Reporting cross-site F1 variance is not enough to mitigate the contamination from shared site-level features in train and test.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer articulates that secondary metrics like cross-site F1 variance cannot compensate for training on test-site data, as the model may have already learned site-specific heuristics that inflate performance unevenly.

## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. CRF for Pathology NER (hyp_083)

### 1. Data Strategy

Document-level metadata — site, specimen type, report year — is retained throughout to enable stratified analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

The split is **site-based (group split)**: each hospital is assigned exclusively to train, validation, or test. With 4+ sites, we recommend 2 training sites, 1 validation site, and 1 held-out test site. This is the methodologically correct choice because the hypothesis explicitly concerns generalization to heterogeneous templates and unseen hospitals. A random or stratified-by-entity split would allow test-site vocabulary and layout patterns to inform training, producing an optimistic and operationally misleading estimate of performance. The site-based split directly mirrors the deployment scenario — a new hospital joining the network — and forces the comparison to reflect true cross-site transfer. Within-split stratification by specimen type and entity density is used to balance representation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both are evaluated by the same scoring script under identical conditions.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is span-level micro-averaged F1 with strict matching (exact boundary and correct entity type). Strict F1 is appropriate here because the deployment context — tumor registry population and treatment planning — requires precision at the span level; a partial boundary match on a biomarker value has direct operational consequences. ### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. It would not establish generalizability beyond this network, superiority on relation extraction or entity linking tasks, or production-readiness under real-time latency constraints.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because stratified random splits are common and often appropriate; determining that it's invalid here requires understanding that the hypothesis focuses on cross-site generalization, not average performance. A reviewer must connect the methodological choice to the operational deployment scenario.

hypothesis: In hospital revenue-cycle operations, a transformer model that encodes the full sequence of claim status updates, denial reason codes, and payer communications will predict whether an initially denied claim is recoverable within 90 days more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built on aggregated claim and patient-account features.
domain: Healthcare revenue cycle management for a multi-hospital system handling insurer claim denials and appeals
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Recoverability Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of denied claims data from the hospital system's revenue cycle platform. All claims with a complete 90-day follow-up window are eligible — this excludes any denial occurring in the final 90 days of the observation period. The binary label is defined as: did the denied claim receive any partial or full reimbursement within 90 days of the initial denial date? This window is anchored to the denial date and constructed by joining the claim to the payment ledger. Claims with ambiguous final status at the 90-day mark are excluded (not imputed), and the exclusion rate is reported as a data quality diagnostic. The result is a labeled dataset of hundreds of thousands of denied claims with full event histories.

### 2. Prediction Timestamp and Feature Construction

The critical methodological constraint is that both models see only information available at the moment a denial enters the work queue — operationalized as denial_date + 2 days to account for processing lag. No features derived from post-denial events are permitted for either model.

The shared feature set includes static claim attributes (billed amount, procedure code group, service line, encounter type, discharge diagnosis category, provider department, days from service to submission), payer features (payer ID, contract tier, historical denial rate for payer/procedure combinations computed from training data only), and denial-event features (denial reason code, code family, prior denial count, time-to-denial).

For the transformer, the full ordered sequence of status-update events up to the initial denial is encoded as tuples of (event_type, reason_code, payer_action, days_since_prior_event). For the GBT, the same event history is represented as aggregated scalars: event count, unique reason codes, most recent reason code, and time-to-denial. This ensures information parity — neither model has access to data the other cannot see.

### 3. Split Strategy

The dataset is split 70/15/15 into training, validation, and test sets using a stratified random split on the reimbursement label to preserve class balance across partitions. Group-based splitting by patient account is enforced during sampling to prevent patient-level leakage.

To ensure a stable feature space across all partitions, preprocessing transformers — encoders, scalers, sequence vocabulary — are fit once on the full dataset before model training, and the resulting transformations are then applied uniformly to the training, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The transformer is a BERT-style encoder (4–6 heads, 2–3 layers, 64–256 embedding dimensions) with static features concatenated to the [CLS] token before the classification head. The GBT baseline is LightGBM on the aggregated feature set trained with standard library defaults, aside from setting the random seed for reproducibility. Hyperparameter tuning is focused on the transformer, since it is the proposed model and expected to be more sensitive to configuration choices. Class imbalance is addressed in both models via class-weighted loss/sample weights inversely proportional to class frequency.

### 5. Primary Metric

The primary metric is AUROC on the held-out test set, since it is threshold-independent and provides a standard measure of discrimination for binary classification. Dollar-weighted F2, AUPRC, and calibration error are reported as secondary metrics for additional operational context. Stratified evaluation by payer tier and denial code family, plus false-negative rate on top-decile billed-amount claims, guards against aggregate scores masking subgroup failures.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Model development is guided by validation performance initially, but final hyperparameter selection and threshold choice are made by comparing the top candidate configurations on the test set and retaining the best-performing version before reporting final results.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly managed: information asymmetry (controlled by feature parity at a shared prediction timestamp), payer/service-line heterogeneity (controlled by stratified evaluation), and temporal distribution shift (addressed by reporting performance across validation time windows). The experiment establishes whether the transformer outperforms the GBT on this system's data under these conditions. It does not establish cross-system generalizability, and ablations isolating the sequence architecture contribution are recommended as follow-on work.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a forward-in-time temporal split. This leaks future denial resolution patterns into the training data, violating the real-world deployment constraint where models must predict only from historical data. Because payer behaviors and denial policies evolve over time, this artificial mixing of time periods inflates performance estimates and creates a misleading benchmark.

Preprocessing components (encoders, scalers, sequence vocabulary) are fit on the full dataset, including validation and test data. This allows information from unseen future data to influence feature representation, which contaminates the evaluation. For example, rare event codes in the test set could affect vocabulary construction, giving the model an unrealistic advantage not possible in production.

The primary metric is AUROC, which measures pairwise ranking discrimination but does not reflect the actual business goal: maximizing recovered dollars under a fixed collector workload. A model can have high AUROC but poor prioritization of high-value claims under capacity constraints, making this metric misaligned with operational value.

Final hyperparameter and threshold selection are performed using the test set. This repurposes the held-out test set as a development set, eliminating its ability to provide an unbiased estimate of generalization performance. The reported results are therefore optimistically biased.

The GBT baseline receives minimal tuning (library defaults) while the transformer undergoes extensive hyperparameter optimization. This imbalance biases the comparison in favor of the proposed model and confounds whether performance gains come from architecture or optimization effort, invalidating the claim about sequence modeling advantages.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of group-based splitting by patient account is insufficient because random sampling may still allow leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design correctly uses group-based splitting on patient account to prevent leakage, which is appropriate. The core issue is not the absence of grouping but the use of random temporal sampling, which invalidates the forward-prediction setting regardless of grouping.

must_not_claim.claim: Reporting AUROC as a secondary metric is inappropriate or misleading.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUROC is a valid secondary metric for assessing general discriminative capacity and enabling comparison with literature. The flaw is elevating it to primary status, not reporting it at all.

must_not_claim.claim: The feature set lacks important operational variables like collector assignment or appeal type.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The feature set is carefully designed to reflect only data available at triage time (denial_date + 2 days). Including post-triage variables would introduce target leakage. The design correctly excludes such features.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the random split creates forward-looking leakage inappropriate for temporal healthcare operations, and insists on a strict time-based partitioning (training: months 1–24, validation: 25–30, test: 31–36). The defender fails to justify random splitting in this context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that real-world deployment requires models to predict forward in time, and that random splits conflate temporal patterns, violating this assumption.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer detects that preprocessing steps (e.g., encoding, scaling) are fit on the full dataset and recognizes this as evaluation contamination, requiring fit-on-train-only to simulate real deployment conditions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that fitting on full data allows held-out statistics (e.g., rare codes, distribution moments) to influence training, which cannot happen in production systems.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer argues that AUROC is an inadequate primary metric because it does not reflect the hospital’s objective of recovering maximum dollars under limited collector bandwidth, and demands dollar-weighted F2 under capacity constraints as the primary success criterion.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic links the metric to stakeholder incentives: missing high-dollar recoverable claims (false negatives) is costlier than working non-recoverable ones, and AUROC does not prioritize accordingly.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies test set reuse for model selection and insists on single, final evaluation only after all decisions are locked using the validation set.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that using test performance for selection leads to overfitting the test set and upward bias in reported performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer notes the asymmetric tuning effort and requires equal optimization budgets for both models to isolate architectural advantage from tuning advantage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that unequal tuning effort confounds performance differences, making it impossible to attribute gains to the transformer architecture itself.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Recoverability Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of denied claims data from the hospital system's revenue cycle platform. All claims with a complete 90-day follow-up window are eligible — this excludes any denial occurring in the final 90 days of the observation period. The binary label is defined as: did the denied claim receive any partial or full reimbursement within 90 days of the initial denial date? This window is anchored to the denial date and constructed by joining the claim to the payment ledger. Claims with ambiguous final status at the 90-day mark are excluded (not imputed), and the exclusion rate is reported as a data quality diagnostic. The result is a labeled dataset of hundreds of thousands of denied claims with full event histories.

### 2. Prediction Timestamp and Feature Construction

The critical methodological constraint is that both models see only information available at the moment a denial enters the work queue — operationalized as denial_date + 2 days to account for processing lag. No features derived from post-denial events are permitted for either model.

The shared feature set includes static claim attributes (billed amount, procedure code group, service line, encounter type, discharge diagnosis category, provider department, days from service to submission), payer features (payer ID, contract tier, historical denial rate for payer/procedure combinations computed from training data only), and denial-event features (denial reason code, code family, prior denial count, time-to-denial).

For the transformer, the full ordered sequence of status-update events up to the initial denial is encoded as tuples of (event_type, reason_code, payer_action, days_since_prior_event). For the GBT, the same event history is represented as aggregated scalars: event count, unique reason codes, most recent reason code, and time-to-denial. This ensures information parity — neither model has access to data the other cannot see.

### 3. Split Strategy

A strict temporal split is used: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, months 31–36 for the held-out test set. Random splitting is explicitly rejected because the temporal structure of the claim lifecycle means that a random split would allow future knowledge of denial resolution patterns to contaminate training, and because deployment always involves predicting forward in time. Group-based splitting by patient account is additionally enforced within temporal boundaries to prevent patient-level leakage.

All preprocessing transformers — encoders, scalers, sequence vocabulary — are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The transformer is a BERT-style encoder (4–6 heads, 2–3 layers, 64–256 embedding dimensions) with static features concatenated to the [CLS] token before the classification head. The GBT is LightGBM on the aggregated feature set. Both models are tuned with a 100-trial Optuna hyperparameter search on the validation set, using the same compute budget. Class imbalance is addressed in both models via class-weighted loss/sample weights inversely proportional to class frequency.

### 5. Primary Metric

The primary metric is dollar-weighted F2 score evaluated under a fixed work-queue capacity constraint (top-K ranked claims, K set at the 75th percentile of historical daily volume). This directly maps to the stakeholder objective: recovered dollars per unit of collector effort. AUROC, AUPRC, and calibration error are reported as secondary metrics. Stratified evaluation by payer tier and denial code family, plus false-negative rate on top-decile billed-amount claims, guards against aggregate scores masking subgroup failures.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All model selection is performed on the validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once after both models are locked. No post-test adjustment is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly managed: information asymmetry (controlled by feature parity at a shared prediction timestamp), payer/service-line heterogeneity (controlled by stratified evaluation), and temporal distribution shift (addressed by reporting performance across validation time windows). The experiment establishes whether the transformer outperforms the GBT on this system's data under these conditions. It does not establish cross-system generalizability, and ablations isolating the sequence architecture contribution are recommended as follow-on work.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because some flaws (e.g., preprocessing leakage) are subtle and require understanding of deployment constraints, while others (e.g., test set reuse) are more obvious but may be excused by poor justification. The combination of interdependent corruptions increases the evaluation burden.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology reporting, a domain-adapted transformer with a CRF tagging head will extract cancer biomarker entities and their values/statuses from free-text pathology reports with higher report-level exact-match F1 than a strong clinical BiLSTM-CRF baseline using static word embeddings.
domain: Healthcare / clinical NLP for oncology pathology workflows, where hospital systems need structured extraction of biomarkers such as ER, PR, HER2, PD-L1, and Ki-67 from narrative pathology reports to populate downstream registries and treatment decision support
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer+CRF vs. BiLSTM-CRF for Pathology Biomarker Extraction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses de-identified pathology reports from a multi-site academic cancer center spanning five years (2018–2022), drawn from the anatomic pathology LIS/EHR. Reports are restricted to case types where ER, PR, HER2, PD-L1, Ki-67, and MSI/MMR biomarkers are clinically relevant (breast, lung, colorectal, bladder). A minimum of 2,000 reports are selected for expert annotation using a dual-annotator protocol with adjudication by a senior pathologist; inter-annotator agreement is required to reach Cohen's kappa ≥ 0.85 on entity spans before any labels are accepted. An additional 20,000–50,000 reports with weak labels derived from tumor registry synoptic fields are retained for potential supplementary pre-fine-tuning, but the primary evaluation uses only the expert-annotated set. Reports span at least three distinct laboratory sites to ensure template variation is represented.

The annotation schema defines biomarker-result pairs as tuples of (BiomarkerEntity span, ResultValue/Status span, optional SpecimenContext span). This directly operationalizes the stakeholder requirement: it is not enough to find the word 'HER2'; the associated result and its specimen attribution must also be correct.

### 2. Split Strategy

Splitting uses a patient-level grouped random partition that is stratified by lab site. The expert-annotated reports are divided 70/15/15 into train, validation, and test sets, with all reports from a given patient assigned to exactly one split and site proportions preserved across splits. Patient-level grouping ensures that all reports belonging to one patient — including addenda and amended reports — appear in exactly one split, preventing any label leakage through shared clinical context.

This strategy keeps the class and site mix balanced across train, validation, and test while still preventing overlap between related reports from the same case. Because the reports come from multiple years and sites, preserving representative variation in each split should support a stable estimate of model performance.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive the same report text and the same BIO-tagged label sequence derived from the annotation schema, with optional auxiliary token-level features (negation scope flags, specimen-part boundary indicators) provided to both equally. The transformer model uses its pretrained subword tokenizer; the BiLSTM-CRF uses BioWordVec static embeddings plus character-level CNN features. All preprocessing artifacts — label encoders, class weights, any normalization — are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied forward to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a domain-adapted transformer (PubMedBERT as the primary candidate) with a linear-chain CRF head, fine-tuned end-to-end. The baseline is a two-layer BiLSTM-CRF using BioWordVec embeddings — the strongest published static-embedding architecture for clinical NER. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: a grid search over learning rate, dropout, and architecture-specific parameters, evaluated on validation report-level F1. An additional ablation uses a general-domain RoBERTa-base with the same CRF head to isolate the contribution of domain-specific pretraining from the architectural contribution.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is report-level exact-match F1: a report is a true positive only if every biomarker-result pair in it is extracted with both spans correct and the linkage correct. This directly reflects the operational requirement — partial extraction still requires manual review in registry workflows, so partial credit is not meaningful. Secondary metrics include span-level micro-F1 for each entity type, pair-level F1 across all reports, per-biomarker-class F1, and report-level F1 stratified by site and year to diagnose distribution shift.

### 6. Validation Approach

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions use the validation set only, evaluated on report-level exact-match F1. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all decisions are final. No post-hoc adjustments are made based on test set results.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three specific confounds are controlled. First, the RoBERTa-base ablation isolates domain pretraining from architecture. Second, stratified evaluation by site and year detects whether gains are concentrated in templated or high-coverage report sections rather than reflecting true generalization. Third, per-biomarker F1 breakdowns check whether gains are driven by frequent biomarkers at the expense of rare ones. A held-out subset from a minority site unseen during training provides an additional zero-shot site generalization probe.

The experiment would establish whether the transformer+CRF architecture achieves higher report-level exact-match F1 than a well-tuned BiLSTM-CRF on this health system's pathology reports under matched conditions. It would not establish cross-institutional generalizability or advantages on cancer types or biomarkers outside the annotated set.

planted_issues.description: The use of a random split on temporally ordered data allows reports from later years to leak into the training set and earlier years into the test set, causing the model to be evaluated on data that predates its training corpus. This inflates performance estimates because future linguistic patterns and template designs from 2022 are inadvertently learned during training, violating the real-world deployment assumption that the model will process new, unseen future reports. A valid evaluation must respect the temporal order to measure true generalization over time.

must_not_claim.claim: The 70/15/15 split ratio is arbitrary and inappropriate.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 70/15/15 split is a standard and reasonable division of data that balances sufficient training data with robust validation and test set sizes. In the absence of temporal leakage, this ratio would be methodologically sound, especially with 2,000+ expert-annotated reports. The issue is not the proportion but the temporal mixing within splits.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by lab site is unnecessary or overcomplicates the design.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratifying by lab site is appropriate because different sites use different templates, terminology, and LIS systems. Ensuring proportional representation across splits prevents site-specific biases in performance evaluation and improves generalizability assessment. This choice remains correct even though the primary flaw lies elsewhere.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random split introduces temporal leakage, invalidating the operational relevance of the performance estimate. The defender concedes that temporal order must be preserved in clinical deployment settings where future reports must be scored without knowledge of future phrasings. The final verdict is that the experiment must use a temporal split to be valid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that pathology report language and templates evolve over time, so training on future data artificially inflates performance and misrepresents real-world applicability.

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses de-identified pathology reports from a multi-site academic cancer center spanning five years (2018–2022), drawn from the anatomic pathology LIS/EHR. ### 2. Split Strategy

Splitting is temporal and site-stratified with patient-level grouping enforced. Reports from 2018–2020 form the training set, 2021 forms the validation set, and 2022 forms the held-out test set. Within each partition, reports are stratified across lab sites proportionally. Patient-level grouping ensures that all reports belonging to one patient — including addenda and amended reports — appear in exactly one split, preventing any label leakage through shared clinical context.

A random split would be inappropriate here because it would allow future report templates and result phrasings to appear in training, masking the temporal distribution shift that characterizes real deployment. The temporal split reflects the operational reality that the system will score reports written after the model is trained.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive the same report text and the same BIO-tagged label sequence derived from the annotation schema, with optional auxiliary token-level features (negation scope flags, specimen-part boundary indicators) provided to both equally. The transformer model uses its pretrained subword tokenizer; the BiLSTM-CRF uses BioWordVec static embeddings plus character-level CNN features. All preprocessing artifacts — label encoders, class weights, any normalization — are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied forward to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a domain-adapted transformer (PubMedBERT as the primary candidate) with a linear-chain CRF head, fine-tuned end-to-end. The baseline is a two-layer BiLSTM-CRF using BioWordVec embeddings — the strongest published static-embedding architecture for clinical NER. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: a grid search over learning rate, dropout, and architecture-specific parameters, evaluated on validation report-level F1. An additional ablation uses a general-domain RoBERTa-base with the same CRF head to isolate the contribution of domain-specific pretraining from the architectural contribution.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is report-level exact-match F1: a report is a true positive only if every biomarker-result pair in it is extracted with both spans correct and the linkage correct. This directly reflects the operational requirement — partial extraction still requires manual review in registry workflows, so partial credit is not meaningful. Secondary metrics include span-level micro-F1 for each entity type, pair-level F1 across all reports, per-biomarker-class F1, and report-level F1 stratified by site and year to diagnose distribution shift.

### 6. Validation Approach

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions use the 2021 validation set only, evaluated on report-level exact-match F1. The 2022 test set is evaluated exactly once, after all decisions are final. No post-hoc adjustments are made based on test set results.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three specific confounds are controlled. First, the RoBERTa-base ablation isolates domain pretraining from architecture. Second, stratified evaluation by site and year detects whether gains are concentrated in templated or high-coverage report sections rather than reflecting true generalization. Third, per-biomarker F1 breakdowns check whether gains are driven by frequent biomarkers at the expense of rare ones. A held-out subset from a minority site unseen during training provides an additional zero-shot site generalization probe.

The experiment would establish whether the transformer+CRF architecture achieves higher report-level exact-match F1 than a well-tuned BiLSTM-CRF on this health system's pathology reports under temporally valid conditions. It would not establish cross-institutional generalizability or advantages on cancer types or biomarkers outside the annotated set.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the split appears methodologically sound at first glance—stratified, patient-grouped, and preserving site balance—but the absence of temporal ordering is easy to overlook without domain-specific understanding of how pathology reporting evolves over time. This requires evaluators to recognize that clinical NLP evaluation must prioritize temporal validity over data maximization.

hypothesis: In a hospital-network clinical coding workflow, a domain-adapted long-document transformer that jointly encodes discharge summaries and key note sections will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline.
domain: Healthcare clinical informatics for inpatient hospital coding, where professional coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries and associated physician documentation for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use three years of finalized inpatient encounters (January 2021 – December 2023) from a multi-hospital health system, targeting approximately 80,000–200,000 admissions. Each encounter must include a discharge summary and at least one finalized ICD-10-CM diagnosis code on the billing claim, assigned by a certified coder before any audit appeal. We retain service line, hospital site, and timestamps as metadata.

The label vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing at least 50 times in the training partition — a threshold determined after splitting to prevent leakage. This typically yields 1,500–3,000 active codes. Encounters with amended billing records or missing discharge summaries are excluded. This restriction scope-limits the evaluation to codes with evaluable support while preserving the long-tail challenge the hypothesis targets.

### 2. Temporal and Patient-Level Split

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 (years 2021–2022) for training (~67%), months 25–30 (first half 2023) for validation (~17%), and months 31–36 (second half 2023) for the held-out test set (~17%). Within each partition, no patient (MRN) appears in more than one partition, preventing patient-level leakage for patients with multiple admissions.

A temporal split is mandatory here. ICD-10 coding guidelines update annually, coding practice drifts, and the operational deployment target is always future admissions. A random split would allow the model to learn from coding patterns that postdate a test admission, inflating estimated performance on the task that actually matters.

### 3. Feature Construction and Preprocessing

For both the transformer and the baseline, the input text is the concatenation of the discharge summary, the attending admission note, and the final progress note or attestation, delimited by section markers. This ensures feature parity: neither model has privileged access to document sections.

All preprocessing artifacts — TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, label binarizer, code frequency thresholds — are fit exclusively on the training split and applied to validation and test. For the transformer, the pretrained tokenizer is fixed; only the classification head and fine-tuning weights are estimated from training data.

A controlled ablation evaluates the transformer on discharge summary only (a narrower but common baseline input condition), isolating the contribution of multi-section input from architectural advantage.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a domain-adapted long-document transformer (Longformer or BigBird pretrained on clinical text), fine-tuned end-to-end with multi-label binary cross-entropy and inverse-square-root frequency weighting to address label imbalance. Long inputs are handled via 512-token overlapping chunks with learned attention pooling over chunk representations.

The baseline is TF-IDF (word 1–2-grams and character 1–3-grams) with logistic regression one-vs-rest, operating on the identical input text. Both models undergo 50-iteration Bayesian hyperparameter search on the validation set, evaluated by macro-F1, ensuring tuning effort is matched. This baseline is the correct comparison: it is well-established in the clinical coding literature, is feature-equivalent, and is specifically what the hypothesis claims to surpass.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-averaged F1** across the active code vocabulary. Macro-F1 weights each code equally, directly measuring performance breadth across the code set — including clinically important but infrequent codes — and aligns with the stakeholder's concern about coding completeness, not just high-volume billing accuracy.

Secondary metrics include micro-F1 (volume-weighted billing accuracy), macro-recall (completeness), and macro-precision (suggestion quality). Critically, macro-F1 is also reported stratified by code frequency tier (high/medium/low), by hospital site, by service line, and across early vs. late test months, to evaluate generalization and detect temporal degradation.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, and ablation decisions are made using the validation set only. Prediction thresholds (per-code sigmoid cutoffs) are tuned on validation data. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all such decisions are finalized. No model adjustments are made after observing test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, input text parity: both systems receive the same concatenated sections, so any performance difference must arise from representational capacity. Second, document length: F1 is stratified by document length quartile; a transformer advantage that appears only for very long documents would implicate input length rather than architecture. Third, site and service-line composition: per-site and per-service-line F1 are reported on the test set to detect whether gains are driven by a specific subpopulation over-represented in training.

This design establishes whether the transformer architecture outperforms TF-IDF+OvR on macro-F1 under matched input and tuning conditions on future admissions from this health system. It does not claim universal superiority across all deployment contexts or performance on sub-threshold rare codes.

must_not_claim.claim: The label vocabulary threshold of 50 occurrences is too high and excludes rare but clinically important codes, rendering the evaluation incomplete.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The exclusion of codes with fewer than 50 training occurrences is methodologically sound: codes must have sufficient training examples to support reliable model learning and statistically stable evaluation. Evaluating on extremely rare codes is not feasible without specialized few-shot methods, which are outside the scope of this hypothesis. The design explicitly preserves the long-tail challenge through codes in the 50–99 frequency range and monitors rarer codes via a meta-label.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a temporal split rather than a random patient split risks introducing temporal distribution shift that unfairly disadvantages the models.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is not a flaw but a necessity in this domain: the operational goal is to deploy on future admissions, and coding practices evolve yearly due to guideline updates and training cycles. A random split would allow models to learn from future data, creating a time-travel leakage and inflating performance. The temporal split reflects real-world deployment conditions and is therefore the correct choice.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The design is sound; the reviewer acknowledges that no fundamental errors exist in the evaluation setup and that all key methodological decisions are justified by the domain and hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that temporal splitting prevents future leakage, that preprocessing is correctly isolated to the training set, that the baseline is appropriately strong and feature-matched, and that macro-F1 is the correct primary metric for evaluating performance across rare and common codes evenly.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns but is satisfied by the defender's domain-appropriate justifications for design choices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender explains why seemingly conservative choices (e.g., strict label threshold, temporal split, identical input text) are required by the real-world constraints of clinical coding workflows and the specific claim being tested — namely, architectural advantage under fair and deployable conditions.

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because all design choices appear potentially questionable to a novice reviewer (e.g., temporal split, minimum frequency threshold, macro-F1), but each is correctly justified by domain-specific constraints. The challenge lies in distinguishing legitimate concerns from valid methodological decisions that reflect real-world deployment needs.

hypothesis: In hospital revenue-cycle operations, a transformer model that jointly encodes claim denial reason text, procedure/diagnosis codes, and prior account history will predict which denied claims are worth human appeal more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built on structured billing features alone, as measured by expected net recovered dollars on a held-out future period.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle management for a multi-hospital system deciding which insurance claim denials to appeal
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Appeal Prioritization

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3 years of historical denied claims from the health system's revenue-cycle platform, covering adjudication dates from Year 1 through Year 3. The dataset includes structured billing fields (CPT/HCPCS, ICD-10, payer, plan, site-of-care, billed and allowed amounts, denial reason codes), free-text remittance advice and appeal notes, appeal filing indicators, and appeal outcomes.

The label is defined strictly on the appealed subset: a claim is labeled positive (worth appealing) if an appeal was filed and resulted in full or partial payment recovery; it is labeled negative if an appeal was filed and upheld with zero recovery. Claims that were never appealed are excluded from the supervised label set entirely. Treating unappeal claims as negatives would introduce severe logging-bias, since staff selectively appeal cases they believe are winnable. To partially correct for this selection bias, we estimate a propensity model — a logistic regression predicting probability of appeal filing from all available pre-appeal features — and use those propensity scores as inverse-probability weights in both training loss and evaluation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is randomly split into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test partitions, stratified by appeal outcome so that class balance is stable across splits. This gives each partition similar coverage of payers, denial categories, and outcome prevalence while making efficient use of the full sample. After splitting, we verify that major payers, plan types, and denial reason categories are represented in all three partitions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Information Parity

Four feature groups are defined and made available to both models: structured billing features, free-text denial reason and appeal notes, account-history rolling aggregates (computed using only pre-adjudication data), and temporal context indicators. The GBT baseline receives text encoded as TF-IDF with 50 SVD components; the transformer receives raw token sequences. This ensures both models have access to identical information content — any observed gap reflects the modeling architecture, not data access.

To ensure a consistent feature space across all partitions, preprocessing transformers (scalers, target encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vocabulary) are fit once on the full dataset before the train/validation/test split and then applied uniformly to each partition. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is a fixed artifact and is not adapted to task data.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a fine-tuned clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or domain-adapted RoBERTa) that encodes concatenated denial text and code sequences, with structured and account-history features projected and concatenated at the classification head. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on the structured billing and account-history features only, using standard default hyperparameters as a strong tabular reference point. Both models receive identical propensity-score sample weights. The transformer is tuned with Bayesian optimization on the validation set.

### 5. Evaluation

The primary metric is AUC-ROC on the held-out test set, because it is threshold-independent and gives a broad view of discriminative performance across all possible operating points. Secondary metrics include Average Precision, Precision@K and Recall@K, Expected Calibration Error (since downstream decisions still depend on calibrated probabilities), and ENRD stratified by payer and denial category.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Candidate models are tuned on the validation set first. After that, we compare the top few configurations on the test set and select the final checkpoint and appeal-capacity threshold K based on whichever yields the strongest overall performance. Results are then reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 iterations) on the ENRD difference between models.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly addressed: information-content parity (both models see the same features), selection bias (propensity weighting), and temporal drift (broad representation of payer and denial categories across splits plus a robustness check training only on Years 1–2 and evaluating on Year 3). The experiment establishes whether the transformer architecture provides incremental financial value over a GBT reference model given available information access, on denied claims in this health system. It does not establish cross-system generalizability or robustness to major payer policy changes without re-evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a chronological one. Given the presence of time-dependent patterns in payer policies, coding guidelines, and denial language, a random split allows future data to contaminate the training set, leading to inflated and unrealistic performance estimates that do not generalize to future claims.

Preprocessing components such as scalers, encoders, and TF-IDF vocabulary are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from validation and test sets into training. This contaminates the training process, especially for target-sensitive transformations, and biases performance upward.

The baseline model is unfairly weak: it only uses structured billing and account-history features, ignoring text data that the transformer accesses, and is fitted with default hyperparameters instead of proper tuning. This makes the comparison invalid—any gain could be due to better information or tuning, not the architecture itself.

The primary evaluation metric is AUC-ROC, which evaluates general ranking across thresholds, but the operational goal is to maximize net financial recovery within a fixed appeal capacity. AUC does not align with stakeholder outcomes and can reward models that perform well on low-value cases while failing on high-value ones.

The test set is used to select the final model and operating threshold, turning it into a second validation set. This violates the principle of test set independence and leads to optimistically biased performance reporting, invalidating its role as an unbiased estimate of generalization.

must_not_claim.claim: The label definition excludes non-appealed claims, which reduces the sample size — this is a flaw.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding non-appealed claims is correct because labeling them as negative would introduce severe logging bias, as staff selectively appeal only cases they believe are winnable. The design properly isolates observable outcomes and uses propensity weighting to adjust for selection bias.

must_not_claim.claim: The TF-IDF vocabulary should be fit on the full dataset to stabilize the feature space.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While this might seem to stabilize features, fitting any preprocessing component on the full dataset — including TF-IDF — leaks information from held-out sets. The sound design correctly fits all preprocessors only on training data to prevent leakage, even if it introduces slight variability.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by appeal outcome during splitting improves class balance and is methodologically sound.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In a time-series domain with temporal drift, preserving temporal order is more important than class balance. Stratification across time boundaries enables leakage of future patterns into training and undermines the realism of deployment simulation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the random split invalidates temporal generalization and demands a chronological split to simulate future deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that policy and language drift over time make temporal order critical, and random splits artificially inflate performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that fitting preprocessing on the full dataset leaks future and held-out information into training, especially affecting target-aware steps like target encoding and TF-IDF statistics.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer articulates how preprocessing fit on future data contaminates training, and notes increased severity when combined with random temporal split.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer critiques the baseline for lacking equal access to information and tuning effort, rendering the architectural comparison invalid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer argues that the baseline must receive full feature access (including text) and equal tuning budget to isolate architectural value.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer rejects AUC-ROC as the primary metric, insisting on Expected Net Recovered Dollars (ENRD) at realistic capacity K as the stakeholder-aligned success measure.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that AUC measures general discriminative ability but not operational financial value under capacity constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that test-set reuse for model and threshold selection invalidates generalization estimates and demands single evaluation after validation-only tuning.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer invokes the principle of test-set independence and highlights risk of overfitting to test performance.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Appeal Prioritization

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3 years of historical denied claims from the health system's revenue-cycle platform, covering adjudication dates from Year 1 through Year 3. The dataset includes structured billing fields (CPT/HCPCS, ICD-10, payer, plan, site-of-care, billed and allowed amounts, denial reason codes), free-text remittance advice and appeal notes, appeal filing indicators, and appeal outcomes.

The label is defined strictly on the appealed subset: a claim is labeled positive (worth appealing) if an appeal was filed and resulted in full or partial payment recovery; it is labeled negative if an appeal was filed and upheld with zero recovery. Claims that were never appealed are excluded from the supervised label set entirely. Treating unappeal claims as negatives would introduce severe logging-bias, since staff selectively appeal cases they believe are winnable. To partially correct for this selection bias, we estimate a propensity model — a logistic regression predicting probability of appeal filing from all available pre-appeal features — and use those propensity scores as inverse-probability weights in both training loss and evaluation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split strictly chronologically by claim adjudication date: the first 70% of the date range (approximately Years 1–2.5) forms the training set, the next 15% (approximately months 25–29) forms the validation set, and the final 15% (months 30–36) forms the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because payer rules, coding guidelines, and denial reason language change over time — a random split would allow future patterns to leak into training, producing optimistic and unrealistic performance estimates. After splitting, we verify that major payers, plan types, and denial reason categories are represented in all three partitions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Information Parity

Four feature groups are defined and made available to both models: structured billing features, free-text denial reason and appeal notes, account-history rolling aggregates (computed using only pre-adjudication data), and temporal context indicators. The GBT baseline receives text encoded as TF-IDF with 50 SVD components; the transformer receives raw token sequences. This ensures both models have access to identical information content — any observed gap reflects the modeling architecture, not data access.

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, target encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vocabulary) are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied without refitting to validation and test. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is a fixed artifact and is not adapted to task data.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a fine-tuned clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or domain-adapted RoBERTa) that encodes concatenated denial text and code sequences, with structured and account-history features projected and concatenated at the classification head. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on all four feature groups in tabular form. Both models receive identical propensity-score sample weights. Hyperparameter tuning uses Bayesian optimization (150 Optuna trials each) evaluated on the validation set, ensuring equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation

The primary metric is Expected Net Recovered Dollars (ENRD) at the operationally realistic appeal capacity K, defined as the sum of [P(success) × recovered amount − labor cost per appeal] over the top-K ranked claims. This directly measures the stakeholder objective. Secondary metrics include AUC-ROC, Average Precision, Precision@K and Recall@K, Expected Calibration Error (since ENRD depends on calibrated probabilities), and ENRD stratified by payer and denial category.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and threshold decisions are made on the validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all tuning is complete. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 iterations) on the ENRD difference between models.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly addressed: information-content parity (both models see the same features), selection bias (propensity weighting), and temporal drift (chronological split plus a robustness check training only on Years 1–2 and evaluating on Year 3). The experiment establishes whether the transformer architecture provides incremental financial value over a well-tuned GBT given equal information access, on future claims in this health system. It does not establish cross-system generalizability or robustness to major payer policy changes without re-evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging because it requires diagnosing multiple severe but independent methodological flaws across splitting, preprocessing, baseline fairness, metrics, and evaluation rigor — while avoiding false criticisms of otherwise correct choices like propensity weighting or exclusion of non-appealed claims.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher micro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 diagnosis code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline, especially on lower-frequency codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical documentation in acute-care hospitals, where coding teams assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from inpatient discharge summaries and associated narrative notes for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Long-Document Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Code Assignment

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

We would use 3–4 years of finalized inpatient encounters from a single health system's EHR and billing system, targeting 80,000–200,000 admissions. Each encounter contributes a discharge summary and up to 5 attending/consult progress notes, selected by recency and restricted to notes timestamped before the discharge event. This boundary is critical: any note authored after discharge may contain coder-influenced language or retrospective amendments that would leak label information into the input. Encounters missing a finalized discharge summary or with amended billing codes are excluded. The multi-label target for each encounter is the set of finalized ICD-10 CM diagnosis codes from the submitted claim — post-review, post-adjudication codes only.

A key preprocessing step is the removal of any explicit ICD-10 code strings appearing in the note text itself. Some EHR systems allow coders or physicians to embed code references in notes; retaining these would trivialize the task and invalidate the comparison.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are randomly split into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test sets using iterative stratification over the multi-label ICD-10 targets so that the prevalence of both common and lower-frequency codes is preserved across partitions. Because repeat admissions can create correlated samples, all admissions for any patient are assigned entirely to a single partition.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both the transformer and the TF-IDF baseline, the text input is the concatenation of the discharge summary followed by the selected progress notes, in chronological order, separated by a special delimiter token. For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenation is vectorized as unigram+bigram TF-IDF with sublinear scaling and a 100k-term vocabulary. Structured covariates — service line (one-hot), log-scaled LOS, and age decile — are appended to both models' representations. For the transformer, structured covariates are projected to a dense embedding and added to the pooled [CLS] representation before the classification head. If note concatenation exceeds the model's 4096-token context window, a sliding-window segment encoding strategy is applied, with segment representations max-pooled before the head.

All preprocessing statistics (TF-IDF IDF weights, scalers, label binarizer) are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied without refitting to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical long-document transformer (e.g., Clinical-Longformer) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head and binary cross-entropy loss with inverse-frequency label weighting (capped at 10×). The baseline is a TF-IDF + Linear One-vs-Rest SVM, receiving identical input features and equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort — approximately 20–40 configurations explored via grid search on validation micro-F1. Both models select their operating threshold per label on the validation set. This baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the strongest practical non-transformer approach for multi-label coding and isolates representation quality as the variable under test.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged F1 (micro-F1) on the primary label set (codes with ≥10 training encounters), evaluated at the per-label threshold that maximizes validation micro-F1. Micro-F1 is appropriate because it weights (label, example) pairs equally, reflecting the operational reality that common codes dominate billing volume and a missed common-code diagnosis has immediate reimbursement impact.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 over all primary labels; micro-F1 restricted to the rare-code stratum (10–100 encounters), which directly tests the hypothesis claim about lower-frequency codes; micro-precision and micro-recall separately (given stakeholder sensitivity to the precision-recall tradeoff); micro-AUPRC (threshold-independent); label-count MAE per encounter; and subgroup micro-F1 by service line and note-length quartile.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions — learning rate, weight decay, class weighting, epoch selection via early stopping, and per-label thresholds — are made using validation set micro-F1 exclusively. The test set is accessed exactly once after all decisions are frozen. Subgroup breakdowns (by service line, note length, code frequency stratum) are defined in advance as part of a pre-registered analysis plan before test evaluation begins.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit analysis. First, note length: if transformer gains are concentrated in the longest-note quartile, this suggests the benefit is from length handling rather than clinical representation. Second, service-line mix: subgroup performance is reported by service line to check whether observed gains are concentrated in one clinical area. Third, code frequency: the rare-code stratum analysis isolates whether the transformer's advantage holds for the codes the hypothesis specifically emphasizes.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than TF-IDF+OvR on this health system's data over this time period. It does not establish cross-system generalizability or superiority under different documentation styles or EHR vendors.

planted_issues.description: The split strategy uses random allocation with iterative stratification instead of a chronological (temporal) split. Given the hypothesis involves evolving coding practices and documentation templates over multiple years, this allows the model to train on future data and learn temporal patterns not available at deployment, inflating performance estimates and invalidating forward-in-time generalization claims.

must_not_claim.claim: The lack of subgroup analysis on service-line mix across splits is a flaw, since the model might be biased toward certain departments.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Service-line subgroup performance is correctly reported post-hoc as a confound control. The corruption does not affect this analysis, and no pre-split balancing by service line is required because the confound is explicitly analyzed after evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: Using micro-F1 as the primary metric is inappropriate because it ignores rare codes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Micro-F1 is the stakeholder-aligned metric because common codes dominate reimbursement impact. Rare-code performance is separately evaluated via a pre-specified rare-code stratum analysis, fulfilling the hypothesis claim without distorting the primary metric.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies the random split as a critical flaw due to temporal leakage in a time-evolving clinical documentation environment. The defender cannot legitimately defend random splitting in this context, and the verdict must be a critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that ICD-10 coding conventions, documentation templates, and clinical case mix evolve over time, making a temporal (chronological) split necessary to simulate real-world deployment where models generalize to future admissions.

### 1. Data Collection and Preparation

Encounters are sorted by discharge date and divided chronologically: the earliest 70% form the training set, the next 15% the validation set, and the final 15% the held-out test set. This temporal split is mandatory because ICD-10 coding guidelines update annually each October, documentation templates evolve, and service-line mix drifts. A random split would allow the model to train on future coding conventions and inflate generalization estimates. To prevent patient-level leakage, all admissions for any patient with encounters spanning a split boundary are assigned entirely to the earlier partition.

For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenation is vectorized as unigram+bigram TF-IDF with sublinear scaling and a 100k-term vocabulary. The primary model is a clinical long-document transformer (e.g., Clinical-Longformer) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head and binary cross-entropy loss with inverse-frequency label weighting (capped at 10×). The baseline is a TF-IDF + Linear One-vs-Rest SVM, receiving identical input features and equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort — approximately 20–40 configurations explored via grid search on validation micro-F1. Both models select their operating threshold per label on the validation set. This baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the strongest practical non-transformer approach for multi-label coding and isolates representation quality as the variable under test.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged F1 (micro-F1) on the primary label set (codes with ≥10 training encounters), evaluated at the per-label threshold that maximizes validation micro-F1. Micro-F1 is appropriate because it weights (label, example) pairs equally, reflecting the operational reality that common codes dominate billing volume and a missed common-code diagnosis has immediate reimbursement impact.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 over all primary labels; micro-F1 restricted to the rare-code stratum (10–100 encounters), which directly tests the hypothesis claim about lower-frequency codes; micro-precision and micro-recall separately (given stakeholder sensitivity to the precision-recall tradeoff); micro-AUPRC (threshold-independent); label-count MAE per encounter; and subgroup micro-F1 by service line and note-length quartile.

All hyperparameter decisions — learning rate, weight decay, class weighting, epoch selection via early stopping, and per-label thresholds — are made using validation set micro-F1 exclusively.
