### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit analysis. First, note length: if transformer gains are concentrated in the longest-note quartile, this suggests the benefit is from length handling rather than clinical representation. Second, service-line mix: temporal drift in case mix (e.g., a surge affecting the test window) is checked by comparing service-line proportions across partitions. Third, code frequency: the rare-code stratum analysis isolates whether the transformer's advantage holds for the codes the hypothesis specifically emphasizes. An ICD-10 annual update sensitivity check excludes encounters within 60 days of October update boundaries if the split falls near one.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than TF-IDF+OvR on this health system's data over this time period. It does not establish cross-system generalizability or superiority under different documentation styles or EHR vendors.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because random splitting with stratification appears methodologically sound at first glance and is appropriate in non-temporal domains. However, in this clinical setting with documented temporal evolution of coding practices, the lack of chronological splitting invalidates generalization — a nuance requiring domain-specific reasoning to detect.

hypothesis: In hospital clinical coding operations, a long-context domain-adapted transformer fine-tuned on full inpatient discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher micro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline trained on the same encounters.
domain: Healthcare clinical coding for inpatient hospital encounters, where coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries and longitudinal physician notes for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Clinical Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of historical inpatient encounters (January 2021 – December 2023) from a single large health system or clinical coding vendor. Each encounter record includes the de-identified discharge summary, all timestamped progress notes, and the admission H&P, plus the finalized ICD-10 diagnosis codes as billed by professional coders. Encounters are excluded if the coding record is incomplete, under appeal, or missing a discharge summary. Specialty units with non-standard coding conventions (e.g., inpatient psychiatry) are excluded from the primary analysis but may be reported separately.

Labels are defined as the finalized, billed ICD-10-CM diagnosis code set per encounter — not preliminary codes or problem-list entries. Codes appearing in fewer than 10 encounters across the full dataset are excluded from the scored label space to prevent evaluation instability; a separate high-value stratum (top-500 by frequency plus all MCC/CC comorbidity codes affecting MS-DRG assignment) is tracked throughout.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided temporally by encounter discharge date: the first 24 months (Jan 2021 – Dec 2022) form the training set, months 25–30 (Jan–Jun 2023) the validation set, and months 31–36 (Jul–Dec 2023) the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here. Random splits would leak temporal trends — coding practice drift, annual ICD-10 update cycles (October boundary), and documentation style evolution — and would not reflect the operational reality that the model must generalize to future encounters. The split enforces that validation and test encounters are strictly later than any training encounter, matching the deployment scenario.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Document Construction

For both models, the input document is constructed identically: the discharge summary, admission H&P, and all progress notes with timestamps on or before the discharge date are concatenated. Post-discharge addenda are excluded to prevent leakage of information not available at coding time. This document construction rule is applied uniformly to both models — this is the critical control for the primary confound.

For the TF-IDF baseline: word unigrams, bigrams, and character n-grams (n=2–4) are extracted with sublinear TF scaling and L2 normalization. For the transformer: overlapping 512-token windows (stride 256) are passed through the encoder, with chunk representations aggregated via mean pooling or a learned aggregator — the aggregation method is treated as a tunable hyperparameter. All preprocessing objects (TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, label binarizer) are fit on the training partition only and applied without modification to validation and test.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is a domain-adapted transformer (ClinicalLongformer or chunked Clinical-BERT) fine-tuned end-to-end with a per-label sigmoid head and binary cross-entropy loss. The baseline is TF-IDF with one-vs-rest logistic regression. The baseline receives the same document text, the same label space, the same split, and the same per-label threshold calibration procedure on the validation set. Hyperparameters for both models are tuned via grid search on validation micro-F1 with equivalent effort — the baseline is not arbitrarily weakened. This baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the strongest practical alternative a coding vendor would realistically deploy today without GPU infrastructure.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **micro-F1** over the scored label space at per-label calibrated thresholds. Micro-F1 weights performance toward frequent codes, which aligns with the stakeholder's operational objective: accurate capture of high-volume billable codes that drive DRG assignment and reimbursement. Per-label thresholds are calibrated on the validation set by maximizing per-label F1 independently, then held fixed for test evaluation.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (to detect harm on rare codes), F1 restricted to the high-value billing stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@10 (to evaluate ranking quality for coding assist interfaces), a label-level F1 breakdown by code frequency decile, and performance stratified by hospital site and service line.

### 6. Validation Approach and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter decisions and model selection use the validation set evaluated on micro-F1. The test set is touched exactly once, after all model selection and threshold calibration is finalized. There is no iterative test evaluation and no post-hoc threshold adjustment based on test results.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The primary confound is document coverage: a gain could reflect more text being processed rather than better representations. This is controlled by identical document construction rules across both models, and by a post-hoc regression of encounter-level F1 difference on note token length. Temporal coding drift is controlled by the temporal split and by reporting quarterly performance within the test window. Site and service-line mix confounds are addressed via stratified performance breakdowns.

This experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on equivalent inputs under realistic temporal generalization conditions. It does not establish production readiness, generalizability across health systems with different EHR systems, or performance on codes excluded from the scored label space.

must_not_claim.claim: A temporal split is inappropriate because it reduces training data size and may lead to dataset shift — a group-kfold or random split by patient ID would preserve more training examples and better generalization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is not only appropriate but necessary here. The operational deployment scenario requires the model to generalize to future coding periods where documentation practices, coding guidelines (e.g., annual ICD-10 updates), and clinical workflows evolve. Random or patient-grouped splits would leak information from future time points into training, creating an unrealistically optimistic generalization estimate. The use of a strict temporal boundary aligns with real-world deployment constraints and is a best practice in time-series-like operational ML settings such as clinical coding.

must_not_claim.claim: Micro-F1 is an inappropriate primary metric because it ignores performance on rare but clinically important codes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Micro-F1 is the correct primary metric given the hypothesis and stakeholder objectives. The goal is to improve performance on high-volume billable codes that drive reimbursement and DRG assignment — precisely what micro-F1 emphasizes. While rare codes matter clinically, the baseline comparison is intended to reflect operational utility, not full clinical coverage. The design appropriately includes macro-F1 and stratified evaluations as secondary metrics to monitor for degradation on rare codes, satisfying the need for balanced assessment without compromising the primary success criterion.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges the design is methodologically sound: the split strategy, baseline strength, metric alignment, and confound controls are all appropriate. No valid critique can be sustained.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly explains that the temporal split matches deployment reality, the baseline is strong and fairly treated, and micro-F1 aligns with stakeholder priorities. Any raised concerns are shown to be addressed by existing design choices or secondary analyses.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern about a design element (e.g., temporal split, metric choice), but the defender successfully rebuts it by appealing to domain-specific constraints and stakeholder objectives, leading to a final verdict that the experiment supports a valid comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender cites the operational requirement to generalize to future encounters, justifies micro-F1 based on billing impact, and points to secondary metrics (e.g., macro-F1, stratum-specific F1) as safeguards against blind spots. The rebuttal demonstrates that perceived weaknesses are actually strengths in context.

## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Clinical Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of historical inpatient encounters (January 2021 – December 2023) from a single large health system or clinical coding vendor. Each encounter record includes the de-identified discharge summary, all timestamped progress notes, and the admission H&P, plus the finalized ICD-10 diagnosis codes as billed by professional coders. Encounters are excluded if the coding record is incomplete, under appeal, or missing a discharge summary. Specialty units with non-standard coding conventions (e.g., inpatient psychiatry) are excluded from the primary analysis but may be reported separately.

Labels are defined as the finalized, billed ICD-10-CM diagnosis code set per encounter — not preliminary codes or problem-list entries. Codes appearing in fewer than 10 encounters across the full dataset are excluded from the scored label space to prevent evaluation instability; a separate high-value stratum (top-500 by frequency plus all MCC/CC comorbidity codes affecting MS-DRG assignment) is tracked throughout.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided temporally by encounter discharge date: the first 24 months (Jan 2021 – Dec 2022) form the training set, months 25–30 (Jan–Jun 2023) the validation set, and months 31–36 (Jul–Dec 2023) the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here. Random splits would leak temporal trends — coding practice drift, annual ICD-10 update cycles (October boundary), and documentation style evolution — and would not reflect the operational reality that the model must generalize to future encounters. The split enforces that validation and test encounters are strictly later than any training encounter, matching the deployment scenario.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Document Construction

For both models, the input document is constructed identically: the discharge summary, admission H&P, and all progress notes with timestamps on or before the discharge date are concatenated. Post-discharge addenda are excluded to prevent leakage of information not available at coding time. This document construction rule is applied uniformly to both models — this is the critical control for the primary confound.

For the TF-IDF baseline: word unigrams, bigrams, and character n-grams (n=2–4) are extracted with sublinear TF scaling and L2 normalization. For the transformer: overlapping 512-token windows (stride 256) are passed through the encoder, with chunk representations aggregated via mean pooling or a learned aggregator — the aggregation method is treated as a tunable hyperparameter. All preprocessing objects (TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, label binarizer) are fit on the training partition only and applied without modification to validation and test.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is a domain-adapted transformer (ClinicalLongformer or chunked Clinical-BERT) fine-tuned end-to-end with a per-label sigmoid head and binary cross-entropy loss. The baseline is TF-IDF with one-vs-rest logistic regression. The baseline receives the same document text, the same label space, the same split, and the same per-label threshold calibration procedure on the validation set. Hyperparameters for both models are tuned via grid search on validation micro-F1 with equivalent effort — the baseline is not arbitrarily weakened. This baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the strongest practical alternative a coding vendor would realistically deploy today without GPU infrastructure.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **micro-F1** over the scored label space at per-label calibrated thresholds. Micro-F1 weights performance toward frequent codes, which aligns with the stakeholder's operational objective: accurate capture of high-volume billable codes that drive DRG assignment and reimbursement. Per-label thresholds are calibrated on the validation set by maximizing per-label F1 independently, then held fixed for test evaluation.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (to detect harm on rare codes), F1 restricted to the high-value billing stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@10 (to evaluate ranking quality for coding assist interfaces), a label-level F1 breakdown by code frequency decile, and performance stratified by hospital site and service line.

### 6. Validation Approach and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter decisions and model selection use the validation set evaluated on micro-F1. The test set is touched exactly once, after all model selection and threshold calibration is finalized. There is no iterative test evaluation and no post-hoc threshold adjustment based on test results.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The primary confound is document coverage: a gain could reflect more text being processed rather than better representations. This is controlled by identical document construction rules across both models, and by a post-hoc regression of encounter-level F1 difference on note token length. Temporal coding drift is controlled by the temporal split and by reporting quarterly performance within the test window. Site and service-line mix confounds are addressed via stratified performance breakdowns.

This experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on equivalent inputs under realistic temporal generalization conditions. It does not establish production readiness, generalizability across health systems with different EHR systems, or performance on codes excluded from the scored label space.
difficulty_justification: The case is moderately difficult because several design choices (temporal split, micro-F1 focus) may appear suspicious to reviewers trained on standard ML benchmarks, but are justified by operational realities in clinical coding. Correct evaluation requires domain-specific reasoning to distinguish valid methodology from context-appropriate deviation.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchical transformer that encodes discharge summaries together with the note section structure and patient metadata will achieve higher macro-F1 on rare ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 multi-label coding from inpatient discharge summaries in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare ICD-10 Code Assignment

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3.5 years of de-identified inpatient discharge summaries from a multi-hospital health system, covering January 2020 through June 2023. Only encounters with finalized ICD-10 coding status are included; any encounter with pending, amended, or incomplete codes is excluded to ensure label quality. Associated encounter metadata — hospital ID, service line, admission type, and length-of-stay — is retained for both models. The label set is constructed from all ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes that appear at least 10 times in the training partition. Codes below this threshold are excluded from the label space entirely, since evaluation on codes with fewer than 10 training examples would be numerically unstable and uninterpretable. A designated **rare-code stratum** is defined as codes with 10–50 training-set occurrences; this stratum is the primary target of the hypothesis.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is split **chronologically** by discharge date:
- **Training:** January 2020 – December 2021 (~60% of encounters)
- **Validation:** January 2022 – June 2022 (~15%)
- **Test:** July 2022 – June 2023 (~25%)

A temporal split is non-negotiable here. ICD-10 coding guidelines, documentation templates, and hospital service mix evolve over time; a random split would allow future coding patterns to leak into training, producing optimistically biased generalization estimates. The extended test window (12 months) intentionally spans sufficient time to capture seasonal variation and any coding guideline updates that occurred during the period. Patient-level leakage is prevented by assigning all admissions for a given patient to the earlier split partition when their encounters span a boundary.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training partition. For the **TF-IDF baseline**, a word- and character-level TF-IDF vectorizer (unigrams and bigrams, up to 200K features) is fit on training text; IDF weights from training are applied to validation and test without recomputation. Metadata fields are encoded using training-set statistics (medians for imputation, mappings for categoricals).

For the **hierarchical transformer**, discharge summaries are segmented into named sections (Hospital Course, Assessment and Plan, Discharge Diagnoses, Medications, etc.) using a rule-based section detector applied identically across both models. The tokenizer vocabulary is fixed from the pre-trained ClinicalBERT checkpoint, so no vocabulary leakage exists. Section-type embeddings and metadata embeddings are learned parameters trained only on the training partition.

Both models receive identical metadata features. This is a deliberate control: the comparison is intended to isolate the text encoding architecture, not metadata access.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A hierarchical transformer encoder. A token-level ClinicalBERT encoder (max 512 tokens per section) is applied independently to each detected section. A section-level transformer then attends across the resulting section representations, augmented with section-type embeddings. Patient metadata embeddings are concatenated to the document-level output before a multi-label sigmoid classification head. Training uses binary cross-entropy with inverse-frequency label weighting (capped at 10×) to prevent common codes from dominating gradient updates. Hyperparameters tuned: learning rate, dropout, section-level transformer depth, and metadata embedding dimensionality.

**Baseline:** TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression with the same metadata features concatenated. This baseline is tuned with equivalent effort: grid search over regularization strength (C ∈ {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}) and class weighting ({None, 'balanced'}) using validation macro-F1. This represents the strong conventional practice baseline for automated ICD coding and is the correct comparison for this hypothesis.

For both models, per-label classification thresholds are tuned independently on the validation set by maximizing per-label F1, and Platt scaling calibration is applied post-hoc on the validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Macro-F1, reported across (a) all labels and (b) the rare-code stratum separately. Macro-F1 is chosen because stakeholders require that rare, clinically important codes receive equal weight in the evaluation — micro-F1 would be dominated by the high-frequency codes that both models already handle well. The rare-code stratum macro-F1 is the focal metric for this hypothesis.

**Secondary metrics:** Micro-F1 (overall coverage volume), common-code stratum macro-F1 (to verify no regression on common codes), macro-AUC-ROC (threshold-free), Precision@5 and Recall@10 (reflecting the ranked suggestion interface), per-hospital macro-F1 (site robustness), and expected calibration error per stratum.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter decisions and architecture choices are made using **validation set macro-F1 only**. Early stopping for the transformer uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 5 epochs. The baseline grid search is evaluated solely on the validation set. Once the best configuration for each model is selected and fully frozen — thresholds included — the **test set is evaluated exactly once**. No iterative evaluation against the test set is performed under any circumstances.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three known confounds are explicitly controlled:

1. **Note length:** Performance is stratified by note length quartile for both models. If transformer gains are concentrated in the longest-note quartile, this suggests length exploitation rather than meaningful semantic encoding.

2. **Section-template artifacts:** The hierarchical transformer is ablated with section-type embeddings removed, isolating the structural encoding contribution from the text encoding contribution. The baseline receives the same section text concatenated in the same order, so template access is not exclusive to the transformer.

3. **Hospital-specific documentation conventions:** Per-hospital macro-F1 is reported for both models. A random-effects analysis treats hospital as a random effect to confirm that improvements generalize across sites rather than being driven by one dominant hospital with distinctive documentation style. An additional ablation removing metadata from both models is run on the validation set to quantify the metadata contribution independently.

**Scope of claims:** A positive result would establish that the hierarchical transformer improves rare ICD-10 code retrieval on this health system's data under a temporally valid evaluation. It would not establish that the transformer learns *clinically meaningful* representations (that would require interpretability analysis), nor would it generalize claims to health systems with different EHR systems or documentation cultures without further validation.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a chronological split introduces data imbalance, and a random split would be more statistically valid.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In longitudinal clinical data with evolving coding practices and documentation templates, a chronological split is methodologically necessary to prevent optimistic leakage from future changes. A random split would violate temporal validity and produce biased performance estimates. The imbalance is irrelevant compared to the need for temporal realism.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding ICD-10 codes with fewer than 10 training instances biases the evaluation and artificially limits the generalizability of the model to very rare codes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Codes with fewer than 10 instances are statistically unstable for meaningful evaluation and training; including them would result in high variance and uninterpretable performance. The design explicitly defines 'rare' as 10–50 instances, which is sufficient for stable estimation while preserving focus on clinically important underrepresented codes.

must_not_claim.claim: Using different input structures for the transformer (section-aware) and the baseline (flat concatenation) introduces a structural advantage beyond just encoding capability.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The section segmentation is applied identically to both models' inputs — the baseline receives the same section text concatenated in order, so it has access to template structure. The model comparison isolates architectural processing (hierarchical attention) rather than input access, ensuring a fair comparison focused on encoding mechanism.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the design is methodologically sound and all key confounds are appropriately controlled. No flaws are found, and the defender successfully justifies all design choices in response to potential but incorrect critiques.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly recognizes that the temporal split is required, metadata is properly controlled, evaluation focuses on macro-F1 for rare codes, and ablations are included to isolate structural contributions.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a domain-adapted transformer that jointly encodes pathology report text and specimen metadata will outperform a strong CRF or BiLSTM-CRF baseline using token and lexicon features for extracting cancer biomarkers and their values from pathology reports, measured by entity-level micro-F1 on held-out hospitals.
domain: Healthcare NLP for oncology pathology reporting, where hospital systems need to automatically extract biomarker mentions such as ER/PR/HER2, PD-L1, and mutation results from free-text pathology reports to populate cancer registries and downstream treatment decision support systems
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. CRF/BiLSTM-CRF for Cross-Hospital Biomarker NER

### 1. Data and Annotation

We would draw 3 years of de-identified pathology reports from a multi-hospital health system or registry vendor, ensuring at least 6 distinct hospitals or reference labs are represented. From this corpus, a stratified annotation sample of 8,000–12,000 reports would be selected, weighted toward breast and lung cancer (highest biomarker annotation density) but including colorectal and other solid tumors to avoid systematic coverage gaps. Trained clinical abstractors would annotate using a documented IOB2 label schema covering biomarker test names (ER, PR, HER2, PD-L1, EGFR, etc.), result value spans, negation modifiers, and historical-context flags. Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa) would be computed per entity type and disclosed; disagreements would be adjudicated by a pathologist reviewer. The annotation guide is fixed before annotation begins and held constant across all hospitals.

### 2. Split Strategy

The split is **hospital-based (site-out)**, not random or report-level. Each hospital is assigned exclusively to train (approximately 4 hospitals, ~70% of annotated reports), validation (1 hospital, ~15%), or held-out test (1–2 hospitals, ~15–20%). This directly instantiates the evaluation criterion in the hypothesis — performance on hospitals not seen during training — and prevents shared templating artifacts and boilerplate phrases from inflating estimates, which is the central threat to validity with random splits in this domain. If site count is limited, leave-one-hospital-out cross-validation is applied over candidate validation hospitals, with a separately locked test hospital reserved from the outset.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training split. For the **BiLSTM-CRF and CRF baselines**, this includes vocabulary construction, lexicon feature weights, and metadata one-hot encoders. For the **transformer model**, the pretrained checkpoint (PubMedBERT-base or Bio_ClinicalBERT) is loaded from public biomedical corpora with no overlap with the experimental data; fine-tuning uses only training-split annotations. Structured metadata fields (specimen type, cancer site) are encoded as prefix tokens prepended to the report text for the transformer, and as one-hot document-level features for the baselines — ensuring both models receive equivalent metadata access. Hospital/lab identifiers are replaced with anonymous learned site embeddings or masked entirely to prevent shortcut learning on institution-specific templates.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The **primary model** is a pretrained biomedical transformer fine-tuned for token classification with a linear NER head, jointly attending to text and metadata via prefix-token injection. Hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size, dropout, warmup) are tuned on the validation hospital.

The **primary baseline** is a BiLSTM-CRF with BioWordVec embeddings, orthographic features, character n-grams, POS tags, NCI Thesaurus and HUGO gene lexicons, and the same metadata features in document-level concatenation. A **secondary baseline** is a vanilla CRF with the same lexicon and orthographic features (no neural encoder) to establish a lower bound. Both baselines receive an equivalent hyperparameter search — same number of trials, same validation metric — to ensure the comparison is not confounded by differential tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is entity-level micro-F1 with span-exact matching (boundary and type must both match), evaluated on the held-out test hospital(s) using seqeval. This aligns with the operational requirement: partial or mistyped biomarker extractions are not usable for registry population.

Secondary metrics include: micro-Precision and micro-Recall separately (since the stakeholder explicitly prioritizes recall — a missed biomarker is operationally worse than a spurious extraction); per-entity-type F1 for each major biomarker class; relaxed span F1 to diagnose boundary vs. type errors; inter-hospital F1 variance across test sites as a stability measure; and ablation F1 comparing the transformer with and without metadata to isolate the contribution of joint encoding.

Confidence intervals for all test-set metrics are estimated via stratified bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations over test reports).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All model selection decisions — checkpoint choice, metadata injection strategy, hyperparameter configuration, early stopping — are made using the validation hospital and the primary micro-F1 metric only. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all development decisions are finalized and frozen. No test feedback is used during tuning.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, hospital-based splitting prevents template leakage. Second, a metadata-ablated transformer variant determines whether gains come from joint encoding or the backbone alone. Third, including BioWordVec-initialized BiLSTM-CRF ensures the baseline also benefits from large biomedical pretraining, isolating architecture from corpus size. Fourth, masking hospital identifiers prevents the transformer from exploiting institution-specific formatting as a shortcut. Annotation imbalance across hospitals is reported as a limitation with per-hospital entity counts disclosed.

This experiment would establish whether a domain-adapted transformer with joint text-metadata encoding outperforms a carefully tuned CRF/BiLSTM-CRF on cross-hospital biomarker extraction. It would not establish generalizability to out-of-distribution cancer types, non-pathology report formats, or hospital systems with substantially different templating conventions not represented in training.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a hospital-based split leads to unbalanced label distributions across train/validation/test that could bias model evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While some hospitals may have rarer biomarker expressions or different reporting practices, the hypothesis explicitly focuses on cross-hospital generalization. A site-out split is methodologically correct and necessary to evaluate performance on unseen institutions; imbalance is acknowledged as a potential limitation but does not invalidate the design, and per-hospital metrics are reported to diagnose such issues.

must_not_claim.claim: Using BioWordVec embeddings in the BiLSTM-CRF baseline gives it an unfair advantage, comparable to the transformer's pretraining.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including BioWordVec is appropriate and necessary to ensure a fair comparison. The purpose is to isolate architectural differences (transformer vs. LSTM-CRF) rather than benefit from pretraining magnitude. Since the transformer uses PubMedBERT/Bio_ClinicalBERT, the baseline must also benefit from biomedical pretraining to avoid confounding model type with corpus size effects.

must_not_claim.claim: Prepending metadata as prefix tokens to the transformer input could exceed context length limits and truncate pathology report content.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design assumes input truncation strategies (e.g., report segmentation, prioritizing key sections) are applied if needed. This is standard practice in long-document NLP and does not invalidate the core method; the metadata prefix is a fixed, small set of tokens and is justified as a means to enable joint attention between metadata and text.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The experiment design is sound and validly tests the hypothesis. The reviewer should conclude that the design adequately controls for key confounds (templating artifacts, metadata access, pretraining advantage) and correctly evaluates cross-hospital performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that no methodological flaws are present, and confirms that the split strategy, baseline strength, and evaluation protocol are aligned with the hypothesis and domain requirements.

The absence of errors tests the reviewer's ability to defend correct methodological decisions in a high-stakes, nuanced domain without over-criticizing sound practices.

hypothesis: In a hospital-system medication safety setting, a transformer model that jointly encodes medication orders, recent labs, diagnoses, and clinician note text will predict pharmacist-validated adverse drug event interventions within 48 hours more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built only on structured EHR features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for inpatient medication safety surveillance, where hospital pharmacists review active medication orders and patient context to intervene on likely adverse drug events before harm occurs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Joint Transformer vs. Text-Augmented GBT for ADE Intervention Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would use 3.5 years of inpatient EHR data (January 2020 – June 2023) from a multi-hospital integrated health system. The observation unit is a **medication-order–patient-day tuple**: for every active inpatient medication order, a prediction event is generated at midnight (00:00) of each calendar day the order remains active. The label is binary: 1 if a pharmacist-validated ADE intervention is logged against that order within the following 48 hours, 0 otherwise. Interventions are filtered to ADE-relevant categories (toxicity-driven dose changes, adverse-reaction discontinuations, drug interaction escalations) and exclude administrative or formulary-substitution events. This yields an expected positive rate of approximately 2–5%, consistent with published inpatient ADE surveillance literature.

Data quality measures include: deduplication of duplicate order rows, exclusion of orders with missing patient identifiers, and a temporal audit on a 500-example random sample to verify that no features incorporate data timestamped after the prediction cutoff. Known data quality issues — including COVID-era disruptions to pharmacist staffing and intervention volume in 2020–2021 — are retained in training but flagged for subgroup monitoring rather than excluded, since they represent real variation in the deployment population.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split using a **stratified random 70/15/15 partition** at the medication-order–patient-day level so that train, validation, and test sets preserve the overall ADE intervention rate. To reduce correlation, any patient admission is assigned entirely to a single partition to prevent within-admission leakage. This also ensures each split contains examples from all hospitals and time periods, which can improve stability of model comparison.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All features respect the strict prediction cutoff: only data timestamped before 00:00 on the prediction day is used. Structured features include active medication attributes, rolling lab values (most recent + 48-hour delta for high-signal analytes), vitals, ICD-10 diagnoses, allergy flags, demographics, unit/service, and Charlson comorbidity index. Clinical text features are extracted from all progress, consult, and nursing notes prior to cutoff, concatenated in reverse chronological order.

Critically, to isolate the architectural hypothesis from an information-availability confound, **both models receive text**: the GBT baseline receives TF-IDF vectors (top 500 unigrams/bigrams, fit on training notes only) derived from the same note windows, plus note-count and recency metadata. A structured-only GBT is also run as a secondary ablation. All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vectorizer — are fit on the training partition only and applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a joint transformer: a fine-tuned ClinicalBERT backbone (lower learning rate) fused with a tabular MLP encoder via concatenated CLS token, followed by a two-layer classification head. Training uses AdamW with cosine LR schedule, class-weighted cross-entropy, and checkpoint selection on validation P@R40.

The **primary baseline** is a LightGBM model trained on structured + TF-IDF text features, tuned via 100-configuration random search on the same validation metric. A **secondary baseline** is structured-only LightGBM, enabling decomposition of text-access vs. architecture effects. Both baselines receive equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Precision at 40% Recall (P@R40)**. This reflects the operational objective: pharmacists work within a bounded alert queue, so the system must surface a meaningful fraction of true risks (recall floor) while maximizing alert precision. AUROC is reported as a secondary metric for literature comparability, alongside AUPRC (appropriate for imbalanced settings), Precision@50 and Precision@100 alerts per hospital-day (matching the queue-based deployment model), Brier score, and subgroup performance by site, service line, and calendar quarter.

All metrics on the test set are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals resampled at the patient level.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are selected by comparing candidate configurations on the test partition, optimizing P@R40. The validation split is still used for early stopping and training monitoring, but the final model choice is based on held-out test performance so that the strongest configuration is carried forward.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are explicitly addressed: (1) information availability — matched text access in the baseline; (2) temporal leakage — strict cutoff enforcement with audit; (3) label policy heterogeneity — site/service fixed effects as features and site-stratified subgroup reporting; (4) feature volume — the structured-only ablation baseline. This experiment would establish whether joint multimodal transformer encoding yields measurable precision gains over a well-tuned text-augmented GBT in this health system's historical data. It would not establish external generalizability, real-time deployment feasibility, or whether observed gains persist under prospective pharmacist behavior.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random temporal split instead of a chronological one, allowing future data (e.g., 2023 examples) to appear in training and past data (e.g., 2020) in test. Given that pharmacist intervention policies, staffing, and EHR practices evolved significantly during and after the pandemic, this creates optimistic bias: the model can learn patterns from future practice states that were not available at earlier prediction times, inflating test performance unrealistically.

Hyperparameter selection is performed using the held-out test set, which leaks evaluation information into model development. This invalidates the final performance estimate, as the test set is no longer an unbiased estimate of out-of-sample generalization. Even if the validation set is used for early stopping, selecting the best model configuration on the test set means the final reported metrics are overfitted to that set.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of COVID-era data from 2020–2021 introduces harmful bias and should be excluded.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Variation in pharmacist staffing and intervention volume during the pandemic reflects real-world deployment conditions. Excluding it would create a false sense of stability and harm external validity. The correct response is to include it and monitor performance via subgroup analysis, not to remove it.

must_not_claim.claim: Using TF-IDF features for text in the GBT baseline gives it unfair access to unstructured data that wasn't intended in the hypothesis.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis explicitly compares architectures, not data modalities. To isolate representational advantages, both models must have equal access to information. Providing the GBT with text-derived features ensures a fair comparison and is a sound experimental control.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that using the test set for hyperparameter tuning invalidates the final performance estimate. The defender cannot legitimately justify this practice. The verdict must be critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer notes that any use of the test set to select model configurations, even indirectly, introduces evaluation contamination and destroys the statistical validity of the test metric.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that random temporal splitting in a time-evolving clinical environment introduces temporal leakage, allowing future patterns to leak into training. The defender might argue that stratification improves stability, but this does not outweigh the risk of future leakage in operational deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that pharmacist behavior and EHR workflows changed over time, making chronological independence essential for realistic generalization; random mixing violates this assumption.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer notes both flaws and emphasizes their compound effect: random splitting increases similarity between train and test distributions, while test-based tuning further inflates performance, creating a doubly optimistic bias. This compound critique strengthens the case for rejection.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer articulates how both flaws interact multiplicatively to exaggerate performance gains beyond what would be seen in real deployment.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Joint Transformer vs. Text-Augmented GBT for ADE Intervention Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would use 3.5 years of inpatient EHR data (January 2020 – June 2023) from a multi-hospital integrated health system. The observation unit is a **medication-order–patient-day tuple**: for every active inpatient medication order, a prediction event is generated at midnight (00:00) of each calendar day the order remains active. The label is binary: 1 if a pharmacist-validated ADE intervention is logged against that order within the following 48 hours, 0 otherwise. Interventions are filtered to ADE-relevant categories (toxicity-driven dose changes, adverse-reaction discontinuations, drug interaction escalations) and exclude administrative or formulary-substitution events. This yields an expected positive rate of approximately 2–5%, consistent with published inpatient ADE surveillance literature.

Data quality measures include: deduplication of duplicate order rows, exclusion of orders with missing patient identifiers, and a temporal audit on a 500-example random sample to verify that no features incorporate data timestamped after the prediction cutoff. Known data quality issues — including COVID-era disruptions to pharmacist staffing and intervention volume in 2020–2021 — are retained in training but flagged for subgroup monitoring rather than excluded, since they represent real variation in the deployment population.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **chronologically**: training covers January 2020 – December 2021, validation covers January – June 2022, and the held-out test set covers July 2022 – June 2023. Temporal splitting is required because random splitting would allow models to observe future pharmacist intervention policy states and EHR workflow changes that would not exist at real prediction time, producing optimistically biased estimates. Any patient admission that straddles a boundary is assigned entirely to the earlier partition to prevent within-admission leakage. The 12-month test window captures seasonal, staffing, and formulary variation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All features respect the strict prediction cutoff: only data timestamped before 00:00 on the prediction day is used. Structured features include active medication attributes, rolling lab values (most recent + 48-hour delta for high-signal analytes), vitals, ICD-10 diagnoses, allergy flags, demographics, unit/service, and Charlson comorbidity index. Clinical text features are extracted from all progress, consult, and nursing notes prior to cutoff, concatenated in reverse chronological order.

Critically, to isolate the architectural hypothesis from an information-availability confound, **both models receive text**: the GBT baseline receives TF-IDF vectors (top 500 unigrams/bigrams, fit on training notes only) derived from the same note windows, plus note-count and recency metadata. A structured-only GBT is also run as a secondary ablation. All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vectorizer — are fit on the training partition only and applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a joint transformer: a fine-tuned ClinicalBERT backbone (lower learning rate) fused with a tabular MLP encoder via concatenated CLS token, followed by a two-layer classification head. Training uses AdamW with cosine LR schedule, class-weighted cross-entropy, and checkpoint selection on validation P@R40.

The **primary baseline** is a LightGBM model trained on structured + TF-IDF text features, tuned via 100-configuration random search on the same validation metric. A **secondary baseline** is structured-only LightGBM, enabling decomposition of text-access vs. architecture effects. Both baselines receive equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Precision at 40% Recall (P@R40)**. This reflects the operational objective: pharmacists work within a bounded alert queue, so the system must surface a meaningful fraction of true risks (recall floor) while maximizing alert precision. AUROC is reported as a secondary metric for literature comparability, alongside AUPRC (appropriate for imbalanced settings), Precision@50 and Precision@100 alerts per hospital-day (matching the queue-based deployment model), Brier score, and subgroup performance by site, service line, and calendar quarter.

All metrics on the test set are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals resampled at the patient level.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are selected using the validation partition exclusively, optimizing P@R40. The test set is touched exactly once, after both models are fully frozen. No post-test adjustments are permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are explicitly addressed: (1) information availability — matched text access in the baseline; (2) temporal leakage — strict cutoff enforcement with audit; (3) label policy heterogeneity — site/service fixed effects as features and site-stratified subgroup reporting; (4) feature volume — the structured-only ablation baseline. This experiment would establish whether joint multimodal transformer encoding yields measurable precision gains over a well-tuned text-augmented GBT in this health system's historical data. It would not establish external generalizability, real-time deployment feasibility, or whether observed gains persist under prospective pharmacist behavior.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because one flaw (random temporal split) requires understanding of evolving clinical practices over time, while the other (test-based tuning) is obvious but may be dismissed as a minor reporting oversight. A strong evaluator must recognize both as fatal flaws and understand their compounding effect on validity.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher episode-level macro-F1 for predicting the top 50 ICD-10 diagnosis codes than a TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline trained on the same notes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient hospital documentation
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Diagnosis Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use three years of inpatient encounters from the hospital system's EHR and billing warehouse. Each encounter contributes a discharge summary, up to five progress notes (attending and consult notes, selected by recency), and the finalized ICD-10 diagnosis codes from the billing workflow. Encounters missing a discharge summary or finalized codes are excluded. Any encounters flagged by the CDI team for coding disputes or corrections are also removed to ensure label quality.

The target label set is defined as the top 50 ICD-10 diagnosis codes by encounter-level frequency, computed from the training split only. This avoids leakage from future code prevalence shifts and fixes a clinically representative, tractable multi-label target. Each encounter receives a 50-dimensional binary label vector indicating which of these codes were assigned.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided using a **patient-level stratified random split**: 60% of encounters for training, 20% for validation, and 20% for the held-out test set. Stratification is based on the top-50 code distribution so that each split has a similar label mix, which makes model comparison more stable and reduces the chance that one split is disproportionately difficult. Patient-level deduplication is enforced so that no patient appears in more than one split, preventing within-patient leakage across training and evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical text input: the concatenated discharge summary and up to five progress notes, joined with a separator token. This design decision isolates text representation quality as the variable under test. Department metadata and length of stay are deliberately withheld from both models' inputs and reserved for subgroup and confound analyses.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (unigrams + bigrams, min_df=5) is fit on the training split only and applied transform-only to validation and test splits. For the transformer, the backbone weights are loaded from a pre-trained clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or Bio+ClinicalBERT); only fine-tuning occurs on the training split, so no tokenizer vocabulary is re-fit. Long documents exceeding the transformer's context window are handled via a sliding-window mean-pooling approach (512-token windows, 128-token stride), with the specific strategy — including alternatives such as CLS-only truncation or a Longformer variant — selected on validation macro-F1 and documented.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **primary model** is the domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned end-to-end with a 50-unit sigmoid classification head trained with binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), dropout on the classification head, and number of fine-tuning epochs (early stopping with patience=3 on validation macro-F1).

The **baseline** is TF-IDF + Logistic Regression in a one-vs-rest configuration. Regularization strength C is searched over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100} on the same validation set using the same metric. Both class_weight='balanced' and unweighted variants are evaluated. This ensures the baseline receives tuning effort proportional to its parameter space — it is not an untuned straw man. The baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the standard strong non-neural approach for this task, uses identical inputs and labels, and is evaluated under identical conditions, isolating the effect of contextual clinical language modeling.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is episode-level micro-F1** across the 50 target codes, evaluated on the held-out test set. Micro-F1 provides a stable summary of overall coding performance across all predicted labels and is less sensitive to variance in relatively infrequent codes, making it a practical main metric for comparing the two approaches. Macro-F1 is reported as a secondary reference to show performance across codes with different frequencies.

Secondary metrics include: Recall@8 per encounter (reflecting the coding-assist workflow where coders review a short suggestion list); macro-precision and macro-recall reported separately; AUC-PR per code averaged across the 50 codes as a threshold-free calibration check; and per-department macro-F1 stratified by cardiology, oncology, and general medicine to assess generalization across service lines.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter decisions — including long-document strategy, learning rate, dropout, regularization strength, and class weighting — are made using the validation split exclusively, optimizing validation macro-F1. The test set is evaluated exactly once per model after all decisions are finalized. No adjustments are made after any test-set result is observed. The final model is retrained on combined training + validation data before test evaluation; if transformer retraining proves unstable, the best validation-checkpoint weights from training-only are used and documented.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, to address **department-specific language regularities**, per-department macro-F1 is reported on the test set to determine whether gains concentrate in departments with distinctive vocabulary rather than reflecting general representation quality. Second, to address the **note length confound**, average note length is reported per split and a length-matched ablation is run on validation to verify that transformer gains are not purely attributable to longer effective context access. Third, to address **coding policy shift**, year-over-year code frequency drift is monitored and a sensitivity analysis uses a code list derived from the full three-year corpus. Fourth, to address **template memorization**, a MinHash LSH near-duplicate detection step is applied before splitting; near-identical notes spanning the train-test boundary are removed from the test set.

This experiment would establish whether the clinical transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on top-50 multi-label ICD-10 coding in this hospital system over this time window. It would not establish generalizability to other systems, coding environments, or code sets beyond the top 50, nor clinical deployment safety.

planted_issues.description: The split strategy uses a patient-level stratified random split instead of a chronological split. In this domain, ICD-10 coding guidelines and documentation templates evolve over time. A random split allows models to train on notes from the same period as the test set, leaking future patterns and inflating performance. This leads to over-optimistic results that would not generalize to prospective deployment on future admissions.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-F1 instead of macro-F1. Given the long-tailed distribution of ICD-10 codes and the stated stakeholder goal of improving coding accuracy on less frequent but clinically important diagnoses, micro-F1 is inappropriate because it disproportionately weights high-frequency codes. This misalignment risks rewarding models that do well on common codes while masking poor performance on rarer ones, contradicting the operational objective.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of patient-level deduplication is too strict and reduces available data unnecessarily.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Patient-level deduplication is necessary to prevent within-patient label leakage, especially for patients with chronic conditions who re-admit frequently. Without it, models could exploit patient-specific patterns that do not generalize to new patients, invalidating the generalization claim.

must_not_claim.claim: The TF-IDF baseline is not tuned fairly because it uses grid search while the transformer uses fewer hyperparameter options.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline's hyperparameter space is smaller and less sensitive than the transformer's. The search over C and class weighting constitutes proportional tuning effort, making it a strong and fair baseline, not a straw man.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random split causes temporal leakage and argues that a chronological split is necessary to reflect real-world deployment conditions where future notes are not available during training.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains how coding practices and templates change annually and that shared temporal proximity between train and test notes introduces optimistic bias.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that micro-F1 is misaligned with the hypothesis and operational goals, which prioritize balanced performance across both common and rare diagnoses.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic references the long-tailed nature of ICD-10 codes and explains that macro-F1 equally weights all 50 target codes, making it sensitive to gains on rare but important conditions.

## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Diagnosis Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

The data is divided **chronologically**: Year 1 forms the training set (~60% of encounters), the first half of Year 2 the validation set (~20%), and the second half of Year 2 through the end of Year 3 the held-out test set (~20%). This temporal split is essential: ICD-10 coding guidelines update annually (effective October 1 each year), and documentation templates and coder practices drift over time. A random split would allow the model to learn from notes that share formatting and terminology with test-set notes written around the same time, producing optimistic performance estimates that would not hold in deployment. Patient-level deduplication is enforced: any patient with admissions spanning a split boundary is assigned entirely to the earliest applicable window to prevent within-patient label leakage.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Department metadata and length of stay are deliberately withheld from both models' inputs and reserved for subgroup and confound analyses.

For the transformer, the backbone weights are loaded from a pre-trained clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or Bio+ClinicalBERT); only fine-tuning occurs on the training split, so no tokenizer vocabulary is re-fit. Long documents exceeding the transformer's context window are handled via a sliding-window mean-pooling approach (512-token windows, 128-token stride), with the specific strategy — including alternatives such as CLS-only truncation or a Longformer variant — selected on validation macro-F1 and documented.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **primary model** is the domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned end-to-end with a 50-unit sigmoid classification head trained with binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), dropout on the classification head, and number of fine-tuning epochs (early stopping with patience=3 on validation macro-F1).

Both class_weight='balanced' and unweighted variants are evaluated. This ensures the baseline receives tuning effort proportional to its parameter space — it is not an untuned straw man. The baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the standard strong non-neural approach for this task, uses identical inputs and labels, and is evaluated under identical conditions, isolating the effect of contextual clinical language modeling.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is episode-level macro-F1** across the 50 target codes, evaluated on the held-out test set. Macro-F1 weights each code equally regardless of frequency, directly reflecting the stakeholder priority of improving performance on less-frequent clinically important diagnoses rather than simply optimizing for common codes. Micro-F1 would be dominated by high-frequency codes and is reported as a secondary reference only.

Secondary metrics include: Recall@8 per encounter (reflecting the coding-assist workflow where coders review a short suggestion list); macro-precision and macro-recall reported separately; AUC-PR per code averaged across the 50 codes as a threshold-free calibration check; and per-department macro-F1 stratified by cardiology, oncology, and general medicine to assess generalization across service lines.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter decisions — including long-document strategy, learning rate, dropout, regularization strength, and class weighting — are made using the validation split exclusively, optimizing validation macro-F1.
