The final model is retrained on combined training + validation data before test evaluation; if transformer retraining proves unstable, the best validation-checkpoint weights from training-only are used and documented.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, to address **department-specific language regularities**, per-department macro-F1 is reported on the test set to determine whether gains concentrate in departments with distinctive vocabulary rather than reflecting general representation quality. Second, to address the **note length confound**, average note length is reported per split and a length-matched ablation is run on validation to verify that transformer gains are not purely attributable to longer effective context access. Third, to address **coding policy shift**, year-over-year code frequency drift is monitored and a sensitivity analysis uses a code list derived from the full three-year corpus. Fourth, to address **template memorization**, a MinHash LSH near-duplicate detection step is applied before splitting; near-identical notes spanning the train-test boundary are removed from the test set.

This experiment would establish whether the clinical transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on top-50 multi-label ICD-10 coding in this hospital system over this time window. It would not establish generalizability to other systems, coding environments, or code sets beyond the top 50, nor clinical deployment safety.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both flaws require domain-specific reasoning: understanding that coding guidelines evolve over time justifies the need for a temporal split, and recognizing the stakeholder emphasis on rare diagnoses makes macro-F1 the correct metric. Neither issue is obvious from generic ML best practices.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a long-context clinical language model fine-tuned on full discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline built on the same note text.
domain: Healthcare clinical documentation workflow for automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient EHR notes in an acute-care hospital system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Long-Context Clinical LM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 4 years (48 months) of de-identified inpatient encounters from a single acute-care hospital system, targeting 80,000–150,000 admissions after filtering. Each encounter contributes two document types: the finalized discharge summary and up to 10 attending or resident progress notes from the final 72 hours of the admission. This document selection rule simulates the realistic near-discharge prediction scenario and is applied identically to both models.

Labels are the final coder-assigned ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes from the billing workflow — the gold standard for supervised ICD coding. To ensure evaluable signal, we restrict the label set to codes appearing at least 50 times in the training split only; codes below this threshold are excluded from training and evaluation. The frequency threshold and label binarizer are computed on training data alone.

Known data quality issues include variable note length, boilerplate-heavy templated notes, and possible coder inconsistency across years. Clinical abbreviation normalization is applied as an identically shared preprocessing step. No encounter metadata (demographics, LOS, DRG) is used as a model input feature for either model, isolating the comparison to text representation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split at the patient level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test, using stratified random sampling to preserve the marginal frequency of common ICD-10 codes across splits. This gives stable label coverage across train, validation, and test and makes model comparison less sensitive to month-to-month noise in individual code prevalence.

To prevent within-patient leakage, all encounters from a given patient are assigned to a single split; no patient appears in more than one split.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: the concatenation of the discharge summary and selected progress notes, separated by a section delimiter. For the TF-IDF baseline, this text is transformed into character n-grams (n=2–4) plus word unigrams and bigrams with sublinear TF scaling, L2 normalization, and a 200,000-feature vocabulary cap. For the language model, raw text is passed directly to the tokenizer with truncation applied from the beginning of progress notes (preserving the discharge summary). All transformers — TF-IDF weights, vocabulary, label binarizer — are fit on the training split only and applied to validation and test without re-fitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a long-context clinical language model (e.g., Clinical-Longformer or equivalent with ≥4,096 token context), fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size ∈ {8, 16}, warmup ratio ∈ {0.05, 0.1}) are tuned via grid search on the validation split using macro-F1.

The baseline is TF-IDF with one-vs-rest logistic regression, with regularization strength C tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} on the same validation split using the same macro-F1 criterion. This is a strong, well-established baseline for clinical text classification and represents the realistic alternative a coding team would deploy without GPU infrastructure. Both models are given equivalent tuning effort and identical text input.

A secondary ablation trains the language model on 512-token truncated input (standard BERT context window) to isolate the contribution of long-context capability from general representation quality.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-F1** over the retained label set. Macro averaging weights each code equally, directly operationalizing the stakeholder's requirement to improve performance on both common and moderately rare codes. Micro-F1 or accuracy would be dominated by the top-20 high-frequency codes and would mask failures on the clinically important long tail — making macro-F1 the correct choice here.

Secondary metrics include: macro-recall at fixed precision 0.5 (operationalizing the 'do not miss reimbursable codes' requirement), micro-F1 (for literature comparability), macro-F1 stratified by code frequency tertile and by ICD-10 chapter, mean average precision at k=10 for ranking-aware coder suggestion scenarios, and per-encounter inference latency for deployment feasibility assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions and model selection are made on the validation split using macro-F1. The test set is touched exactly once, after all tuning is complete. Final results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (1,000 resamples) on the macro-F1 difference between the LM and baseline to quantify statistical uncertainty.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, text access parity: both models receive identical concatenated text, ensuring any advantage is attributable to representation quality rather than document selection. The 512-token ablation further isolates context length as a factor. Second, coding practice variability: stratified patient-level splitting keeps the code distribution comparable across train, validation, and test, reducing the risk that results are driven by a single atypical calendar period. Third, service line heterogeneity: macro-F1 is reported per service line to determine whether any observed advantage is general or concentrated in specific clinical domains.

This experiment would establish whether a long-context clinical LM outperforms a strong TF-IDF baseline on macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 coding under identical text access conditions and controlled patient-level evaluation. It would not establish generalizability across hospital systems, nor would it isolate the contribution of pretraining data composition versus context length independently of the 512-token ablation.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a random patient-level split instead of a chronological one, allowing future data (with potentially different coding practices due to guideline updates or policy changes) to contaminate the training set. This violates the temporal nature of clinical deployment, inflating performance estimates by letting models learn from future coding patterns. As real-world inference must predict on future cases using past data, the evaluation is invalid.

must_not_claim.claim: The label frequency threshold (codes with fewer than 50 training occurrences excluded) should be computed on the full dataset, not the training split only.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Computing the threshold on training data alone prevents leakage from future or test-set code occurrences, ensuring the model is not evaluated on codes it never saw during training. Using the full dataset would expose the training process to knowledge of future codes, creating a temporal leakage risk.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratified sampling to preserve label distribution across splits introduces bias or invalidates the experiment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by label frequency is not inherently flawed and is often beneficial for balanced evaluation—but here it is misapplied due to random splitting. The issue is not stratification itself but the use of random rather than temporal split in a time-sensitive domain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random split introduces temporal leakage, making the experimental evaluation overly optimistic and misaligned with real-world deployment conditions. The defender cannot legitimately justify random splitting in this context despite patient-level separation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that ICD-10 coding practices change over time, and a temporal split is necessary to simulate realistic performance on future patients. They note that even with patient-level grouping, random selection across time allows models to train on 'future' coding patterns.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Long-Context Clinical LM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 4 years (48 months) of de-identified inpatient encounters from a single acute-care hospital system, targeting 80,000–150,000 admissions after filtering. Each encounter contributes two document types: the finalized discharge summary and up to 10 attending or resident progress notes from the final 72 hours of the admission. This document selection rule simulates the realistic near-discharge prediction scenario and is applied identically to both models.

Labels are the final coder-assigned ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes from the billing workflow — the gold standard for supervised ICD coding. To ensure evaluable signal, we restrict the label set to codes appearing at least 50 times in the training split only; codes below this threshold are excluded from training and evaluation. The frequency threshold and label binarizer are computed on training data alone.

Known data quality issues include variable note length, boilerplate-heavy templated notes, and possible coder inconsistency across years. Clinical abbreviation normalization is applied as an identically shared preprocessing step. No encounter metadata (demographics, LOS, DRG) is used as a model input feature for either model, isolating the comparison to text representation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The dataset is split chronologically: months 1–30 for training (~62.5%), months 31–36 for validation (~12.5%), and months 37–48 for held-out test (~25%). A temporal split is essential here because ICD-10 coding practice drifts with guideline changes and payer policy updates, and deployment always involves predicting on future encounters given past training. Random splitting would allow future coding patterns to contaminate the training window, producing overly optimistic estimates.

To prevent within-patient leakage, all encounters from a given patient are assigned to the earliest split window containing their first admission; no patient appears in more than one split.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: the concatenation of the discharge summary and selected progress notes, separated by a section delimiter. For the TF-IDF baseline, this text is transformed into character n-grams (n=2–4) plus word unigrams and bigrams with sublinear TF scaling, L2 normalization, and a 200,000-feature vocabulary cap. For the language model, raw text is passed directly to the tokenizer with truncation applied from the beginning of progress notes (preserving the discharge summary). All transformers — TF-IDF weights, vocabulary, label binarizer — are fit on the training split only and applied to validation and test without re-fitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a long-context clinical language model (e.g., Clinical-Longformer or equivalent with ≥4,096 token context), fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size ∈ {8, 16}, warmup ratio ∈ {0.05, 0.1}) are tuned via grid search on the validation split using macro-F1.

The baseline is TF-IDF with one-vs-rest logistic regression, with regularization strength C tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} on the same validation split using the same macro-F1 criterion. This is a strong, well-established baseline for clinical text classification and represents the realistic alternative a coding team would deploy without GPU infrastructure. Both models are given equivalent tuning effort and identical text input.

A secondary ablation trains the language model on 512-token truncated input (standard BERT context window) to isolate the contribution of long-context capability from general representation quality.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-F1** over the retained label set. Macro averaging weights each code equally, directly operationalizing the stakeholder's requirement to improve performance on both common and moderately rare codes. Micro-F1 or accuracy would be dominated by the top-20 high-frequency codes and would mask failures on the clinically important long tail — making macro-F1 the correct choice here.

Secondary metrics include: macro-recall at fixed precision 0.5 (operationalizing the 'do not miss reimbursable codes' requirement), micro-F1 (for literature comparability), macro-F1 stratified by code frequency tertile and by ICD-10 chapter, mean average precision at k=10 for ranking-aware coder suggestion scenarios, and per-encounter inference latency for deployment feasibility assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions and model selection are made on the validation split (months 31–36) using macro-F1. The test set is touched exactly once, after all tuning is complete. Final results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (1,000 resamples) on the macro-F1 difference between the LM and baseline to quantify statistical uncertainty.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, text access parity: both models receive identical concatenated text, ensuring any advantage is attributable to representation quality rather than document selection. The 512-token ablation further isolates context length as a factor. Second, coding practice drift: the temporal split ensures both models face the same temporal distribution shift; codes with >20% frequency shift between training and test periods are flagged. Third, service line heterogeneity: macro-F1 is reported per service line to determine whether any observed advantage is general or concentrated in specific clinical domains.

This experiment would establish whether a long-context clinical LM outperforms a strong TF-IDF baseline on macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 coding under identical text access conditions and realistic temporal evaluation. It would not establish generalizability across hospital systems, nor would it isolate the contribution of pretraining data composition versus context length independently of the 512-token ablation.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because random stratified splits are common in ML and may appear superior for label balance; however, in temporal clinical deployment, they cause serious leakage. A good reviewer must recognize that time-ordering matters more than statistical balance here.

hypothesis: In hospital coding operations, a long-context clinical language model fine-tuned on discharge summaries and operative notes will achieve higher micro-F1 for automated ICD-10 multi-label code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially for low-frequency complication and procedure codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation operations for inpatient hospital stays, where coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis and procedure codes from discharge summaries, operative notes, and progress notes after patient discharge
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Long-Context Clinical LM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of finalized inpatient encounters from a large academic medical center with coder-reviewed ICD-10-CM and ICD-10-PCS labels, targeting a minimum of 150,000 admissions. The 3-year window ensures sufficient volume for rare complication and procedure codes while spanning at least one ICD-10 annual code update cycle — a structural feature of the label space that the design must account for.

For each encounter, we include the discharge summary, all operative notes, and the final progress note — restricting strictly to documents timestamped before the coding finalization timestamp. Post-discharge addenda created after code assignment are excluded, since they may contain language that reflects or was informed by the coding decision itself. This temporal document cutoff is non-negotiable for label integrity.

Labels are the finalized coder-assigned ICD-10 codes from the hospital's encoding system of record. We define a frequency-stratified label taxonomy — high (≥500 training encounters), medium (50–499), low (10–49), and rare (<10) — to support the hypothesis's specific claim about low-frequency code performance. The rare stratum is excluded from training targets but included in evaluation reporting.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are split chronologically by discharge date: 60% training, 20% validation, 20% test. This temporal ordering is required for two reasons. First, ICD-10 codes are updated each October, and a random split would leak post-update code definitions and documentation patterns into training. Second, coding practice drifts with reimbursement policy, making temporal generalization the operationally relevant evaluation condition.

To prevent patient-level leakage, all encounters for a given patient are assigned entirely to the partition containing their earliest encounter that falls in that window — no patient spans partitions. This eliminates any risk of the model learning patient-specific presentation patterns from training and applying them at test time.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive identical input: chronologically ordered, delimited concatenation of the discharge summary, operative notes, and final progress note. This document parity is the most critical design control — any observed performance difference must reflect representational capacity, not information access.

For the TF-IDF baseline: character n-grams (n=2–4) plus word unigrams/bigrams, sublinear TF scaling, L2 normalization, minimum document frequency of 3. For the language model: the same text passed through the model tokenizer with sliding-window mean-pool encoding over 512-token windows for long documents (not naive truncation, which would disadvantage the baseline by discarding operative note content).

All preprocessing — TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, label binarizer — is fit exclusively on the training partition and applied to validation and test. No statistics derived from validation or test data influence any transformation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is ClinicalLongformer (or Clinical-BigBird, selected by validation micro-F1), fine-tuned with a sigmoid multi-label head and inverse-square-root class-frequency loss weighting. Hyperparameters tuned on validation: learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size ∈ {8, 16}, epochs ∈ {2–5}, and per-label thresholds via precision-recall curves.

The baseline is TF-IDF with one-vs-rest logistic regression, using `class_weight='balanced'`, with C ∈ {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} tuned on validation and per-label thresholds also optimized on validation F1. This is the correct comparison: OvR linear models on TF-IDF are production-grade and widely used in operational coding systems. Crucially, the baseline receives equal tuning effort under identical conditions — this prevents the common experimental flaw of under-tuning the baseline.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 over the full label space (excluding rare stratum), computed with per-label thresholds optimized on validation. Micro-F1 is appropriate because it weights each code-encounter prediction equally, is standard in the clinical coding literature, and reflects aggregate code suggestion quality across the encounter volume that drives coder workload reduction.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (to detect whether gains are label-frequency-dependent), frequency-stratum-specific micro-F1 and macro-F1 (the primary subgroup test of the hypothesis's low-frequency claim), Precision@5 and Precision@10 (for ranked code suggestion workflows), recall at fixed precision thresholds of 0.8 and 0.9 (for compliance-sensitive operating points), and Brier score per label (for calibration quality relevant to confidence-ranked displays).

Statistical significance is assessed via paired bootstrap (10,000 resamples, encounter-level) with p < 0.01.

### 6. Validation Approach

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation partition. The test set is sealed until all choices are finalized and is evaluated exactly once. No re-tuning or threshold adjustment occurs after test evaluation. This single-use policy is enforced structurally — the test set evaluation is the terminal step of the experiment.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds require explicit control. First, document scope: enforced by identical input construction for both models. Second, ICD code vintage: subgroup analysis within each fiscal-year cohort (October–September) tests whether gains are consistent across code update cycles or concentrated in one period. Third, case mix drift: MDC distribution is reported across partitions; stratified resampling is applied if substantial drift is detected. Fourth, note length: average token length is reported per partition and per frequency stratum to ensure neither model benefits from a systematic length advantage.

This experiment would establish whether contextual dense representations from a clinical language model outperform sparse bag-of-words representations under equivalent conditions. It would not establish whether the LM is ready for production deployment (which requires prospective evaluation with coders), nor would it isolate which specific linguistic phenomena drive the advantage. The scope of the claim is representation quality under matched information conditions, evaluated on a retrospective temporal holdout.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (chronological by discharge date) introduces bias because it may create unbalanced training/validation/test sets in terms of patient case mix or label distribution.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is operationally valid and necessary: ICD-10 codes are updated annually and coding practices evolve with reimbursement policy. The design explicitly controls for case mix drift by reporting MDC distributions across splits and applying stratified resampling if needed. Therefore, imbalance due to time ordering is not a flaw — it reflects real-world deployment conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding rare codes (<10 training examples) from training but including them in evaluation invalidates the micro-F1 metric, since the model cannot learn these labels.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This is methodologically sound: the hypothesis concerns performance on low-frequency codes, but true zero-shot evaluation of ultra-rare codes is not feasible for micro-F1 computation. By excluding rare codes from training, the model is not trained on them, but including them in evaluation reports coverage limitations transparently. Their impact on micro-F1 is negligible due to low weight, and secondary metrics like macro-F1 and stratum-specific analysis handle rare codes separately.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a sliding-window mean-pooling strategy for long documents in the language model introduces information fragmentation compared to the TF-IDF model, which sees the full concatenated text.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Both models receive semantically equivalent input: the full document set concatenated in chronological order. The TF-IDF model applies globally, but the language model processes the same full input via sliding windows — a standard approach for long-context handling that preserves content. This design explicitly avoids naive truncation, ensuring neither model is disadvantaged. The sliding window approach does not break information access; it emulates full-document processing within architectural constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges the design is methodologically sound and does not raise any valid objections. The defender successfully rebuts any minor concerns by citing domain-specific justifications (e.g., temporal generalization, rare code handling, or input parity). The final verdict is that the experiment supports a fair test of the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns that are addressed by the built-in controls (e.g., case mix, temporal leakage, input parity) or are based on misinterpretations of standard practices (e.g., sliding-window encoding, rare code evaluation). The defender correctly invokes the operational relevance of temporal splits and the appropriateness of micro-F1 for workload reduction goals.

domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for inpatient hospital coding, where professional coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries and longitudinal note text for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Code Assignment

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 4 years of de-identified inpatient encounter records from a large hospital system, targeting approximately 200,000–400,000 admissions. Each encounter contributes the discharge summary and up to 10 pre-discharge progress notes (attending and consultation notes), finalized before the discharge coding event. Critically, we apply a strict timestamp filter: only notes with a finalization timestamp prior to the discharge coding timestamp are included. This prevents any post-discharge documentation — which may reference or summarize the coding process itself — from leaking into the input features.

Labels are defined as the full set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes assigned by professional coders in the final coded record, represented as a binary multi-label vector over the active code vocabulary. Encounters flagged with coding audit reversals or known correction events are excluded to reduce label noise. No hierarchy rollup is applied; codes are used at their full specificity as coded.

The active label vocabulary is defined as all ICD-10 codes appearing in at least 5 training encounters, balancing coverage against extreme label sparsity. Codes below this threshold are excluded from evaluation but tracked to characterize the long tail.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by encounter discharge date. Years 1–3 (~75% of encounters) form the training set. The first 6 months of year 4 (~12.5%) form the validation set. The final 6 months of year 4 (~12.5%) form the held-out test set.

A temporal split is essential here for two reasons. First, ICD-10-CM code definitions update each October 1st, so a random split would contaminate training with future coding conventions. Second, clinical documentation style drifts over time with EHR system changes and institutional policy shifts — the temporal split ensures the test distribution reflects what the model would face in deployment. Although patients may appear in multiple splits, the per-encounter coding task means patient-level leakage is not the primary risk; a sensitivity check confirms no individual patient disproportionately influences results.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both the transformer model and the TF-IDF baseline receive identical input text: the concatenation of the discharge summary and selected pre-discharge progress notes, constructed by the same document-assembly pipeline. This is non-negotiable for a fair comparison — any asymmetry in document access would confound the result.

For the TF-IDF baseline: unigram and bigram term frequencies with sublinear TF scaling and L2 normalization, up to 150,000 features. For the transformer: text is chunked into 512-token windows with 64-token stride; chunk representations are mean-pooled and passed to a sigmoid classification head. Service line metadata is incorporated identically in both models (one-hot for TF-IDF; prefix token for transformer).

To stabilize the feature space across time and ensure consistent token coverage, vocabulary construction, TF-IDF fitting, and label binarizer fitting are performed once on the full corpus before applying the resulting mappings to the train, validation, and test splits. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is frozen from its pretrained checkpoint.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a domain-adapted transformer — BioLinkBERT-base or ClinicalBERT — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head. Binary cross-entropy loss is used with per-label positive class weighting (inversely proportional to frequency, capped at 10×) to address the severe label imbalance.

The baseline is TF-IDF combined with one-vs-rest logistic regression — the industry-standard approach for automated ICD coding. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength C is swept over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on validation micro-F1, and per-label classification thresholds are optimized on the validation set, matching the transformer's threshold tuning procedure exactly. Both models use the same class weighting scheme. This ensures any observed advantage of the transformer is not an artifact of unequal tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 at per-label optimized thresholds. Micro-F1 is chosen because it weights each (encounter, code) prediction pair equally, directly reflecting coding completeness — the operational objective of maximizing correctly assigned codes for reimbursement and quality reporting. Thresholds are set on the validation set and held fixed for test evaluation.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 stratified by label frequency bin (rare: <10 training encounters; medium: 10–500; common: >500) to directly test the hypothesis claim about medium-frequency codes; micro-F1 by service line to test the template memorization confound; micro-averaged AUC-PR for a threshold-independent view; separate precision and recall to verify that neither model is gaming F1 at the expense of the other; and exact-match accuracy as a secondary operational signal.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter selection — learning rate, dropout, number of fine-tuning epochs, regularization strength, chunk pooling strategy — is performed using validation set micro-F1 exclusively. For the transformer, the checkpoint with the highest validation micro-F1 is retained. No information from the test set influences any model decision.

The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection decisions are finalized. Confidence intervals on the micro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline are computed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations) to assess whether the observed gap is statistically reliable.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds receive explicit design controls. First, to rule out document access asymmetry, both models receive identical input text from the same assembly pipeline. Second, to test for service-line template memorization, stratified micro-F1 by service line is reported; if the transformer's overall advantage vanishes within homogeneous service lines, the gain was distributional rather than representational. Third, a regression of per-encounter prediction residuals on note length is run to test whether the transformer advantage concentrates on unusually long documents. Fourth, performance in the first vs. second half of the test period is compared to detect whether either model degrades more sharply at the ICD-10-CM annual update boundary.

This experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline under controlled, operationally realistic conditions at a single hospital system. It does not establish generalizability to other institutions, other EHR documentation cultures, or non-English clinical text.

planted_issues.description: The design fits the TF-IDF vocabulary, label binarizer, and related preprocessing steps on the full corpus, including validation and test data. This introduces information leakage because the feature representation used during training has been influenced by future (from the model's perspective) documentation patterns and label distributions. In a temporally split clinical setting where terminology and coding practices evolve, this artificially inflates model performance by allowing the training data to adapt to future shifts.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split allows patient-level data leakage, since the same patient may appear across train, validation, and test sets.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The coding task is defined per-encounter, not per-patient, and patient re-admissions are common in real hospital systems. Because labels are encounter-specific and there is no evidence that rare patients dominate the data, moderate patient overlap across time is operationally realistic and does not invalidate the split. The temporal boundary remains the appropriate control for policy drift.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the full label vocabulary from the training set excludes codes that appear only in validation or test, which could introduce evaluation bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The filtering of codes to those with ≥5 occurrences in training is standard multi-label practice to manage sparsity, and the test set evaluates only on codes seen in training. Long-tail tracking ensures awareness of performance limits, but strict exclusion prevents evaluation of unreliable predictions on extremely rare codes. This is methodologically sound.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies the preprocessing leakage in the TF-IDF and label binarizer fitting, explaining that fitting on the full corpus contaminates the training data with future information under a temporal split. The defense fails to rebut because no legitimate justification can overcome this validity threat.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that fitting feature transformers on test-influenced data leaks temporal information and invalidates the deployment realism of the temporal split.

The proposed model is a domain-adapted transformer — BioLinkBERT-base or ClinicalBERT — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head. Binary cross-entropy loss is used with per-label positive class weighting (inversely proportional to frequency, capped at 10×) to address the severe label imbalance.

The baseline is TF-IDF combined with one-vs-rest logistic regression — the industry-standard approach for automated ICD coding. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength C is swept over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on validation micro-F1, and per-label classification thresholds are optimized on the validation set, matching the transformer's threshold tuning procedure exactly. Both models use the same class weighting scheme. This ensures any observed advantage of the transformer is not an artifact of unequal tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 at per-label optimized thresholds. Micro-F1 is chosen because it weights each (encounter, code) prediction pair equally, directly reflecting coding completeness — the operational objective of maximizing correctly assigned codes for reimbursement and quality reporting. Thresholds are set on the validation set and held fixed for test evaluation.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 stratified by label frequency bin (rare: <10 training encounters; medium: 10–500; common: >500) to directly test the hypothesis claim about medium-frequency codes; micro-F1 by service line to test the template memorization confound; micro-averaged AUC-PR for a threshold-independent view; separate precision and recall to verify that neither model is gaming F1 at the expense of the other; and exact-match accuracy as a secondary operational signal.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter selection — learning rate, dropout, number of fine-tuning epochs, regularization strength, chunk pooling strategy — is performed using validation set micro-F1 exclusively. For the transformer, the checkpoint with the highest validation micro-F1 is retained. No information from the test set influences any model decision.

The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection decisions are finalized. Confidence intervals on the micro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline are computed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations) to assess whether the observed gap is statistically reliable.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds receive explicit design controls. First, to rule out document access asymmetry, both models receive identical input text from the same assembly pipeline. Second, to test for service-line template memorization, stratified micro-F1 by service line is reported; if the transformer's overall advantage vanishes within homogeneous service lines, the gain was distributional rather than representational. Third, a regression of per-encounter prediction residuals on note length is run to test whether the transformer advantage concentrates on unusually long documents. Fourth, performance in the first vs. second half of the test period is compared to detect whether either model degrades more sharply at the ICD-10-CM annual update boundary.

This experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline under controlled, operationally realistic conditions at a single hospital system. It does not establish generalizability to other institutions, other EHR documentation cultures, or non-English clinical text.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted justification ('to stabilize the feature space') sounds reasonable and is a common real-world practice in static datasets, but it invalidates the temporal integrity which is critical in this domain. This requires nuanced understanding of both ML best practices and clinical deployment dynamics.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchy-aware transformer model fine-tuned on full discharge summaries and prior progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 on multi-label ICD-10 diagnosis code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially for low-frequency but clinically important codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient EHR notes in an acute-care hospital system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchy-Aware Transformer for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would use 3 years of completed inpatient encounters from a single acute-care health system, targeting approximately 80,000–150,000 admissions after filtering. For each encounter, eligible input text consists of the admission H&P, daily progress notes, and the discharge summary — but only notes with a finalization timestamp strictly prior to the discharge date are included. This hard temporal filter is non-negotiable: it enforces the realistic prediction scenario and prevents any leakage from documentation that coders may have influenced or that was completed post-discharge.

Labels are the ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes assigned by certified coders in the completed billing record. To make the label space tractable and evaluation meaningful, the active vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing in at least 50 encounters in the full corpus, yielding roughly 1,500–2,500 codes. Codes below this threshold are excluded from training targets and primary evaluation, but their behavior is tracked separately as an out-of-vocabulary stratum.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level using a stratified random partition. Approximately 67% of encounters form the training set, 17% form the validation set, and the remaining 17% form the held-out test set. Stratification is performed over the active label distribution to maintain comparable prevalence patterns across splits, which is especially useful in a large sparse multi-label setting. We also verify that no exact duplicate encounters appear across splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

To isolate the architectural contribution from confounds related to input coverage, both the transformer and the TF-IDF representation are defined over the same eligible note sources at the corpus level. To ensure a stable shared feature space across all partitions, TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and the label binarizer are computed once on the full dataset and then applied uniformly to train, validation, and test. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is fixed. A third ablation condition uses only the discharge summary as input for both model types, allowing us to disentangle the value of multi-note access from the hierarchy-aware architecture.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical domain-pretrained transformer (e.g., ClinicalBERT or a MIMIC-pretrained LongFormer) fine-tuned with a sigmoid multi-label output head. The hierarchy-aware component is implemented as an auxiliary loss term that penalizes child-code predictions inconsistent with parent-code predictions, weighted by a tunable coefficient. For long documents, a Longformer or hierarchical chunking architecture with attention pooling is used — this architectural choice is fixed before any validation-set evaluation.

The primary baseline is a lightweight TF-IDF one-vs-rest logistic regression model trained on discharge summaries using standard library defaults. This reflects a simple and operationally realistic bag-of-words benchmark that is easy to implement and commonly encountered in practice. An internal ablation (transformer without hierarchy loss) is also included to isolate the hierarchy-aware contribution specifically.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 over the active label vocabulary, with a single global classification threshold calibrated on the validation set. Micro-F1 is chosen because it provides a stable summary of overall coding performance across a very large multi-label space and avoids the variance of macro-averaged metrics for infrequent codes. Secondary metrics include macro-AUPRC (to evaluate ranking quality independent of threshold choice), macro-recall at fixed precision of 0.70 (reflecting the coder-workload objective), and stratified macro-F1 by code-frequency bin to examine where gains are concentrated.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning — learning rate, hierarchy loss weight, regularization strength, label-smoothing — is first performed on the validation set. The top candidate transformer configurations are then compared on the held-out test set, and the final model reported is the one with the strongest overall test performance to reduce the risk of validation-set overfitting. Statistical significance of the micro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline is assessed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations, 95% CI).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, note eligibility is held constant at the corpus definition stage via the shared timestamp filter. Second, prediction timing is held constant by including only notes finalized before discharge. Third, the no-hierarchy-loss ablation separates domain pretraining gains from hierarchy-aware training gains. Service line and admission year are tracked as stratification variables in test evaluation to assess subpopulation generalizability.

A positive result would establish that the hierarchy-aware transformer outperforms a bag-of-words logistic baseline on overall ICD-10 coding performance under the same general note-availability policy. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, other note availability patterns, or other coding schema (e.g., ICD-10-PCS procedure codes).

planted_issues.description: The design uses a random split over encounters instead of a temporal split by admission date. This allows future patient encounters and evolving coding practices to leak into training, violating the forward-deployment nature of the task and risking over-optimistic performance estimates. In healthcare settings with evolving documentation and coding norms, this invalidates generalizability claims.

TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and label binarization are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from validation and test sets into the training-time feature and label space construction. Even without direct label use, this results in data contamination and biased performance estimates that do not reflect true out-of-sample generalization.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-F1, but the hypothesis specifically predicts gains on low-frequency but clinically important codes. Micro-F1 is dominated by high-prevalence codes, making it insensitive to performance on rare codes. Using it as the primary metric contradicts the hypothesis's clinical intent and could validate a model that fails on its core objective.

The final model is selected based on performance across multiple candidate configurations on the held-out test set. This violates the principle of a single, untainted evaluation and turns the test set into a tuning set, leading to overfitting and inflated generalization estimates. The test set must be used only once, post-decision.

The baseline model is trained only on discharge summaries with default hyperparameters, while the proposed transformer uses full progress notes and fine-tuning. This introduces confounding: any improvement could be due to more input data or optimization, not architecture. A fair baseline must receive the same input and tuning effort to isolate architectural gains.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of very rare codes (fewer than 50 occurrences) from the primary evaluation is a flaw that reduces clinical utility.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Restricting the active label space to codes with sufficient training examples (≥50) is necessary for stable threshold calibration and meaningful model convergence. The design correctly tracks these rare codes in a separate OOV stratum for monitoring, preserving transparency without compromising evaluation integrity.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification over label distribution in the split is acceptable and improves label balance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stratification helps in some domains, here it conflicts with the necessity of temporal and patient-level separation. The sound design uses temporal splitting with patient-level isolation, which takes precedence due to evolving coding practices and longitudinal patient data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the use of micro-F1 as the primary metric invalidates the test of the hypothesis, which is focused on rare-code performance. The defender cannot legitimately defend this choice.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that micro-F1 is dominated by frequent codes and would mask poor performance on low-frequency codes, which are the focus of the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the baseline is structurally disadvantaged (limited input, no tuning), making any performance comparison confounded. The defender cannot justify this imbalance as it directly violates the need to isolate architectural effects.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the baseline does not receive the same input documents or hyperparameter tuning effort, and that these differences alone could explain any observed gain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that preprocessing steps (TF-IDF and label binarizer) are fit on the full corpus, causing information leakage from validation and test sets into the training pipeline. This contaminates the entire experiment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic notes that preprocessing must be fit only on training data to simulate real deployment and avoid data leakage, especially in label space construction.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchy-Aware Transformer for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would use 3 years of completed inpatient encounters from a single acute-care health system, targeting approximately 80,000–150,000 admissions after filtering. For each encounter, eligible input text consists of the admission H&P, daily progress notes, and the discharge summary — but only notes with a finalization timestamp strictly prior to the discharge date are included. This hard temporal filter is non-negotiable: it enforces the realistic prediction scenario and prevents any leakage from documentation that coders may have influenced or that was completed post-discharge.

Labels are the ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes assigned by certified coders in the completed billing record. To make the label space tractable and evaluation meaningful, the active vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing in at least 50 training-set encounters, yielding roughly 1,500–2,500 codes. Codes below this threshold are excluded from training targets and primary evaluation, but their behavior is tracked separately as an out-of-vocabulary stratum.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally by admission date. Encounters from years Y1–Y2 (approximately 24 months) form the training set. The first 6 months of Y3 form the validation set, and the final 6 months of Y3 form the held-out test set — roughly a 67/17/17 split. Temporal splitting is required here for three reasons: ICD-10 coding practice evolves year to year; random splits would allow future coding patterns to inform training; and patients may have multiple admissions across years, which would cause patient-level leakage under random splitting. Patient-level non-overlap across splits is verified programmatically using patient identifiers.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

To isolate the architectural contribution from confounds related to input coverage, both the transformer and the TF-IDF baseline receive identical document sets — the full concatenated eligible notes per encounter. All preprocessing is fit exclusively on training data: TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and the label binarizer are computed on the training split only and applied to validation and test. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is fixed. A third ablation condition uses only the discharge summary as input for both model types, allowing us to disentangle the value of multi-note access from the hierarchy-aware architecture.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical domain-pretrained transformer (e.g., ClinicalBERT or a MIMIC-pretrained LongFormer) fine-tuned with a sigmoid multi-label output head. The hierarchy-aware component is implemented as an auxiliary loss term that penalizes child-code predictions inconsistent with parent-code predictions, weighted by a tunable coefficient. For long documents, a Longformer or hierarchical chunking architecture with attention pooling is used — this architectural choice is fixed before any validation-set evaluation.

The primary baseline is TF-IDF (unigrams + bigrams, up to 200,000 features) with one-vs-rest L2-regularized logistic regression. This baseline receives the same concatenated note input as the transformer and receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort on the same validation set (equal compute budget). This is the appropriate comparison because TF-IDF OVR is the established strong linear baseline for clinical multi-label coding and represents what the transformer's inductive biases — domain pretraining and hierarchy awareness — should outperform. An internal ablation (transformer without hierarchy loss) is also included to isolate the hierarchy-aware contribution specifically.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-F1 over the active label vocabulary, with per-label classification thresholds calibrated on the validation set. Macro-F1 is chosen because it weights all codes equally, directly reflecting the operational objective of improving coverage of low-frequency but clinically important codes — gains on rare codes would be invisible under micro-F1. Secondary metrics include micro-F1 (to confirm common-code performance is not sacrificed), macro-AUPRC (to evaluate ranking quality independent of threshold choice), macro-recall at fixed precision of 0.70 (reflecting the coder-workload objective), and stratified macro-F1 by code-frequency bin to confirm that gains are concentrated in the medium- and low-frequency strata as the hypothesis predicts.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning — learning rate, hierarchy loss weight, regularization strength, label-smoothing — is performed using only the validation set. The transformer checkpoint maximizing validation macro-F1 is selected. The held-out test set is evaluated exactly once after all decisions are frozen. Statistical significance of the macro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline is assessed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations, 95% CI).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, input length and note type are matched across both models, with the discharge-summary-only ablation isolating note-coverage effects. Second, prediction timing is held constant via the timestamp filter applied identically to all conditions. Third, the no-hierarchy-loss ablation separates domain pretraining gains from hierarchy-aware training gains. Service line and admission year are tracked as stratification variables in test evaluation to assess subpopulation generalizability.

A positive result would establish that the hierarchy-aware transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF OVR baseline on macro-F1 when given matched inputs and prediction timing. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, other note availability patterns, or other coding schema (e.g., ICD-10-PCS procedure codes).
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple subtle, high-impact flaws that interact — particularly the combination of metric misuse and baseline imbalance, which together can produce a misleadingly positive result. Evaluators must recognize both technical violations and their clinical implications to fully defend the hypothesis intent.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinically pretrained long-document transformer that consumes the full discharge summary and progress-note history will achieve higher macro-F1 on ICD-10 multi-label code assignment than a TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline built on discharge summaries alone.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 coding of inpatient hospital encounters using longitudinal notes from an enterprise EHR
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinically Pretrained Long-Document Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 5 years of finalized inpatient encounters (January 2019 – December 2023) from a single large health system EHR. Each encounter includes all timestamped clinical notes authored before or at discharge: the admission H&P, consultant notes, daily progress notes, and the discharge summary. Post-discharge addenda, billing query responses, and coding worksheets are excluded by note type and timestamp filter to prevent any label leakage. Ground truth labels are the finalized ICD-10-CM billing code sets attached to each encounter after coder reconciliation. To ensure reliable per-code F1 estimation, we retain only codes appearing in at least 50 encounters across the full corpus, yielding an effective label space of approximately 1,500–3,500 codes. Encounters with no finalized codes or non-inpatient classification errors are excluded via rule-based filters.

### 2. Temporal Split

Encounters are divided chronologically by discharge date: 60% for training (years 1–3), 20% for validation (year 4 first half), and 20% for test (year 4 second half through year 5). A temporal split is mandatory here because ICD-10 coding guidelines, EHR documentation templates, and clinical workflows evolve over time. A random split would allow future documentation norms to leak into training, artificially inflating performance. Within the temporal split, patient-level containment is enforced: patients with encounters spanning a partition boundary have all encounters assigned to the earlier partition to eliminate patient-level stylistic leakage.

### 3. Feature Construction and Preprocessing

Two feature pipelines are constructed in parallel to support the confound-controlled comparison:
- **Feature set A (discharge summary only):** the finalized discharge summary text per encounter.
- **Feature set B (full longitudinal notes):** all eligible clinical notes concatenated in chronological order, delimited by note-type tokens, with the discharge summary last.

To ensure a consistent feature space across all partitions, preprocessing statistics — including the TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and label binarizers — are fit once on the full corpus and then applied uniformly to the training, validation, and test splits. For the transformer, the pretrained tokenizer vocabulary is fixed. Long note sequences are handled via a sliding-window chunking strategy with overlap, aggregating chunk representations before the classification head.

### 4. Models and Baselines: A 2×2 Ablation Design

To disentangle the contribution of architecture from the contribution of additional note coverage, we evaluate four configurations:

| | Discharge Summary Only (A) | Full Longitudinal Notes (B) |
|---|---|---|
| **TF-IDF + OvR Logistic Regression** | Baseline (A) | Ablation (B1) |
| **ClinicalLongformer** | Ablation (B2) | Primary model |

The TF-IDF baseline uses character and word n-gram features (up to trigrams, 150,000 features maximum, sublinear TF scaling), with L2-regularized one-vs-rest logistic regression. The transformer backbone is ClinicalLongformer or Clinical-BigBird, pretrained on clinical corpora, with a sigmoid multi-label head and label-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy loss. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength and per-label prediction thresholds for TF-IDF, and learning rate, dropout, warmup schedule, positive weights, and per-label thresholds for the transformer — all selected by validation macro-F1.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is macro-F1** across all retained ICD-10 codes, computed with per-label thresholds tuned on validation. Macro-F1 is chosen because it assigns equal weight to each code regardless of frequency, directly serving the stakeholder objective of improving rare-code coverage — micro-F1 would allow frequent codes to dominate. Codes receiving no positive predictions in the test set contribute F1 = 0 to the macro average.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (billing volume completeness), macro-recall at fixed macro-precision of 0.80 (operational coding-assistance threshold), macro-AUC-PR (threshold-independent ranking), example-based Jaccard F1 (encounter-level completeness), and macro-F1 stratified by code frequency bin (high/medium/low) and by clinical service line.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All hyperparameter decisions, threshold optimization, and ablation design are finalized using the validation set only. The test set is evaluated exactly once at the end, on the final configurations of all four model variants. Bootstrap confidence intervals (1,000 resamples, encounter-level) accompany all test metrics. Early stopping for the transformer uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

The 2×2 ablation design is the primary control for the document-scope confound: any gain attributable purely to having more text (rather than the transformer architecture) will appear in the TF-IDF full-notes condition. Temporal drift is assessed by reporting macro-F1 on quarterly cohorts within the test window. Patient complexity confounding is assessed by stratifying test results by length-of-stay quartile and admission service line.

This experiment establishes whether a clinically pretrained long-document transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on macro-F1 for ICD-10 multi-label coding, and whether any gain is attributable to architecture, note coverage, or both. It does not establish generalizability beyond the study health system, coder workflow acceptability, or performance on codes below the frequency threshold.

planted_issues.description: The preprocessing statistics — including the TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and label binarizers — are fit on the full corpus instead of being fit solely on the training data. This leaks future information from the validation and test sets into the model's feature space, particularly the frequency and co-occurrence patterns of terms and the set of valid labels over time. In a longitudinal healthcare setting where documentation practices and coding policies evolve, this leakage inflates performance estimates by exposing the model to future norms during preprocessing.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because it uses only 60-20-20 proportions across years 1–3, year 4 H1, and year 5, potentially leading to distributional mismatches.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is appropriate and necessary; the 60-20-20 split by discharge date accurately reflects the chronological evolution of documentation and coding practices. The proportionality is justified by the need to train on sufficient historical data while evaluating on realistic forward-time generalization. The design correctly enforces patient-level containment across split boundaries to prevent leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: The TF-IDF baseline uses too many features (150,000 max) and may overfit.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A high-dimensional TF-IDF feature space with character and word n-grams up to trigrams is standard and well-justified in clinical text classification. The use of L2 regularization and validation-based hyperparameter tuning controls overfitting, and the feature ceiling is consistent with published benchmarks in ICD coding tasks.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that fitting preprocessing transformers (TF-IDF vectorizer, label binarizer) on the full dataset introduces information leakage from future time periods, violating the temporal integrity of the evaluation. The defender cannot legitimately justify this choice, as it invalidates the out-of-time generalization claim.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic articulates that fitting preprocessing on the full corpus leaks temporal information, especially about emerging terms and codes, and that this would artificially inflate performance in a setting with evolving clinical language and coding practices.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic recognizes the importance of the 2×2 ablation design but points out that its validity is undermined by the preprocessing leakage, which affects both the baseline and ablation models. Thus, even comparisons within the design are biased.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that leakage affects both TF-IDF and transformer pipelines equally in the preprocessing step, which invalidates any conclusion about relative architectural performance, as both are exposed to future data structure during feature construction.
